When \textit{look} is a verb, as in \textit{I looked round the room}, you are focusing on the action of looking.

When you use \textit{look} as a noun in a delexical structure, you are naming a event, something that is complete.

This structure often seems to be preferred.

Note that the verb corresponding to the delexical structure is often intransitive.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{made a signal}.
    
    She \underline{signaled} for a taxi.
    
    A couple \underline{were having a drink} at a table by the window.
    
    A few students \underline{were drinking} at the bar.
    
    She \underline{gave an amused laugh}.
    
    They both \underline{laughed}.
    
    He \underline{gave a vague reply}.
    
    They \underline{replied} to his letter.
\end{example}
There are also some verbs that are transitive.
\begin{example}
    Fans tried to \underline{get a glimpse} of the singer.
    
    I \underline{glimpsed} a bright flash of gold on the left.
    
    He \underline{gave a little sniff}.
    
    She \underline{sniffed} the air.
    
    Comis \underline{took a photograph} of her.
    
    They \underline{photographed} the pigeons in Trafalgar Square.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.36} The noun that is the object of the delexical verb is often in the singular and is usually preceded by \textit{a} or \textit{an}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{made a remark} about the weather.
    
    She \underline{gave a cry} when I came in.
    
    I \underline{might take a stroll}.
\end{example}
There are some countable nouns that are almost always used in the singular after a delexical verb.

Here is a list of these nouns:
\begin{example}
    cry, feel, grouse, grumble, need, read, smell, taste, try
\end{example}
Note that these words are more commonly used as verbs in the language as a whole.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.36} You can also use a delexical verb followed by a plural noun.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{took little sips} of the cold drink.
    
    He \underline{took photographs} of Vita in her summer house.
    
    The newspaper made unpleasant remarks about his wife.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.37} It is also occasionally possible to follow a delexical verb with an uncountable noun.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{have made progress} in both science and art.
    
    Cal \underline{took charge} of this side of their education.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.38} 1 difference in meaning between using a structure containing a delexical verb and a verb with a similar meaning is that the delexical structure can give the impression that the event you are describing is brief.

E.g., \textit{She gave a scream} suggests that there was only 1 quick scream, whereas \textit{She creamed} does not suggest that the event was brief.
\begin{example}
    Mr. Sutton \underline{gave a shout} of triumph.
    
    Zoe \underline{gave a sigh} of relief.
    
    He \underline{gave a laugh}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.39} Another reason for choosing a delexical structure is that you can add further details about the event by using adjectives in front of the noun, rather than by using adverbs.

It is more natural, e.g., to say \textit{He gave a quick furtive glance round the room} than to say \textit{He glanced quickly and furtively round the room}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{gave a long lecture} about Roosevelt.
    
    She \underline{had a good cry}.
    
    He was forced to \underline{make a humiliating apology}.
    
    These legends \underline{hold a romantic fascination} for many Japanese.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.40} There are some nouns used in delexical structures that do not correspond in form to a verb that has a similar meaning to the delexical structure.

Sometimes there is such a verb, but the form is slightly different.
\begin{example}
    Work experience allows students to \underline{make more effective career decisions}.
    
    I \underline{decided} I wouldn't resign after all.
    
    He \underline{made the shortest speech} I've ever heard.
    
    Iain \underline{spoke} candidly about the crash.
\end{example}
In other cases, there is no corresponding verb with a similar meaning at all and so there is no other structure that can be used.
\begin{example}
    He had been out all day \underline{taking pictures} of the fighting.
    
    That is \underline{a very foolish attitude to take}.
    
    She \underline{made a number of relevant points}.
    
    Try not \underline{to make so much noise}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.41} In most cases, only 1 delexical verb is used with any particular noun.

%
The following examples show nouns that are used after \textit{have}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{have a desperate need} to communicate.
    
    They \underline{had a fundamental belief} in their own superiority.
    
    She \underline{had a good cry}.
    
    Let's not \underline{have a quarrel}.
    
    We should \underline{have a talk}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that are used after \textit{have}:
\begin{example}
    argument, belief, chat, cry, dance, disagreement, fall, fight, grouse, grumble, need, quarrel, respect, sleep, talk
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.42} The following examples show nouns that are used after \textit{take}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{takes no interest} in his children.
    
    $\ldots$ kids \underline{taking turns} to use a playground slide.
    
    He \underline{was taking no chances}.
    
    She was prepared \underline{to take great risks}.
    
    Davis \underline{took the lead} in blaming the pilots.
    
    The Government fought against suggestions that it should \underline{take full blame} for the affair.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that are used after \textit{take}.

The 1st set of nouns are countable nouns; the 2nd set of nouns are uncountable nouns or always either singular or plural:
\begin{example}
    attitude, chance, decision, interest, photo, photograph, picture, risk, turn
    
    blame, care, charge, consequences, form, lead, offence, office, power, responsibility, shape, time, trouble
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.43} Many nouns can be used after \textit{give}.

%
Some of these nouns refer to noises that people make, or expressions they make with their face.

Using \textit{give} with 1 of these nouns often suggests that the action is involuntary or that it is not necessarily directed at other people.

E.g., \textit{She gave a scream} suggests that she could not help screaming.
\begin{example}
    The young cashier \underline{gave a patient sigh}.
    
    Roger \underline{gave a grin} of sheer delight.
    
    He \underline{gave a shrill gasp} of shock.
    
    Both of them \underline{gave an involuntary little giggle}.
    
    He \underline{gave a soft chuckle}.
\end{example} 
Here is a list of nouns that refer to noises people make, or expressions they make with their face:
\begin{example}
    chuckle, cry, gasp, giggle, grin, groan, laugh, scowl, scream, shout, shriek, sigh, smile, sniff, snigger, whistle, yell
\end{example}
Another group of nouns are often preceded by an indirect object (i.e., an object that refers to the person or thing that received the action) because they describe activities that involve someone else, apart from the subject.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{gave us a wonderfully warm welcome}.
    
    Elaine \underline{gave him a hug}.
    
    He \underline{gave her hand a squeeze}.
    
    He \underline{gave him a good kick}.
    
    She \underline{gave him a long kiss}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that can be preceded by an indirect object:
\begin{example}
    clue, glance, hint, hug, kick, kiss, look, punch, push, ring, shove, slap, squeeze, welcome
\end{example}
A 3rd group of nouns refer to actions involving something being said.
\begin{example}
    The poetry professor is required \underline{to give a lecture} every term.
    
    Lord Young \underline{will be giving a 1st-hand account} of the economic difficulties the Russians are struggling to overcome.
    
    Senator Brown \underline{has given warning} that conflict over the plans could lead to a constitutional crisis.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that refer to actions involving something being said:
\begin{example}
    account, advice, answer, example, information, interview, lecture, news, reason, report, speech, summary, talk, thought, warning
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.44} Many nouns can be used after \textit{make}.

%
The delexical structures using a lot of these nouns are closely related to \textbf{reporting structures}, which are explained in Chap. 7.

There is usually a related verb that can be used followed by a reported clause.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{made a remark} about the weather.
    
    Allen \underline{remarked} that at times he thought he was back in America.
    
    Now and then she \underline{makes a comment} on something.
    
    He \underline{commented} that he was only doing his job.
    
    I \underline{haven't made a full confession}, sir.
    
    Fox \underline{confessed} that he had stolen the money.
    
    The cricketers \underline{made a public protest} against apartheid.
    
    She \underline{protested} that his comments were sexist.
    
    I \underline{made a secret signal} to him.
    
    The Bank of England \underline{signaled} that there would be no change in interest rates.
    
    You \underline{made the right decision}.
    
    1 candidate resigned, \underline{deciding} that banking was not for her.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that are used after \textit{make} and have a related reporting verb:
\begin{example}
    arrangement, claim, comment, confession, decision, promise, protest, remark, signal, suggestion
\end{example}
Other nouns used with \textit{make} express actions involving something being said, or describe change, results, effort, and so on.
\begin{example}
    I'll \underline{make some enquiries} for you.
    
    They agreed \underline{to make a few minor changes}.
    
    They \underline{made an emotional appeal} for their daughter's safe return.
    
    He \underline{made an attempt} to calm down.
    
    He \underline{has made a significant contribution} to the success of the business.
\end{example}
Here is a list of other nouns that are used after \textit{make}:
\begin{example}
    appeal, attempt, change, charge, contribution, effort, enquiry, impression, noise, point, progress, recovery, sound, speech, start, success
\end{example}
Note that, unlike the other nouns in this list, \textit{progress} is uncountable.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.45} There are some nouns that can be used after either \textit{have} or \textit{take}.

In general \textit{have} is more common with these nouns in British English and \textit{take} is more common in American English.

There is sometimes a slight difference in emphasis: using \textit{have} puts more emphasis on the experience, and using \textit{take} puts more emphasis on the performer of the action.

%
1 group of these nouns refer to physical activities.
\begin{example}
    I'd rather \underline{have a swim}.
    
    \underline{Have a drink}.
    
    She decided to \underline{take a stroll} along the beach.
    
    I \underline{took a bath}, my 2nd that day.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that refer to physical activities:
\begin{example}
    bath, break, drink, holiday, jog, paddle, rest, run, shower, stroll, swim, vacation (\emph{American}), walk
\end{example}
Another group refer to actions that involve using our senses.
\begin{example}
    She should let a doctor \underline{have a look} at you.
    
    Even Sally \underline{had a little sip} of wine.
    
    A Harvard scientist was once allowed in to \underline{have a peep}.
    
    Mark \underline{took a bite} of meat.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that refer to such actions:
\begin{example}
    bite, feel, look, peep, sip, smell, sniff, taste
\end{example}

\subsection{Verbs that can be used both with and without an object}
\noindent\fbox{3.46} There are several reasons why you can use verbs both with and without an object.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.47} 1 important reason for using verbs both with and without an object is that many verbs have more than 1 common meaning.

E.g., the verb \textit{run} is used without an object when it is used in the sense \textit{to move quickly}.

But \textit{run} has an object when it is used in the sense \textit{to manage} or \textit{operate}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{runs} in order to keep fit.
    
    She \underline{runs a hotel}.
    
    She \underline{reflected} for a moment and then decided to back out.
    
    The figures \underline{reflected} the company's attempts to increase its profile.
    
    I \underline{can manage} perfectly well on my own.
    
    I \underline{can} no longer \underline{manage my life}.
    
    She \underline{moved} rather gracefully.
    
    The whole incident \underline{had moved her} profoundly.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.48} Here is a list of verbs that can be used both with and without an object, depending on which meaning you are using:
\begin{example}
    add, aim, beat, blow, call, change, cheat, count, draw, dress, drive, escape, exercise, fit, fly, follow, hang, head, hold, hurt, leak, lose, manage, meet, miss, move, pass, play, point, press, propose, reflect, run, shoot, show, sink, spread, stand, stretch, strike, study, tend, touch, turn, win
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.49} Many verbs in English can be used with or without an object, with the same basic meaning.

The object is not needed when it is obvious what type of thing you are talking about.

%
E.g., you could say either \textit{She eats food slowly} or \textit{She eats slowly}.

It is obvious in this context that what she eats is food, and so you only mention food if you want to emphasize the fact (which is unlikely), or if you want to say what kind of food she eats.

%
With verbs like these, you normally use an object only when you want to be specific or when you want to contrast what happened on 1 specific occasion with what happens normally.

E.g., you would say \textit{I've been studying history}, as opposed to \textit{I've been studying}, only if you want or need to mention the subject specifically, or if you normally study something else.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a healthy person who \underline{eats} sensibly.
    
    Twice a week he \underline{eats an apple} for lunch.
    
    He raised his own glass and \underline{drank}.
    
    He \underline{drank a good deal of coffee}.
    
    He had won, and she \underline{had helped}.
    
    She \underline{could help him} to escape.
    
    I \underline{cooked} for about 8 directors.
    
    She had never \underline{cooked dinner} for anyone.
    
    I washed and \underline{ironed} for them.
    
    She \underline{ironed my shirt}.
    
    Rudolph \underline{waved} and went into the house.
    
    She smiled and \underline{waved her hand}.
    
    She sat and \underline{typed}.
    
    She \underline{typed a letter} to the paper in question.
\end{example}
You need to give the object when it is different from the one that people would normally associate with the verb.

E.g., \textit{to wave} is usually interpreted as meaning to \textit{wave your hand}, so if something else is being waved, you have to mention it.
\begin{example}
    He waved \underline{a piece of paper} in his left hand.
    
    Charlie washed \underline{Susan's feet}.
\end{example}
You also mention the object when you want to say something specific about it.
\begin{example}
    He washed \underline{his summer clothes} and put them away.
    
    Bond waved \underline{a cheerful hand}.
    
    I could save \underline{quite a lot of money}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.50} Here is a list of verbs that can be used without an object when it is obvious what sort of thing is involved:
\begin{example}
    borrow, change, clean, cook, draw, drink, drive, dust, eat, film, help, iron, learn, lend, marry, paint, park, point, read, ride, save, sing, smoke, spend, steal, study, type, wash, wave, write
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.51} There is another group of verbs that usually have an object but that can be used without an object with the same meaning.

E.g., if you have already mentioned the place where something happened, you can say \textit{I left}, without naming the place again.
\begin{example}
    At least she thanked them and \underline{left}.
    
    He turned away an walked quickly up the passage. I locked the door and \underline{followed}.
    
    I was in the middle of a quiet meal when the tanks \underline{attacked}.
    
    She did not look round when he \underline{entered}.
    
    The sentry fired at the doctor and fortunately \underline{missed}.
    
    Only 200 or 300 men belonged to the Union before the wr, now thousands \underline{joined}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.52} Here is a list of verbs that can be used without an object when the object has already been mentioned:
\begin{example}
    accept, aim, answer, approach, ask, attack, begin, bite, blow, board, call, check, choose, consider, direct, dry, enter, explain, fit, follow, forget, gain, guess, improve, join, judge, know, lead, leave, lose, mind, miss, move, notice, observe, offer, order, pass, phone, play, produce, pull, push, remember, ring, rule, search, serve, share, sign, strike, telephone, understand, watch, win
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.53} If you think that the object may not be obvious from what has been said or if you particularly want to draw attention to it, you mention it.
\begin{example}
    All I know is that Michael and I never \underline{left the house}.
    
    Miss Lindley \underline{followed Rose} into the shop.
    
    They were unaware they had \underline{attacked a British warship}.
    
    A man \underline{entered the shop} and demanded money.
    
    She threw the 1st dart and \underline{missed the board} altogether.
    
    I \underline{had joined an athletic club} in Chicago.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.54} There are not many verbs that always have an object or never have an object.

The decision about whether or not to mention an object is left to the users.

If they think that the people reading or listening to them will have no difficulty in working out what person or thing is affected by the action, then they can leave out the object.

If they think that this will not be clear, they will use an object in order to prevent misunderstanding.

The main reasons for omitting the object are that it is obvious from the meaning of the verb itself, or that it is obvious from what has already been said.

\subsection{Verbs that can take an object or a prepositional phrase}
\noindent\fbox{3.55} There is a small group of verbs that can be followed by either an object or a prepositional phrase.

The verb \textit{fight} is 1 of these verbs, so that, e.g., you can say \textit{He fought the enemy} or \textit{He fought against the enemy}.
\begin{example}
    The Polish Army \underline{fought the Germans} for nearly 5 weeks.
    
    He \underline{was fighting against history}.
    
    The New Zealand rugby team \underline{played South Africa's Springboks}.
    
    In his youth, Thomas \underline{played against Glamorgan}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.56} There is usually little difference in meaning between using the verb on its own and following it with a preposition.

E.g., there is very little difference in meaning between \textit{brush} and \textit{brush against, gnaw} and \textit{gnaw at}, and \textit{hiss} and \textit{hiss at} in the following examples.
\begin{example}
    Her arm \underline{brushed my cheek}.
    
    Something \underline{brushed against the back of the shelter}.
    
    Rabbits often \underline{gnaw the woodwork of their cages}.
    
    Insects \underline{had been gnawing at the wood}.
    
    They \underline{hissed the Mayor} at the ceremony.
    
    Frederica \underline{hissed at him}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.57} Here is a list of verbs that can be use with an object or a prepositional phrase, with little difference in meaning:
\begin{example}
    boo (at), brush (against), check (on), distinguish (between), enter (for), fight (against/with), gain (in), gnaw (at), hiss (at), infiltrate (into), jeer (at), juggle (with), mock (at), mourn (for), nibble (at), play (against), rule (over), sip (at), sniff (at), tug (at), twiddle (with)
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.58} Many of the verbs that can take an object or a prepositional phrase are verbs, e.g. \textit{wander} and \textit{cross}, that describe physical movement.

The preposition is one that indicates place, and so allows you to emphasize the physical position of the subject in relation to the object.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{wandered the halls of the Art Institute}.
    
    He \underline{wandered through the streets of New York}.
    
    I \underline{crossed the Mississippi}.
    
    The car \underline{had crossed over the river} to Long Island.
    
    We \underline{climbed the mountain}.
    
    I \underline{climbed up the tree}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that describe movement, and examples of the prepositions that can follow them:
\begin{example}
    chase (after), climb (up), cross (over), jump (over), leap (over), reach (across), roam (over/through), run (across), skirt (round), walk (through), wander (through)
\end{example}

\subsection{Changing your focus by changing the subject: \textit{I opened the door, The door opened}}
\noindent\fbox{3.59} Some verbs allow you to describe an action from the point of view of the performer of the action or from the point of view of something that is affected by the action.

This means that the same verb can be used with an object, or without an object, and without the original performer being mentioned.

%
In the 1st example below, \textit{the door} is the object of the verb \textit{opened}, but in the 2nd example \textit{the door} is the subject of \textit{opened} and there is no mention of who opened the door.
\begin{example}
    I opened \underline{the door} and peered into the room.
    
    Suddenly \underline{the door} opened.
    
    An explosion shook \underline{the rooms}.
    
    \underline{The whole room} shook.
\end{example}
Note that the object of the transitive verb, which is the subject of the intransitive verb, usually refers to a thing, not a person.

%
Verbs that can have the same thing as their object, when transitive, or their subject, when intransitive, are called \textbf{ergative verbs}.

There are several hundred ergative verbs in regular use in current English.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.60} Many ergative verbs describe events that involve a change from 1 state to another.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{was slowing} his pace.
    
    She was aware that the aircraft's taxiing pace \underline{had slowed}.
    
    I \underline{shattered} the glass.
    
    Wine bottles \underline{had shattered} all over the pavement.
    
    They \underline{have closed} the town's only pub.
    
    The firm \underline{has changed} its name.
    
    Over the next few months their work pattern \underline{changed}.
    
    The driver \underline{stopped} the car.
    
    A big car \underline{stopped}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.61} Here is a list of ergative verbs that describe events involving a change of some kind:
\begin{example}
    age, begin, bend, bleach, break, burn, burst, change, close, continue, crack, darken, decrease, diminish, disperse, double, drown, dry, empty, end, fade, finish, grow, improve, increase, open, quicken, rot, shatter, shrink, shut, slow, split, spread, start, stick, stop, stretch, tear, thicken, widen, worsen
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.62} There are many other ergative verbs that relate specifically to certain areas of meaning.

E.g., some relate to food and cooking, others describe physical movement, and others involve a vehicle as the object of the transitive verb or the subject of the intransitive verb.
\begin{example}
    I\underline{'ve boiled} an egg.
    
    The porridge \underline{is boiling}.
    
    I\underline{'m cooking} spaghetti.
    
    The rice \underline{is cooking}.
    
    The birds \underline{turned} their heads sharply at the sound.
    
    Vorster's head \underline{turned}.
    
    She \underline{rested} her head on his shoulder.
    
    Her head \underline{rested} on the edge of the table.
    
    She \underline{had crashed} the car twice.
    
    Pollock's car \underline{crashed} into a clump of trees.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.63} Here is a list of verbs relating to food, physical movement, and vehicles:
\begin{example}
    bake, boil, cook, defrost, fry, melt, roast, simmer, thicken
    
    balance, drop, move, rest, rock, shake, spin, stand, steady, swing, turn
    
    back, crash, drive, fly, park, reverse, run, sail
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.64} Note that some verbs are used ergatively with 1 or 2 nouns only.

E.g., you can say \textit{He fired a gun} or \textit{The gun fired}.

You can also say \textit{He fired a bullet}, but you would not normally say \textit{The bullet fired}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{rang} the bell.
    
    The bell \underline{rang}.
    
    A car \underline{was sounding} its horn.
    
    A horn \underline{sounded} in the night.
    
    He \underline{had caught} his sleeve on a splinter of wood.
    
    The hat \underline{caught} on a bolt and tore.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.65} Here is a list of verbs that can be used ergatively with the noun, or type of noun, that is given:
\begin{example}
    catch (an article of clothing), fire (a gun, rifle, pistol), play (music), ring (a bell, the alarm), show (an emotion e.g. fear, anger), sound (a horn, the alarm)
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.66} There are a few ergative verbs that usually have an adverb or some other phrase or clause when they are used intransitively.

This is because you choose this structure when you want to emphasize how something behaves when affected in some way, and so the person who does the action is not important.
\begin{example}
    I like the new Range Rover. It \underline{handles beautifully}.
    
    Wool \underline{washes well} if you treat it carefully.
\end{example}
Here is a list of ergative verbs that are usually followed by some extra information when they are used intransitively:
\begin{example}
    clean, freeze, handle, mark, polish, sell, stain, wash
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.67} Note that ergative verbs perform a similar function to the \textbf{passive} because they allow you to avoid mentioning who or what does the action.

E.g., you could say \textit{Jane froze a lot of peas from the garden}.

If you were not interested in who froze them but in what she froze, you could say \textit{A lot of peas were frozen}, using the passive.

If you were interested in how they froze, you could say, \textit{The peas from the garden froze really well}, making use of the fact that the verb is ergative.

%
For information about the \textbf{passive}, see \textbf{9.8--9.24}.

\subsection{Verbs that involve people affecting each other with the same action: \textit{John \& Mary argued}}
\noindent\fbox{3.68} Some verbs can describe processes that involve 2 people or 2 groups of people doing the same thing to each other.

E.g., \textit{John and Mary argued} means that John argued with Mary and Mary argued with John.
\begin{example}
    The pair of you \underline{have argued} about that for years.
    
    He came out and we \underline{hugged}.
    
    They \underline{competed} furiously.
\end{example}
These verbs are called \textbf{reciprocal verbs}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.69} 1 of the structures in which you use reciprocal verbs is where the 2 groups are put together in a plural subject and the verb is then used without an object.
\begin{example}
    Their faces \underline{touched}.
    
    Their children \underline{are always fighting}.
    
    They \underline{kissed}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.70} When you want to emphasize that both participants are equally involved in the action, you put \textit{each other} or \textit{one another} after the verb.
\begin{example}
    We embraced \underline{each other}.
    
    They kissed \underline{each other} in greeting.
    
    They fought \underline{each other} desperately for it.
    
    The 2 boys started hitting \underline{one another}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reciprocal verbs that are us transitively with the pronouns \textit{each other} and \textit{one another}:
\begin{example}
    consult, cuddle, embrace, engage, fight, hug, kiss, marry, match, meet, touch
\end{example}
With some verbs you need to use a preposition, usually \textit{with}, in front of \textit{each other} or \textit{one another}.
\begin{example}
    You've got to be able to communicate \underline{with each other}.
    
    3rd World countries are competing \underline{with each other} for a restricted market.
    
    The 2 actors began to engage \underline{with one another}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reciprocal verbs that must be followed by \textit{with} before the pronouns \textit{each other} and \textit{one another}.
\begin{example}
    agree, alternate, argue, balance, clash, coincide, collide, combine, communicate, conflict, consult, contend, contrast, converse, co-operate, disagree, engage, integrate, mate, merge, mix, quarrel, struggle
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used with a preposition other than \textit{with}:
\begin{example}
    compete (against/with), correspond (to/with), fight (against/with), part (from), relate (to), separate (from), talk (to/with)
\end{example}
Note that \textit{consult, engage}, and \textit{fight} can be used either with an object or with a preposition.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.71} In the examples given above, the speaker or writer believes that both people or groups are equally involved in the event, because both are the subject.

However, the user may want to focus on 1 person more than the other.

In this case, a noun that refers to that person is put in subject position.

%
If the verb can be used with an object, a noun referring to the other participant is used as the object of the verb.
\begin{example}
    He embraced \underline{her}.
    
    She married \underline{a young engineer}.
    
    You could meet \underline{me} at a restaurant.
    
    He is responsible for killing \underline{many people}.
\end{example}
If the verb needs a preposition after it, the other noun is used as the object of the preposition.
\begin{example}
    Our return coincided \underline{with the arrival of bad weather}.
    
    Youths clashed \underline{with police} in Belfast.
    
    The distribution of aid corresponds \underline{to need}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.72} People sometimes make 1 person or group the subject when the event is a violent or unpleasant one, in order to make them appear aggressive or responsible for the violence.

E.g., the headline \textit{Police clash with youths} might suggest that the police were responsible for the clash, even though the youths also clashed with the police.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Paul} collided with a large man in a sweat-stained shirt.
    
    \underline{The role of worker} conflicts with the role of parent.
    
    She liked him even when \underline{she} was quarreling with him.
\end{example}

\subsection{Verbs that can have 2 objects: \textit{give someone something}}
\noindent\fbox{3.73} Sometimes you may want to talk about an event that involves someone in addition to the people or things that are the subject and object of the clause.

This 3rd participant is someone who benefits from the action or receives something as a result.

They become the \textbf{indirect object} of the clause.

The \textbf{direct object}, as usual, is the person or thing that something is done to.

E.g., in \textit{I gave John a book, John} is the indirect object and \textit{the book} is the direct object.

%
The indirect object is put immediately after the verb, in front of the direct object.
\begin{example}
    Dad gave \underline{me a car}.
    
    Can you pass \underline{me the sugar} please?
    
    She brought \underline{me a boiled egg and toast}.
    
    He had lent \underline{Tim the money}.
    
    A man promised \underline{him a job}.
    
    The distraction provided \underline{us a chance to relax}. (Am)
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.74} Instead of putting the indirect object in front of the direct object, it is possible to put it in a phrase beginning with \textit{to} or \textit{for} that comes after the direct object.
\begin{example}
    He handed his room key \underline{to the receptionist}.
    
    Ralph passed a message \underline{to Jack}.
    
    He gave it \underline{to me}.
\end{example}
This structure is used particularly in cases where you want to focus on the indirect object.

You can use it, e.g., when the indirect object is significantly longer than the direct object.
\begin{example}
    He had taught English \underline{to all the youth of Ceylon and India}.
    
    He copied the e-mail \underline{to every single 1 of his staff}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.75} It is normal to use this prepositional structure when the direct object is a pronoun e.g. \textit{it} or \textit{them}.
\begin{example}
    I took the bottle and offered \underline{it to Oakley}.
    
    Woodward finished the 2nd page and passed \underline{it to the editor}.
    
    It was the only pound he had and he gave \underline{it to the little boy}.
    
    God has sent \underline{you to me}.
\end{example}
Note that in informal spoken English, some people put the indirect object in front of the direct object when both objects are pronouns.

E.g., some people say \textit{He gave me it} rather \textit{He gave it to me}.

Both pronouns are unstressed and both refer to information that is already known, and so it does not matter what order they come in.

%
If you want to put the indirect object in a phrase that begins with a preposition, you use the preposition \textit{to} with some verbs, especially ones where the direct object is something that is transferred from 1 person to another.
\begin{example}
    Mr Schell wrote a letter the other day \underline{to the New York Times}.
    
    I had lent my apartment \underline{to a friend} for the weekend.
    
    I took out the black box and handed it \underline{to her}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can have an indirect object introduced by \textit{to}:
\begin{example}
    accord, advance, award, bring, deal, feed, forward, give, grant, hand, lease, leave, lend, loan, mail, offer, owe, pass, pay, play, post, quote, read, rent, repay, sell, send, serve, show, sing, take, teach, tell, write
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.77} If the action you are describing involves 1 person doing something that will benefit another person, you can use the preposition \textit{for} to introduce the indirect object.
\begin{example}
    He left a note \underline{for her} on the table.
    
    He poured more champagne \underline{for the 3 of them}.
    
    She brought presents \underline{for the children}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can have an indirect object introduced by \textit{for}:
\begin{example}
    book, bring, build, buy, cash, cook, cut, design, fetch, find, fix, get, guarantee, keep, leave, make, mix, order, paint, pick, play, pour, prepare, reserve, save, secure, set, sing, spare, take, win, write
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.78} [i] Note that the verbs \textit{bring, leave, play, sing, take}, and \textit{write} are in both of the lists (\textbf{3.76--3.77}).

That is because there are a few verbs that can take either \textit{to} or \textit{for} in front of the indirect object, depending on the meaning you want to express.

E.g., \textit{Karen wrote a letter to her boyfriend} means that the letter was addressed to her boyfriend and was for him to read.

\textit{Karen wrote a letter for her boyfriend} means that her boyfriend wanted to send someone else a letter and Karen was the person who actually wrote it.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.79} [i] There are some verbs that take 2 objects where the indirect object almost always comes in front of the direct object rather than being introduced by \textit{to} or \textit{for}.

E.g., you say \textit{He begrudged his daughter the bread she ate} and \textit{She allowed her son only 2 pounds a week}.

It would be very unusual to say \textit{She allowed 2 pounds a week to her son}.

%
Here is a list of verbs that do not usually have their indirect object introduced by \textit{to} or \textit{for}:
\begin{example}
    allow, ask, begrudge, bet, cause, charge, cost, deny, draw, envy, forgive, grudge, promise, refuse
\end{example}
Note that \textit{wish} can be used as this sort of verb when its direct object is a word or phrase like \textit{luck, good luck}, or \textit{happy birthday}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.80} When you use a passive form of a verb with 2 objects, either the direct object or the indirect object can become the subject.

See \textbf{9.20} for full information.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.81} [i] When the subject and the indirect object refer to the same person, you can use a \textbf{reflexive pronoun} as the indirect object.
\begin{example}
    I'm going to buy \underline{myself} some new clothes.
    
    \underline{He} had got \underline{himself} a car.
    
    \underline{He} cooked \underline{himself} an omelette.
\end{example}
\textbf{Reflexive pronouns} are explained in paragraphs \textbf{1.111--1.118}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.82} Most of the verbs listed above as verbs that take 2 objects can be used with the same meaning with just a direct object.
\begin{example}
    He left \underline{a note}.
    
    She fetched \underline{a jug} from the kitchen.
\end{example}
However, the following verbs always or usually have both a direct object and an indirect object:
\begin{example}
    accord, advance, allow, deny, give, hand, lend, loan, show, tell
\end{example}
A few verbs can be used with the person who benefits from the action, or receives something, as the direct object.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{fed the baby} when she woke.
    
    I \underline{forgive you}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of these verbs:
\begin{example}
    ask, envy, feed, forgive, pay, teach
\end{example}

\subsection{Phrasal verbs: \textit{I sat down, She woke me up}}
\noindent\fbox{3.83} There is a special group of verbs that consist of 2 or 3 words.

These are called \textbf{phrasal verbs}.

They consist of
\begin{itemize}
    \item a verb followed by an adverb:
    \begin{example}
        He \underline{sat down}.
        
        The noise gradually \underline{died away}.
        
        The cold weather \underline{set in}.
    \end{example}
    \item a verb followed by a preposition (sometimes called a \textbf{prepositional verb}):
    \begin{example}
        She \underline{looked after} her invalid mother.
        
        She \underline{sailed through} her exams.
        
        She \underline{fell down} the steps and broke her ankle.
    \end{example}
    \item or a verb followed by an adverb and a preposition:
    \begin{example}
        You may \underline{come up against} unexpected difficulties.
        
        I \underline{look forward to} reading it.
        
        Fame has \underline{crept up on} her almost by accident.
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
By combining a verb and an adverb or preposition in this way, you can extend the usual meaning of the verb or create a new meaning, different from any that the verb has on its own.

You cannot, therefore, always guess the meaning of a phrasal verb from the usual meanings of the verb and the adverb or preposition.

E.g., if someone says \textit{I give up} they do not give anything to anyone, nor is there any upward movement involved.

%
In the case of a few phrasal verbs, the 1st part is not found independently as a verb at all.

E.g., there are phrasal verbs \textit{sum up, tamper with}, and \textit{zero in on}, but no verbs \textit{sum, tamper}, or \textit{zero}.

%
Note that phrasal verbs are never written as a single word or with a hyphen.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.84} Most phrasal verbs consist of 2 words.

These are explained below in paragraphs \textbf{3.85--3.110}.

3-word phrasal verbs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{3.111-3.113}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.85} Some phrasal verbs are used without an object.

These plural verbs are generally verb plus adverb combinations.
\begin{example}
    Rosamund \underline{went away} for a few days.
    
    The boys \underline{were fooling around}.
    
    She \underline{must have dozed off}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.86} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that consist of a verb and an adverb with no object:
\begin{example}
    back away/down/off, balance out, barge in, bear up, boil over, bounce back, bow down/out, branch out, break away/out, butt in, camp out, cast about, catch on, change down, check up, chip in, climb down, close in, cloud over, club together, come about/along/apart/away/back/down/forward/in/on/out/round/to/up, cool off, creep in, crop up, cry off, cuddle up, curl up, cut in, die away/down/out, dine out, double back, doze off, drag on, drop back/by/out, ease up, ebb away, end up, fade away/out, fall apart/away/back/behind/out/over/through, fight back, fizzle out, flare up, fool around, forge ahead, get about/ahead/along/by/up, give in, glaze over, go ahead/along/around/away/back/down/on/out/under/up, grow up, hang back/together, hit out, hold on, land up, lash out, let up, lie back/down, live in, look ahead/back/in, loom up, make off, meet up, melt away, mount up, move off/over, nod off, opt out, own up, pass away, pay up, pine away, play around, pop up, press ahead/on, push ahead/on, rear up, ride up, ring off, rise up, roll about/in/over, rot away, run away/out, rush in, seize up, sell up, set in, settle down/in/up, shop around, simmer down, sink in, sit around/back/down, slip up, speak up, splash out, spring up, stand back/down/in/out, start out, stay in/on/up, steam up, step aside/back/down/in, stick around, stock up, stop by/off/over, tag along, tail away/off, taper off, tick over, touch down, tune in, wade in, wait about/up, walk out, waste away, watch out, wear off, weigh in
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.87} Other phrasal verbs used in intransitive clauses are verb plus preposition combinations.

These are sometimes called \textbf{prepositional verbs}.
\begin{example}
    Ski trips now \underline{account for} nearly half of all school visits.
    
    I\underline{'m} just \underline{asking for} information.
    
    $\ldots$ the arguments that \underline{stem from} gossip.
\end{example}
Note that the nouns at the end of the above examples (\textit{nearly half of all school visits, information}, and \textit{gossip}) are objects of the prepositions and not direct objects of the verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.88} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that consist of a verb with no object and a preposition:
\begin{example}
    abide by, account for, allow for, answer for, ask after/for, bank on, bargain for, break into/with, brood on, bump into, burst into, call for/on, care for, come across/between/by/for/from/into/under/upon, count on, cut across, dawn on, deal with, dispose of, draw on, drink to, dwell on, eat into, embark on, enter into, expand on, fall for/into/on, feel for, flick through, frown upon, get at/into/over, go about/against/for, grow on, hang onto, head for, hit on, hold with, jump at, keep to, laugh at/into, lay into, leap at, level with, lie behind, live for/off/with, look after/into/to, make for, meet with, part with, pick at/on, pitch into, plan for/on, play at/on, pole at, pore over, provide for, puzzle over, rattle through, reason with, reckon on/with/without, rise above, romp through, run across/into/to, sail through, see to, seize on, set about, settle for/on, skate over, smile on, stand for, stem from, stick at/by, stumble across/on, take after/against, tamper with, tangle with, trifle with, tumble to, wade through, wait on, walk into, watch for, worry at
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.89} In the case of some intransitive phrasal verbs, the 2nd word (\textit{across, around, down}, etc) is a preposition if the 2nd thing involved needs to be mentioned, or can be an adverb if the 2nd thing involved is clear from the context.
\begin{example}
    I could \underline{hang around your office}.
    
    We'll have to \underline{hang around} for a while.
    
    They all \underline{crowded around the table}.
    
    Everyone \underline{crowded around} to see him jump into the water.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.90} Here is a list of intransitive phrasal verbs whose 2nd word is a preposition if the other thing involved needs to be mentioned, or an adverb if it does not:
\begin{example}
    ask around, bend over, break through, bustle about, come across/after/along/by/down/in/off/on/over/ through/up, cross over, crowd around, do without, fall behind/down/off, gather around, get in/off/on/round, go about/along/ down/in/round/through/up/without, hang around, join in, knock about, lag behind, lean over, lie about, look round/through, lounge about, move about, pass by/over, push by/past/through, rally round, run around/down/up, scrape through, see round, shine through, show through, sit around, spill over, stand around, stop by, trip over
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.91} Some phrasal verbs are nearly always used with an object.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{put} our drinks \underline{down} on the bar.
    
    I \underline{finished} my meal \underline{off} as quickly as I could.
    
    She \underline{read} the poem \underline{out} quietly.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.92} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that consist of a transitive verb and an adverb:
\begin{example}
    add on, beat up, blot out, board up, bring about/along/back/down/forward/in/off/out/round/up, buy out/up, call off/up, carry off/out, cast aside, catch out, chase up, chat up, clean out, conjure up, count out, cross off/out, cut back/down/off/up, deal out, dig up, do up, drag in/out/up, dream up, drink in, drive out, drum up, eat away/up, explain away, fight off, fill in/up, filter out, find out, fix up, follow up, frighten away, gather up, give away/back/off, hammer out, hand down/in/on/out/over/round, have on, hire out, hold down/up, hunt down, hush up, keep back, kick out, knock down/out/over, lap up/down/on/out, leave behind/out, let down/in/off/out, lift up, live down, melt down, mess up, mix up, nail down, note down, order about, pack off, pass down/over/round, patch up, pay back/out, phrase in/out, pick off/out, piece together, pin down/up, play back/down, plug in, point out, print out, pull apart/down, push about/around/over, put about/around/away/down/forward/off/on/out/through/together/up, read out, reason out, reel off, rinse out, rip off/up, rope in/off, rub in/out, rule out, rush through, scale down, screen off, seal off, see off, seek out, sell off, send up, set apart/aside/back/down, shake off/up, shoot down, shrug off, shut away/in/off/out, size up, smooth over, snap up, soak up, sort out, sound out, spell out, spin out, stamp out, step up, stick down, summon up, switch on, take apart/away/back/down/in/on/up, talk over/round, tear apart/down/up, tell apart/off, think over/through/up, thrash out, throw away/off/on/out, tidy away, tie down/up, tip off/up, tire out, tone down, top up, track down, trade in, try on/out, turn down/on, use up, warn off, wash away, weed out, weigh out/up, whip up, win back/over, wipe away/out/up
\end{example}
Phrasal verbs that consist of a transitive verb and a preposition are explained in paragraphs \textbf{3.107--3.110}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.93} A large group of phrasal verbs can be used both with and without an object.

%
Often this is because a phrasal verb has more than 1 meaning.

E.g., \textit{break in} does not have an object when it is used in the sense of \textit{get into a place by force}.

But \textit{break in} does take an object when it is used in the sense of \textit{get someone used to a new situation}.
\begin{example}
    If the door is locked, I will try to \underline{break in}.
    
    He believes in \underline{breaking in} his staff gradually.
    
    A plane \underline{took off}.
    
    Gretchen \underline{took off} her coat.
    
    The engine \underline{cut out}.
    
    She \underline{cut out} some colored photographs from a magazine.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.94} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that can be used both with an without an object, depending on which meaning is being used:
\begin{example}
    add up, bail out, black out, break in, call in, carry on, clear out, cut out, draw on/out/up, dress up, drop off/round, fight back, finish up, get down/in/out/together, give up, hang out, hold off/out, join up, keep away/down/in/off/on/out/up, kick off, knock about/off, lay off, leave off, look out/up, make out/up, mess about, miss out, pass off/on, pay off, pick up, pull in, put in, roll up, run down/off/over, set forth/off/out, show off/up, split up, stick out/up, stow away, strike out, string along, sum up, switch off, take off, tear off, throw up, tuck in, turn away/back/in/round/up, wind down/up, work out, wrap up
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.95} There are a few phrasal verbs that have only 1 meaning, but that can be used either with or without an object.

It is possible to use them without an object because the object is either obvious or can be guessed in a particular context.
\begin{example}
    It won't take me a moment to \underline{clear away}.
    
    I'll help you \underline{clear away} the dishes.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.96} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that have only 1 meaning but that can be used with or without an object:
\begin{example}
    answer back, breathe in/out, call back, clear away/up, cover up, drink up, help out, open up, take over, tidy away, wash up
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.97} Just as with ordinary verbs, some phrasal verbs are \textbf{ergative verbs}; i.e., you can use the object of the transitive verb as the subject of the intransitive verb.
\begin{example}
    The guerrillas blew up \underline{the restaurant}.
    
    \underline{The gasworks} blew up.
    
    I won't wake \underline{him} up just yet.
    
    \underline{He} woke up in the middle of the night.
\end{example}
See paragraphs \textbf{3.59--3.67} for information about ergative verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.98} Here is a list of ergative phrasal verbs:
\begin{example}
    back up, block up, blow up, book in, break off/up, buck up, build up, burn up, check in, cheer up, close down, dry up, get off, heat up, hurry up, line up, move on, open up, peel off, pull through, rub off, shut up, sign up, slow down, spread out, start off, thaw out, wake up, warm up, wear down/out
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.99} In the case of some ergative phrasal verbs, the 2nd word can be a preposition if the other thing involved needs to be mentioned, or can be an adverb if the other thing involved is clear from the context.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ leaves that \underline{had been blown off the trees}.
    
    My hat \underline{blew off}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.100} Here is a list of ergative phrasal verbs whose 2nd word can be a preposition or an adverb:
\begin{example}
    blow off, chip off, get down/through/up, move down/up, peel off, poke through, stick in/on
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.101} When you are using a phrasal verb with an object that is a short noun phrase, you usually have a choice as to where you put the object.

It can be placed either after the 2nd word of the phrasal verb or after the 1st word and before the 2nd word.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{filled up} his car with petrol.
    
    She \underline{filled} my glass \underline{up}.
    
    He \underline{handed over} the box.
    
    Mrs Kaul \underline{handed} the flowers \underline{over} to Judy.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.102} However, when the object consists of a long noun phrase, it is more likely to come after the 2nd word of the phrasal verb, so that the 2 parts of the phrasal verb are not separated too widely.

In this way, attention is focused on the information contained in the noun phrase, rather than on the 2nd word of the phrasal verb.
\begin{example}
    Police have been told to \underline{turn back} all refugees who try to cross the border.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.103} When the object is a pronoun e.g. \textit{me, her}, or \textit{it}, it usually comes before the 2nd word of the phrasal verb.

This is because it is not new information, and so it is not put in a position of prominence at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    I waited until he had \underline{filled} it \underline{up}.
    
    He \underline{tied} her \underline{up} and bundled her into the car.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.104} If the object of a phrasal verb is an abstract noun e.g. \textit{hope, confidence}, or \textit{support}, it usually comes after the 2nd world of the phrasal verb.

So, although you can say \textit{He built his business up}, you usually say \textit{We are trying to build up trust with the residents}.

Similarly, although you can say \textit{He put my parents up for the night}, you normally say \textit{The peasants are putting up a lot of resistance}.
\begin{example}
    The newspapers \underline{whipped up} sympathy for them.
    
    They attempted to \underline{drum up} support from the students.
    
    He didn't \underline{hold out} much hope for them.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.105} With a small number of phrasal verbs, the object is always placed between the 1st and the 2nd words of the verb.

E.g., you can say \textit{I can't tell your brothers apart} but not \textit{I can't tell apart your brothers}.
\begin{example}
    Captain Dean \underline{was} still \underline{ordering} everybody \underline{about}.
    
    I \underline{answered} my father \underline{back} and took my chances.
\end{example}
Note that most of these verbs take a human object.

%
Here is a list of phrasal verbs that always belong in this group when they are used with an object:
\begin{example}
    answer back, ask in, bash about, bind over, book in, bring round/to, brush off, call back, carry back, catch out, churn up, count in, drag down, dress down, drop round, feel out, get away, hear out, help along, invite in/out/over, jolly along, keep under, knock about, mess about, move about, much about, order about, play along/through, pull about/to, push about/around/to, run through, see through, send ahead/away/up, shut up, sit down, slap around, stand up, stare out, string along, talk round, tear apart, tell apart, tip off, trust up, turf out
\end{example}
Some phrasal verbs have more than 1 transitive sense, but belong in this group when they are used with 1 particular meaning.

E.g., \textit{take back} belongs in this group when it means \textit{remind someone of something} but not when it means \textit{regain something}.
\begin{example}
    The smell of chalk \underline{took} us all \underline{back} to our schooldays.
    
    $\ldots$ his ambition of \underline{taking back} disputed territory.
\end{example}
Here is a list of phrasal verbs that belong in this group when used with a particular meaning:
\begin{example}
    bowl over, bring down/out, buoy up, cut off, do over, draw out, get back/out, give up, have on, hurry up, keep up, kick around, knock out/up, nail down, pass on, pin down, pull apart, push around, put down/out, see out, set up, shake up, show around/up, start off, straighten out, take back/in/off, throw about, toss about, trip up, turn on, ward off, wind up
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.106} Remember that when a phrasal verb consists of an intransitive verb followed by a preposition, the noun phrase always comes after the preposition, even when it is a pronoun.
\begin{example}
    A number of reasons \underline{can account for} this change.
    
    They \underline{had dealt with} the problem intelligently.
    
    If I went away and left you in the flat, would you \underline{look after} it?
\end{example}
There is a list of phrasal verbs that consist of an intransitive verb and a preposition in paragraph \textbf{3.88}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.107} Some phrasal verbs consist of a transitive verb and a preposition.

They have 1 noun phrase after the 1st word, as the object of the verb, and a 2nd noun phrase after the 2nd word, as the object of the preposition.
\begin{example}
    They agreed to \underline{let} him \underline{into} their secret.
    
    The farmer threatened to \underline{set} his dogs \underline{on} them.
    
    They\underline{'ll hold} that \underline{against} you when you apply next time.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.108} Here is a list of phrasal verbs that consist of a transitive verb and a preposition:
\begin{example}
    build into/on, draw into, drum into, frighten into, hold against, keep to, lay before, leave off, let into, lumber with, make of, put on/onto/through, read into, set against/back/on, talk into, thrust upon, write into
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.109} In the case of some transitive phrasal verbs, the 2nd word is a preposition if the 3rd thing involved needs to be mentioned, but it is an adverb if the 3rd thing involved is clear from the context.
\begin{example}
    Rudolph \underline{showed them around the theater}.
    
    Rudolph \underline{showed them around}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.110} Here is a list of transitive phrasal verbs whose 2nd word can be a preposition or an adverb:
\begin{example}
    cross off, dab on, hawk around, hurl about, keep off, knock off, lop off, push around, scrub off, show around, shut in, sink in
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.111} Most phrasal verbs consists of 2 words: a verb and an adverb, or a verb and a preposition.

However, some phrasal verbs consists of 3 words: a verb, an adverb, and a preposition.

This type of verb is sometimes called a \textbf{phrasal-prepositional verb}.

%
Most 3-word phrasal verbs are intransitive.

The preposition at the end is followed by its own object.
\begin{example}
    His girlfriend \underline{walked out on} him.
    
    You're not going to \underline{get away with} this!
    
    She sometimes finds it hard to \underline{keep up with} her classmates.
    
    The local people have to \underline{put up with} a lot of tourists.
    
    Terry Holbrook \underline{caught up with} me.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.112} Here is a list of intransitive 3-word phrasal verbs:
\begin{example}
    be in for, be on to, bear down on, boil down to, break out of, brush up on, bump up against, burst in on, call out for, catch up with, chime in with, clamp down on, clean up after, come across as, come down on/to/with, come in for, come on to, come out in/of/with, come up against/to/with, crack down on, creep up on, crowd in on, cry out against/for, cut back on, date back to, do away with, double back on, face up to, fall back on, fall in with, get away with, get down to, get in to, get off with, get on to/with, get round to, get up to, give up to, go along with, go back on, go down with, go in for, go off with, go over to, go through with, grow out of, keep in with, keep on at, keep up with, kick out against, lead up to, live up to, look down on, look forward to, look out for, look up to, make away with, make off with, make up to, match up to, measure up to, miss out on, monkey about with, play along/around with, put up with, read up on, run away/off with, run up against, shy away from, sit in on, snap out of, stick out/up for, suck up to, take up with, talk down to, tie in with, walk away from/with, walk off with, walk out on, wriggle out of, zero in on
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.113} A few 3-word phrasal verbs are transitive.

The direct object of the verb comes immediately after the verb.

A 2nd noun phrase is put after the preposition, as normal.
\begin{example}
    I'll \underline{let} you \underline{in on} a secret.
    
    Kroop tried to \underline{talk} her \underline{out of} it.
    
    They \underline{put} their success \underline{down to} hard work.
\end{example}
Here is a list of transitive 3-word phrasal verbs:
\begin{example}
    do out of, frighten out of, let in for/on, play off against, pot down as/to, take out/up on, talk out of
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.113} [!] In standard written English it is not possible to have indirect objects with phrasal verbs.

The only objects you can have are direct objects of the verb and objects of prepositions.

In informal spoken English, however, a few phrasal verbs do have both a direct and an indirect object.

In such cases, the indirect object is placed between the verb and the particle, and the direct object follows.
\begin{example}
    Would you \underline{break me off} a piece of chocolate, please?
    
    We \underline{brought her back} some special cookies from Germany.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.115} There is 1 way in which a preposition that is part of a phrasal verb behaves differently from an ordinary preposition.

%
Normally, when the object of a preposition is put at the beginning of a question or a relative clause, it can be preceded by the preposition, especially in formal speech or writing.

E.g., you can say \textit{From which student did you get the book?} and \textit{the document on which he put his signature}.

%
However, if the preposition is part of a phrasal verb, it cannot be put before its object in such structures.

You have to say \textit{What are you getting at?} not \textit{At what are you getting?}, and \textit{the difficulties which he ran up against} not \textit{the difficulties against which he ran up}.
\begin{example}
    Who were they laughing \underline{at}?
    
    This was 1 complication he had not bargained \underline{for}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.116} Most phrasal verbs that contain a transitive verb can be used in the \textbf{passive}.

So can a few phrasal verbs that contain an intransitive verb and a preposition.

See paragraphs \textbf{9.17--9.23}.

\subsection{Compound verbs: \textit{ice-skate, baby-sit}}
\noindent\fbox{3.114} Some verbs, e.g. \textit{cross-examine} and \textit{test-drive}, consist of 2 words.

They are sometimes called \textbf{compound verbs}.
\begin{example}
    He would have been \underline{cross-examined} on any evidence he gave.
    
    He asked to \underline{test-drive} a top-of-the-range vehicle.
    
    It is not wise to \underline{hitch-hike} on your own.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.118} [!] It is important to realize that you cannot always guess the meaning of a compound verb if you are not already familiar with it.

E.g., to \textit{soft-soap} does not mean to use soap that is soft; it means to flatter someone in order to persuade them to do something for you.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.119} Compound verbs are usually written with a hyphen.
\begin{example}
    No one had \underline{cross-referenced} the forms before.
    
    Children \underline{ice-skated} on the sidewalks.
\end{example}
However, some compound verbs may be written with a space between the words and some may be written as single words.

E.g., both \textit{roller-skate} and \textit{roller skate} are used, as are \textit{baby-sit} and \textit{babysit}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.120} Many compound verbs consist of a noun plus a verb.
\begin{example}
    It may soon become economically attractive to \underline{mass-produce} hepatitis vaccines.
\end{example}
Others consist of an adjective plus a verb.
\begin{example}
    Somebody had \underline{short-changed} him.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.121} A few compound verbs consist of words that seem strange because they are not normally used as single words on their own, e.g. \textit{pooh-pooh} and \textit{shilly-shally}.

These verbs are usually used in informal conversation rather than formal writing.
\begin{example}
    Sally had \underline{pooh-poohed} the idea of 3 good meals a day.
    
    Come on, don't \underline{shilly-shally}. I want an answer.
\end{example}
Other compound verbs look strange because they have been borrowed from foreign languages, e.g. \textit{ad-lib} and \textit{kow-tow}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{ad-libbed} so much that the writers despaired of them.
    
    He resents having to \underline{kow-tow} to anyone or anything.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.122} Some compound verbs do not have an object.
\begin{example}
    Many people \underline{window-shopped} in the glass of the great store.
    
    If you keep to the rules, you may \underline{roller-skate}.
    
    He has learned to \underline{lip-read}.
    
    I'm learning to \underline{water-ski}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of compound verbs that do not have an object:
\begin{example}
    baby-sit, back-pedal, hitch-hike, ice-skate, jack-knife, kow-tow, lip-read, name-drop, play-act, roller-skate, shilly-shally, touch-type, water-ski, window-shop, wolf-whistle
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.123} Other compound verbs typically have an object:
\begin{example}
    You can \underline{spin-dry} it and it will still retain its shape.
    
    I didn't have time to \underline{blow-dry} my hair.
    
    At 1st we \underline{cold-shouldered} him.
    
    They \underline{ill-treated} our ancestors.
\end{example}
Here is a list of compound verbs that typically have an object:
\begin{example}
    back-comb, blow-dry, cold-shoulder, court-martial, cross-check, cross-examine, cross-question, cross-reference, double-cross, double-glaze, dry-clean, field-test, force-feed, frog-march, ghost-write, ill-treat, pooh-pooh, proof-read, rubber-stamp, short-change, short-weight, soft-soap, spin-dry, spoon-feed, stage-manage, tape-record, toilet-train, wrong-foot
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.124} A 3rd group fo compound verbs may be used with or without an object.
\begin{example}
    Kate had to \underline{double-park} outside the flat.
    
    Murray \underline{double-parked} his car and jumped out.
    
    I tried to \underline{ad-lib} a joke.
    
    The commentator decided to \underline{ad-lib}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of compound verbs that can be transitive or intransitive:
\begin{example}
    ad-lib, bottle-feed, breast-feed, bulk-buy, chain-smoke, criss-cross, deep-fry, double-check, double-park, mass-produce, short-circuit, sight-read, spring-clean, stir-fry, tie-dye
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.125} Only the 2nd part of a compound verb inflects.

If the 2nd part is used on its own as a verb, the compound verb usually inflects in the same way as the verb on its own.

%
See the Reference section for an explanation of how to inflect verbs.

\section{Linking verbs}
\noindent\fbox{3.126} If you want to describe someone or something, e.g. to say who or what they are or what qualities they have, you use 1 of a special set of verbs.

These verbs are called \textbf{linking verbs}.
\begin{example}
    Cigarette smoking \underline{is} dangerous to your health.
    
    The station \underline{seemed} a very small one.
    
    He \underline{looked} English.
    
    I \underline{became} enormously fond of her.
\end{example}
The most common linking verbs are \textit{be, become, look, remain}, and \textit{seem}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.127} A linking verb links a subject and an adjective or a noun phrase (called a \textbf{complement}).

The subject, as usual, comes 1st and the adjective or noun phrase comes after the verb.

The adjective or noun phrase describes or identifies the subject.
\begin{example}
    Her general knowledge is \underline{amazing}.
    
    The children seemed \underline{frightened}.
    
    That's \underline{a very difficult question}.
    
    She's \underline{the head of a large primary school}.
    
    Suleiman Salle became \underline{the 1st President of Eritrea}.
\end{example}
Linking verbs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{3.132--3.154}.

%
Some other intransitive verbs are sometimes used in a similar way to linking verbs.

See paragraphs \textbf{3.155--3.160}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.128} Some verbs, e.g. \textit{make} and \textit{find}, are used with an \textbf{object complement}, i.e., both an object and an adjective or a noun phrase.

The adjective or noun phrase describes the object.
\begin{example}
    The lights \underline{made me sleepy}.
    
    I \underline{found the forest quite frightening}.
\end{example}
These verbs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{3.161--3.171}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.129} You can often use a phrase beginning with a preposition after a linking verb.
\begin{example}
    The 1st-aid box is \underline{on the top shelf}.
    
    I began to get \underline{in a panic}.
\end{example}
For more information about phrases and clauses that can be used after ling verbs, see paragraphs \textbf{3.172--3.181}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.130} \textit{Be} is often used with \textit{it} as an impersonal subject.

This structure is used to comment on places, situations, actions, experiences, and facts.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It was} very quiet in the hut.
    
    \underline{It was} awkward keeping my news from Ted.
    
    \underline{It's} strange you should come today.
\end{example}
Some other linking verbs are occasionally used in a similar way.

%
The use of \textit{it} as an impersonal subject is explained in paragraphs \textbf{9.31--9.45}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.131} \textit{Be} is often used with \textit{there} as its subject to indicate the existence of something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{There is} another explanation.
    
    \underline{There is} a rear bathroom with a paneled bath.
\end{example}
This use of \textit{there} is explained in paragraphs \textbf{9.46--9.55}.

\subsection{Adjectives after linking verbs: \textit{He seems happy, I'm tired}}
\noindent\fbox{3.132} Many \textbf{adjectives} can be used after linking verbs.
\begin{example}
    I am \underline{proud} of these people.
    
    They seemed \underline{happy}.
    
    You don't want them to become \underline{suspicious}.
    
    They have remained \underline{loyal} to the Government.
\end{example}
They can be modified in various ways or have various structures after them.
\begin{example}
    We were \underline{very happy}.
    
    Your suspicious are \underline{entirely correct}.
    
    Their hall was \underline{larger than his whole flat}.
    
    He was \underline{capable of extraordinary kindness}.
\end{example}
Adjectives used after linking verbs, and the structures used with them, are explained in paragraphs \textbf{2.41--2.53}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.133} Here is a list of verbs that can be used as linking verbs with a following adjective:
\begin{example}
    be
    
    appear, feel, look, prove, seem, smell, sound, taste
    
    become, come, fall, get, go, grow, keep, remain, stay, turn
\end{example}
Note that the verbs in 3rd group refer to changing or to saying the same.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.134} [i] Some verbs in the 2nd group listed above have special features.

%
\textit{Appear, prove}, and \textit{seem} are often followed by \textit{to be} and an adjective, instead of directly by an adjective.
\begin{example}
    Mary was breathing quietly and \underline{seemed to be asleep}.
    
    Some people \underline{appeared to be immune to the virus}.
\end{example}
See paragraph \textbf{3.192} for information on using a \textit{to}-infinitive after these verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.135} With some verbs in the 2nd group, especially \textit{feel, look}, and \textit{seem}, you can use the \textit{-ed} participle of a verb as an adjective.
\begin{example}
    The other child looked \underline{neglected}.
    
    The quarrel of the night before seemed \underline{forgotten}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.136} When you are using the 2nd group of verbs to say what qualities someone or something seems to have, you may want to mention the person whose viewpoint you are giving.

You can do this by using a phrase beginning with the preposition to.

It usually comes after the adjective.
\begin{example}
    They looked all right \underline{to me}.
    
    It sounds unnatural \underline{to you}, I expect.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.137} [!]  You cannot use all adjectives with all linking verbs.

Some verbs, e.g. \textit{be} and \textit{look}, are used with a wide range of adjectives and some are used with a restricted range.

E.g., \textit{taste} is used only with adjectives that describe the taste of something; \textit{go} is used mainly with adjectives that indicate color or a negative state; and \textit{fall} is used mainly with \textit{asleep, ill}, and \textit{silent}.
\begin{example}
    Sea water \underline{tastes nasty}.
    
    It \underline{tasted sweet} like fruit juice.
    
    Jack \underline{went red}.
    
    It all \underline{went horribly wrong}.
    
    The world \underline{has gone crazy}.
    
    He \underline{fell asleep} at the table.
    
    The courtroom \underline{fell silent}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Nouns after linking verbs: \textit{She is a teacher, It remained a secret}}
\noindent\fbox{3.138} \textbf{Nouns} can be used after the following linking verbs:
\begin{example}
    be, become, remain
    
    feel, look, prove, seem, sound
    
    constitute, make, represent
    
    comprise, form
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.139} You can use descriptive nouns or noun phrases after \textit{be, become, remain, feel, look, prove, seem, sound, constitute}, and \textit{represent} to say what qualities someone or something has.
\begin{example}
    Their policy on higher education is \underline{an unmitigated disaster}.
    
    He always seemed \underline{a controlled sort of man}.
    
    I feel \underline{a bit of a fraud}.
    
    The results of these experiments remain \underline{a secret}.
    
    Any change would represent \underline{a turnaround}.
\end{example}
\textit{Make} is only used as a linking verb with a noun that indicates whether someone is good at a particular job.
\begin{example}
    He'll make \underline{a good president}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.140} With \textit{be, become, remain, feel, look, prove, seem}, and \textit{sound}, you can use a noun phrase based on \textit{one}.

%
The noun phrase consists of \textit{a} or \textit{an} followed by the adjective and \textit{one}, if the subject is singular.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{The school is large}, you can say \textit{The school is a large one}.
\begin{example}
    The sound is \underline{a familiar one}.
    
    The impression the region gives is still \underline{a rural one}.
\end{example}
If the subject is plural, you can use the adjective followed by \textit{ones}.
\begin{example}
    My memories of a London childhood are \underline{happy ones}.
\end{example}
\textit{One} can also be followed by a \textbf{prepositional phrase} or a \textbf{relative clause}.
\begin{example}
    Their story was indeed \underline{1 of passion}.
    
    The problem is \underline{one that always faces a society when it finds itself threatened}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.141} If you want to make a statement about the size, age, color, or shape of something, you can use a noun phrase based on \textit{size, age, color}, or \textit{shape} after the linking verbs mentioned in the previous paragraph.

The noun phrase begins with a determiner and has an adjective in front of the noun or the preposition \textit{of} after it.
\begin{example}
    It's just \underline{the right shape}.
    
    The opposing force would be about \underline{the same size}.
    
    The walls are \underline{a delicate pale cream color}.
    
    His body was \underline{the color of bronze}.
    
    It is only \underline{the size of a mouse}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.142} You can use noun phrases beginning with \textit{a} or \textit{an}, or plural noun phrases without a determiner after \textit{be, become, remain, comprise}, and \textit{form}, to say what type of person or thing someone or something is.
\begin{example}
    He is \underline{a geologist}.
    
    I'm not \underline{an unreasonable person}.
    
    He is now \underline{a teenager}.
    
    The air moved a little faster and became \underline{a light wind}.
    
    They became \underline{farmers}.
    
    Promises by MPs remained just \underline{promises}.
    
    These arches formed \underline{a barrier to the tide}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.143} You can use names or noun phrases referring to a particular person or thing after \textit{be, become, remain, constitute, represent, comprise}, and \textit{form} to talk about exactly who or what someone or something is.
\begin{example}
    This is \underline{Desiree, my father's 2nd wife}.
    
    He's now \underline{the Director of the Office of Management and Budget}.
    
    The winner of the competition was \underline{Ross Lambert of Forest Hill Primary School}.
    
    The downstairs television room became \underline{my room for receiving visitors}.
    
    $\ldots$ the 4 young men who comprised \underline{the TV crew}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.144} [i] When you use a noun indicating a unique job or position within an organization, you do not have to put a determiner in front of the noun.
\begin{example}
    At 1 time you wanted to be \underline{President}.
    
    He went on to become \underline{head of 1 of the company's largest divisions}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.145} \textbf{Personal pronouns} are sometimes used after linking verbs to indicate identity.

Note that the object pronouns are used, except in very formal speech or writing.
\begin{example}
    It's \underline{me} again.
\end{example}
\textbf{Possessive pronouns} are also used after linking verbs, to indicate identity or to describe something.
\begin{example}
    This one is \underline{yours}.
    
    This place is \underline{mine}.
\end{example}
\textbf{Indefinite pronouns} are sometimes used to describe something, usually with a qualifying structure after them.
\begin{example}
    It's \underline{nothing serious}.
    
    You're \underline{someone who does what she wants}.
\end{example}
When pronouns are used after linking verbs, the linking verb is usually \textit{be}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.146} The use of noun phrases containing \textbf{measurement nouns} after \textit{be} and other verbs is explained in paragraphs \textbf{2.252--2.254}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.147} Some verbs function as ling verbs when they are followed by a particular preposition.

%
The object of the preposition describes or classifies the subject of the verb.
\begin{example}
    His fear \underline{turned into unreasoning panic}.
    
    Taylor's fascination with bees \underline{developed into an obsession}.
    
    An autobiography really \underline{amounts to a whole explanation of yourself}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verb and preposition combinations that function as linking verbs:
\begin{example}
    amount to, change into/to, develop into, grow into, morph into, turn into/to
\end{example}
These all have the same basic meaning as \textit{become}, except for \textit{amount to}, which has a similar meaning to \textit{constitute}.

%
The phrasal verb \textit{make up} also functions as a linking verb.
\begin{example}
    Wood \underline{made up 65\% of the Congo's exports}.
\end{example}
