\subsection{Commenting}
\noindent\fbox{3.148} There are several ways in which a \textbf{\textit{to}-infinitive} can be used to comment on someone or something in relation to an action.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.149} If you want to say that someone shows a particular quantity when they do something, you can use the structure: subject $+$ linking verb $+$ adjective or noun phrase $+$ \textit{to}-infinitive.
\begin{example}
    Most people think I am \underline{brave to do this}.
    
    I think my father was \underline{a brave man to do what he did}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.150} You can use a similar structure to say that someone or something would do a particular task better than anyone or anything else.
\begin{example}
    He was absolutely \underline{the right man to go to Paris and negotiate}.
    
    She may be \underline{an ideal person to look after the children}.
    
    He is \underline{just the man to calm everyone down}.
\end{example}
You cannot use adjectives alone in constructions of this kind.

You use a noun phrase containing an adjective e.g. \textit{right, ideal}, or \textit{best}, or \textit{just the} followed by the noun \textit{person, man}, or \textit{woman}.

E.g., you can say \textit{He was the ideal person to lead the expedition}, but you cannot say \textit{He was ideal to lead the expedition}.

%
Instead of a \textit{to}-infinitive, you can sometimes use a phrase beginning with the preposition \textit{for}.

In this structure, an adjective can be used alone.
\begin{example}
    He's not \underline{the right man for it}.
    
    They are \underline{ideal for this job}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.151} If you want to express your feeling about an event, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive after a noun phrase that follows a linking verb.
\begin{example}
    It seemed such \underline{a terrible thing to happen}.    
\end{example}
The \textit{to}-infinitive consists of \textit{to} and an intransitive verb, usually \textit{happen}.

%
You cannot use an adjective alone in constructions of this type.

E.g., you can say \textit{It was a wonderful thing to happen}, but you cannot say \textit{It was wonderful to happen}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.152} If you want to say that someone is willing or unwilling to do something, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive after an adjective that follows a linking verb.
\begin{example}
    They were \underline{willing to risk losing their jobs}.
    
    I am \underline{anxious to meet Mrs Burton-Cox}.
    
    She is \underline{eager to succeed}.
    
    He is \underline{unwilling to answer questions}.
    
    I was \underline{reluctant to involve myself in this private fight}.
\end{example}
You cannot use nouns in constructions of this type.

E.g., you can say \textit{He was willing to come}, but you cannot say \textit{He was a willing person to come}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.153} If you want to show your opinion of something by describing what the experience of doing something to it is like, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive after an adjective or noun phrase that follows a linking verb.
\begin{example}
    Silk is \underline{comfortable to wear}.
    
    It's \underline{a nice thing to have}.
    
    Telling someone they smell is \underline{a hard thing to do}.
    
    She was \underline{easy to talk to}.
\end{example}
Note that the \textit{to}-infinitive must be the \textit{to}-infinitive of a transitive verb or of an intransitive verb followed by a preposition.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.154} If you want to show your opinion of an action, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive that has an object.
\begin{example}
    They thought this was \underline{a sensible thing to do}.
    
    This is \underline{a very foolish attitude to take}.
\end{example}
The \textit{to}-infinitive is usually \textit{to do, to make}, or \textit{to take}.

%
You cannot use an adjective alone in constructions of this kind.

E.g., you can say \textit{It was a silly thing to do}, but you cannot say \textit{It was silly to do}.

\subsection{Other verbs with following adjectives: \textit{He stood still}}
\noindent\fbox{3.155} Some intransitive verbs can be followed by adjectives in the same way as linking verbs.
\begin{example}
    George \underline{stood motionless} for at least a minute.
    
    Pugin \underline{died insane} at the early age of 40.
\end{example}
However, it is clear that these verbs are not just linking verbs.

\textit{George stood motionless} does not mean the same as \textit{George was motionless}.

In the sentence \textit{George stood motionless}, the verb \textit{stand} is performing 2 functions: it is telling us that George was standing, and it is also acting as a linking verb between \textit{George} and the adjective \textit{motionless}.

%
Here is a list of verbs that can be used in this way:
\begin{example}
    hang, lie, sit, stand
    
    gaze, stare
    
    emerge, escape, go, pass, survive
    
    blush, flame, gleam, glow, run
    
    be born, die, return
\end{example}
Ways in which these verbs can be used with following adjectives are discussed in the following paragraphs.

%
Adjectives are sometimes used in combination with other verbs, but are separated from the main clause by a comma.

This use is dealt with in paragraph \textbf{8.147}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.156} [i] You can use adjectives describing states after \textit{hang, lie, sit}, and \textit{stand}.
\begin{example}
    I used to \underline{lie awake} watching the rain seep through the roof.
    
    A sparrow \underline{lies dead} in the snow.
    
    Francis Marroux \underline{sat ashen-faced} behind the wheel.
    
    She \underline{stood quite still}, facing him.
\end{example}
\textit{Gaze} and \textit{stare} can be used in a similar way with a limited set of adjectives.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{stared} at him \underline{wide-eyed}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.157} You can use some combinations of verbs and adjectives to say that something does not happen to someone or something, or that someone does not have something.

%
\textit{Go, pass, emerge, escape}, and \textit{survive} are often used in combinations like these.

The adjectives they combine with are often formed by adding \textit{un-} to \textit{-ed} participles.
\begin{example}
    Your efforts won't \underline{go unnoticed}.
    
    The guilty \underline{went unpunished}.
    
    Somehow, his reputation \underline{emerged unblemished}.
    
    Fortunately we all \underline{escaped unscathed}.
    
    Mostly, they \underline{go unarmed}.
    
    The children always went barefoot.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.158} Verbs e.g. \textit{blush, flame, gleam, glow}, and \textit{run} can be used with color adjectives after them to say what color something is or what color it becomes.
\begin{example}
    They blew into the charcoal until it \underline{glowed red}.
    
    The trees \underline{flamed scarlet} against the grass.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.159} \textit{Die, return}, and the passive verb \textit{be born} can be followed by either adjectives or noun phrases.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{died young}.
    
    He \underline{died a disappointed man}.
    
    At the end of the war, he \underline{returned a slightly different man}.
    
    He \underline{was born a slave}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.160} Some combinations of verb and adjective are fixed phrases.

You cannot use the verb in front of any other adjective.
\begin{example}
    I wanted to \underline{travel light}.
    
    The children \underline{ran wild}.
    
    The joke \underline{was wearing thin} with use.
\end{example}

\subsection{Describing the object of a verb}
\noindent\fbox{3.161} You can put an adjective after the object of some transitive verbs.

This adjectives describes the object, and is often called an \textbf{object complement}.
\begin{example}
    Willie's remarks made her \underline{uneasy}.
    
    I find the British legal system \underline{extremely complicated}.
\end{example}
Some of these verbs are used to say that someone or something is changed or that someone is given a new job.

Others are used to describe a person's opinion of someone or something.

%
For information on how to use these verbs in the passive, see paragraph \textbf{9.21}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.162} If you want to say that someone or something causes a person or thing to have a particular quality, you can use 1 of a group of intransitive verbs, followed by an adjective.
\begin{example}
    He said waltzes \underline{made him dizzy}.
    
    They\underline{'re driving me crazy}.
    
    Then this captor \underline{had knocked him unconscious}.
    
    She \underline{painted her eyelids deep blue}.
    
    He \underline{wiped the bottle dry}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used in this way:
\begin{example}
    cut, drive, get, knock, make, paint, pat, pick, plane, render, rub, send, shoot, sweep, turn, wipe
\end{example}
Most of these verbs can be followed by only 1 adjective or a very small range of adjectives.

However \textit{make} and \textit{render} can be used with a wide range of adjectives.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.163} You can also use \textit{keep, hold}, and \textit{leave} with an object followed by an adjective, to say that someone or something is caused to remain in a particular state.
\begin{example}
    The light through the thin curtains \underline{had kept her awake}.
    
    \underline{Leave the door open}.
    
    \underline{Hold it straight}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.164} If you want to say that someone is given an important job, you can use \textit{make, appoint, crown}, or \textit{elect} with an object followed by a noun phrase referring to the job.
\begin{example}
    In 1910 Asquith \underline{made him a junior minister}.
\end{example}
The noun used in this way does not usually have a determiner when it refers to a unique job.
\begin{example}
    Ramsay MacDonald appointed him \underline{Secretary of State for India}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.165} Some transitive verbs with the general meaning \textit{consider} can be used with an adjective or noun phrase to say what someone's opinion of a person or thing is.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{consider him an embarrassment}.
    
    Do you \underline{find his view of America interesting}?
\end{example}
Here is a list of these verbs:
\begin{example}
    account, believe, consider, deem, find, hold, judge, presume, reckon, think
\end{example}
\textit{Prove} can also be followed by an object complement, although it means \textit{show}, not \textit{consider}.
\begin{example}
    He had \underline{proved them wrong}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.166} These verbs are often used in the passive.

\textit{Believe, presume, reckon}, and \textit{think} are nearly always used in the passive in these structures.
\begin{example}
    Her body was never found and she \underline{was presumed dead}.
    
    30 bombers \underline{were believed shot down}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.167} All the verbs listed in paragraph \textbf{3.165} except \textit{account} can also be used with a \textit{to}-infinitive  clause after their object showing what someone thinks a person or thing is like or does.
\begin{example}
    We believed him \underline{to be innocent}.
\end{example}
See paragraph \textbf{3.206} for information abotu using a \textit{to}-infinitive clause after the object of these verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.168} You can use the verbs listed in \textbf{3.165} with \textit{it} as their object followed by an object complement and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause to show someone's opinion of an action.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{She found breathing difficult}, you can say \textit{She found it difficult to breathe}.
\begin{example}
    Gretchen found \underline{it} difficult \underline{to speak}.
    
    He thought \underline{it} right \underline{to resign}.
    
    He considered \underline{it} his duty \underline{to go}.
\end{example}
These are examples of \textit{it} being used in an impersonal way.

For more information about the impersonal use of \textit{it}, see paragraphs \textbf{9.31--9.45}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.169} If you want to say that people use a particular word, word group, or name to describe or refer to someone or something, you can use the word, word group, or name after 1 of a group of transitive verbs.
\begin{example}
    People who did not like him \underline{called him dull}.
    
    They \underline{called him an idiot}.
    
    Everyone \underline{called her Molly}.
    
    He \underline{was declared innocent}.
    
    They \underline{name the place} Tumbo Kutu.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used in this way.

The 1st group is followed by an adjective; the 2nd group is followed by a noun phrase; and the 3rd group is followed by a name.
\begin{example}
    call, certify, declare, label, pronounce, term
    
    brand, call, declare, designate, label, proclaim, term
    
    call, christen, dub, name, nickname
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.170} The passive verbs \textit{be entitled, be headed}, and \textit{be inscribed} are followed by a title or inscription.
\begin{example}
    The draft document \underline{was entitle `A way forward'}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.171} A few transitive verbs can be followed by an adjective to say that someone or something is in a particular state when something happens to them, or is preferred to be in that state.
\begin{example}
    More than 40 people \underline{were burned alive}.
    
    $\ldots$ a soup that can \underline{be served cold}.
    
    They \underline{found it dead}.
    
    Do you \underline{want it white or black}?
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used in this way:
\begin{example}
    burn, eat, find, leave, like, prefer, serve, show, want
\end{example}
Sometimes and \textit{-ed} participle or an \textit{-ing} participle describing a state is used.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{found herself caught} in a strong tidal current.
    
    Maureen came in and \underline{found Kate sitting} on a chair staring at the window.
\end{example}

\subsection{Using a prepositional phrase after a linking verb}
\noindent\fbox{3.172} When you want to give information about someone or something by describing their circumstances, you can sometimes use a \textbf{prepositional phrases} after a linking verb.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.173} You can use many kinds of prepositional phrase after \textit{be}.
\begin{example}
    He was still \underline{in a state of shock}.
    
    I walked home with Bill, who was \underline{in a very good mood}.
    
    She had an older brother who was \underline{in the army}.
    
    I'm \underline{from Dortmund} originally.
    
    $\ldots$ people who are \underline{under pressure}.
    
    Your comments are \underline{of great interest to me}.
    
    This book is \underline{for any woman who has a child}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.174} Some other linking verbs can be used with a more restricted range of prepositional phrases.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{seemed in excellent health}.
    
    We do ask people to \underline{keep in touch} with us.
    
    These methods have gradually \underline{fallen into disuse}.
    
    He \underline{got into trouble} with the police.
\end{example}
Here is a list of other linking verbs that are used with prepositional phrases:
\begin{example}
    appear, fall, feel, get, keep, remain, seem, stay
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.175} Some verbs that are always or often followed by an adjective can also be used with prepositional phrases relating to place.
\begin{example}
    She's \underline{in California}.
    
    I'll stay \underline{here} with the children.
    
    The cat was now lying \underline{on the sofa}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of these verbs:
\begin{example}
    be, keep, remain, stay
    
    hang, lie, sit, stand
\end{example}
For more information about prepositional phrases and adverbs relating to place, see paragraphs \textbf{6.73--6.92} and \textbf{6.53--6.72}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.176} \textit{Be} can be used with time expressions to say when something took place or will take place.
\begin{example}
    That final meeting was \underline{on Nov 3}.
\end{example}
For more information about time expressions, see paragraphs \textbf{4.85--4.111}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.177} Prepositional phrases can also be used in transitive structures to say that someone or something is caused to be in a particular state.
\begin{example}
    They'll get me \underline{out of trouble}.
    
    The fear of being discovered kept me \underline{on the alert}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Talking about what role something has or how it is perceived: the preposition \textit{as}}
\noindent\fbox{3.178} Prepositional phrases beginning with \textit{as} can be used after some verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.179} Prepositional phrases beginning with \textit{as} are used after certain intransitive verbs to show what role or function the subject has, or what identity they pretend to have.
\begin{example}
    Bleach removes color and acts \underline{as an antiseptic and deodoriser}.
    
    He served \underline{as Kennedy's ambassador to India}.
    
    The sitting room doubles \underline{as her office}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be followed by \textit{as} in this way:
\begin{example}
    act, come, double, function, pass, pose, serve
\end{example}
\textit{Work} can also be used in this way, when it has a human subject.
\begin{example}
    She works \underline{as a counselor} with an AIDS charity.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.180} A number of transitive verbs can be used with \textit{as} after their object.

%
With some, a noun phrase is used after \textit{as}.

The \textit{as} phrase describes the role of the object or what it is thought to be.
\begin{example}
    I wanted to use him \underline{as an agent}.
    
    I treated business \underline{as a game}.
\end{example}
Here is a lit of transitive verbs that can be used with \textit{as} and a noun phrase:
\begin{example}
    brand, cast, categorize, certify, characterize, choose, class, condemn, consider, define, denounce, depict, describe, diagnose, elect, establish, give, hail, identify, intend, interpret, label, name, perceive, recognize, regard, scorn, see, suggest, take, treat, use, view
\end{example}
With others, an adjective is used after \textit{as}.

The adjective indicates what quality or characteristic the object is thought to have.
\begin{example}
    Party members and officials \underline{described him as brilliant}.
    
    They \underline{regarded manual work as degrading}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of transitive verbs that can be used with \textit{as} and an adjective:
\begin{example}
    brand, categorize, certify, characterize, class, condemn, define, denounce, depict, describe, diagnose, establish, label, perceive, regard, scorn, see, view
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.181} \textit{Look upon, refer to}, and \textit{think of} are also used with \textit{as} in this way.

\textit{As} must be followed by a noun when used with \textit{refer to}.
\begin{example}
    In some households the man \underline{was referred to as} the master.
\end{example}

\section{Talking about closely linked actions: using 2 main verbs together}
\noindent\fbox{3.182} This section describes the ways in which you use 2 main verbs together to talk about 2 actions or states that are closely linked.

%
These 2 actions may be performed by the same person.

See paragraphs \textbf{3.189--3.201}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{stopped speaking}.
    
    Davis \underline{likes to talk} about horses.
\end{example}
Alternatively, the actions may be performed by different people.

If they are, the performer of the 2nd action is the object of the 1st verb.

See paragraphs \textbf{3.202--3.212}.
\begin{example}
    I don't \underline{want them to feel} I've slighted them.
    
    1 of the group began pumping her chest to \underline{help her breathe}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.183} Note that the 1st verb needs the 2nd verb after it because it does not give enough information on its own.

E.g., \textit{I want} does not give enough information to be a useful statement, but \textit{I want to talk to you} does.

%
Some of the verbs dealt with below, e.g. \textit{want} and \textit{like}, can also be ordinary transitive verbs, with a noun phrase after them.

Transitive verbs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{3.14--3.25}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.184} If you want to talk about 2 actions that are less closely linked, you refer to each action in a separate clause.

Ways of combining clauses are explained in Chap. 8.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.185} The 1st verb involved in this type of structure is the main verb of the structure.

It usually inflects for tense and agrees in number with the subject; it is \textbf{finite}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{wanted} to come home.
    
    Lonnie \underline{wants} to say sorry.
    
    More and more people \underline{are coming} to appreciate the contribution that these people make to our society.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.186} The 2nd verb in the combination does not inflect for tense or change its form at all; it is \textbf{non-finite}.
\begin{example}
    She tried \underline{to read}.
    
    They had been trying \underline{to read}.
\end{example}
Information about finite and non-finite forms can be found in the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.187} There are 4 non-finite verb forms that are used for the 2nd verb in this type of structure:
\begin{itemize}
    \item the \textit{-ing} participle
    \item the \textit{to}-infinitive
    \item the infinitive without \textit{to}
    \item the \textit{-ed} participle
\end{itemize}
Note that the infinitive without \textit{to} and the \textit{-ed} participle form of the verb are used in only a few combinations.

%
Other kinds of \textit{-ing} form and infinitive are also sometimes used.
\begin{example}
    Those very close to the blast risk \underline{being burnt}.
    
    Neither Rita nor I recalled ever \underline{having seem} her.
    
    She wanted \underline{to be reassured}.
    
    They claimed \underline{to have shot down} 22 planes.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.188} The position of \textit{not} in negative structures of this type is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.59--5.60}.

\subsection{Talking about 2 actions done by the same person}
\noindent\fbox{3.189} When you are talking about 2 actions that are done by the same person, you use the 2nd verb directly after the 1st.
\begin{example}
    Children \underline{enjoy playing} alongside each other.
    
    You \underline{deserve to know} the truth.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.190} Some verbs are always followed by an \textit{-ing} participle clause in structures of this kind.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{admitted lying} to him.
    
    Have you \underline{finished reading} the paper?
    
    He \underline{missed having} someone to dislike.
    
    I \underline{recall being} very impressed with the official anthems.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used with an \textit{-ing} participle, but not a \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    admit, adore, appreciate, avoid, celebrate, commence, consider, contemplate, defer, delay, deny, describe, detest, discontinue, dislike, dread, endure, enjoy, fancy, finish, go, imagine, keep, lie, loathe, mention, mind, miss, postpone, practice, recall, report, resent, resist, risk, sit, stand, stop, suggest
\end{example}
These verbs are also sometimes used with a passive \textit{-ing} form.
\begin{example}
    They enjoy \underline{being praised}.
\end{example}
\textit{Admit, celebrate, deny, mention}, and \textit{recall} are quite often used with a perfect \textit{-ing} form.
\begin{example}
    Carmichael had denied \underline{having seen} him.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.191} [i] Note that \textit{need} can be used with an \textit{-ing} participle after it, but the \textit{-ing} participle then has the same meaning as a passive \textit{to}-infinitive.

E.g., \textit{The house needs cleaning} means the same as \textit{The house needs to be cleaned}.

%
\textit{Require} and \textit{want} are also occasionally used in the same way, although some people do not like this use o \textit{want}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.192} Other verbs are used with a \textit{to}-infinitive.
\begin{example}
    Mrs Babcock had always \underline{longed to go} to Ireland.
    
    She \underline{forgot to bring} a suitcase.
    
    She \underline{wishes to ask} a favor of you.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used with a \textit{to}-infinitive, and rarely or over with an \textit{-ing} participle:
\begin{example}
    ache, afford, agree, aim, appear, arrange, ask, attempt, care, choose, claim, consent, dare, decide, demand, deserve, desire, disdain, endeavor, expect, fail, fight, forget, grow, happen, help, hesitate, hope, intend, learn, live, long, manage, mean, need, neglect, offer, opt, pay, plan, pledge, prepare, pretend, promise, prove, reckon, refuse, resolve, scorn, seek, seem, survive, swear, tend, threaten, trouble, venture, volunteer, vote, vow, wait, want, wish
\end{example}
Most of these verbs can be used with a passive infinitive.
\begin{example}
    She refused \underline{to be photographed}.
    
    He deserves \underline{to be shot}.
\end{example}
The following verbs from the above list are not usually used with a passive infinitive, because of their meanings:
\begin{example}
    claim, dare, forget, intend, learn, manage, mean, neglect, pretend, threaten, trouble, venture
\end{example}
\textit{Appear, claim, happen, pretend, prove, seem}, and \textit{tend} are quite often used with a perfect infinitive.
\begin{example}
    They seemed \underline{to have disappeared}.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{help} is also followed by the infinitive without \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    Coffee \underline{helped keep} him alert.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.193} [i] Note that \textit{afford} is always preceded by a modal, and that \textit{care} is normally used with a negative.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Can} we \underline{afford to ignore} this source of power as other sources of energy are diminishing?
    
    $\ldots$ a kitchen for someone who \underline{doesn't care to cook}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.194} The use of \textit{have} followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.244}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.195} A few verbs can be used with either an \textit{-ing} participle or a \textit{to}-infinitive without changing the meaning of the verb.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{started raining}.
    
    A very cold wind \underline{had started to blow}.
    
    We both \underline{love dancing}.
    
    He \underline{loves to talk} about his work.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be followed either by an \textit{-ing} participle or a \textit{to}-infinitive without greatly changing the meaning:
\begin{example}
    attempt, begin, bother, cease, continue, deserve, fear, hate, like, love, prefer, start
\end{example}
Note that \textit{bother} is often used with a negative or a broad negative.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{didn't bother complaining} about it.
    
    We \underline{hardly} even \underline{bother to clean} it.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.196} [i] With a few verbs, the meaning is altered depending on whether you use an \textit{-ing} participle or a \textit{to}-infinitive.

These verbs are \textit{come, go on, remember, try}, and \textit{regret}.

%
If someone \textit{comes running, flying}, or \textit{hurtling} somewhere, they move in that way.

If you \textit{come to do something}, you gradually start doing it.
\begin{example}
    When they heard I was leaving, they both \underline{came running} out.
    
    People \underline{came to believe} that all things were possible.
\end{example}
If you \textit{try to do something}, you attempt it, to see if you can do it.

If you \textit{try doing something}, you do it in order to find out if it is effective.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{tried to think} calmly.
    
    \underline{Try lying down} in a dark room for a while. That usually helps.
\end{example}
With the other verbs, the difference in meaning relates to the timing of the action.

%
If you \textit{go on doing something}, you continue to do it, but if you \textit{go on to do something}, you subsequently start doing it.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{went on arguing} into the night.
    
    She \underline{went on to talk} about the political consequences.
\end{example}
If you \textit{remember doing something}, you did it in the past, but if you \textit{remember to do something}, you do it at the present time.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{remember promising} that I would try.
    
    We \underline{must remember} to say thank you.
\end{example}
Similarly, if you \textit{regret doing something}, you have already done it, but if you \textit{regret to do something}, you have to do it at the present time.
\begin{example}
    She did not \underline{regret accepting} his offer.
    
    I \underline{regret to say} rents went up.
\end{example}
\textit{Regret} is only used with the \textit{to}-infinitive of small number of verbs that share the meaning of giving or receiving information.

These verbs are:
\begin{example}
    announce, inform, learn, say, see, tell
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.197} [!] When you have a choice between an \textit{-ing} participle and a \textit{to}-infinitive, you do not use the \textit{-ing} participle if the 1st verb is in a progressive form.
\begin{example}
    The 3rd World \underline{is beginning to export} to the West.
    
    The big clouds \underline{were starting to cover} the sun.
    
    Educational budgets \underline{are continuing to increase}.
\end{example}
With verbs that cannot be followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive, you normally use a noun phrase instead of the \textit{-ing} participle.
\begin{example}
    I knew Miss Head would just be finishing \underline{her cello practice}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.198} Note that a few verbs, principally \textit{need, want, have, buy}, and \textit{choose}, are used with an object and a \textit{to}-infinitive when talking about 2 actions performed by the same person.

The \textit{to}-infinitive must be transitive.

It is understood as relating to the noun, rather than being closely connected with the 1st verb.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{need a car to drive} to work.
    
    She \underline{chose the correct one to put} in her bag.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.199} Note that verbs that refer to a deliberate action are sometimes followed by a clause expressing purpose.

Here, \textit{to} means \textit{in order to}.
\begin{example}
    Several women \underline{moved to help} her.
    
    The captain \underline{stopped to reload} the machine-gun.
\end{example}
Note that the 1st verb has a complete meaning of its own; the 2nd verb is giving a reason for the 1st action, not completing the information about it.

%
See paragraphs \textbf{8.43--8.46} for more details on expressing purpose.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.200} [i] When the base form of \textit{try} is used, e.g. as an \textbf{imperative} or with a \textbf{modal}, it is sometimes used with \textit{and} followed by the base form of the 2nd verb, rather than with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

The 2 actions seem to be separate, because of the \textit{and}, but are in fact very closely linked.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Try and get} a torch or a light, it's terribly dark down here.
    
    I'll \underline{try and answer} the question,
\end{example}
Some speakers consider this to be informal or incorrect.

%
\textit{Come} and \textit{go} are often used in a similar way with \textit{and}, in simple forms as well as in the base form.

The verb after \textit{and} can also inflect.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come and see} me whenever you feel depressed.
    
    I \underline{went and fetched} another glass.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.201} In informal spoken English, \textit{get} is sometimes used with an \textit{-ed} participle directly after it, in a structure with a passive meaning.
\begin{example}
    Then he \underline{got killed} in a plane crash.
\end{example}
[AE] When \textit{get} is used to form passives in the past and present perfect, American English normally uses the participle \textit{gotten}, rather than \textit{got}.
\begin{example}
    Her foot had \underline{gotten caught} between some rocks. (Am)
\end{example}

\subsection{Talking about 2 actions done by different people}
\noindent\fbox{3.202} If you want to talk about 2 closely linked actions that are performed by different people, you follow the 1st verb with an object.

This object then functions as the subject of the 2nd verb.

E.g., in \textit{She asked Ginny to collect the book, Ginny} is the person who is asked, and she is also the person who performs the action of collecting the book.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{saw him looking} at my name on the door.
    
    You can't \underline{stop me seeing} him!
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.203} Note that when the 2nd verb is an \textit{-ing} participle, a possessive determiner is sometimes used in front of it, instead of a pronoun.

This is rather formal.
\begin{example}
    These professional ethics prevent \underline{their} discussing their clients with the public.
    
    She did not like \underline{my} living in London.
\end{example}
Note that a possessive determiner is only used in this way when the 2nd verb can have a human subject.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.202} If you want to talk about 2 closely linked actions that are performed by different people, you follow the 1st verb with an object.

This object then functions as the subject of the 2nd verb.

E.g., in \textit{She asked Ginny to collect the book, Ginny} is the person who is asked, and she is also the person who performs the action of collecting the book.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{saw him looking} at my name on the door.
    
    You can't \underline{stop me seeing} him!
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.204} Some verbs are used with an object and an \textit{-ing} participle.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{caught Hooper looking} at him.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used with an object and an \textit{-ing} participle:
\begin{example}
    catch, describe, feel, find, hear, imagine, keep, leave, like, notice, observe, picture, prevent, save, see, send, spot, stop, want, watch
\end{example}
\textit{Listen to} also belongs in this group.

The object after it is the object of the preposition \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{listened to Kaspar talking}.
\end{example}
These verbs are sometimes used with a passive \textit{-ing} form, but not usually with a perfect \textit{-ing} form.
\begin{example}
    She felt herself \underline{being spun around}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.205} Some of the verbs in the above paragraph can also be used with an infinitive without \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{felt her hair rise} on the back of her neck.
    
    Dr Hochstadt \underline{heard her gasp}.
\end{example}
There is a slight change of meaning depending on which form is used.

If you choose the \textit{-ing} participle, you emphasize that the action continued happening for a period of time.
\begin{example}
    But I stayed there, \underline{listening to her singing}.
    
    I looked over and \underline{saw Joe staring} at me.
\end{example}
If you choose the infinitive without \textit{to}, you emphasize that the action was completed.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{listened to Jenny finish} the sonnet.
    
    It was the 1st time she \underline{had heard him speak} of his life.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used with an \textit{-ing} participle or an infinitive without \textit{to}, with the change of meaning described above:
\begin{example}
    feel, hear, listen to, notice, observe, see, watch
\end{example}
Note that these verbs can be used in the active only when they are followed by an infinitive without \textit{to}.

See paragraph \textbf{3.208}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.206} Other verbs are used with an object and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    His sister \underline{had taught him to sew}.
    
    I \underline{encourage students to do} these exercises at home.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used with an object and a \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    advise, allow, ask, beg, cause, challenge, choose, command, compel, dare, defy, enable, encourage, expect, forbid, force, get, help, induce, inspire, instruct, intend, invite, lead, leave, like, mean, move, oblige, order, pay, permit, persuade, prefer, press, programme, prompt, recruit, remind, teach, tell, train, trust, urge, use, want, warn
\end{example}
Note that some of the verbs in the above list are used for reporting orders, requests, and advice.

For more information on this use, see paragraph \textbf{7.39}.

%
Here is a list of verbs that are always or usually used in the passive when followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    allege, assume, believe, claim, consider, deem, discover, estimate, feel, find, know, learn, prove, reckon, report, require, rumor, say, see, think, understand
\end{example}
They refer to saying, thinking, or discovering.

The \textit{to}-infinitive that follows them is most commonly \textit{be} or \textit{have}, or a perfect infinitive.
\begin{example}
    The house \underline{was believed to be} haunted.
    
    He \underline{was proved to be} wrong.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.207} If you do not know who the subject of the 2nd verb is, or you do not want to mention them, you can use a passive construction.
\begin{example}
    A gardener was immediately sacked if he \underline{was caught smoking}.
    
    I \underline{was asked to come} for a few days to help them.
\end{example}
The following verbs are not usually used in the passive when followed by an \textit{-ing} participle:
\begin{example}
    feel, imagine, like, listen to, prevent, save, stop, want
\end{example}
The following verbs are not usually used in the passive when followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    defy, get, like, prefer, want
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.208} [i] \textit{Hear, observe}, and \textit{see} are not used with a \textit{to}-infinitive when they are active but they can be used with either an \textit{-ing} participle or a \textit{to}-infinitive when they are passive.

%
You use them with an \textit{-ing} participle when you want to show that the action described by the 2nd verb took place over a period of time.
\begin{example}
    A terrorist \underline{was seen standing} in the middle of the road.
    
    Her companions \underline{could be heard playing} games.
\end{example}
If a \textit{to}-infinitive is used, you are implying that the action was completed.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{could} distinctly \underline{be seen to hesitate}.
    
    The baby \underline{was} seldom \underline{heard to cry}.
\end{example}
See also paragraph \textbf{3.205}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.209} There are some verbs used with another verb with a \textit{to}-infinitive that are followed by the preposition \textit{for} and its object, rather than by a direct object.

The object of \textit{for} is the performer of the 2nd action.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{called for action to be taken} against the unions.
    
    I \underline{waited for him to speak}.
\end{example}
Note that the \textit{to}-infinitive is often a passive one.

%
Here is a list of verbs that can be used in this way:
\begin{example}
    appeal, apply, arrange, ask, call, clamor, long, opt, pay, plead, press, vote, wait, wish, yearn
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.210} A few verbs are followed by an object and an infinitive without \textit{to}, not an \textit{-ing} participle or a \textit{to}-infinitive.

They are \textit{let, make}, and \textit{have} in the sense of \textit{cause to happen} or \textit{experience}.
\begin{example}
    Jenny \underline{let him talk}.
    
    My father \underline{made me go} for the interview.
    
    He lay in a darkened room and \underline{had her bring} him meals on trays.
\end{example}
Verbs that can be used either with an infinitive without \textit{to} or with an \textit{-ing} participle are explained in paragraph \textbf{3.205}.

%
\noindent\fbox{3.211} A special use of \textit{have} when used with another verb is to say that the subject causes something to be dealt with by someone else.

In this case, \textit{have} is followed by an object referring to the thing dealt with, and then by the \textit{-ed} participle of a transitive verb or of an intransitive verb followed by a preposition.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{have my hair cut} every 6 weeks.
\end{example}
This structure is also used to say that something belonging to the subject of \textit{have} is affected in some way.
\begin{example}
    She'd just lost her job and \underline{had some money stolen}.
\end{example}
If you want to mention the performer of the 2nd action, you use \textit{by} followed by a noun.
\begin{example}
    He had to have his leg massaged \underline{by his trainer}.
\end{example}
\textit{Get} can also be used with an object and an \textit{-ed} participle to talk about causing something to be dealt with or affected in some way.
\begin{example}
    We must \underline{get the car repaired}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{3.212} \textit{Want} is also used with an object and an \textit{-ed} participle, to show that you would like something to be done.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{want the whole approach changed}.
    
    I don't \underline{want you hurt}.
\end{example}
\textit{Need} is used in a similar way, usually when the object is something that belongs to the subject.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{need your eyes tested}.
\end{example}

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------%

\chapter{Expressing time: tenses \& time adverbials}

\section{Introduction}
\noindent\fbox{4.1} When you are making a statement, you usually need to make it clear whether you are talking about a situation that exists now, existed in the past, or is likely to exist in the future.

There are different ways of expressing time: \textbf{tense} is one; the use of \textbf{time adverbials} is another.

%
A tense is a verb form that indicates a particular point in time or period of time.

%
The form belonging to a particular tense is obtained by the addition of \textbf{inflections} to the base form of the verb.

In English, time is also indicated by the inclusion of \textbf{auxiliaries} or \textbf{modals} in the verb phrase.
\begin{example}
    smile$\ldots$ smiled
    
    was smiling$\ldots$ has been smiling$\ldots$ had smiled
    
    will smile$\ldots$ may smile
\end{example}
Some verbs have irregular forms for past tenses.
\begin{example}
    fight$\ldots$ fought
    
    go$\ldots$ went
\end{example}
For information about all these forms, see the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.2} Sometimes the point in time is clear from the tense of the verb, and no other time reference is required.

However, if you want to draw attention to the time of the action, you use a \textbf{time adverbial}.

%
A time adverbial may be
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(i)] an adverb (e.g. \textit{afterwards, immediately}),
    \item[(ii)] a prepositional phrase (e.g. \textit{at 8 o'clock, on Monday}), or
    \item[(iii)] a noun phrase (e.g. \textit{the next day, last week}).
\end{itemize}
\begin{example}
    She's moving \underline{tomorrow}
    
    He was better after undergoing surgery \underline{on Saturday}.
    
    Record profits were announced \underline{last week}.
\end{example}
For more general information about adverbials, see the beginning of Chap, 6.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.3} Time adverbials normally come at the end of a clause, after the verb or after its object if there is one.

You can put more focus on the time by placing the adverbial at the beginning of the clause.
\begin{example}
    We're getting married \underline{next year}.
    
    \underline{Next year}, the museum is expecting even more visitors.
    
    I was playing golf \underline{yesterday}.
    
    \underline{Yesterday} the atmosphere at the factory was tense.
\end{example}
if the time adverbial is an adverb, it can also come immediately after \textit{be} or after the 1st auxiliary in a verb phrase.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{is now} pretty well-known in this country.
    
    Cooper \underline{had originally} been due to retire last week.
    
    Public advertisements for the post \underline{will soon} appear in the national press.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.4} Some verb forms are used to say that an event takes place continuously over a period of time, or is repeated several times.

You may also want to say how long something lasts, or how often it happens.

To do this, \textbf{adverbials of duration} (e.g. \textit{for a long time}) and \textbf{adverbials of frequency} (e.g. \textit{often, every year}) are used.
\begin{example}
    America has \underline{always} been highly influential.
    
    People are \underline{sometimes} scared to say what they really think.
    
    Hundreds of people are killed \underline{every year} in fires.
    
    They would go on talking \underline{for hours}.
\end{example}
Adverbials of frequency are explained and listed in paragraphs \textbf{4.114--4.122}.

Adverbials of duration are explained and listed in paragraphs \textbf{4.123--4.144}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.5} The following paragraphs describe the ways in which you can talk about the present, the past, and the future.

After each of these, there is a section on the ways in which you use time adverbials with each tense.

%
There are sometime adverbials that are used mainly with the past tenses.

These are explained in paragraph \textbf{4.41}.

Time adverbials that are used with future forms can be found in paragraphs \textbf{4.60--4.62}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.6} This chapter deals only with the choice of tense in \textbf{main clauses}.

%
Sometimes, the point in time is not indicated by a time adverbial, but by a \textbf{subordinate clause}.

Subordinate clauses of time are introduced by conjunctions that refer to time, e.g. \textit{since, until, before}, and \textit{after}.

%
For information about the tense of the verb in the subordinate clause, see paragraph \textbf{8.9}.

\section{The present}
\noindent\fbox{4.7} In situations where you are discussing an existing state of affairs, you use a verb that is in the present tense.

Usually, the verb tense is sufficient to show that you are referring to the present.

You normally use a time adverbial for emphasis, or to refer to something that is unrelated to the present moment.

\subsection{The present in general: the present simple}
\noindent\fbox{4.8} If you want to talk about your thoughts and feelings at the present moment, or about your immediate reactions to something, you use the \textbf{present simple}.
\begin{example}
    I'\underline{m} awfully busy.
    
    They both \underline{taste} the same.
    
    Gosh, he \underline{looks} awful.
    
    I \underline{want} a breath of fresh air.
\end{example}
You can also use the present simple to talk about a physical feeling that is affecting you or someone else.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{feel} heavy. I do. I \underline{feel} drowsy.
    
    My stomach \underline{hurts}.
\end{example}
Note, however, that if you are talking about physical perceptions e.g. seeing and hearing, you normally use the modal \textit{can}, although the present simple is occasionally used.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{can see} the fishing boats coming in.
    
    I \underline{can smell} it. Can't you?
    
    I \underline{see} a flat stretch of ground.
    
    I \underline{hear} approaching feet.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.9} If you want to talk about a settled state of affairs that includes the present moment but where the particular time reference is not important, you use the present simple.
\begin{example}
    My dad \underline{works} in Saudi Arabia.
    
    He \underline{lives} in the French Alps near the Swiss border.
    
    He \underline{is} a very good brother. We \underline{love} him.
    
    She'\underline{s} a doctor's daughter.
    
    Meanwhile, Atlantic City \underline{faces} another dilemma.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.10} If you want to say that something is always or generally true, you use the present simple.
\begin{example}
    Near the equator, the sun \underline{evaporates} greater quantities of water.
    
    A molecule of water \underline{has} 2 atoms of hydrogen and 1 of oxygen.
    
    A chemical reaction \underline{occurs} in the fuel cell.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.11} If you want to talk something that a particular person or thing does regularly or habitually, you use the present simple.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do} you \underline{smoke}?
    
    I \underline{get up} early and \underline{eat} my breakfast listening to the radio.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.12} You usually use the present simple when you are discussing what happens in a book, play, or film.
\begin{example}
    In the film he \underline{plays} the central character of Charles Smithson.
    
    In those early chapters, he \underline{keeps} himself very much in the background.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.13} [i] You can use the present simple of the verb \textit{say} when you are describing something you have read in a book.
\begin{example}
    The criminal justice system, \underline{the author says}, has failed to keep pace with the drug problem.
    
    \underline{The Bible says} love of money is the root of all evil.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.14} On radio and television, commentators often use the present simple when they are describing an event e.g. a sports match or a ceremony at the time that it is happening.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{turns}, he \underline{shoots}, he \underline{scores}!
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.15} When you are reporting what someone said to you at a point in the recent past, you can use the present simple of a reporting verb e.g. \textit{hear} or \textit{tell}.
\begin{example}
    I've never been paragliding myself, but they \underline{tell} me it's a really exciting sport.
    
    Tamsin's a good cook, I \underline{hear}.
    
    Grace \underline{says} you told her to come over here.
\end{example}
For more detailed information about reporting verbs, see Chap. 7.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.16} When you are commenting on what you are saying or doing, you use the present simple of a verb e.g. \textit{admit, promise, reject}, or \textit{enclose}.

For more information on this type of verb, see paragraphs \textbf{7.64--7.67}.
\begin{example}
    This, I \underline{admit}, was my favorite activity.
    
    I \underline{enclose} a small cheque which may come in handy.
    
    I \underline{leave} it for you to decide.
\end{example}

\subsection{The present progressive}
\noindent\fbox{4.17} If you want to talk about an activity that is in progress, you use the \textbf{present progressive}.
\begin{example}
    We\underline{'re having} a meeting. Come and joint in.
    
    What am I \underline{doing}? I\underline{'m looking} out of the window.
    
    My head \underline{is aching}.
    
    I\underline{'m} already \underline{feeling} tense.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.18} If you want to emphasize the present moment or to indicate that a situation is temporary, you use the present progressive.
\begin{example}
    Only 1 hospital, at Angal, \underline{is functioning}.
    
    We\underline{'re trying} to create a more democratic society.
    
    She\underline{'s spending} the summer in Europe.
    
    I\underline{'m working} as a British Council Officer.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.19} You also use the present progressive to talk about changes, trends, development, and progress.
\begin{example}
    The village \underline{is changing} but it is still undisturbed.
    
    His handwriting \underline{is improving}.
    
    World energy demand \underline{is increasing} at a rate of about 3\% per year.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.20} If you want to talk about a habitual action that takes place regularly, especially one that is new or temporary, you use the present progressive.
\begin{example}
    You\underline{'re going} out a lot these days.
    
    Do you know if she\underline{'s} still \underline{playing} these days?
    
    She\underline{'s seeing} a lot more of them.
\end{example}

\subsection{Time adverbials with reference to the present}
\noindent\fbox{4.21} You do not normally need to use an additional time adverbial with present forms of verbs, but you can add them in order to emphasize the immediate present or general present, or to contrast the present with the past or future.
\begin{example}
    They're getting on quite well \underline{at the moment}.
    
    We're safe \underline{now}.
    
    What's the matter with you \underline{today}, Marnie?
    
    I haven't got a grant \underline{this year}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.22} If you are using the present simple to talk about something that is always or generally true, you can reinforce or weaken your statement by using an adverb.
\begin{example}
    Babies \underline{normally} lose weight in the beginning.
    
    The attitude is \underline{usually} 1 of ridicule.
    
    \underline{Traditionally}, the Japanese prefer good quality clothes.
\end{example}
Here is a list of common adverbs that can be used to modify your statement in this way:
\begin{example}
    always, generally, mainly, normally, often, traditionally, usually
\end{example}
The use of the present simple to talk about general truths is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.10}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.23} When you use the present simple to say that an action takes place regularly, you can use an \textbf{adverbial of frequency} e.g. \textit{often} or \textit{sometimes} to be more specific about how often it happens.
\begin{example}
    Several groups meet \underline{weekly}.
    
    I visit her about \underline{once every 6 months}.
    
    It \underline{seldom} rains there.
    
    I \underline{never} drink alone.
\end{example}
The use of the present simple to talk about regular activities is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.11}.

%
More information about adverbials of frequency, including a list of the most common ones, can be found in paragraphs \textbf{4.114--4.122}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.24} The present progressive is also used with words like \textit{always} and \textit{forever} when you want to emphasize how often the action takes place.

This use expresses disapproval or annoyance.

The adverb is placed after the auxiliary verb.
\begin{example}
    You're \underline{always} looking for faults.
    
    It's \underline{always} raining.
    
    And she's \underline{always} talking to him on the telephone.
    
    They are \underline{forever} being knocked down by cars.
\end{example}
The use of the present progressive to talk about frequent, habitual actions is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.20}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.25} Note the some adverbs that refer to the present time, e.g. \textit{now} and \textit{today}, are also used to express past time.

However, there are a few adverbs and other time adverbials that are almost always used with present verb forms:
\begin{example}
    I'm not planning on having children \underline{at present}.
    
    $\ldots$ the camping craze that is \underline{currently} sweeping America.
    
    \underline{Nowadays} fitness is becoming a generally accepted principle of life.
\end{example}
The following is a list of time adverbials that are normally only used with present verb forms:
\begin{example}
    at present, currently, in this day and, age, nowadays, presently, these days
\end{example}
Note that in this list the word \textit{presently} means \textit{now}.

\section{The past}
\noindent\fbox{4.26} When you are talking about the past, a time adverbial is necessary to specify the particular time in the past you are referring to.

The time reference can be established in a previous clause, and the verbs in the following clauses are therefore put in the past tense.
\begin{example}
    It was very cold \underline{that night}. Over my head \underline{was} a gap in the reed matting of the roof.
    
    The house was damaged by fire \underline{yesterday}. No-one \underline{was} injured.
\end{example}

\subsection{Stating a definite time in the past: the past simple}
\noindent\fbox{4.27} If you want to say that an event occurred or that something was the case at a particular time in the past, you use the \textbf{past simple}.
\begin{example}
    The Israeli Prime Minister \underline{flew} into New York yesterday to start his visit to the US.
    
    Our regular window cleaner \underline{went off} to Canada last year.
    
    On Feb 1 1968 he \underline{introduced} the Industrial Expansion Bill.
    
    They \underline{gave} me medication to help me relax.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.28} If you want to say that a situation existed over a period of time in the past, you also use the past simple.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{lived} in Paris during his last years.
    
    Throughout his life he \underline{suffered} from epilepsy.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.29} If you are talking about something that happened in the past, and you mention a situation that existed at that time, you use the past simple.

You can do this whether or not the situation still exists.
\begin{example}
    All the streets in this part of Watford \underline{looked} alike.
    
    About 50 miles from the university there \underline{was} 1 of India's most famous and ancient Hindu temples.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.30} If you want to talk about an activity that took place regularly or repeatedly in the past, but that no longer occurs, you use the past simple.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{walked} a great deal when I was a boy.
    
    Each week we \underline{trekked} to the big house.
\end{example}
\textit{Would} and \textit{used to} can also be used to say that something happened regularly in the past but no longer does so.

See paragraphs \textbf{5.114--5.255} for more information.

\subsection{Actions in progress in the past: the past progressive}
\noindent\fbox{4.31} If you want to focus on action in progress or repeated actions that occurred in the past, you use the \textbf{past progressive}.
\begin{example}
    Her tooth \underline{was aching}, her burnt finger \underline{was hurting}.
    
    He \underline{was looking} ill.
    
    Everyone \underline{was begging} the captain to surrender.
    
    I \underline{was meeting} thousands of people and \underline{getting to know} no one.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.32} If you want to contrast a situation with an event that happened just after that situation existed, you use the past progressive to describe the 1st situation.

You then use the past simple to describe and draw attention to the event that occurred after it.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{were} all \underline{sitting} round the fire waiting for my brother to come home. He \underline{arrived} about 6 in the evening.
    
    I \underline{was waiting} angrily on Monday morning when I \underline{saw} Mrs. Miller.
\end{example}

\subsection{The past in relation to the present: the present perfect}
\noindent\fbox{4.33} If you want to mention something that happened in the past but you do not want to state a specific time, you use the \textbf{present perfect}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{have raised} \pounds190 for a swimming pool.
    
    I \underline{have noticed} this trait in many photographers.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.34} [!] You cannot use time adverbials that place the action at a definite time in the past with the present perfect.

E.g., you cannot say \textit{I have done it yesterday}.

%
You can, however, use an \textbf{adverbial of duration}.
\begin{example}
    The settlers have left the bay \underline{forever}.
    
    I ate brown rice, which I have \underline{always} hated, and vegetables from my garden.
\end{example}
Adverbials of duration are explained and listed in paragraphs \textbf{4.123--4.142}.

%
You can also use \textit{since} and \textit{for} with the present perfect because they are used in this way they refer to a definite time.
\begin{example}
    They have been back every year \underline{since then}.
    
    She has worked for him \underline{for 10 years}.
\end{example}
For more information on \textit{since} see paragraph \textbf{4.137}.

Other uses of \textit{for} are explained in paragraphs \textbf{4.125--4.128}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.35} If you want to talk about an activity or situation that started at some time in the past, continued, and is still happening now, you use the present perfect or the \textbf{present perfect progressive}.
\begin{example}
    All my adult life \underline{I have waited} for the emergence of a strong center party.
    
    She\underline{'s} always \underline{felt} that films should be entertaining.
    
    National productivity \underline{has been declining}.
    
    I \underline{have been dancing} since I was a child.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.36} If you want to emphasize the duration of a recent event, you use the present perfect progressive.
\begin{example}
    She\underline{'s been crying}.
    
    Some people will say that what I \underline{have been describing} is not a crisis of industry.
    
    The Department of Aboriginal Affairs \underline{has} recently \underline{been conducting} a survey of Australian Aborigines.
\end{example}

\subsection{Events before a particular time in the past: the past perfect}
\noindent\fbox{4.37} If you want to talk about a past event or situation that occurred before a particular time in the past, you use the \textbf{past perfect}.
\begin{example}
    1 day he noticed that a culture plate \underline{had become} contaminated by a mould.
    
    Before the war, he \underline{had worked} as a bank manager.
    
    She \underline{had lost} her job as a real estate agent and was working as a waitress.
    
    I detested games and \underline{had} always \underline{managed} to avoid children's parties.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.38} If you want to emphasize the recentness and the duration of a continuous activity that took place before a particular time in the past, you use the \textbf{past perfect progressive}.
\begin{example}
    Until now the rumors that \underline{had been circulating} were exaggerated versions of truth.
    
    The doctor \underline{had been working} alone.
    
    He died in hospital where he \underline{had been receiving} treatment for cancer.
    
    They \underline{had been hitting} our trucks regularly.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.39} If you want to say that something was expected, wished for, or intended before a particular time in the past, you use the past perfect or the past perfect progressive.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{had} naturally \underline{assumed} that once there was a theater everybody would want to go.
    
    It was the remains of a 10-rupee note which she \underline{had hoped} would last till the end of the week.
    
    It was not as nice on the terrace as Clarissa \underline{had expected}.
    
    I \underline{had been expecting} some miraculous obvious change.
\end{example}

\subsection{Time adverbials with reference to the past}
\noindent\fbox{4.40} When you are using past verb forms, you normally use a time adverbial to indicate that you are talking about the past.
\begin{example}
    \underline{At 1 time} the arts of reading and writing were classed among the great mysteries of life for the majority of people.
    
    I've made some poor decisions \underline{lately}, but I'm feeling much better now.
    
    It was very splendid \underline{once}, but it's only a ruin now.
    
    It's Mark who lost his wife. \underline{A year last January}.
    
    It was terribly hot \underline{yesterday}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.41} Time adverbials can refer either to a specific time, or to a more general indefinite period of time.

%
The lists below give the most common indefinite time adverbials that are used mainly with past verb forms.

With the exception of \textit{since} and \textit{ever since}, which come at the end of a clause, you put them after the auxiliary or modal in a verb phrase that has more than 1 word; if you use them with the past simple you put them in front of the verb.

%
The words in the following list can be used with all past verb forms:
\begin{example}
    again, already, earlier, ever since, finally, 1st, in the past, just, last, previously, recently, since
\end{example}
The words in the following list can be used with all past verb forms except the present perfect:
\begin{example}
    afterwards, at one time, eventually, formerly, immediately, next, once, originally, subsequently
\end{example}
Note that \textit{once} here means \textit{at some time in the past}.

For its uses an adverb of frequency, see paragraph \textbf{4.115}.

%
For the uses of \textit{since} as a preposition in time adverbials, see paragraph \textbf{4.137}.

%
Some time adverbials used with past verb forms are more specific.

They include words like \textit{yesterday, ago, other}, and \textit{last}.

Note that \textit{ago} is placed after the noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    I saw him \underline{yesterday evening}.
    
    We bought the house from her \underline{the day before yesterday}.
    
    \underline{3 weeks ago} I was staying in San Francisco.
    
    I saw my goddaughter \underline{the other day}.
    
    It all happened \underline{a long time ago}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.42} [!] You say \textit{last night}, not \textit{yesterday night}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.43} There are some cases where you have to specify the time reference.

In other cases, you may simply want to make the timing of the action clear, or emphatic.

These cases are described below.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.44} When you use the past simple to describe habitual or regular activities, you can use an \textbf{adverbial of frequency} to indicate the regularity or repetition of the activity.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{often agreed} to work quite cheaply.
    
    \underline{Sometimes} he \underline{read} so much that he \underline{became} confused.
    
    Etta \underline{phoned} Guppy \underline{every day}.
\end{example}
The use of the past simple to describe habitual actions is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.30}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.45} If you are using the past progressive to talk about repeated actions, you can add an adverb of frequency e.g. \textit{always} or \textit{forever} after the auxiliary to emphasize the frequency of the action or to express your annoyance about it.
\begin{example}
    In the immense shed where we worked, something \underline{was always going wrong}.
    
    She \underline{was always knitting} - making sweaters or baby clothes.
    
    Our builder \underline{was forever going} on skiing holidays.
\end{example}
The use of the past progressive to describe repeated actions is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.31}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.46} When you use the present perfect to mention something that is still relevant to the present, you can add an adverb of frequency to show that the action was repeated.
\begin{example}
    I've \underline{often} wondered why we didn't move years ago.
    
    Political tensions have \underline{frequently} spilled over into violence.
\end{example}
The use of the present perfect to talk about situations that are still relevant is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.33}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.47} Note that if you are talking about a quality, attitude, or possession that still exists or is still relevant, you need to ues the present perfect with an adverbial of duration.
\begin{example}
    We\underline{'ve had} it \underline{for 15 years}.
    
    He\underline{'s always liked} you, you know.
    
    I \underline{have known} him \underline{for years}.
    
    My people \underline{have been} at war \underline{since 1917}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.48} If you use the present perfect and the present perfect progressive to mention a continuing activity that began in the past, you can add an adverbial of duration to show how long it has been going on.
\begin{example}
    \underline{For about a week} he \underline{had been complaining} of a bad headache.
    
    They \underline{have been meeting} regularly \underline{for 2 years}.
    
    He \underline{has looked} after me well \underline{since his mother died}.
\end{example}
The use of the present perfect and the present perfect progressive to talk about activities that began in the past is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.35}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.49} When you use the past perfect to describe a repeated even that took place before a particular time in the past, you use an adverbial of frequency to show how often it was repeated.
\begin{example}
    Posy \underline{had always sought} her out even then.
    
    The housekeeper mentioned that the dog \underline{had attacked} its mistress \underline{more than once}.
\end{example}
The use of the past perfect to describe events that occurred before a particular time in the past are explained in paragraph \textbf{4.37}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.50} If you are using the past perfect to talk about a situation that did not change in the past, you use an adverbial of duration to emphasize the length of time during which it existed.
\begin{example}
    They weren't really our aunt and uncle, but we \underline{had always known} them.
    
    \underline{All through those many years} he \underline{had never ever lost track of} my father.
    
    His parents \underline{had been married for 12 years} when he was born.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.51} If you are using the past perfect progressive to mention a recent, continuous activity, you can specify when it began.
\begin{example}
    The Home Office \underline{had until now been insisting} on giving the officers only 10 days to reach a settlement.
    
    \underline{Since then}, the mother \underline{had been living} with her daughter.
\end{example}
Adverbials of frequency or duration can also be added for emphasis.
\begin{example}
    The drive increased the fatigue she \underline{had been feeling for hours}.
    
    The rain \underline{had been pouring all night}.
\end{example}
The use of the past perfect progressive to talk about a recent, continuous activity is explained in paragraph \textbf{4.38}.

\section{Expressing future time}
\noindent\fbox{4.52} It is not possible to talk with as much certainty about the future as it is about the present or the past.

Any reference you make to future events is therefore usually an expression of what you think might happen or what you intend to happen.

\subsection{Indicating the future using \textit{will}}
\noindent\fbox{4.53} If you want to say that something is planned to happen, or that you think it is likely to happen in the future, you use the \textbf{modal} \textit{will} in front of the base form of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Nancy \underline{will arrange} it.
    
    These \underline{will be} dealt with in Chap. 7.
    
    `I \underline{will check},' said Brody.
    
    When \underline{will} I \underline{see} them?
    
    What do you think Sally \underline{will do}?
    
    You \underline{will come} back, won't you?
\end{example}
If the subject is \textit{I} or \textit{we}, the modal \textit{shall} is sometimes used instead of \textit{will} to talk about future events.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall do} everything I can to help you.
    
    You \underline{will stay} at home and I \underline{shall go} to your office.
    
    `We \underline{shall give} him some tea,' Naomi said.
\end{example}
%
[AE] This is not common in modern American English.

%
The modals \textit{will} and \textit{shall} are used in several other ways, usually with some element of future time.

For more information, see Chap. 5.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.54} If you want to talk about general truths and to say what can be expected to happen if a particular situation arises, you use \textit{will}.
\begin{example}
    When peace is available, people \underline{will go} for it.
    
    An attack of malaria can keep a man off work for 3 days. He \underline{will earn} nothing and his family \underline{will go} hungry.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.55} If you are sure that something will happen because arrangements have been made, you can use the \textbf{future progressive}.
\begin{example}
    I\underline{'ll be seeing} them when I've finished with you.
    
    She\underline{'ll be appearing} tomorrow and Sunday at the Royal Festival Hall.
    
    I\underline{'ll be waiting} for you outside.
    
    I understand you\underline{'ll be moving} into our area soon.
    
    They'll spoil our picnic. I\underline{'ll be wondering} all the time what's happening.
    
    Our people \underline{will be going} to their country more.
\end{example}
Note that a time adverbial or an adverbial of frequency is normally required with the future progressive.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.56} If you are referring to something that has not happened yet but will happen before a particular time in the future, you can use the \textbf{future perfect}.
\begin{example}
    By the time you get to the school, the concert \underline{will have finished}.
    
    Maybe by the time we get there he\underline{'ll} already \underline{have started}.
    
    By then, maybe you\underline{'ll have heard} from your sister.
\end{example}
Note that you must indicate the specific future time referred to by using a time adverbial or another clause.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.57} If you want to indicate the duration of an event at a specific time in the future, you can use the \textbf{future perfect progressive}.
\begin{example}
    By the time the seasons ends, I \underline{will have been playing} for 15 months without a break.
    
    The register \underline{will have been running} for a year in May.
\end{example}
Note that you need to use a time adverbial to indicate the future time and an adverbial of duration to state how long the event will last.

\subsection{Other ways of talking about the future}
\noindent\fbox{4.58} If you are stating an intention that something will happen, of if you have some immediate evidence that something will happen fairly soon, you can use \textit{be going to} followed by an \textbf{infinitive}.
\begin{example}
    I\underline{'m going to explore} the neighborhood.
    
    Evans knows lots of people. He\underline{'s going to help} me. He\underline{'s going to take} me there.
    
    You\underline{'re going to have} a heart attack if you're not careful.
    
    We\underline{'re going to see} a change in the law next year.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.59} You can use \textit{be due to} and \textit{be about to} to refer to planned future events that you expect to happen soon.

They are followed by infinitive clauses.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{is due to start} as a courier shortly.
    
    The work \underline{is due to be started} in the summer.
    
    Another 385 people \underline{are about to lose} their jobs.
    
    \underline{Are} we \underline{about to be taken over} by the machine?
\end{example}

\subsection{Time adverbials with reference to the future}
\noindent\fbox{4.60} The \textbf{present simple} is used to talk about timetabled or scheduled events.

The \textbf{present progressive} is used to state firm plans that you have for the future.

A time adverbial is necessary unless you are sure that the hearer or reader knows that you are talking about the future.
\begin{example}
    My last train \underline{leaves} Euston \underline{at 11.30}.
    
    The UN General Assembly \underline{opens} in New York \underline{later this month}.
    
    \underline{Tomorrow morning} we meet up to \underline{exchange} contracts.
    
    I\underline{'m leaving} at the end of this week.
    
    My mum \underline{is coming} to help look after the new baby.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.61} When you want to make a general or vague reference to future time, you use an adverbial that refers to indefinite time.
\begin{example}
    I'll drop by \underline{sometime}.
    
    \underline{Sooner or later} he'll ask you to join him there.
    
    \underline{In future} she'll have to take sedentary work of some sort.
\end{example}
Here is a list of indefinite time adverbials that are used mainly with future forms:
\begin{example}
    in future, in the future, 1 of these days, some day, sometime, sooner or later
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.62} Adverbials that include the word \textit{tomorrow} are mainly used with references to future time.
\begin{example}
    We'll try somewhere else \underline{tomorrow}.
    
    Shall I come \underline{tomorrow night}?
    
    He'll be here \underline{the day after tomorrow}.
    
    \underline{This time tomorrow} I'll be in New York.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.63} Some adverbials that are mainly used with reference to future time include the word \textit{next}.

If you are using a specific day or month e.g. \textit{Saturday} or \textit{October}, you can put \textit{next} either before or after the day or month.

Otherwise, \textit{next} is placed in front of the time reference.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Next week} Michael Hall will be talking about music.
    
    \underline{Next summer} your crops will be very much better.
    
    I think we'll definitely be going \underline{next year}.
    
    Will your accommodation be available \underline{next October}?
    
    The boots will be ready by \underline{Wednesday next}.
    
    A post mortem examination will be held on \underline{Monday next}.
    
    She won't be able to do it \underline{the week after next}.
\end{example}
[AE] In American English, \textit{next} is always placed in front of the time reference.

\section{Other uses of verb forms}
\noindent\fbox{4.64} So far in this chapter, the commonest and simplest uses of the various verb forms have been dealt with.

However, there are also some less common uses of tenses.

\subsection{Vivid narrative}
\noindent\fbox{4.65} Stories are normally told using the past.

However, if you want to make a story seem vivid, as if it were happening now, you can use the \textbf{present simple} for actions and states and the \textbf{present progressive} for situations.
\begin{example}
    There'\underline{s} a loud explosion behind us. Then I \underline{hear} Chris giggling. Sylvia \underline{is} upset.
    
    The helicopter \underline{climbs over} the frozen wasteland.
    
    Chris \underline{is crying} hard and others \underline{look} over from the other tables.
    
    He \underline{sits down} at his desk chair, \underline{reaches} for the telephone and \underline{dials} a number.
\end{example}

\subsection{Forward planning from a time in the past}
