\noindent\fbox{4.66} There are several ways of talking about an event that was in the future at a particular time in the past, or that was expected to occur.

These are described in the following paragraphs.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.67} The \textbf{past progressive} can be used to talk about events planned in the past, especially with some common verbs e.g. \textit{come} and \textit{go}.
\begin{example}
    4 of them \underline{were coming} for Sunday lunch.
    
    Her daughter \underline{was going} to a summer camp tomorrow.
    
    My life \underline{was joining} me later with the 2 children.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.68} The \textbf{past simple} of \textit{be} can be used in structures used to express future events, e.g. \textit{be going to, be about to}, and \textit{be due to}.

The implication is usually that the expected event has not happened or will not happen.

For more information on \textit{be going to}, see paragraph \textbf{5.233}.
\begin{example}
    I thought for a moment that she \underline{was going to cry}.
    
    He \underline{was about to raise} his voice at me but stopped himself.
    
    The ship \underline{was due to sail} the following morning.
\end{example}

\subsection{Referring to states rather than activities}
\noindent\fbox{4.69} Certain verbs are mainly used in the present simple or past simple rather than the present progressive or past progressive.

These are called \textbf{stative verbs}.

The most common stative verbs are listed in the Referene section.

They include verbs that refer to lasting emotions and mental states, e.g. \textit{love, like, want} and \textit{know}; verbs that refer to the senses, e.g. \textit{see} and \textit{hear}; and verbs that refer to permanent states, e.g. \textit{keep, fit} and \textit{belong}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do} you like football?
    
    I \underline{want} to come with you.
    
    Where do you \underline{keep} your keys?
    
    Then I \underline{heard} a noise.
\end{example}
Generally, these sentences cannot be expressed as, e.g., \textit{Are you liking football?, I'm wanting to come with you, Where are you keeping your keys?} or \textit{Then I was hearing a noise}.

%
However, a few of these verbs are sometimes used with present and past progressive forms, particularly in informal spoken English.

You can use the progressive form with these verbs when you want to emphasize that a state is new or temporary, or when you want to focus on the present moment.
\begin{example}
    Rachel \underline{is loving} 1 benefit of the job - the new clothes.
    
    I\underline{'m liking} grapes these days too.
    
    I\underline{'m wanting} the film to be deliberately old-fashioned.
\end{example}
Some people think this usage is incorrect, and it is usually avoided in formal texts.

%
Here is a list of verbs that are traditionally considered to be stative verbs, but that are sometimes used with present and past progressive forms:
\begin{example}
    forget, guess, imagine, lack, like, love, remember, want
\end{example}
You can use the present perfect progressive or past perfect progressive with some stative verbs in both formal and informal contexts.
\begin{example}
    I\underline{'ve been wanting} to speak to you about this for some time.
    
    John \underline{has been keeping} birds for about 3 years now.
    
    Then she heard it. The sound she \underline{had been hearing} in her head for weeks.
\end{example}

\section{Using time adverbials to indicate past, present, or future}
\noindent\fbox{4.70} In many statements, it is the time adverbial rather than the verb form that carries the time reference.

%
E.g., a common use is to put time adverbials that normally refer to future time with the present simple or present progressive when it is used to refer to future actions.

They can also be used with references to the future that are made in the past.
\begin{example}
    The company \underline{celebrates} its 50th anniversary \underline{this year}.
    
    After all, you\underline{'re coming} back \underline{next week}.
    
    The farmer just laughed and rode away. So \underline{the next week I tried} my luck at another farm.
    
    We \underline{arranged} to meet \underline{in 3 weeks' time}.
\end{example}
The adverbs \textit{now, today, tonight}, and expressions involving \textit{this} refer to a period of time that includes the present moment.

They are used fairly commonly with all verb forms.

This is because an event can be located before, during, or after the time specified by the verb form.
\begin{example}
    I was \underline{now} in a Scottish regiment.
    
    Your boss will \underline{now} have no alternative but to go to his superiors and explain the situation.
    
    1 of my children wrote to me \underline{today}.
    
    I will ski no more \underline{today}.
    
    It's dark \underline{today}.
    
    `I went to the doctor \underline{this morning},' she said.
    
    He won't be able to fight \underline{this Friday}.
    
    I'm doing my ironing \underline{this afternoon}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.71} If you want to refer to time that follows a particular event or period of time, you use an adverb e.g. \textit{soon} or \textit{later}.

You can use an adverb e.g. \textit{beforehand} or \textit{earlier} to refer to time that preceded a particular period of time or an event.
\begin{example}
    Sita was delighted with the house and \underline{soon} began to look on it as home.
    
    It'll have to be replaced \underline{soon}.
    
    He \underline{later} settled in Peddle, a small town near Grahamstown.
    
    I'll explain \underline{later}.
    
    I was very nervous \underline{beforehand}.
    
    You'll be having a bath and going to the hairdresser's \underline{beforehand}.
    
    She had seen him only \underline{5 hours earlier}.
\end{example}
This type of time reference is common with past and future forms.

It is sometimes used with present forms when they are used to refer to past, future, or habitual actions.
\begin{example}
    Sometimes I \underline{know beforehand} what I'm going to talk about.
    
    I \underline{remember the next day} at school going round asking the boys if they'd ever seen a ghost.
    
    But \underline{afterwards}, as you \underline{read} on, you \underline{relate} back to it.
\end{example}
Here is a list of adverbials that are used to refer to time in a relative way:
\begin{example}
    afterwards, at once, before long, eventually, finally, immediately, in a moment, instantly, later, later on, presently, shortly, soon, suddenly, within minutes, within the hour
    
    the next day/week/month/year, the following day/week/month/year, the day/week/month/year after
    
    beforehand, early, earlier, earlier on, in advance, late, 1 day, on time, punctually
\end{example}
Note that in this list \textit{presently} means \textit{soon}.

%
You can use \textit{early} to show that something happens before the expected or planned time, and \textit{late} to indicate that it happens after that time.

\textit{On time} and \textit{punctually} are used to show that something happens at the planned time.

%
These adverbs come after the verb or at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    Tired out, he had gone to bed \underline{early}.
    
    If you get to work \underline{early}, you can get a lot done.
    
    He had come to the political arena \underline{late}, at the age of 62.
    
    We went quite \underline{late} in the afternoon.
    
    If Atkinson phoned \underline{on time}, he'd be out of the house in well under an hour.
    
    He arrived \underline{punctually}.
\end{example}
With \textit{early} and \textit{late} you can also use the comparative forms \underline{earlier} and \underline{later}.
\begin{example}
    I woke \underline{earlier} than usual.
    
    \underline{Later}, the dealer saw that it had been sold.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{early, late}, and \textit{on time} are also used after linking verbs.
\begin{example}
    The door bell rang. Barbara was appalled. `They're \underline{early}.'
    
    The Paris train was slightly \underline{late}.
    
    What time is it now? This bus is usually \underline{on time}.
\end{example}
For more information on \textbf{linking verbs}, see paragraphs \textbf{3.126--3.181}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.72} You can also specify a time by relating it to an event, using a qualifying expression or a relative clause after the time adverbial.
\begin{example}
    I didn't sleep well \underline{the night before the prosecution}.
    
    I called him \underline{the day I got back}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.73} You can also use some prepositions to relate events to each other, or to particular period of time.

These prepositions are listed in paragraph \textbf{4.100}, and there is a full explanation in paragraphs \textbf{4.103--4.108}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{After the war}, he returned to teaching.
    
    Joseph had been married \underline{prior to his marriage to Mary}.
    
    Wages have fallen \underline{during the last 2 months}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.74} If you want to refer to a \textit{necessary time}, beyond which an event will no longer be relevant, useful, or successful, you can use \textit{in time}.
\begin{example}
    I had to walk fast to reach the restaurant \underline{in time}.
    
    He leapt back, \underline{in time} to dodge the train.
\end{example}
If something happens before the necessary time, you can use \textit{too early}, and if it happens after the necessary time, you can use \textit{too late}.
\begin{example}
    Today they grow up \underline{too early}.
    
    It's much \underline{too early} to assess the community service scheme.
    
    They arrived \underline{too late} for the information to be any good.
    
    It's \underline{too late} to change that now.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.75} If the time you are referring to in the past or future has already been mentioned, you can use the adverb \textit{then}.
\begin{example}
    We kept 3 monkeys \underline{then}.
    
    We were all so patriotic \underline{then}.
    
    It'll be too late \underline{then}.
\end{example}
To be more specific, you can use \textit{that} with the name of a day, month, season, and so on, or with a general time word.
\begin{example}
    William didn't come in \underline{that Tuesday}.
    
    So many people will be pursuing other activities \underline{that night}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Emphasizing the unexpected: continuing, stopping, or not happening}
\noindent\fbox{4.76} If you want to comment on the existence of the relationship between past, present, and future situations, you can use 1 of the following adverbials:
\begin{example}
    already, any longer, any more, as yet, no longer, so far, still, up till now, up to now, yet
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.77} If you want to say that a situation exists up to the present time, you use \textit{still}.

If you use \textit{be} as a main verb or an auxiliary verb, you put \textit{still} after \textit{be} or the auxiliary.

If you use any simple verb except \textit{be} you put \textit{still} in front of the verb.

\textit{Still} often suggests that the continuation of the situation is surprising or undesirable.
\begin{example}
    It's a marvel that I'm still alive to tell the tale.
    
    Male prejudice \underline{still exists} in certain quarters.
    
    Years had passed and they were \underline{still} paying off their debts.
\end{example}
In negative statements that use the \textit{n't} contraction, \textit{still} is placed in front of \textit{be} or the auxiliary.
\begin{example}
    We've been working on it for over 2 years now. And it \underline{still isn't} finished.
    
    We \underline{still don't know} where we're going.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.78} You can also use \textit{still} in front of a \textit{to}-infinitive to say that something has not happened yet, although it is expected to, or you feel that it should.
\begin{example}
    The Government \underline{had still to agree} on the provisions of the bill.
    
    The problems \underline{were still to come}.
    
    There \underline{are many other questions still to be answered}.
\end{example}
\textit{Still} is not used in negative statements in this way; see paragraph \textbf{4.79} for a similar use of \textit{yet}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.79} If you want to indicate that something has not happened up to the present time, but is likely to happen in the future, you use \textit{yet} with a negative.

\textit{Yet} usually comes at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    We don't know the terms \underline{yet}.
    
    I haven't set any work \underline{yet}. I suppose I shall some day.
    
    They haven't heard \underline{yet}.
\end{example}
If you want to sound more emphatic, you can put \textit{yet} before a simple verb or after the auxiliary and negative word.
\begin{example}
    No one \underline{yet knows} exactly what it means.
    
    Her style \underline{had not yet matured}.
\end{example}\textit{Yet} can also be used in questions, where it is usually put at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    Has she had the baby \underline{yet}?
    
    Has Mr. Harris arrived \underline{yet}?
\end{example}
In American English, when asking whether asking whether something has happened, it is more unusual to use the past simple with \textit{yet}.
\begin{example}
    Did you eat \underline{yet}?
    
    Did the kids see that movie \underline{yet}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.80} You can also use \textit{yet} in affirmative statements to say that something that is expected has not happened up to the present time.

In this case, \textit{yet} is followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    The true history of art in post-war America \underline{is yet to be written}.
    
    He \underline{had yet to attempt} to put principles into practice.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.81} \textit{Yet} is also used in affirmative statements with superlatives to show that the statement applies up to the present, but may not apply in the future.

\textit{Yet} normally comes at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    This is the \underline{best} museum we've visited \underline{yet}.
    
    Mr. Fowler said that February had produced the \underline{best} results \underline{yet}.
    
    This is the \underline{biggest} and \underline{best} version \underline{yet}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.82} If you want to say that a situation that has existed up to the present time may change in the future, you can use \textit{as yet, so far, up to now}, or \textit{up till now}.

They are normally placed either at the beginning or the end of the clause.

They are also occasionally placed after an auxiliary verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{As yet}, no group has claimed responsibility for the attack.
    
    Only Mother knows \underline{as yet}.
    
    \underline{So far}, the terms of the treaty have been carried out according to schedule.
    
    You've done well \underline{so far}, Mrs Rutland.
    
    \underline{Up till now}, the most extraordinary remark I remember was made by you.
    
    $\ldots$ something he had \underline{up to now} been reluctant to provide.
    
    It's been quiet \underline{so far}.
    
    You haven't once \underline{up till now} come into real contact with our authorities.
\end{example}
Note that these expressions can be used in affirmative and negative statements.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.83} If you want to say that a past situation does not exist in the present, you can use \textit{no longer}, or a negative with \textit{any longer} or \textit{any more}.
\begin{example}
    She was \underline{no longer} content with a handful of coins.
    
    They did\underline{n't} know \underline{any longer} what was funny and what was entertaining.
    
    They do\underline{n't} live together \underline{any more}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.84} If you want to emphasize that a situation exists, rather than not yet having occurred, you use \textit{already},

It is usually put in front of any simple verb except \textit{be}, or after \textit{be} as a main verb, or following an auxiliary verb.
\begin{example}
    The energy \underline{already exists} in the ground.
    
    Senegal \underline{already has} a well established film industry.
    
    He was just a year younger than Rudolph, but \underline{was already} as tall and much stockier.
    
    My watch says 9 o'clock. And it\underline{'s already} too hot to sleep.
    
    Britain \underline{is already exporting} a little coal.
    
    We \underline{have already advertised} your post in the papers.
\end{example}
In American English, when stating that something has happened, it is more usual to use the past simple with \textit{already}.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{already advertised} your post in the papers.
    
    The kids \underline{already saw} that movie.
\end{example}
You can put \textit{already} at the beginning or the end of the clause for emphasis.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Already} robberies and lootings have increased.
    
    I was happy for her; she looked better \underline{already}.
\end{example}
\textit{Already} is not often used with the past simple, except with the verbs \textit{be, have}, and \textit{know}.

%
Note that \textit{already} cannot normally be used in negative statements, but can be used in negative \textit{if}-clauses, negative questions, and relative clauses.
\begin{example}
    Refer certain types of death to the coroner if this has not \underline{already} been done.
    
    Those who have not \underline{already} left are being advised to do so.
    
    What does it show us that we haven't \underline{already} felt?
\end{example}

\section{Time adverbials \& prepositional phrases}

\subsection{Specific times}
\noindent\fbox{4.85} Specific time adverbials are used after the verb \textit{be} when you want to state the current time, day, or year.
\begin{example}
    `Well what time is it now?' - `It\underline{'s 1 o'clock}'.
    
    It \underline{was a perfect May morning}.
    
    6 weeks isn't all that long ago, it\underline{'s January}.
\end{example}
They are also often used in prepositional phrases to say when something happened, or when it is expected to happen.
\begin{example}
    I got there \underline{at about 8 o'clock}.
    
    The submarine caught fire \underline{on Friday morning}.
    
    That train gets in \underline{at 1800 hours}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.86} Clock times are usually expressed in terms of hours and parts of an hour or minutes, e.g. \textit{1 o'clock, 5 minutes past 1, 1:20, half past 1}.

The day is usually divided into 2 sets of 12 hours, so it is sometimes necessary to specify which set you mean by adding \textit{a.m., p.m.}, or a prepositional phrase e.g. \textit{in the morning} or \textit{in the evening}.

%
In many official contexts, a 24 hour system is used.

%
If the hour is known, only the minutes are specified: \textit{5 past, 10 to, quarter to, half past} and so on.

\textit{Midday} and \textit{noon} are occasionally used.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.87} The most frequently used words for periods of the day are \textit{morning, afternoon, evening}, and \textit{night}.

There are also some words that refer to the rising and setting of the sun, e.g. \textit{dusk} and \textit{sunset}, and others that refer to mealtimes.
\begin{example}
    On a warm, cloudy \underline{evening}, Colin went down to the river.
    
    They seem to be working from \underline{dawn} to \underline{dusk}.
    
    Most of the trouble comes outside the classroom, at \underline{break-time} and \underline{dinnertime}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of words that are used to talk about periods of the day:
\begin{example}
    morning, afternoon, evening, night
    
    dawn, daybreak, 1st light, sunrise, dusk, sunset, nightfall
    
    daytime, night-time, breakfast-time, break-time, lunchtime, teatime, dinnertime, suppertime, bedtime
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.88} The 7 days of the week are \textbf{proper nouns}:
\begin{example}
    Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
\end{example}
Saturday and Sunday are often referred to as \textit{the weekend}, and the other days as \textit{weekdays}.

%
A few days in the year have special names, e.g.:
\begin{example}
    New Year's Day, Valentine's Day, Presidents' Day, Good Friday, Easter Monday, 4th of July, Labor Day, Halloween, Thanksgiving, Christmas Eve/Day, Boxing Day, New Year's Eve
\end{example}
You can also name a day by giving its date using an \textbf{ordinal} number.
\begin{example}
    `When does your term end?' - `\underline{1st of July}'.
    
    The Grand Prix is to be held here on the \underline{18th July}.
    
    Her season of films continues until \underline{October the 9th}.
\end{example}
You can omit the month if it is clear from the context which month you are referring to.
\begin{example}
    So Monday will be the \underline{17th}.
    
    St Valentine's Day is on the \underline{14th}.
\end{example}
There is more information about ordinals in the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.89} The 12 months of the year are \textbf{proper nouns}:
\begin{example}
    January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November, December
\end{example}
There are 4 seasons: \textit{spring, summer, autumn} (usually \textit{fall} in American English) and \textit{winter}.

\textit{Springtime, summertime}, and \textit{wintertime} are also used.

%
Some periods of the year have special names; e.g., \textit{Christmas, Easter}, and \textit{the New Year}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.90} Years are referred to in English by numbers.

When you are speaking, you refer to years before 2000 as \textit{nineteen sixty-seven} (1967), or \textit{seventeen hundred} (1700), e.g.
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\ldots$ the 11th of January, \underline{1967}.
    
    A 2nd conference was held in Feb \underline{1988}.
    
    My mother died in \underline{1945}.
\end{itemize}
When you are speaking, you refer to years between 2000 and 2009 as \textit{two thousand} (2000) or \textit{two thousand and eight} (2008), e.g.

%
Years after 2009 are said as either \textit{two thousand and ten} (2010), \textit{two thousand and eleven} (2011), etc. or as \textit{twenty ten} (2010), \textit{twenty eleven} (2011), etc.

%
To refer to periods longer than a year, decades (10 years) and centuries (a hundred years) are used.

Decades start with a year ending in 0 and finish with a year ending in 9: \textit{the 1960s} (1960 to 1969), \textit{the 1820s} (1820 to 1829).

If the century is already known, it can be omitted: \textit{the 20s, the twenties, the Twenties}.

%
To be more specific, e.g. in historical dates, \textit{AD} is added before or after the numbers for years or centuries after Jesus is believed to have been born: \textit{1659 AD, AD 1650, AD 1650--53, 1650--53 AD}.

Some writers who prefer to avoid referring to religion use \textit{CE}, which stands for \textit{the Common Era: 1650 CE}.

%
\textit{BC} (which stands for \textit{Before Christ}) is added after the numbers for years or centuries before Jesus is believed to have been born: \textit{1500 BC, 15--1200 BC}.

An alternative abbreviation that does not refer to religion is \textit{BCE}, which stands fro \textit{Before the Common Era}.

%
Centuries start with a year ending in 00 and finish with a year ending in 00.

Ordinals are used to refer to them.

The \textit{1st century} was from \textit{0 AD} to \textit{99 AD}, the \textit{2nd century} was \textit{100--199 AD}, and so on, so the period \textit{1800--1899 AD} was the \textit{19th century} and the current century is the \textit{twenty-first century} (2000--2099 AD).

Centuries can also be written using numbers: \textit{the 21st century}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.91} If you want to say when something happens, you use \textit{at} with clock times, periods of the year, and periods of the day except for \textit{morning, evening, afternoon}, and \textit{daytime}.
\begin{example}
    Our train went \underline{at 2.25}.
    
    I got up \underline{at 8 o'clock}.
    
    The train should arrive \underline{at a quarter to one}.
    
    We go to church \underline{at Easter} and \underline{Christmas}.
    
    I went down and fetched her back \underline{at the weekend}.
    
    On Tuesday evening, just \underline{at dusk}, Brody had received an anonymous phone call.
    
    He regarded it as his duty to come and read to me \underline{at bedtime}.
    
    \underline{At night} we kept them shut up in a wire enclosure.
    
    Let the fire burn out now. Who would see smoke \underline{at night-time} anyway?
\end{example}
You can also use \textit{at} with \textit{time} and similar words e.g. \textit{moment} and \textit{juncture} and with units of clock time e.g. \textit{hour} and \textit{minute}.
\begin{example}
    General de Gaulle duly attended the military ceremony \underline{at the appointed time}.
    
    It was \underline{at this juncture} that his luck temporarily deserted him.
    
    If I could have done it \underline{at that minute} I would have killed him.
    
    There were no lights \underline{at this hour}, and roads, bungalows, and gardens lay quiet.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.92} You can also use \textit{at} when you want to relate the time of 1 event to another event e.g. a party, journey, election, and so on.
\begin{example}
    I had 1st met Kruger \underline{at a party} at the British Embassy.
    
    She represented the Association \underline{at the annual meeting of the American Medical Association} in Chicago.
    
    It is to be reopened \underline{at the annual conference} in 3 weeks' time.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.93} \textit{At} is also used with ages, stages of development, and points within a larger period of time.
\begin{example}
    \underline{At the age of 20}, she married another Spanish dancer.
    
    He left school \underline{at 17}.
    
    \underline{At an early stage of the war} the British Government began recruiting a team of top mathematicians and electronics experts.
    
    We were due to return to the United Kingdom \underline{at the beginning of March}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.94} If you want to mention the period of time in which something happens, you use \textit{in} with centuries, years, seasons, months, and the periods of the day \textit{morning, afternoon}, and \textit{evening}.

You also ues \textit{in} with \textit{daytime} and \textit{night-time}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{In the 16th century} there were 3 tennis courts.
    
    It's true that we expected a great deal \underline{in the 60s}.
    
    Americans visiting Sweden \underline{in the early 1950s} were astounded by its cleanliness.
    
    If you were to go on holiday on the continent \underline{in wintertime} what sport could you take part in?
    
    To be in Cornwall at any time is a pleasure; to be here \underline{in summer} is a bonus.
    
    It's a lot cooler \underline{in the autumn}.
    
    She will preside over the annual meeting of the Court \underline{in December}.
    
    \underline{In September} I traveled to California to see the finished film.
    
    I'll ring the agent \underline{in the morning}.
    
    Well, she does come in to clean the rooms \underline{in the day-time}.
\end{example}
Note that if \textit{morning, afternoon}, and \textit{evening} are used with a modifier or a following phrase or clause, you use \textit{on}.

See paragraph \textbf{4.96} for details.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.95} \textit{In} is also used when you want to specify a period of time, minutes, hours, days, and so on, using an \textbf{ordinal}.
\begin{example}
    Vehicle sales \underline{in the 1st 8 months} of the year have plunged by 24.4 per cent.
    
    $\ldots$ \underline{in the early hours} of the morning.
\end{example}
\textit{In} is also used with some other nouns referring to events and periods of time.
\begin{example}
    My father was killed \underline{in the war}.
    
    Everyone does unusual jobs \underline{in wartime}.
    
    \underline{In winter}, we tend to get up later.
    
    2 people came to check my room \underline{in my absence}.
\end{example}
Ordinals are explained in paragraphs \textbf{2.232--2.239}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.96} If you want to mention the day when something happens, you use \textit{on}.

You can do this with named days, with days referred to by ordinals, and with days referred to by a special term e.g. \textit{birthday} or \textit{anniversary}.
\begin{example}
    I'll send the cheque round \underline{on Monday}.
    
    Everybody went to church \underline{on Christmas Day}.
    
    I hear you have bingo \underline{on Wednesday}.
    
    Pentonville Prison was set up \underline{on Boxing Day}, 1842.
    
    He was born \underline{on Apr 3, 1925} at 40 Grosvenor Road.
    
    $\ldots$ the grey suit Elsa had bought for him \underline{on his birthday}.
    
    Many of Eisenhower's most cautious commanders were even prepared to risk attack \underline{on the 8th or 9th}.
    
    $\ldots$ addressing Parliament \underline{on the 36th anniversary} of his country's independence.
\end{example}
You can use \textit{the} with named days for emphasis or contrast, and \textit{a} to indicate any day of that name.
\begin{example}
    He died \underline{on the Friday} and was buried \underline{on the Sunday}.
    
    We get a lot of calls \underline{on a Friday}.
\end{example}
You also use \textit{on} with \textit{morning, afternoon, evening}, and \textit{night} when they are modified or when they are followed by extra information in the form of a phrase or a clause.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ at 2.30 p.m. \underline{on a calm afternoon}.
    
    There was another important opening \underline{on the same evening}.
    
    Tickets will be available \underline{on the morning of the performance}.
    
    It's terribly good of you to turn out \underline{on a night like this}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.97} \textit{On} is also used with words referring to travel e.g. \textit{journey, trip, voyage, flight}, and \textit{way} to say when something happened.
\begin{example}
    But \underline{on that journey}, for the 1st time, Luce's faith in the eventual outcome was shaken.
    
    Eileen was accompanying her father to visit friends made \underline{on a camping trip} the year before.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.98} \textit{On} can be used in a slightly formal way with nouns and \textit{-ing} forms referring to actions or activities to show that 1 event occurs after another.
\begin{example}
    I shall bring the remaining 700 pounds \underline{on my return} in 11 days.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.99} On the few occasions when people have to specify a time and date exactly, e.g. in legal English or formal documents, the usual order is: clock time, followed by period of day, day of week, and date.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ at 8 o'clock on the morning of Oct 29, 1618.
    
    $\ldots$ on the night of Thursday Jul 16.
\end{example}

\subsection{Non-specific times}
\noindent\fbox{4.100} If you want to be less precise about when something happened, you can use a word like \textit{around} or \textit{about}.
\begin{example}
    At \underline{about} 4 o'clock in the morning, we were awoken by a noise.
    
    The device that exploded at \underline{around} midnight on Wednesday severely damaged the 4th-floor bar.
    
    The supply of servants continued until \underline{about} 1950, then abruptly dried up.
    
    The attack began \underline{shortly before} dawn.
\end{example}
Here is a list of words and expressions that you can use if you want to be less precise about when something happened:
\begin{example}
    about, almost, around, just after/before, nearly, round about, shortly after/before, soon after, thereabouts
\end{example}
\textit{About, almost, around, nearly}, and \textit{round about} are usually used with clock times or years.

With \textit{about, around}, and \textit{round about}, the preposition \textit{at} can often be omitted in informal English.
\begin{example}
    Then quite suddenly, \underline{round about} midday, my mood began to change.
    
    \underline{About} 9 o'clock he went out to the kitchen.
\end{example}
It is also possible to use prepositions to relate events to less specific points or periods of time, e.g. when the exact time of an event is not known, or when events happen gradually, continuously, or several times.
\begin{example}
    He developed central chest pain \underline{during the night}.
    
    For, also \underline{over the summer}, his book had come out.
\end{example}
Here is a list of prepositions that are used to relate events to a non-specific time:
\begin{example}
    after, before, by, during, following, over, prior to
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.101} [!] \textit{Almost} or \textit{nearly} can only be used after the verb \textit{be}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.102} You can also use \textit{or thereabouts} after the time adverbial.
\begin{example}
    Back in 1975 \underline{or thereabouts} someone lent me an article about education.
    
    $\ldots$ at 4 o'clock \underline{or thereabouts}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.103} \textit{During} can be used instead of \textit{in} with periods of the day, months, seasons, years, decades, and centuries.
\begin{example}
    We try to keep people informed by post \underline{during September}.
    
    She heated the place \underline{during the winter} with a huge wood furnace.
    
    \underline{During 1973} an Anti-Imperialist Alliance was formed.
    
    \underline{During the Sixties} various levies were imposed.
    
    \underline{During the 17th century} incendiary weapons were invented.
    
    They used to spend the whole Sunday at chapel but most of them behaved shockingly \underline{during the week}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.104} \textit{During} is used with most event nouns to show that 1 event takes place while another is occurring.
\begin{example}
    \underline{During his stay in prison}, he has written many essays and poems.
    
    $\ldots$ trying to boost police morale \underline{during a heated battle with rioters}.
    
    The young princes were protected from press intrusion \underline{during their education}.
    
    Some families live in the kitchen \underline{during a power cut}.
    
    \underline{During the journey} I came to like and respect them.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.105} [!] \textit{During the week} means on weekdays, in contrast to the weekend.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.106} \textit{Over} can be used with \textit{winter, summer}, and special periods of the year to show that an event occurred throughout the period or at an unspecified time during it.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ to keep their families going \underline{over the winter}.
    
    My friends had a marvelous time \underline{over the New Year}.
\end{example}
\textit{Over} is also used when referring to a period of time immediately before or after the time of speaking or the time being talked about.
\begin{example}
    The number will increase considerably \underline{over the next decade}.
    
    They have been doing all they can \underline{over the past 24 hours}.
    
    We packed up the things I had accumulated \underline{over the last 4 years}.
\end{example}
\textit{Over} can be used with meals and items of food or drink to show that something happens while people are eating or drinking.
\begin{example}
    Davis said he wanted to read it \underline{over lunch}.
    
    Can we discuss it \underline{over a cup of coffee}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.107} You can also be more general by stating the relationship between an event and a period of time or specific point in time.

%
\textit{Before, prior to}, and \textit{after} are used to relate events to a time.
\begin{example}
    She gets up \underline{before 6}.
    
    If you're stuck, come back and see me \underline{before Thursday}.
    
    $\ldots$ the construction of warships by the major powers \underline{prior to 1914}.
    
    City Music Hall is going to close down \underline{after Easter}.
    
    He will announce his plans \underline{after the holidays}.
\end{example}
They can also be used to relate 1 event to another.
\begin{example}
    I was in a bank for a while \underline{before the war}.
    
    She gave me much helpful advice \underline{prior to my visit to Turkey}.
    
    Jack left \underline{after breakfast}.
    
    He was killed in a car accident \underline{4 years after their marriage}.
    
    \underline{After much discussion}, they had decided to take the coin to a jeweler.
\end{example}
\textit{Following, previous to} and \textit{subsequent to} can also be used with events.
\begin{example}
    He has regained consciousness \underline{following a stroke}.
    
    He suggests that Ross was prompted \underline{previous to the parade}.
    
    The testimony and description of 1 witness would be supplied prior to the interview; those of the other 2 \underline{subsequent to it}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.108} \textit{Before} and \textit{after} can also be used to show the order of events when the same person does 2 actions or 2 people do the same action.
\begin{example}
    I should have talked about that \underline{before anything else}.
    
    He knew Nell would probably be home \underline{before him}.
    
    I do the floor \underline{after the washing-up}.
\end{example}
You can also sometimes use \textit{earlier than} or \textit{later than}.
\begin{example}
    Smiling develops \underline{earlier than laughing}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.109} To indicate that 2 or more events happen at the same time, the adverbs \textit{together} and \textit{simultaneously}, or the adverbials \textit{at the same time} and \textit{at once} can be used:
\begin{example}
    Everything had happened \underline{together}.
    
    His fear and his hate grew \underline{simultaneously}.
    
    Can you love 2 women \underline{at the same time}?
    
    I can't be everywhere \underline{at once}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.110} You can also show what order things happen in using adverbs e.g. \textit{1st, next}, and \textit{finally}.

\textit{Simultaneously} and \textit{at the same time} are used in a similar way to link clauses.

This is dealt with in paragraph \textbf{10.53}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.111} \textit{By} is used to emphasize that an event occurs at some time before a specific time, but not later.

\textit{By} is also used to indicate that a process is completed or reaches a particular stage not later than a specific time.
\begin{example}
    \underline{By 11 o'clock}, Brody was back in his office.
    
    The theory was that \underline{by Monday} their tempers would have cooled.
    
    \underline{By next week}, there will be no supplies left.
    
    Do you think we'll get to the top of this canyon \underline{by tomorrow}?
    
    \underline{By now} the moon was up.
    
    But \underline{by then} he was bored with the project.
\end{example}

\subsection{Extended uses of time adverbials}
\noindent\fbox{4.112} Time adverbials can be used after the noun phrase to specify events or periods of time.
\begin{example}
    I'm afraid the meeting \underline{this afternoon} exhausted me.
    
    The sudden death of his father \underline{on Nov 17, 1960} was not a surprise.
    
    $\ldots$ until I started to recall the years \underline{after the 2nd World War}.
    
    No admissions are permitted in the hour \underline{before closing time}.
\end{example}
Clock times, periods of the day, days of the week, months, dates, seasons, special periods of the year, years, decades, and centuries can be used as modifiers before the noun phrase to specify things.
\begin{example}
    Every morning he would set off right after the \underline{8 o'clock} news.
    
    Castle was usually able to catch the \underline{6 35} train from Euston.
    
    He boiled the kettle for his \underline{morning} tea.
    
    He learned that he had missed the \underline{Monday} flight.
    
    I had \underline{summer} clothes and \underline{winter} clothes.
    
    Ash had spent the \underline{Christmas} holidays at Pelham Abbas.
\end{example}
Possessive forms can also be used.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a discussion of \underline{the day's events}.
    
    It was Jim Griffiths, who knew nothing of \underline{the morning's happenings}.
    
    The story will appear in \underline{tomorrow's paper}.
    
    \underline{This week's batch of government statistics} added to the general confusion over the state of the economy.
\end{example}

\section{Frequency \& duration}
\noindent\fbox{4.113} Here is a list of units of time that are used when you are showing how often something happens, or how long it lasts or takes:
\begin{example}
    moment, second, minute, hour, day, night, week,  fortnight, month, year, decade, century
\end{example}
\textit{Fortnight} is used only in the singular.

\textit{Moment} is not used with numbers because it does not refer to a precise period of time, so you cannot say e.g. \textit{It took 5 moments}.

%
[AE] \textit{Fortnight} is not used in American English, where \textit{2 weeks} is used instead.

%
Words for periods of the day, days of the week, months of the year, and seasons are also used, e.g. \textit{morning, Friday, July}, and \textit{winter}.

%
Clock times may also be used.

\subsection{Talking about how frequently something happens}
\noindent\fbox{4.114} Some adverbials show approximately how many times something happens:
\begin{example}
    again and again, a lot, all the time, always, constantly, continually, continuously, ever, frequently, from time to time, hardly ever, infrequently, intermittently, much, never, normally, occasionally, often, over and over, periodically, rarely, regularly, repeatedly, seldom, sometimes, sporadically, usually
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    I \underline{never} did my homework on time.
    
    \underline{Sometimes} I wish I was back in Africa.
    
    We were \underline{always} being sent home.
    
    He laughed \underline{a lot}.
\end{example}
\textit{Never} is a negative adverb.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{never} goes abroad.
\end{example}
\textit{Ever} is only used in questions, negative clauses, and \textit{if}-clauses.
\begin{example}
    Have you \underline{ever} been to a concert?
\end{example}
\textit{Much} is usually used with \textit{not}.
\begin{example}
    The men did\underline{n't} talk \underline{much} to each other.
\end{example}
Some adverbs of frequency e.g. \textit{often} and \textit{frequently} can also be used in the comparative and superlative.
\begin{example}
    Disasters can be prevented \underline{more often} than in the past.
    
    I preached much \underline{more often} than that.
    
    They cried for their mothers \underline{less often} than might have been expected.
    
    $\ldots$ the mistakes that we make \underline{most frequently}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.115} [i] To show how many times something happens, you can use a specific number, \textit{several}, or \textit{many} followed by \textit{times}.
\begin{example}
    We had to ask \underline{3 times}.
    
    It's an experience I've repeated \underline{many times} since.
    
    He carefully aimed his rifle and fired \underline{several times}.
\end{example}
If the number you are using is \textit{one} you use \textit{once} (not \textit{1 time}) in this structure.

If it is \textit{2} you can use \textit{twice}.
\begin{example}
    I've been out with him \underline{once}, that's all.
    
    The car broke down \underline{twice}.
\end{example}
If something happens regularly, you can say how many times it happens within a period of time by adding \textit{a} and a word referring to a period of time.
\begin{example}
    They group met \underline{once a week}.
    
    You only have a meal \underline{3 times a day}.
    
    The committee meets \underline{twice a year}.
\end{example}
You can also use an adverb of frequency e.g. \textit{once} with a unit of time preceded by \textit{everyday} to say that something happens a specified number of times regularly within that unit of time.
\begin{example}
    The average Briton moves house \underline{once every 7 and a half years}.
    
    We meet \underline{twice every Sunday}.
    
    \underline{3 times every day}, he would come to the kiosk to check that we were all right.
\end{example}
If an event happens regularly during a specific period of the day, you can use the period of day instead of \textit{times}:
\begin{example}
    I used to go in \underline{3 mornings a week}.
    
    He was going out \underline{5 nights a week}.
\end{example}
A regular rate or quantity can also be expressed by adding \textit{a} and a general time word.

\textit{Per} is sometimes used instead of \textit{a}, especially in technical contexts.
\begin{example}
    He earns about \underline{\pounds1000 a week}.
    
    I was only getting \underline{3 hours of sleep a night}.
    
    $\ldots$ rising upwards at the rate of \underline{300 feet per second}.
    
    He hurtles through the air at \underline{600 miles per hour}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.116} If you want to be less precise about how frequently something happens, you can use 1 of the following words or expressions: \textit{almost, about, nearly, or so, or less}, and \textit{or more}.

%
You can use \textit{almost} and \textit{about} in front of \textit{every}.
\begin{example}
    In the last month of her pregnancy, we went out \underline{almost} every evening.
\end{example}
You can also use \underline{almost} in front of \textit{-ly} time adverbs derived from general time words, e.g. \textit{monthly, weekly}, and \textit{daily}.
\begin{example}
    Small scale confrontations occur \underline{almost} daily in many states.
\end{example}
\textit{Or so, or less}, and \textit{or more} are used after frequency expressions, but not after adverbs of frequency.
\begin{example}
    Every hour \underline{or so}, my shoulders would tighten.
    
    If the delay is 2 hours \underline{or more}, the whole cost of the journey should be refunded.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.117} If you want to say that something happens at regular intervals, you can use \textit{every} followed by either a general or a specific unit of time.

\textit{Each} is sometimes used instead of \textit{every}.
\begin{example}
    We'll go hunting \underline{every day}.
    
    You get a lump sum and you get a pension \underline{each week}.
    
    Some people write out a new address book \underline{every January}.
\end{example}
\textit{Every} can also be used with a number and the plural of the unit of time.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Every 5 minutes} the phone would ring.
\end{example}
The regular or average rate of quantity of something can also be expressed using \textit{every} and \textit{each}.
\begin{example}
    1 fighter jet was shot down \underline{every hour}.
    
    $\ldots$ the 300,000 garments the factory produces \underline{each year}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.118} [i] If something happens during 1 period of time but not during the next period, then happens again during the next, and so on, you can use \textit{every other} followed by a unit of time or a specific time word.

\textit{Every second} is sometimes used instead of \textit{every other}.
\begin{example}
    We wrote to our parents \underline{every other day}.
    
    Their local committees are usually held \underline{every other month}.
    
    He used to come and take them out \underline{every other Sunday}.
    
    It seemed easier to shave only \underline{every 2nd day}.
\end{example}
Prepositional phrases with \textit{alternate} and a plural time word can also be used.
\begin{example}
    \underline{On alternate Sunday nights}, I tell the younger children a story.
    
    Just do some exercises \underline{on alternate days} at 1st.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.119} The adverbs \textit{1st, next}, and \textit{last} are used to show the stage at which an event takes place.

%
\textit{1st, the 1st time}, and \textit{for the 1st time} can indicate the 1st occurrence of an event.
\begin{example}
    He was, I think, in his early 60s when \underline{1st} encountered him.
    
    They had seen each other \underline{1st} a week before, outside this hotel.
    
    $\ldots$ the tactical war games which were \underline{1st} fought in Ancient Greece.
    
    It rained heavily twice while I was out. \underline{The 1st time} I sheltered under a tree, but the 2nd time I walked through it.
    
    \underline{For the 1st time} Anne Marie felt frightened.
\end{example}
The repetition of an event or situation that has not happened for a long time can be indicated by using \textit{for the 1st time} with \textit{in} and the plural form of a general time word.
\begin{example}
    He was happy and relaxed for the \underline{1st time in years}.
\end{example}
A future occurrence is indicated by \textit{next time} or \textit{the next time}.
\begin{example}
    Don't do it again. I might not forgive you \underline{next time}.
    
    \underline{The next time} I come here, I'm going to be better.
\end{example}
The use of \textit{next} with statements referring to the future is described in paragraph \textbf{4.63}.

%
The most recent occurrence of an event can be indicated by using \textit{last} as an adverb or the noun phrases \textit{last time} or \textit{the last time}.
\begin{example}
    He seemed to have grown a lot since he \underline{last} wore it.
    
    He could not remember when he had \underline{last} eaten.
    
    When did you \underline{last} see him?
    
    You did so well \underline{last time}.
\end{example}
The final occurrence can be indicated by \textit{for the last time}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{For the last time} he waved to the 3 friends who watched from above.
\end{example}
The use of \textit{last} in statements about the past is described in paragraph \textbf{4.41}.

%
You can also use \textit{before, again}, and noun phrases with an ordinal and \textit{time} to say whether an event is a 1st occurrence, or one that has happened before.

%
You can use \textit{before} with a perfect form of a verb to show whether something is happening for the 1st time or whether it is a repeated occurrence.
\begin{example}
    I've never been in a policeman's house \underline{before}.
    
    He's done it \underline{before}.
\end{example}
The adverb \textit{again} is used to talk about a second or subsequent occurrence of an event.

Ordinals can be used with \textit{time}, in noun phrases or in more formal prepositional phrases with \textit{for}, to specify a particular occurrence of a repeated event.
\begin{example}
    Someone rang the front door bell. He stood and listened and heard it ring \underline{again} and then \underline{a 3rd time}.
    
    We have no reliable information about that yet, he found himself saying \underline{for the 3rd time}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.120} Some general time words can be changed into adverbs by adding \textit{-ly} and used to show the frequency of an event.
\begin{example}
    hourly, daily, weekly, fortnightly, monthly, quarterly, yearly
\end{example}
Note the spelling of \textit{daily}.

The adverb \textit{annually} and the adjective \textit{annual} have the same meaning as \textit{yearly}.
\begin{example}
    It was suggested that we give each child an allowance \underline{yearly} or \underline{monthly} to cover all he or she spends.
    
    She phones me up \underline{hourly}.
\end{example}
The same words can be used as adjectives.
\begin{example}
    To this, we add a \underline{yearly} allowance of \pounds65.00 towards repairs.
    
    The media gave us \underline{hourly} updates.
    
    They had a long-standing commitment to making a \underline{weekly} cash payment to mothers.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.121} Prepositional phrases with plural forms of specific time words can also be used to indicate frequency.

E.g., \textit{on} is used with days of the week; \textit{during} and \textit{at} are used with \textit{weekends}.
\begin{example}
    We've had teaching practice \underline{on Tuesdays} and lectures \underline{on Thursdays}.
    
    She does not need help with the children \underline{during weekends}.
    
    We see each other \underline{at weekends}.
\end{example}
%
[AE] In American English, and in informal British English, you can use the plural forms of specific time words without \textit{on}.
\begin{example}
    She only works \underline{Wednesdays} and \underline{Fridays}.
    
    \underline{Thursday mornings} I volunteer at the local senior center.
    
    His radio program broadcasts \underline{Friday nights} at 9.
\end{example}
\textit{In} is used with periods of the day, except \textit{night}.
\begin{example}
    I can't work full time. I only work \underline{in the afternoons}, I have lectures \underline{in the mornings}.
    
    Harry Truman loved to sit in an old rocking chair \underline{in the evenings} and face the lawns behind the White House.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.122} To show that something develops gradually, or happens at regular intervals, you can use a general time word with \textit{by} followed by the same general time word.
\begin{example}
    She was getting older \underline{year by year}, and lonelier, and more ridiculous.
    
    Millions of citizens follow, \underline{day by day}, the unfolding of the drama.
\end{example}
Gradual development can also be indicated by the adverbs \textit{increasingly} and \textit{progressively}.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ the computers and information banks on which our world will \underline{increasingly} depend.
    
    His conduct became \underline{increasingly} eccentric.
    
    As disposable income rises, people become \underline{progressively} less concerned with price.
\end{example}

\subsection{Talking about how long something lasts}
\noindent\fbox{4.123} The following section explains ways of showing how long something lasts or takes.

%
Some adverbs and adverbial expressions are used to show how long an event or state lasts.

Here is a list of adverbs that are used to show duration:
\begin{example}
    always, briefly, forever, indefinitely, long, overnight, permanently, temporarily
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    She glanced \underline{briefly} at Lucas Simmonds.
    
    You won't live \underline{forever}.
    
    The gates are kept \underline{permanently} closed.
\end{example}
\textit{Briefly} and \textit{permanently} can be used in the comparative.
\begin{example}
    This new revelation had much the same outward effect, though \underline{more briefly}.
    
    This is something I would like to do \underline{more permanently}.
\end{example}
The form \textit{long} is only used as an adverb in negatives and questions.
\begin{example}
    I haven't been in England \underline{long}.
    
    How \underline{long} does it take on the train?
\end{example}
In affirmative clauses, it is used in expressions e.g. \textit{a long time} and prepositional phrases e.g. \textit{for a long time}.

However, the comparative and superlative forms \textit{longer} and \textit{longest} can be used in affirmative and negative clauses.
\begin{example}
    Then of course you'll go with Parry. She's been your friend \underline{longer}.
    
    I've been thinking about it a lot \underline{longer} than you.
    
    She remained \underline{the longest}.
\end{example}
In affirmative and negative \textit{if}-clauses, you can use \textit{for long}.
\begin{example}
    If she's away \underline{for long} we won't be able to wait.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.124} However, prepositional phrases are more commonly used.

The following prepositions are used in adverbials of duration:
\begin{example}
    after, before, for, from, in, since, throughout, to, until
\end{example}
The prepositional object can be a noun phrase referring to a specific period of time.

This can be in the singular after the determiner \textit{a} (or \textit{one} for emphasis), or in the plural after a number or quantity expression.

%
The noun phrase can also refer to an indefinite period, e.g. expressions e.g. \textit{a long time, a short while, a while}, or \textit{ages}, or plural time words e.g. \textit{hours}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.125} The preposition \textit{for} indicates how long something continues to happen.
\begin{example}
    Is he still thinking of going away to Italy \underline{for a month}?
    
    The initial battle continued \underline{for an hour}.
    
    This precious happy time lasted \underline{for a month or 2}.
    
    \underline{For the next week}, she did not contact him.
    
    We were married \underline{for 15 years}.
    
    I didn't speak \underline{for a long time}.
    
    She would have liked to sit \underline{for a while} and think.
\end{example}
You use \textit{the} instead of \textit{a} when the period of time is already known, with seasons, periods of the day, and \textit{weekend}, or when you modify the time word with words like \textit{past, coming, following, next, last}, or an \textbf{ordinal}.
\begin{example}
    Tell Aunt Elizabeth you're off \underline{for the day}.
    
    We've been living together \underline{for the past year}.
    
    \underline{For the 1st month or 2} I was bullied constantly.
    
    \underline{For the next few days} he had to stay in bed.
    
    Put them in cold storage \underline{for the winter}.
    
    I said I'm off to Brighton \underline{for the weekend}.
\end{example}
Remember that you do not use a determiner with special periods of the year.
\begin{example}
    At least come \underline{for Christmas}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.126} \textit{For} can also be used with specific time adverbials to show the time when something is to be used, not how long it takes or lasts.
\begin{example}
    Everything was placed exactly where I wanted it \underline{for the morning}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.127} \textit{For} can also be used in negative statements when you want to say that something need not or will not happen until a certain period of time has passed.

\textit{Yet} is often added.
\begin{example}
    It won't be ready to sail \underline{for another 3 weeks}.
    
    I don't have to decide \underline{for a month yet}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.128} \textit{For} is used with a plural noun phrase to emphasize how long something lasts.
\begin{example}
    Settlers have been coming here \underline{for centuries}.
    
    I don't think he's practiced much \underline{for years}.
    
    I've been asking you about these doors \underline{for months}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.129} [i] You can also use a general time word with \textit{after} followed by the same general time word to emphasize that a state continues for a long time or that an action is repeated continuously for a long time.
\begin{example}
    I wondered what kept her in Paris \underline{decade after decade}.
    
    They can go on making losses, \underline{year after year}, without fearing that they will go bust.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.130} \textit{In} is used to indicate that something happens or will happen before the end of a certain period of time.

In more formal English \textit{within} is used.
\begin{example}
    Can we get to the airport \underline{in an hour}?
    
    That coat must have cost you more than I earn \underline{in a year}.
    
    The face of a city can change completely \underline{in a year}.
    
    They should get the job finished \underline{within a few days}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.131} \textit{In} and \textit{within} are also used to show that something only took or takes a short time.
\begin{example}
    The clouds evaporated \underline{in seconds}.
    
    What an expert can do \underline{in minutes} may take you hours to accomplish.
    
    \underline{Within a few months}, the barnyard had been abandoned.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.132} \textit{For} and \textit{in} can be used in negative statements to say that something does not happen during a period of time.

You can use them in this way with specific units of time, and with more general time references.
\begin{example}
    He hadn't had a proper night's sleep \underline{for a month}.
    
    I haven't seen a chart \underline{for 40 years}!
    
    The team had not heard from Stabler \underline{in a month}.
    
    He hasn't slept \underline{in a month}.
    
    I haven't seen him \underline{for years}.
    
    Let's have a dinner party. We haven't had one \underline{in years}.
    
    I haven't fired a gun \underline{in years}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.133} Note that with the verbs \textit{last, wait}, and \textit{stay}, which have duration as part of their meaning, the adverbial can be a noun phrase instead of a prepositional phrase with \textit{for}.
\begin{example}
    The campaign \underline{lasts 4 weeks} at most.
    
    His speech \underline{lasted for exactly 14 and a half minutes}.
    
    `\underline{Wait a minute},' the voice said.
    
    He \underline{stayed a month, 5 weeks, 6 weeks}.
\end{example}
The verbs \textit{take} and \textit{spend} can also indicate duration but the adverbial can only be a noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{took} me \underline{a month} to lose that feeling of being a spectator.
    
    What once \underline{took a century} now \underline{took only 10 months}.
    
    He \underline{spent 5 minutes} washing and shaving.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.134} If you want to be less precise about how long something lasts, you can use 1 of the following words or expressions: \textit{about, almost, nearly, around, more than, less than}, and so on.
\begin{example}
    They've lived there for \underline{more than} 30 years.
    
    They have not been allowed to form unions for \underline{almost} a decade.
    
    The 3 of us traveled around together for \underline{about} a month that summer.
    
    In \underline{less than} a year, I learned enough Latin to pass the entrance exam.
    
    He had been in command of HMS Churchill for \underline{nearly} a year.
\end{example}
When you make a general statement about the duration of something, you can indicate the maximum period of time that it will last or take by using \textit{up to}.
\begin{example}
    Refresher training for \underline{up to} 1 month each year was the rule for all.
\end{example}
You can also use expressions e.g. \textit{or so, or more, or less}, and \textit{or thereabouts} to make the duration less specific.
\begin{example}
    He has been writing about tennis and golf for 40 years \underline{or so}.
    
    Our species probably practiced it for a million years \underline{or more}.
    
    $\ldots$ hopes which have prevailed so strongly for a century \underline{or more}.
\end{example}
\textit{Almost, about, nearly}, and \textit{thereabouts} are also used when talking about when an event takes place; see paragraph \textbf{4.100} for details of this.

\subsection{Talking about the whole of a period}
\noindent\fbox{4.135} If you want to emphasize that something lasts for the whole of a period of time, you can use \textit{all} as a determiner with many general time words.
\begin{example}
    `I've been wanting to do this \underline{all day},' she said.
    
    I've been here \underline{all night}.
    
    They said you were out \underline{all afternoon}.
    
    We've not seen them \underline{all summer}.
\end{example}
You can also use \textit{whole} as a modifier in front of a general time word.
\begin{example}
    It took me \underline{the whole of my 1st year} to adjust.
    
    $\ldots$ scientists who are monitoring food safety \underline{the whole time}.
    
    $\ldots$ people who have not worked for \underline{a whole year}.
\end{example}
You can also use \textit{all through, right through}, and \textit{throughout} with \textit{the} and many general time words, or with a specific decade, year, month, or special period.
\begin{example}
    Discussions and arguments continued \underline{all through the day}.
    
    \underline{Right through the summer months} they are rarely out of sight.
    
    \underline{Throughout the 60s}, man's 1st voyage to other worlds came closer.
\end{example}
Words referring to events are sometimes used instead of the time words to emphasize that something happened for the entire duration of the event.
\begin{example}
    He wore an expression of angry contempt \underline{throughout the interrogation}.
    
    A patient reported a dream that had recurred \underline{throughout her life}.
    
    \underline{All through the cruelly long journey home}, he lay utterly motionless.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.136} If you want to emphasize that something happens all the time, you can list periods of the day or seasons of the year, or mention contrasting ones.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ people coming in \underline{morning, noon, and night}.
    
    I've worn the same suit \underline{summer, winter, autumn and spring}, for 5 years.
    
    Thousands of slave laborers worked \underline{night and day} to build the fortifications.
    
    10 gardeners used to work this land, \underline{winter and summer}.
    
    Each family was filmed \underline{24/7} for 6 weeks.
\end{example}
\underline{24/7} is an abbreviation of \textit{24 hours a day, 7 days a week}.

It is used in informal English and in journalism.

\subsection{Showing the start or end of a period}
\noindent\fbox{4.137} You can also show how long a situation lasts by using prepositional phrases to give the time when it begins or the time when it ends, or both.

%
If you want to talk about a situation that began in the past and is continuing now, or to consider a period of it from a time in the past to the present, you use the preposition \textit{since} with a time adverbial or an event to show when the situation began.

The verb is in the present perfect.
\begin{example}
    I've been here \underline{since 12 o'clock}.
    
    I haven't had a new customer in here \underline{since Sunday}.
    
    \underline{Since January}, there hasn't been any more trouble.
    
    I haven't been out since Christmas.
    
    The situation has not changed \underline{since 2001}.
    
    There has been no word of my friend \underline{since the revolution}.
\end{example}
\textit{Since} is also used to indicate the beginning of situations that ended in the past.

The verb is in the past perfect.
\begin{example}
    I'd been working in London \underline{sine January} at a firm called Kendalls.
    
    He hadn't prayed once \underline{since the morning}.
    
    I'd only had 2 sandwiches \underline{since breakfast}.
\end{example}
\textit{Since} can also be used with other prepositional phrases that indicate a point in time.
\begin{example}
    I haven't seen you \underline{since before the summer}.
\end{example}
The noun phrase after \textit{since} can sometimes refer to a person or thing rather than a time or event, especially when used with a superlative, \textit{1st}, or \textit{only}, or with a negative.
\begin{example}
    They hadn't seen each other \underline{since Majorca}.
    
    I have never had another dog \underline{since Jonnie}.
    
    Ever \underline{since London}, I've been working towards this.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.138} The time when a situation began can also be shown by using the preposition \textit{from} and adding the adverb \textit{on} or \textit{onwards}.

The noun phrase can be a date, an event, or a period.

The verb can be in the past simple or in a perfect form.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ the history of British industry \underline{from the mid 60s on}.
    
    \underline{From the 18th century on}, great private palaces went up.
    
    But \underline{from the mid-1960s onwards} the rate of public welfare spending has tended to accelerate.
    
    The family size starts to influence development \underline{from birth}.
    
    They never perceived that they themselves had forced women into this role \underline{from childhood}.
    
    $\ldots$ the guide who had been with us \underline{from the beginning}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.139} You can also use the preposition \textit{after} to give the time when a situation began.
\begin{example}
    They don't let anybody in \underline{after 6 o'clock}.
    
    \underline{After 1929} I concentrated on canvas work.
    
    He'd have a number of boys to help him through the summertime but \underline{after October} he'd just have the one.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.140} Similarly, if you want to say that a situation continues for some time and then stops, you can indicate the time when it stops by using the preposition \textit{until} with a time adverbial or an event.
\begin{example}
    The school was kept open \underline{until 10 o'clock} 5 nights a week.
    
    They danced and laughed and talked \underline{until dawn}.
    
    She walked back again and sat in her room \underline{until dinner}.
    
    I've just discovered she's only here \underline{until Sunday}.
    
    He had been willing to wait \underline{until the following summer}.
    
    \underline{Until the end of the 18th century} little had been known about Persia.
    
    \underline{Until that meeting}, most of us knew very little about him.
\end{example}
\textit{Until} can also be used in negative clauses to say that something did not or will not happen before a particular time.
\begin{example}
    We won't get them \underline{until September}.
    
    My plane does not leave \underline{until tomorrow morning}.
\end{example}
\textit{Until} is also used with other prepositional phrases that indicate a point in time.
\begin{example}
    I decided to wait \underline{until after Easter} to visit John.
\end{example}
Some people use \textit{till} instead of \textit{until}, especially in informal English.
\begin{example}
    Sometimes I lie in bed \underline{till 9 o'clock}.
\end{example}
\textit{Up to} and \textit{up till} are also sometimes used, mainly before \textit{now} and \textit{then}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Up to now}, I have been happy with his work.
    
    It was something he had never even considered \underline{up till now}.
    
    I had a 3-wheel bike \underline{up to a few years ago} but it got harder and harder to push it along.
    
    \underline{Up to 1989}, growth averaged 1 per cent.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.141} You can also use the preposition \textit{before} to indicate when a situation ends.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Before 1716} Cheltenham had been a small market town.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{4.142} The duration of a situation or event can be shown by saying when it begins and when it ends.

You can use \textit{from} to show when it begins and \textit{to, till}, or \textit{until} to show when it ends.
\begin{example}
    The Blitz on London began with nightly bombings \underline{from Sep 7 to Nov 2}.
    
    They are active in the line \underline{from about January until October}.
    
    They seem to be working \underline{from dawn till dusk}.
\end{example}
You an also use \textit{between} and \textit{and} instead of \textit{from} and \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    The car is usually in the garage \underline{between Sunday and Thursday} in winter.
\end{example}
[AE] In American English, \textit{through} is often placed between the 2 times:
\begin{example}
    The chat show goes out \underline{midnight through 6 a.m}.
\end{example}
If you are using figures to refer to 2 times or years, you can separate them with a dash, instead of using \textit{from} and \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ open \underline{10-5} weekdays, \underline{10-6} Saturdays and \underline{2-6} Sundays.
\end{example}

\subsection{Using time expressions to modify nouns}
\noindent\fbox{4.143} You can also use time expressions involving a \textbf{cardinal number} and a general time word to modify nouns.

Note that an apostrophe is added to the time word.
\begin{example}
    4 of those were sentenced to \underline{15 days' detention}.
    
    They want to take on staff with \underline{2 years' experience}.
\end{example}
This use of cardinal numbers is described in paragraph \textbf{2.231}.

%
\noindent\fbox{4.144} Time expressions are also used as \textbf{compound adjectives} to modify countable nouns.
\begin{example}
    They all have to start off with a \underline{6-month course} in German.
    
    I arrived at the University for a \underline{3-month stint} as a visiting lecturer.
\end{example}
Compound adjectives are explained in paragraphs \textbf{2.94--2.102}.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------%

\chapter{Varying the message: modals, negatives, \& ways of forming sentences}

\noindent\fbox{5.1} This chapter deals with 3 different ways in which the meaning of a sentence can be varied, by altering the order of words or by adding other words to the verb phrase.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.2--5.48} explain how to form statements, questions, orders, and suggestions.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.49--5.49} explain how \textbf{negative words} are used to talk about the opposite of something or the absence of something.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.94--5.258} explain how \textbf{modals} are used to talk about possibility, or to show the attitude of the speaker.

\section{Statements, questions, orders, \& suggestions}
\noindent\fbox{5.2} Sentences are used to do many different things.

%
The most common use is to give information.
\begin{example}
    I went to Glasgow University.
    
    Carol was 1 of my sister's best friends.
\end{example}
Sometimes you use a sentence to obtain information, rather than to give it.
\begin{example}
    Where is my father?
    
    What did you say to Myra?
    
    How long have you been out of this country?
\end{example}
At other times you want to express an opinion, give an order, make a suggestion, or make a promise.
\begin{example}
    That's an excellent idea.
    
    Go away, all of you.
    
    Shall we listen to the news?
    
    If you have any questions, I'll do my best to answer them.
\end{example}
When someone says or writes a sentence, they need to show what they are trying to do with it, so that it will be clear, e.g., that they are asking a question and not making a statement.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.3} Often it is the order of words that indicates which way a sentence is being used.

E.g., if you say \textit{He is Norwegian}, the word order makes it clear that you are making a statement.

If you say \textit{Is he Norwegian?}, the word order indicates that you are asking a question.

%
Another way of showing which way a sentence is being used is to begin it with a verb, rather than with the subject.

E.g., if you say \textit{Give this book to Michael}, it is clear that you are giving an order or instruction, rather than making a statement or asking a question.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.4} There are 3 main ways of showing what type of sentence is being used in English.

These sentence-types are used for forming statements, questions, and ordres.

%
The \textbf{declarative} form is used in most main clauses.

Statements are almost always made using the declarative form.

When a clause is in the declarative, the subject is placed in front of the verb.

%
The declarative is sometimes called the \textbf{indicative}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I want} to talk to Mr Castle
    
    \underline{Gertrude looked} at Anne.
    
    \underline{We'll give} you 15 pounds now.
\end{example}
The \textbf{interrogative} form is usually used in questions.

In clauses, the subject is often placed after the main verb or after an auxiliary verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Is she} very upset?
    
    Where \underline{is my father}?
    
    \underline{Have you} met Harry?
    
    \underline{Did you} give him my letter?
\end{example}
The \textbf{imperative} is used to try to direct someone's actions.

In clauses, the subject is usually omitted and the \textbf{base form} of the verb is used.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come back} this minute.
    
    \underline{Show} me the complete manuscript.
\end{example}
There is a 4th form called the \textbf{subjunctive}.

This is a feature of English verbs that sometimes occurs in subordinate clauses.

The subjunctive is not used to distinguish between different uses of language, and is therefore not dealt with in this chapter.

Clauses in which the subjunctive occurs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{7.43, 8.41}, and \textbf{8.48}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.5} The ways in which these different structures are used are explained in the following paragraphs.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.6--5.9} explain how the declarative is used to make statements.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.10--5.36} explain how the interrogative is used to ask questions.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.37--5.41} explain how the imperative is used to try to direct someone's actions.

%
Other uses of the declarative, interrogative, and imperative are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.42--5.48}.

\subsection{Making statements: the declarative form}
\noindent\fbox{5.6} When you are giving information, you use the \textbf{declarative} form.
\begin{example}
    We ate dinner at 6.
    
    I like reading poetry.
    
    Officials have refused to comment.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.7} When you are expressing an opinion, you usually use the declarative form.
\begin{example}
    I think she is a brilliant writer.
    
    It's a good thing Father is deaf.
    
    He ought ot have let me know he was going out.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.8} When you make a promise, you use the declarative form.
\begin{example}
    I shall do everything I can to help you.
    
    I'll have it sent down by special delivery.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.9} You can emphasize a statement by putting \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} in front of the \textbf{base form} of the verb.
\begin{example}
   I \underline{do feel} sorry for Roger.
   
   A little knowledge \underline{does seem} to be a dangerous thing.
   
   He had no time to spend time with his family, but he \underline{did bring} home a regular salary.
\end{example}

\subsection{Asking questions: the interrogative form}
\noindent\fbox{5.10} When you ask a question, you usually use the \textbf{interrogative} form.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.11} There are 2 main types of question.

%
Questions that can be answered by \textit{yes} or \textit{no} are called \textit{yes/no}-questions.
\begin{example}
    `Is he your only child?' - `Yes.'
    
    `Are you planning to marry soon?' - `No.'
    
    `Can I help you?' - `Yes, I'd like to book a single room, please.'
    
    `Are you interested in racing?' - `Yes, I love it.'
    
    `Are you a singer as well as an actress?' - `No, I'm not a singer at all.'
    
    `Do you like it?' - `Yes, I really like it.'
\end{example}
The actual answer to a \textit{yes/no}-question is not always \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.

E.g., if you ask someone \textit{Do you read in bed?}, they might say \textit{Sometimes} or \textit{Never}.

If you say to someone \textit{Do you like jazz?}, they might say \textit{I think it's great}.
