But the questions \textit{Do you read in bed?} and \textit{Do you like jazz?} are still \textit{yes/no}-questions, because \textit{yes} and \textit{no} are the type of answers the questioner expects.

\textit{Sometimes} will be interpreted as a weak \textit{yes} answer, \textit{never} as a strong \textit{no} answer, and \textit{I think it's great} as a strong \textit{yes} answer.

%
\textit{Yes/no}-questions are fully explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.12--5.22}.

%
The other main type of question begins with a \textit{wh}-word e.g. \textit{what, where}, or \textit{when}.

When you ask a question of this type, the answer cannot be \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.
\begin{example}
    `Who gave you my number?' - `Your mother did.'
    
    `Why didn't you ask me?' - `I was afraid to.'
    
    `Where is he now?' - `He's at university.'
\end{example}
This type of question is called a \textit{wh}-question.

When \textit{wh}-words are used as pronouns or adverbs at the beginning of a \textit{wh}-question, they are called \textbf{interrogative pronouns} or \textbf{interrogative adverbs}.

%
\textit{Wh}-questions are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.23--5.36}.

\subsection{\textit{Yes/no}-questions}
\noindent\fbox{5.12} In a \textit{yes/no}-question, if there is an auxiliary verb, the auxiliary verb comes 1st, followed by the subject, then the main verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Are you staying} here, by any chance?
    
    \underline{Will they win} again?
    
    \underline{Will they like} my garden?
    
    \underline{Can he read} yet?
\end{example}
If there is more than 1 auxiliary verb, the 1st auxiliary verb comes at the beginning of the clause, followed by the subject, followed by the other verbs.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Had he been murdered}?
    
    \underline{Has it been thrown away}, perhaps?
\end{example}
For information about auxiliaries, see the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.13} If there is no auxiliary verb, you put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} at the beginning of the clause, in front of the subject, followed by the base form of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do you understand} what I'm saying?
    
    \underline{Does it hurt} much?
    
    \underline{Did you meet} George in France?
\end{example}
Note that if the main verb is \textit{do}, you still put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} at the beginning of the clause, in front of the subject.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do they do} the work themselves?
    
    \underline{Does David do} this sort of thing often?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.14} If the verb is \textit{be}, you do not use \textit{do}.

You simply put the verb at the beginning of the clause, followed by the subject.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Are you} okay?
    
    \underline{Is she} Ricky's sister?
    
    \underline{Am I} right?
    
    \underline{Was it} lonely without us?
\end{example}
If the verb is \textit{have}, you usually put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} at the beginning of the clause, in front of the subject.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do passengers have} rights?
    
    \underline{Does anyone have} a question?
    
    \underline{Did you have} a good flight?
\end{example}
However, when you use \textit{have} in the sense of \textit{own} or \textit{possess}, you do not need to use \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did}.

You can simply put \textit{have, has}, or \textit{had} at the beginning of the clause.

This is a slightly formal use.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Have we} anything else we ought to talk about 1st?
    
    \underline{Has he} any idea what it's like?
\end{example}
If you use \textit{have got} or \textit{has got} in a \textit{yes/no}-question, you put \textit{have} or \textit{has} at the beginning of the clause, followed by the subject, followed by \textit{got}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Have you got} any brochures on Holland?
    
    \underline{Has she got} a car?
\end{example}
\textit{Have got} and \textit{has got} are explained in paragraph \textbf{3.15}.

\subsection{Making a question more polite}
\noindent\fbox{5.15} You can make a question or request softer or more polite by beginning with n expression like \textit{Do you know$\ldots$} or \textit{Could you tell me$\ldots$}

This type of question is called an \textbf{indirect question}.

%
Here are some expressions you can use to make questions more polite:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Could you tell me$\ldots$
    \item Do you know$\ldots$
    \item Do you have any idea$\ldots$
    \item Would you mind telling me$\ldots$
\end{itemize}
When 1 of these introductory expressions is used, the word order of the rest of the sentence is the same as in an affirmative statement.
\begin{example}
    Could you tell me what time \underline{the train leaves}?
    
    Do you know where \underline{he lives}?
    
    Do you have any idea what\underline{'s happening}?
    
    Would you mind telling me how old \underline{you are}?
\end{example}
When you answer a question of this type, the answer is usually \textit{yes} or \textit{no}, followed by the information asked for.
\begin{example}
    `Could you tell me what time the train leaves?' - `\underline{Yes, it leaves at 3 o'clock.}'
    
    `Do you know where he lives?' - `\underline{Yes, in the farm up on the hill.}'
    
    `Do you have any idea what's happening?' - `\underline{No, I'm afraid not!}'
    
    `Would you mind telling me how old you are?' - `\underline{Not at all. I'm 52.}'
\end{example}
Note that the `positive' answer to a question beginning \textit{Would you mind telling me$\ldots$} is \textit{No} or \textit{Not at all}.

It means \textit{No, I wouldn't mind} ($=$ \textit{Yes, that's fine}).

\subsection{Making a statement into a question: question tags}
\noindent\fbox{5.16} You can ask for confirmation that something is true by making a statement in the declarative, then adding an expression e.g. \textit{isn't it?} or \textit{was she?}

Constructions like these are called \textbf{question tags}.

Question tags are most often used in spoken English.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.17} Question tags are formed using an auxiliary or a form of \textit{be} or \textit{do}, followed by a personal pronoun referring to the subject.

%
If the main clause is in the affirmative, you use a negative tag.

Negative tags are always contracted, except in old-fashioned or very formal English.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It is} quite warm, \underline{isn't it}?
\end{example}
If the main clause is in the negative, you use an affirmative tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You didn't} know I was an artist, \underline{did you}?
\end{example}
If the main clause of your statement has an auxiliary in it, you use the same auxiliary in the tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You will} stay in touch, \underline{won't you}?
\end{example}
If the main clause has the past simple or present simple form of \textit{be} as the main verb, you use this in the tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{They are}, \underline{aren't they}?
\end{example}
If the main clause does not have an auxiliary or the verb \textit{be}, you use \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} in the tag.
\begin{example}
    After a couple of years \underline{the heat gets} too much, \underline{doesn't it}?
    
    \underline{He played} for Ireland, \underline{didn't he}?
\end{example}
Note that the negative tag with \textit{I} is \textit{aren't I}, even though \textit{am} is the auxiliary or main verb in the main clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I'm} controlling it, \underline{aren't I}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.18} If you have an opinion or belief about something and you want to check that it is true, or to find out if someone agrees with you, you can make a statement and add a question tag after it to make it into a question.

%
If you are making an affirmative statement and you want to check that it is true, you use a negative question tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You like} Ralph a lot, don't you?
    
    \underline{They are} beautiful places, \underline{aren't they}?
\end{example}
If you are making a negative statement and want to check that it is true, you use an affirmative tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It doesn't} work, \underline{does it}?
    
    \underline{You won't} tell anyone else all this, \underline{will you}?
\end{example}
You can also use an affirmative tag if your statement contains a broad negative, a negative adverb, or a negative pronoun.
\begin{example}
    That \underline{hardly} counts, \underline{does it}?
    
    You've \underline{never} been to Benidorm, \underline{have you}?
    
    \underline{Nothing} had changed, \underline{had it}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.19} The person you are speaking to replies to the content of your statement rather than to the tag, and confirms an affirmative statement with \textit{yes} and a negative statement with \textit{no}.
\begin{example}
    `\underline{It became stronger}, didn't it?' - `\underline{Yes it did}.'
    
    `\underline{You didn't know that}, di you?' - `No.'
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.20} If you are making a statement about yourself and you want to check if the person you are talking to has the same opinion or feeling, you can put a tag with \textit{you} after your statement.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I think} this is the best thing, \underline{don't you}?
    
    \underline{I love} tea, \underline{don't you}?
\end{example}
Question tags can also be used to show your reaction to something that someone has just said or implied, e.g. to show interest, surprise, or anger.

Note that you use an affirmative tag after an affirmative statement.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You fell} on your back, \underline{did you}?
    
    \underline{You've} been to North America before, \underline{have you}?
    
    Oh, \underline{he wants} us to make films as well, \underline{does he}?
\end{example}
When using \textit{let's} to suggest doing something, you can add the tag \textit{shall we} to check that the people you are talking to agree with you.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Let's} forget it, \underline{shall we}?
\end{example}
If you are suggesting that you do something and you want to check that the person you are speaking to agrees, you can add the tag \textit{shall I?}
\begin{example}
    \underline{I'll} call the doctor, \underline{shall I}?
\end{example}
If you are telling someone to do something and you want to make your order sound less forceful, you can do so by adding a question tag.

The tag is usually \textit{will you}, but \textit{won't you} and \textit{can't you} are also used.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come} into the kitchen, \underline{will you}?
    
    \underline{Look} at that, \underline{will you}?
    
    \underline{See} that she gets safely back, \underline{won't you}?
\end{example} 
When you are using a negative imperative, you can only use \textit{will you} as a tag.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Don't} tell Howard, \underline{will you}?
\end{example}
You can also add an affirmative tag e.g. \textit{are you?} to an affirmative statement if you want to show interest in a piece of information.
\begin{example}
    So, you're leaving us, \underline{are you}?
    
    He's a friend of yours, \underline{is he}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.21} In informal spoken English, you can use a 1-word all-purpose question tag e.g. \textit{right?} or \textit{eh?}
\begin{example}
    You're American, \underline{right?}
    
    He's a lawyer, \underline{right?}
    
    Let's talk about something else, \underline{eh?}
    
    Not good, \underline{eh?}
\end{example}
In some varieties of English, particularly those spoken in India, Singapore and Malaysia, \textit{isn't it?} is used as an all-purpose question tag.
\begin{example}
    We've seen that film already, \underline{isn't it?}
    
    They're arriving tomorrow, \underline{isn't it?}
\end{example}
Informal multicultural British English uses the common all-purpose question tag \textit{innit} (a shortened form of \textit{isn't it}), both with and without a rising question mark.
\begin{example}
    It makes you think though, \underline{innit?}
    
    It's all just a bit of fun, \underline{innit?}
    
    So eventually he gave me the sack, \underline{innit}.
\end{example}

\subsection{\textit{Either/or}-questions}
\noindent\fbox{5.22} You sometimes ask a question in which you mention 2 or more possible answers.

You link the possible answers with \textit{or}.

E.g., you might say \textit{Is he awake or asleep?} or \textit{Do you like your coffee white or black?}

You expect the actual answer to your question to be 1 of the answers you have mentioned.

%
Words, phrases, and clauses can all be linked in this way.

%
Questions like these are sometimes called \textit{either/or}-questions.
\begin{example}
    `Is it \underline{a boy or a girl}?' - `A beautiful \underline{boy}.'
    
    `Was it \underline{healthy or diseased}?' - `\underline{Diseased}, I'm afraid.'
    
    `\underline{Shall we take the bus or do you want to walk?}' - `Let's \underline{walk}, shall we?'
\end{example}

\subsection{\textit{Wh}-questions}
\noindent\fbox{5.23} When you ask someone a \textit{wh}-question, you want them to specify a particular person, thing, place, reason, method, time, or amount.

You do not expect them to answer \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.24} \textit{Wh}-questions begin with a \textit{wh}-word.

%
\textit{Wh}-words are a set of pronouns, adverbs, and determiners which all, with the exception of \textit{how}, began with \textit{wh-}.

Here is a list of the main \textit{wh}-words:
\begin{example}
    how, what, when, where, which, who, whom, whose, why
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.25} When a \textit{wh}-word is the subject of a verb, or when it forms part of the subject, the word order of the clause is the same as that of a clause in the declarative, i.e. the subject is put 1st, followed by the verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Who invited} you?
    
    And then \underline{what happened}?
    
    \underline{Which mattress is} best?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.26} When a \textit{wh}-word is the object of a verb or preposition, or when it forms part of the object, or when it is an adverb, the position of the subject is the usual one in the interrogative form; i.e., it comes after the 1st verb in the clause.
\begin{example}
    What \underline{am I} going to do without you?
    
    Which graph \underline{are you} going to use?
    
    Why \underline{would Stephen} lie to me?
    
    When \underline{would you} be coming down?
\end{example}
If you are using the \textbf{present simple} or the \textbf{past simple} of any verb except \textit{be}, you put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} in front of the subject.
\begin{example}
    What \underline{do you} really think?
    
    Which department \underline{do you} want?
    
    Where \underline{does she} live?
    
    How \underline{do you} know what it's like?
    
    When \underline{did you} last see John Cartwright?
\end{example}
If you are using the present simple or the past simple of \textit{be}, the main verb goes in front of the subject.

You do not use \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did}.
\begin{example}
    Where \underline{is the station}?
    
    How \underline{was your meeting}?
    
    When \underline{was the last time you cleaned the garage}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.27} In conversation, a \textit{wh}-question sometimes consists of a \textit{wh}-word on its own.

%
E.g., if you say to someone \textit{I'm learning to type}, they might say \textit{Why?}, meaning \textit{Why are you learning to type?}
\begin{example}
    `He saw a snake.' - `\underline{Where}?'
    
    `I have to go to Germany.' - `\underline{When}?'
    
    `I knew you were landing today.' - `\underline{How}?'
\end{example}
A \textit{wh}-question can also consist of a noun phrase containing a \textit{wh}-word.

E.g., if you say to someone \textit{I gave your book to that girl}, they might say \textit{Which girl?}, meaning \textit{Which girl did you give my book to?}
\begin{example}
    `He knew my cousin.' - `\underline{Which cousin}?'
    
    `Who was your friend?' - `\underline{What friend}?'
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.28} The pronoun \textit{who} is used to ask questions about a person's identity.

\textit{Who} can be the subject or object of a verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Who} discovered this?
    
    \underline{Who} were here friends?
    
    \underline{Who} is Michael Howard?
    
    \underline{Who} did he marry?
\end{example}
In more formal English, \textit{whom} is sometimes used instead of \textit{who} as the object of a verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Whom} shall we call?
    
    \underline{Whom} did you see?
\end{example}
\textit{Who} and \textit{whom} can also be the object of a preposition.

When \textit{who} is the object of a preposition, the preposition is put at the end of the clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Who} did you dance \underline{with}?
    
    \underline{Who} do I pay this to?
\end{example}
When \textit{whom} is the object of a preposition, the preposition is put at the beginning of the clause, in front of \textit{whom}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{For whom} was he working while in Baghdad?
    
    \underline{To whom} is a broadcaster responsible?
\end{example}
In informal spoken English, \textit{who} is sometimes used after a preposition.
\begin{example}
    So you report \underline{to who}?
\end{example}
This use is especially common when you leave out part of the question.
\begin{example}
    `They were saying horrible things.' - `Really? \underline{To who}?'
    
    `It could be difficult.' - `\underline{For who}?'
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.29} \textit{Whose} is used as a determiner or pronoun to ask which person something belongs to or is associated with.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Whose children} did you think they were?
    
    \underline{Whose coat} was it?
    
    \underline{Whose} is that?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.30} \textit{Which} is used as a pronoun or determiner to ask someone to identify a specific person or thing out of a number of people or things.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Which} is the best restaurant?
    
    \underline{Which} is her room?
    
    \underline{Which} do you like best?
    
    \underline{Which doctor} do you want to see?
\end{example}
When \textit{which} is a determiner, it can be part of the object of a preposition.

The preposition is actually put at the end of the question.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Which station} did you come \underline{from}?
    
    \underline{Which character} did you like most?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.31} \textit{When} is used to ask questions about the time something happened, happens, or will happen.
\begin{example}
    \underline{When} did you find her?
    
    \underline{When} do we have supper?
    
    Ginny, \underline{when} are you coming home?
\end{example}
\textit{Where} is used to ask questions about place, position, or direction.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Where} does she live?
    
    \underline{Where} are you going?
    
    \underline{Where} do you go to complain?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.32} \textit{Why} is used to ask a question about the reason for something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Why} are you here?
    
    \underline{Why} does Amy want to go and see his grave?
    
    \underline{Why} does she treat me like that when we're such old friends?
\end{example}
\textit{Why} is sometimes used without a subject and with the base form of a verb, usually to ask why an action is or was necessary.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Why wake} me up?
    
    \underline{Why bother} about me?
    
    \underline{Why make} a point of it?
\end{example}
\textit{Why not} can be used with the base form of a verb, in order to make a suggestion or to ask why a particular action has not been taken.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Why not end} it now?
    
    \underline{Why not read} a book?
    
    If you have money in the bank, \underline{why not use} it?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.33} \textit{How} is usually used to ask about the method used for doing something, or about the way in which something can be achieved.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How} do we open it?
    
    \underline{How} are you going to get that?
    
    \underline{How} could he explain it to her?
    
    \underline{How} did he know when you were coming?
\end{example}
\textit{How} is also used to ask questions about the way a person feels, about the way someone or something looks, or about the way something sounds, feels, or tastes.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How} are you feeling today?
    
    `\underline{How} do I look?' - `Very nice.'
    
    \underline{How} did you feel when you stood up in front of the class?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.34} \textit{How} can be combined with other words at the beginning of questions.

%
\textit{How many} and \textit{how much} are used to ask what number of things there are or what amount of something there is.

%
\textit{How many} is followed by a plural countable noun.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How many people} are there?
    
    \underline{How many languages} can you speak?
    
    \underline{How many times} have you been?
\end{example}
\textit{How much} is followed by an uncountable noun.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How much money} have we got in the bank?
    
    Just \underline{how much time} have you been devoting to this?
\end{example}
\textit{How many} and \textit{how much} can be used without a following noun when you do not need to make it clear what sort of thing you are talking about.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How many} did you find?
    
    \underline{How much} did he tell you?
    
    \underline{How much} does it cost?
    
    \underline{How much} do they really understand?
\end{example}
\textit{How long} is used to ask about the length of a period of time.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How long} have you lived here?
    
    \underline{How long} will it take?
    
    \underline{How long} can she live like this?
    
    \underline{How long} ago was that?
\end{example}
\textit{How long} is also used to ask questions about the length of something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How long} is this road?
\end{example}
\textit{How far} is used in questions about distance and extent.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How far} can we see?
    
    \underline{How far} is it to Montreal from here?
    
    \underline{How far} have you got with your homework?
\end{example}
You can combine \textit{how} with an adjective when you are asking to what extent something has a particular quality or feature.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How big} is your flat?
    
    \underline{How old} are your children?
\end{example}
\textit{How come?} is an informal way of asking \textit{why?}

It is normally used only in speech.
\begin{example}
    \underline{How come} you know so much about Linda?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.35} \textit{What} can be a pronoun or determiner, or it can be used in combination with \textit{if} or \textit{for}.

%
\textit{What} is used as a pronoun to find out various kinds of specific information, e.g. details of an event, the meaning of a word or expression, or the reason for something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What}'s wrong with his mother?
    
    \underline{What} has happened to him?
    
    \underline{What} is obesity?
    
    \underline{What} keeps you hanging around here?
\end{example}
\textit{What} can be used to ask someone's opinion of something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What} do you think about the present political situation?
\end{example}
\textit{What} is often used as the object of a preposition.

The preposition usually goes at the end of the question.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What} are you interested \underline{in}?
    
    \underline{What} did he die \underline{of}?
    
    \underline{What} do you want to talk \underline{about}?
\end{example}
\textit{What} is used as a determiner to find out the identity of something or to ask what kind of thing it is.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What books} does she read?
    
    \underline{What church} did you say you attend?
\end{example}
\textit{What if} goes in front of a clause in the declarative.

It is used to ask what should be done if a particular difficulty occurs.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What if} it's really bad weather?
    
    \underline{What if} they didn't want to part with it, what would you do then?
\end{example}
You put \textit{what} at the beginning of a question and \textit{for} at the end of it when you want to know the reason for something or the purpose of something.

\textit{What are you staring for?} means the same as \textit{Why are you staring?}

\textit{What is this handle for?} means \textit{What is the purpose of this handle?}
\begin{example}
    \underline{What} are you going \underline{for}?
    
    \underline{What} are those lights for?
\end{example}
In informal spoken English, you can also say \textit{What for?} when someone says what they have done, or what they intend to do, and you want to know the reason for their actions.
\begin{example}
    `I've bought you a present.' - `\underline{What for}?'
\end{example}
\textit{What} can also be used in combination with \textit{about} or \textit{of}.

This use is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.47}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.36} If you want a question to sound more emphatic, you can use \textit{whatever} instead of \textit{what, wherever} instead of \textit{where}, or \textit{whoever} instead of \textit{who}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Whatever} is the matter?
    
    \underline{Wherever} did you get this?
    
    \underline{Whoever} heard of a bishop resigning?
\end{example}

\subsection{Directing other people's actions: the imperative}
\noindent\fbox{5.37} When someone gives a very clear order or instruction, they usually use the \textbf{imperative}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Discard} any clothes you have not worn for more than a year.
    
    \underline{Put} that gun down.
    
    \underline{Tell} your mother as soon as possible.
\end{example}
Written instructions are given in the imperative.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Boil up} a little water with washing up liquid in it.
    
    \underline{Fry} the chopped onion and pepper in the oil.
\end{example}
[i] When they are followed by a 2nd verb, the imperative forms of \textit{come} and \textit{go} are used with \textit{and}, followed by the base form of the 2nd verb, rather than a \textit{to}-infinitive.

The 2 actions seem to be separate, because of the \textit{and}, but they are in fact very closely linked.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come and see} me whenever you need help.
\end{example}
[AE] In spoken American English, the imperative forms of \textit{come} and \textit{go} can be followed directly by the base form of the verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come see} what the dog did to the couch. (Am)
    
    \underline{Go get} some sleep. (Am)
\end{example}
An order can be made more forceful by putting \textit{you} in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You} get in the car.
    
    \underline{You} shut up!
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.38} You can use the imperative when you are giving advice or a warning.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Be} sensible.
    
    You \underline{be} careful.
\end{example}
Often advice or a warning is expressed in a negative form.

You form a negative imperative by putting \textit{don't} or \textit{do not} in front of the base form of the verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Don't be} afraid of them.
    
    \underline{Don't be} discouraged.
    
    \underline{Do not} approach this man under any circumstances.
\end{example}
You can also form a negative imperative by putting \textit{never} in front of the base form of a verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Never make} a social phone call after 9.30 p.m.
\end{example}
Another way of giving advice or a warning its to use 1 of the modals \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} in a declarative sentence.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{should get} to know him better.
    
    You \underline{shouldn't keep} eggs in the refrigerator.
\end{example}
This use is explained in detail in paragraph \textbf{5.215}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.39} You use the imperative when you are trying to get someone to do something.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Come} quickly$\ldots$ \underline{Come} quickly$\ldots$ \underline{Hurry}!
\end{example}
You can make an appeal more forceful by putting \textit{do} in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Oh \underline{do stop} whining!
    
    \underline{Do come} and stay with us in Barbados for the winter.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.40} You can use the imperative with some verbs when you are explaining something and you want the listener or reader to think about a particular thing or possibility, or to compare 2 things.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Take}, for instance, the new proposals for student loans.
    
    \underline{Imagine}, e.g., an assembly line worker in a factory making children's blocks.
    
    But \underline{suppose} for a moment that the automobile industry had developed at the same rate as computers.
    
    E.g., \underline{compare} a typical poor country like Indonesia with a rich one like Canada.
    
    \underline{Consider}, e.g., the contrast between the way schools today treat space and time.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used in this way:
\begin{example}
    compare, consider, contrast, imagine, look at, note, picture, suppose, take
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.41} \textit{Let} is used in imperative sentences in 4 different ways:
\begin{itemize}
    \item it is used to give an order or instruction
    \begin{example}
        \underline{Let} Phillip have a look at it.
    \end{example}
    \item you use it followed by \textit{us} when you are making a suggestion about what you and someone else should do.
    
    \textit{Let us} is almost always shortened to \textit{Let's}
    \begin{example}
        \underline{Let's} go outside.
        
        \underline{Let's} creep forward on hands and knees.
    \end{example}
    \item you use it followed by \textit{me} when you are offering to do something.
    \begin{example}
        \underline{Let me} take your coat.
    \end{example}
    \item in very formal English, it is used to express a wish.
    \begin{example}
        \underline{Let} the joy be universal.
        
        \underline{Let} confusion live!
        
        \underline{Let} the best man or woman win.
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
To make a negative suggestion you use \textit{Let's not or}, in informal American English, \textit{Let's don't}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Let's not} stay till the end.
    
    \underline{Let's don't} ask about the missing books. (Am, informal)
\end{example}

\subsection{Other uses of the declarative, the interrogative, \& the imperative}
\noindent\fbox{5.42} You can confirm that something is true by asking a question using the \textbf{declarative} form.
\begin{example}
    So you admit something is wrong?
    
    Then you think we can keep it?
\end{example}
When you ask a question using the declarative form, you expect a \textit{yes}-type answer, unless you use a negative construction, in which case you expect the answer \textit{no}.
\begin{example}
    `You mean it's still here?' - `Of course.'
    
    `You parents don't mind you being out so late?' - `No, they don't.'
\end{example}
Questions expressed in the declarative form often begin with a conjunction.
\begin{example}
    \underline{So} you're satisfied?
    
    \underline{And} you think that's a good idea?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.43} In informal spoken English, you can give an instruction by using a declarative sentence with \textit{you} as the subject.
\begin{example}
    You put the month and the temperature on the top line.
    
    You take the bus up to the landing stage at 12:30.
    
    You just put it straight in the oven.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.44} When you are making an offer or an invitation, you usually use a \textit{yes/no}-question beginning with a model e.g. \textit{can} or \textit{would}.

This use is explained in detail in paragraphs \textbf{5.173--5.178}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Can} I help you?
    
    \underline{Can} I give you a lift?
    
    \underline{Would} you like me to get something for you?
    
    \underline{Would} you like some coffee?
    
    \underline{Would} you like to go to Ernie's for dinner?
\end{example}
You can also make an offer or invitation in a more informal way by using the \textbf{imperative} form.

Note that you can only do this when it is clear that you are not giving an order.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Have} a cigar.
    
    \underline{Come} to my place.
    
    \underline{Come} in, Mrs Kintner.
\end{example}
You add emphasis by putting \textit{do} in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Do have} a chocolate biscuit.
    
    \underline{Do help} yourselves.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.45} When you are making a request, you usually use a \textit{yes/no}-question beginning with 1 of the modals \textit{could, can}, or \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Could} I ask you a few questions?
    
    \underline{Can} I have my hat pack, please?
    
    \underline{Would} you mind having a word with my husband?
\end{example}
You can also give an order or instruction using a \textit{yes/no}-question beginning with a modal.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Will} you tell Watson I shall be in a little late?
\end{example}
These uses are explained in detail in paragraphs \textbf{5.156--5.166}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.46} When you use a \textit{yes/no}-question to offer help or to make a request, you still expect the answer \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.

However, people sometimes say things that seem like \textit{yes/no}-questions, although they do not expect an answer at all.

They are using the \textit{yes/no}-question form to express a strong feeling, opinion, or impression.

%
E.g., instead of saying \textit{That's an ugly building}, someone might say \textit{Isn't that an ugly building?}

Or instead of saying \textit{You never seem to get upset}, someone might say \textit{Don't you ever get upset?}

%
Questions like these are called \textbf{rhetorical questions}.
\begin{example}
    Is there nothing she won't do?
    
    Can't you see that I'm busy?
    
    Hasn't anyone round here got any sense?
    
    Does nothing ever worry you?
\end{example}
Another kind of rhetorical question consists of a statement followed by a question tag e.g. \textit{are you?} or \textit{is it?}

E.g., someone might say \textit{So you are the new assistant, are you?} or \textit{So they're coming to tea, are they?}
\begin{example}
    So you want to be an actress, do you?
    
    So they're moving house again, are they?
\end{example}
Rhetorical questions can also begin with \textit{how}.

They usually express a feeling of shock or indignation.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{You are very cruel}, someone might say \textit{How can you be so cruel?}
\begin{example}
    \underline{How} can you say such things?
    
    \underline{How} dare you speak to me like that?
\end{example}
\textbf{Rhetorical questions} are dealt with fully in paragraph \textbf{9.94}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.47} You can ask a question consisting of \textit{what about} or \textit{what of} in front of a noun phrase, without a verb.

You ask a question like this to remind someone of something, or to draw their attention to something.

%
With this type of question, you often expect an action, rather than a reply.
\begin{example}
    \underline{What about} the others on the list?
    
    \underline{What about} your breakfast?
    
    But \underline{what of} the women themselves?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.48} There are several ways in which you can make a suggestion:
\begin{itemize}
    \item you can use the modal \textit{could} in a declarative sentence (see paragraph \textbf{5.183})
    \begin{example}
        We \underline{could have} tea.
        
        You \underline{could get} someone to dress up as a pirate.
    \end{example}
    \item you can use a negative \textit{wh}-question beginning with \textit{why}
    \begin{example}
        \underline{Why don't} we just give them what you want?
        
        \underline{Why don't} you write to her yourself?
    \end{example}
    \item you can use a question consisting of \textit{what about} or \textit{how about} in front of an \textit{-ing} form
    \begin{example}
        \underline{What about} becoming an actor?
        
        \underline{How about} using makeup to dramatize your features?
    \end{example}
    \item you can use the imperative.
    \begin{example}
        `\underline{Give} them a reward each,' I suggested.
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
You can also make a suggestion about what you and someone else might do by using \textit{let's}.

This use is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.41}.

\section{Forming negative statements}
\noindent\fbox{5.49} When you want to say that something is not true, is not happening, or is not the case, you normally use a \textbf{negative statement}.

Negative statements contain words like \textit{not, never}, or \textit{nowhere}.

These are called \textbf{negative words}.

%
Here is the list of negative words in English:
\begin{example}
    neither, never, no, nobody, none, no one, nor, not, nothing, nowhere
\end{example}
Negative words indicate the opposite of something or an absence of something.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.50} Another group of words e.g. \textit{scarcely} and \textit{seldom} can be used to make a statement almost negative.

These words are called \textbf{broad negatives}.

They are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.82--5.89}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.51} If a statement about the existence of something has a negative word in it, you use \textit{any} (not \textit{no}) as a determiner in front of the following noun phrase.

You can also use a word beginning with \textit{any-} e.g. \textit{anyone} or \textit{anywhere}.
\begin{example}
    We did\underline{n't} have any money.
    
    He writes poetry and \underline{never} shows it to \underline{anyone}.
    
    It is \underline{impossible} to park the car \underline{anywhere}.
\end{example}
For another use of \textit{any} see paragraph \textbf{2.163}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.52} [!] In standard English, it is almost always unacceptable to use 2 negative words in the same clause.

E.g., you do not say, \textit{I don't never go there}, or \textit{I don't know nothing}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.53} The use of negatives in \textbf{reported speech} is explained in paragraph \textbf{7.13}.

The use of negatives with \textbf{modals} is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.104}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.54} The most commonly used negative word is \textit{not}.

Its use with different verbs corresponds to the way these verbs are used in \textbf{\textit{yes/no}-questions} (see paragraphs \textbf{5.12--5.14}).

%
\noindent\fbox{5.55} When \textit{not} is used with a verb phrase that contains an auxiliary verb, it comes after the 1st verb in the phrase.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{could not exist} in their present form.
    
    They \underline{might not} even \underline{notice}.
    
    The White House \underline{has not commented} on the report.
    
    He \underline{had not attended} many meetings.
    
    I \underline{was not smiling}.
    
    Her teachers \underline{were not impressed} with her excuses.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.56} If there is no auxiliary verb, you put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} after the subject, followed by \textit{not} or \textit{-n't}, followed by the base form of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{do not need} to talk.
    
    He \underline{does not speak} English very well.
    
    I \underline{didn't know} that.
\end{example}
\textit{Be} and \textit{have} are exceptions to this; this is explained in the following paragraphs \textbf{5.57--5.58}.

The shortening of \textit{not} to \textit{-n't} is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.61--5.62}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.57} If the verb is \textit{be}, you do not use \textit{do}.

You simply put \textit{not} or \textit{-n't} after the verb.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{is not} difficult to see why they were unsuccessful.
    
    There \underline{is not} much point in heading south.
    
    This \underline{isn't} my 1st choice of restaurant.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.58} If the verb is \textit{have}, you usually put \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} after the subject, followed by \textit{not} or \textit{-n't}, followed by the base form \textit{have}.
\begin{example}
    The organization \underline{does not have} a good track record.
    
    He \underline{didn't have} a very grand salary.
\end{example}
[AE] You can simply put \textit{not} or \textit{-n't} after the verb, but this use is less common, and almost never used in modern American English.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{hadn't} enough money.
    
    I \underline{haven't} any papers to say that I have been trained.
\end{example}
If you use \textit{have got}, you put \textit{not} or \textit{-n't} after \textit{have}, followed by \textit{got}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{haven't got} the latest figures.
    
    He \underline{hasn't got} a daughter.
\end{example}
\textit{Have got} is explained in paragraph \textbf{3.15}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.59} When \textit{not} is used with an \textit{-ing} form or a \textit{to}-infinitive clause, it is placed in front of the \textit{-ing} form or \textit{to}-infinitive.
\begin{example}
    We stood there, \underline{not knowing} what was expected of us.
    
    He lost out by \underline{not taking} a degree at another university.
    
    Try \underline{not to worry}.
    
    It took a vast amount of patience \underline{not to strangle} him.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.60} When a clause contains an inflected verb and an \textit{-ing} form or \textit{to}-infinitive, you put \textit{not} either with the inflected form or with the \textit{-ing} form or \textit{to}-infinitive, depending on the meaning you want to express.

%
E.g., you can say either \textit{Mary tried not to smile} or \textit{Mary did not try to smile}, but they express different meanings.

The 1st means that Mary tried to avoid smiling.

The 2nd means that Mary did not even try to smile.

%
However, with some verbs that are used with \textit{to}-infinitives, the meaning is the same whether \textit{not} is placed with the main verb or with the \textit{to}-infinitive.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{did not appear} to have done anything.
    
    Henry \underline{appears not to appreciate} my explanation.
    
    It \underline{didn't seem} to bother them at all.
    
    They \underline{seemed not to notice} me.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that are used with \textit{to}-infinitives.

With all of these, the meaning of the clause is the same, whether \textit{not} is put in front of the verb or in front of the \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    appear, expect, happen, intend, plan, seem, tend, want, wish
\end{example}
The use of 2 verbs in a clause to talk about 2 actions or states is dealt with in paragraphs \textbf{3.182--3.212}.

%
Note that with some \textbf{reporting verbs} the meaning is the same whether you put \textit{not} in front of the reporting verb or in front of the main verb.

This is explained in paragraph \textbf{7.13}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.61} In spoken English and in informal written Englis, \textit{not} is often shortened to \textit{-n't} after \textit{be} or \textit{have} or after an auxiliary.

\textit{-n't} is attached to the end of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Maria is\underline{n't} really my aunt at all.
    
    He does\underline{n't} believe in anything.
    
    I have\underline{n't} heard from her recently.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{cannot} is shortened to \textit{can't, shall not} is shortened to \textit{shan't}, and \textit{will not} is shortened to \textit{won't}.

%
Here is a complete list of the shortened forms you can use:
\begin{example}
    aren't, isn't, wasn't, weren't, didn't, doesn't, don't, hadn't, hasn't, haven't, can't, couldn't, mightn't, mustn't, oughtn't, shan't, shouldn't, won't, wouldn't
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.62} [i] Note that if the verb is already shortened and added to its subject, you cannot shorten \textit{not} to \textit{-n't}.

This means, e.g., that you can shorten \textit{she is not} to \textit{she isn't} or \textit{she's not}, but not to \textit{she'sn't}.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{isn't} easy.
    
    It\underline{'s not} easy.
    
    I \underline{haven't} had time.
    
    I\underline{'ve not} had time.
\end{example}
Note that you cannot add \textit{-n't} to am.

You can only use \textit{I'm not} as the shortened form.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I'm not} excited.
\end{example}
The form \textit{aren't I} is used in questions.

%
In questions, \textit{not} is usually shortened to \textit{-n't} and added to the 1st verb in the verb phrase.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Didn't} she win at the Olympics?
    
    \underline{Hasn't} he put on weight?
    
    \underline{Aren't} you bored?
\end{example}
However, in formal English, it is also possible to put \textit{not} after the subject.
\begin{example}
    Did he \underline{not} have brothers?
    
    Was it \underline{not} rather absurd?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.63} You can also use \textit{not} with almost any word or word group in a clause.

E.g., you can use it with nouns, adjectives, adverbs, prepositional phrases, and some quantity expressions e.g. \textit{a lot of}.

You usually do this in order to be more forceful, careful, polite, hesitant, and so on.

The following paragraphs \textbf{5.64--5.65} describe some of these uses.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.64} \textit{Very} is often used after \textit{not} to soften the negative meaning of a clause.

This sounds more polite or hesitant than using \textit{not} without \textit{very}.
\begin{example}
    His attitude is \underline{not very logical}.
    
    It's \underline{not very strong tea}; it won't stain.
    
    He wasn't \underline{a very good actor}.
    
    She shook her head, but \underline{not very convincingly}.
\end{example}
You can use \textit{not} with \textit{absolutely, altogether, entirely}, or \textit{necessarily} in a similar way.

You do this in order to sound more polite or less critical.
\begin{example}
    Previous experience is\underline{n't absolutely necessary}, but it helps.
    
    I was \underline{not altogether sure}.
    
    They are \underline{not entirely reliable}.
    
    Science is \underline{not necessarily hostile} to human values.
\end{example}
You use \textit{not} to show that the thing you are describing does in fact have some good qualities, although you do not want to make them sound better than they really are.

This structure is often used with words that have a negative prefix e.g. \textit{un-} or \textit{-less}.
\begin{example}
    She's \underline{not an unattractive woman}.
    
    It's \underline{not a bad start}.
    
    It's a small point, but \underline{not an unimportant one}.
    
    America is very well developed, but \underline{not limitless}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.65} \textit{Not} is sometimes used to contrast 1 part of a clause with another.

Using \textit{not} in this way emphasizes the positive part of a statement.
\begin{example}
    He held her arm in his hand, \underline{not hard}, but firmly.
    
    We move steadily, \underline{not fast}, \underline{not slow}.
    
    `Were they still interested?' - `\underline{Not just interested}. Overjoyed.'
    
    I will move eventually, but \underline{not from Suffolk}.
    
    It's \underline{not a huge hotel}, but it's very nice.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.66} \textit{Never} is used to say that something was not or will not ever be the case.

%
When it is used with a verb phrase that contains an auxiliary verb, \textit{never} is put after the 1st verb in the verb phrase and in front of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would never trust} my judgment again.
    
    $\ldots$ a type of glass that is rare and \underline{is never used}.
    
    The number of people who died \underline{will never be shown}.
    
    50 years ago, men \underline{were never expected to wash} the dishes or help with the children.
    
    I \underline{had never been} to this big town before.
    
    I\underline{'ve never done} so much work in all my life.
\end{example}
However, you can put \textit{never} in front of the 1st word in the verb phrase in order to emphasize the negative aspect of a statement.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{never would have guessed} if he hadn't told me.
    
    There was no such person - there \underline{never had been}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.67} If the main verb is the present simple or past simple of \textit{be, never} usually comes after the verb.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{was never} too proud to learn.
    
    I\underline{'m never} very keen on keeping a car for more than a year.
    
    There \underline{were never} any people in the house.
\end{example}
However, you can put \textit{never} in front of a simple form of \textit{be} for emphasis.
\begin{example}
    There \underline{never was} enough hot water at home.
    
    It \underline{never was} very clear.
    
    There \underline{never is} any great change.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.68} If the main verb is the present simple or past simple of any verb except \textit{be, never} comes in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{never want} to see you in my classes again.
    
    She \underline{never goes} abroad.
    
    He \underline{never went} to university.
    
    He \underline{never did} any homework.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.69} You can make a negative statement more emphatic by using \textit{never} followed by \textit{do, does}, or \textit{did} in front of the base form of the verb.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{I never met him}, you can say \textit{I never did meet him}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{never did get} their money back.
    
    She \underline{never did find} her real mother.
    
    Some people \underline{never do adjust} to life here.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.70} \textit{Never} can be used at the beginning of imperative structures.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Never} change a wheel near a drain.
    
    \underline{Never} dry clothes in front of an open fire.
\end{example}
Imperative structures are explained in paragraph \textbf{5.4} and in paragraphs \textbf{5.37--5.41}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.71} \textit{No} is an indefinite determiner that is used in front of singular and plural nouns to say that something does not exist or is not available.
\begin{example}
    There was \underline{no money} for an operation.
    
    We had \underline{no parents}, nobody to look after us.
    
    He has \underline{no ambition}.
    
    I could see \underline{no tracks}.
\end{example}
Indefinite determiners are explained in paragraphs \textbf{1.223--1.250}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.72} In spoken English, \textit{-n't} is often used with \textit{any} instead of \textit{no}.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{I had no money}, you can say \textit{I didn't have any money}.
\begin{example}
    They had\underline{n't} meant \underline{any} harm to her.
    
    I ca\underline{n't} see \underline{any} hope in it.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.73} The pronoun \textit{none} is used to say that there is not a single thing or person, or not even a small amount of a particular thing.
\begin{example}
    I waited for comments but \underline{none} came.
    
    The entire area is covered with shallow lakes, \underline{none} more than a few yards in depth.
    
    We have been seeing difficulties where \underline{none} exist.
\end{example}
For another use of \textit{none} see paragraph \textbf{1.155}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.74} \textit{None of} is a quantity expression.
\begin{example}
    \underline{None of} the townspeople had ever seen such weather.
    
    \underline{None of} this has happened without our consent.
\end{example}
For an explanation of \textit{none of} see paragraphs \textbf{2.179--2.185}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.75} There are 4 words beginning with \textit{no-} that are used in negative statements.

\textit{Nothing, no one}, and \textit{nobody} are \textbf{indefinite pronouns}.

\textit{Nowhere} is an \textbf{indefinite place adverb}.
\begin{example}
    There's \underline{nothing} you can do.
    
    \underline{Nobody} in her house knows any English.
    
    There's almost \underline{nowhere} left to go.
\end{example}
Indefinite pronouns are explained in paragraphs \textbf{1.128--1.141}.

%
Indefinite place adverbs are explained in paragraphs \textbf{6.61--6.71}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.76} \textit{Nothing, no one, nobody}, and \textit{nowhere} can be followed by \textit{but} to mean \textit{only}.

E.g., \textit{There was nothing but cheese} means that there was only cheese.
\begin{example}
    I look back on this period with \underline{nothing but} pleasure.
    
    He heard \underline{no one but} his uncles.
\end{example}
Indefinite pronouns and adverbs that begin with \textit{any} can be used in similar structures.

However, in these structures \textit{but} means \textit{except}, rather than \textit{only}.
\begin{example}
    I could never speak about \underline{anything but} business to Ivan.
    
    He never spoke to \underline{anyone but} his wife.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.77} \textit{Neither} and \textit{nor} are used together to say that 2 alternatives are not possible, likely, or true.

\textit{Neither} goes in front of the 1st alternative and \textit{nor} goes in front of the 2nd one.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Neither} Margaret \underline{nor} John was there.
    
    They had \underline{neither} food \underline{nor} money until the end of the week.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.78} \textit{Neither} can be used on its own as a reply, to refer to 2 alternatives that have already been mentioned.
\begin{example}
    `Does that mean yes or no?' - `\underline{Neither}'.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.79} If a clause contains a negative word, particularly \textit{not}, you can use \textit{neither} or \textit{nor} to negate a 2nd clause.

In the 2nd clause, you put \textit{neither} or \textit{nor} 1st, followed by the verb, followed by the subject.
\begin{example}
    This isn't a dazzling achievement, but \underline{neither is it} a negligible one.
    
    These people are not insane, \underline{nor are they} fools.
\end{example}
If there is an auxiliary, it is placed in front of the subject in the 2nd clause.
\begin{example}
    The organization had broken no rules, but \underline{neither had it acted} responsibly.
    
    I don't feel any shame. \underline{Neither do I think} I should.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.80} \textit{Neither} can be used on its own in front of a singular noun referring to each of 2 things when you are making a negative statement about both of them.

E.g., \textit{Neither partner benefited from the agreement} means that there were 2 partners and the negative statement applies to both of them.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Neither report} mentioned the Americans.
    
    \underline{Neither film} was particularly good.
    
    \underline{Neither sex} has a monopoly on thought or emotion.
    
    \underline{Neither parent} is the good one or the bad one.
\end{example}
Note that in this structure \textit{neither} is used with a singular verb.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.81} When \textit{neither} is followed by \textit{of}, it makes a set of 2 things negative.

\textit{Neither of} is followed by a plural noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Neither of us} was having any luck.
    
    \underline{Neither of the boys} screamed.
    
    \underline{Neither of them} was making any sound.
\end{example}
\textit{Neither of} is normally used with a singular verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Neither of these extremes is} desirable.
    
    \underline{Neither of these opinions proves} anything.
\end{example}
However, it is also possible to use a plural verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Neither of the children were} there.
\end{example}

\subsection{Broad negatives: \textit{hardly, seldom}, etc.}
\noindent\fbox{5.82} Another way in which you can make a statement negative is by using a \textbf{broad negative}.

Broad negatives are adverbs like \textit{rarely} and \textit{seldom}, which are used to make a statement almost totally negative.
\begin{example}
    The estimated sales will \underline{hardly} cover the cost of making the film.
    
    We were \underline{scarcely} able to move.
    
    Kuwait lies \underline{barely} 30 miles from the Iranian coast.
\end{example}
Here is a list of the most common broad negatives:
\begin{example}
    barely, hardly, rarely, scarcely, seldom
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.83} The position of a broad negative within a clause is similar to that of \textit{never} (see paragraphs \textbf{5.66--5.68}).

%
\noindent\fbox{5.84} When you use a broad negative with a verb phrase that contains an auxiliary verb, you put it after the 1st word in the verb phrase and in front of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{could scarcely believe} my eyes.
    
    Religion \underline{was rarely discussed} in our house.
    
    His eyes \underline{had hardly closed}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.85} The verb is the present simple or past simple of \textit{be}, the broad negative usually comes in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{seldom bathed}.
    
    Marsha \underline{rarely felt} hungry.
    
    John \underline{hardly} ever \underline{spoke} to the Press.
\end{example}
It is also possible to put a broad negative after the verb, but this is less common.
\begin{example}
    Such moments \underline{happen rarely} in life.
    
    They \underline{met} so \underline{seldom}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.86} If the verb is the present simple or past simple of any verb except \textit{be}, the broad negative usually comes in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{seldom bathed}.
    
    Marsha \underline{rarely felt} hungry.
    
    John \underline{hardly} ever \underline{spoke} to the Press.
\end{example}
It is also possible to put a broad negative after the verb, but this is less common.
\begin{example}
    Such moments \underline{happen rarely} in life.
    
    They \underline{met} so \underline{seldom}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.87} In formal or literary English, a broad negative is sometimes placed at the beginning of a clause for emphasis.

If you are using a verb phrase with an auxiliary, the 1st word in the verb phrase is placed after the broad negative, followed by the subject and then the remainder of the verb phrase.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Seldom has society offered} so wide a range of leisure time activities.
    
    \underline{Hardly he he settled} into his seat when Adam charged in.
\end{example}
If there is no auxiliary, you put the present simple or past simple of \textit{do} after the broad negative, followed by the subject, followed by the base form of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Seldom did a week pass} without a request for assessment.
    
    \underline{Rarely do local matches live up to} expectations.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{barely} and \textit{scarcely} are not often used in this way.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.88} [i] If yo make a \textbf{question tag} out of a statement that contains a broad negative, the tag on the end of the statement is normally in the affirmative, as it is with other negatives.

Questions tags are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.16--5.20}.
\begin{example}
    She's hardly the right person for the job, \underline{is she}?
    
    You seldom see that sort of thing these days, \underline{do you}?
\end{example}
You can modify \textit{rarely} and \textit{seldom} by putting \textit{so, very, too}, or \textit{pretty} in front of them.

You can also modify \textit{rarely} by using \textit{only}.
\begin{example}
    It happens \underline{so rarely}.
    
    Women were \underline{very seldom} convicted.
    
    He \underline{too seldom} makes the 1st greeting.
    
    Most people go to church \underline{only rarely}.
\end{example}
If you want to say there is very little of something, you can use a broad negative with \textit{any} or with a word that begins with \textit{any-}.
\begin{example}
    The bonds show \underline{barely any} interest.
    
    \underline{Hardly anybody} came.
    
    In fact, it is \underline{seldom any} of these.
    
    With \underline{scarcely any} warning, the soldiers charged.
    
    Sometimes 2 or 3 relatives are admitted, but \underline{rarely any} friends.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.89} Instead of using a broad negative, you can use \textit{almost} followed by a negative word e.g. \textit{no} or \textit{never}.

E.g., \textit{There was almost no food left} means the same as \textit{There was hardly any food left}.
\begin{example}
    They've \underline{almost no} money for anything.
    
    The cars thinned out to \underline{almost none}.
    
    They were very private people, with \underline{almost no} friends.
    
    Children \underline{almost never} began conversations.
\end{example}

\subsection{Emphasizing the negative aspect of a statement}
\noindent\fbox{5.90} You can add \textit{at all} to a negative statement in order to make it more emphatic.

You use \textit{at all} with any negative word, with \textit{without}, or with a \textbf{broad negative}.
\begin{example}
    She had \underline{no} writing ability \underline{at all}.
    
    `There's no need,' said Jimmie. `\underline{None at all}'.
    
    He did it \underline{without} any help \underline{at all}.
    
    He \underline{hardly} read anything \underline{at all}.
\end{example}
Broad negatives are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.82--5.88}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.91} You can put \textit{whatsoever} after \textit{none} and \textit{nothing} in order to emphasize the negative aspect of a statement.
\begin{example}
    `You don't think he has any chance of winning?' - `\underline{None whatsoever.}'
    
    There is absolutely no enjoyment in that, \underline{none whatsoever}.
    
    You'll find yourself thinking about \underline{nothing whatsoever}.
\end{example}
If \textit{no} is used as a determiner in a noun phrase, you can put \textit{whatsoever} after the noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    There is \underline{no} need \underline{whatsoever} to teach children how to behave.
    
    There was \underline{no} debate \underline{whatsoever}, not even in Parliament.
\end{example}
You can also use \textit{whatsoever} in negative statements that contain \textit{any} or a word that begins with \textit{any-}.
\begin{example}
    You are not entitled to \underline{any} aid \underline{whatsoever}.
    
    He was devoid of \underline{any} talent \underline{whatsoever}.
    
    I knew I wasn't learning \underline{anything whatsoever}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.92} You can put \textit{ever} after negative words in order to emphasize the negative aspect of a statement.
\begin{example}
    I can't say I \underline{ever} had much interest in fishing.
    
    Nobody \underline{ever} leaves the airport.
    
    I never \underline{ever} believed we would have such success.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.93} There are several expressions that are used to emphasize a negative statement that contains \textit{not}.

These include \textit{in the least, the least bit, in the slightest}, and \textit{a bit}.
\begin{example}
    I don't mind \underline{in the least}, I really don't.
    
    Neither of the managers was \underline{the least bit} repentant afterwards.
    
    I don't really envy you \underline{in the slightest}.
    
    They're not \underline{a bit} interested.
\end{example}
If \textit{in the least} and \textit{in the slightest} are used with verbs, they are placed either immediately after the verb or after the object, if there is one.
\begin{example}
    I wouldn't have objected \underline{in the least}.
    
    She did not worry Billy \underline{in the least}.
    
    The weather hadn't improved \underline{in the slightest}.
\end{example}
If \textit{in the least} is used with an adjective, it is put in front of it.

\textit{In the slightest} usually comes after an adjective.
\begin{example}
    I wasn't \underline{in the least surprised}.
    
    She wasn't \underline{worried in the slightest}.
\end{example}
\textit{The least bit} and \textit{a bit} are used only with adjectives and are placed in front of the adjective.
\begin{example}
    I'm not \underline{the least bit worried}.
    
    They're not \underline{a bit interested}.
\end{example}

\section{Using modals}
\noindent\fbox{5.94} Language is not always used just ot exchange information by making simple statements and asking questions.

Sometimes we want to make requests, offers, or suggestions, or to express our wishes or intentions.

We may want to be polite or tactful, or to indicate our feelings about what we are saying.

%
We can do all these things by using a set of verbs called \textbf{modals}.

Modals are always used with other verbs.

They are special kind of \textbf{auxiliary verb}.

%
Here is a list of the modals used in English:
\begin{example}
    can, could, may, might, must, ought to, shall, should, will, would
\end{example}
In some grammars, \textit{dare, need}, and \textit{used to} are also referred to as modals.

In this grammar, we call these words \textbf{semi-modals}.

They are dealt with separately in paragraphs \textbf{5.248--5.258}.

%
\textit{Ought} is sometimes regarded as a modal, rather than \textit{ought to}.

For a further note about this, see paragraph \textbf{5.101}.

%
Modals are sometimes called \textbf{modal verbs} or \textbf{modal auxiliaries}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.95} The main uses of modals are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.96--5.100}.

Special features of modals are described in paragraphs \textbf{5.101--5.108}.

%
Ways of referring to time when using modals are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.109--5.116}.

Ways of using modals to say whether something is possible are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.117--5.153}.

Ways of using modals when interacting with other people are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.154--5.222}.

%
Expressions that can be used instead of modals are described in paragraphs \textbf{5.223--5.247}.

\textbf{Semi-modals} are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.248--5.258}.

\subsection{The main uses of modals}
\noindent\fbox{5.96} Modals are mainly used when you want to show your attitude towards what you are saying, or when you are concerned about the effect of what you are saying on the person you are speaking or writing to.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.97} When you are giving information, you sometimes use modals to show how certain you are that what you are saying is true or correct.

%
E.g., if you say \textit{Mr Wilkins is the oldest person in the village}, you are giving a definite statement of fact.

If you say \textit{Mr Wilkins must be the oldest person in the village}, the modal \textit{must} shows that you think Mr Wilkins is the oldest person, because you cannot think of anyone in the village who is older than Mr Wilkins.

If you say \textit{Mr Wilkins might be the oldest person in the village}, the modal \textit{might} shows that you think it is possible that Mr Wilkins is the oldest person, because he is very old.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.98} You can use modals to show your attitude towards the things you intend to do, or intend not to do.

E.g., if you say \textit{I won't go without Simon}, you are expressing strong unwillingness to go without Simon.

If you say \textit{I can't go without Simon}, you are saying that there is a special reason for not going without him.

If you say \textit{I couldn't go without Simon}, you are saying that you are unwilling to go without Simon, because to do so would be wrong, or impossible because of the circumstances.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.99} When you use language, you are affecting and responding to a particular person or audience.

Modals are often used to produce a particular effect, and the modal you choose depends on several factors, e.g. the relationship you have with your listener, the formality or informality of the situation, and the importance of what you are saying

%
E.g., it would normally be rude to say to a stranger \textit{Open the door}, although you might say it in an emergency, or you might say it to a close friend or a child.

Normally, you would say to a stranger \textit{Will you open the door?, Would you open the door?}, or \textit{Could you open the door?}, depending on how polite you want to be.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.100} Modals have special uses in 3 kinds of sentence containing more than 1 clause:
\begin{itemize}
    \item they are used in \textbf{reported clauses}
    \begin{example}
        Wilson dropped a hint that he \underline{might} come.
        
        I felt that I \underline{would} like to wake her up.
    \end{example}
    For more information about reported clauses see Chap. 7.
    \item they are used in \textbf{conditional statements}
    \begin{example}
        If he had died when he was 50, he \underline{would} have died healthy.
        
        If only things had been different, she \underline{would} have been far happier with George.
    \end{example}
    For more informational about conditionals see paragraphs \textbf{8.25--8.42}.
    \item they are used in \textbf{purpose clauses}.
    \begin{example}
        He stole under the very noses of the store detectives in order that he \underline{might} be arrested and punished.
        
        He resigned so that he \underline{could} spend more time with his family.
    \end{example}
    For more information about purpose clauses see paragraphs \textbf{8.47--8.48}.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Special features of modals}
\noindent\fbox{5.101} Modals are followed by the \textbf{base form} of a verb.
\begin{example}
    I must \underline{leave} fairly soon.
    
    I think it will \underline{be} rather nice.
    
    They ought to \underline{give} you your money back.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{ought} is sometimes regarded as a modal, rather than \textit{ought to}.

\textit{Ought} is then said to be followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.102} Sometimes a modal is followed by the base form of 1 of the auxiliary verbs \textit{have} or \textit{be}, followed by a participle.

%
When a modal is followed by \textit{be} and an \textit{-ing} participle, this shows that you are talking about the present or the future.
\begin{example}
    People \underline{may be watching}.
    
    You \underline{ought to be doing} this.
    
    The play \underline{will be starting} soon.
\end{example}
When a modal is followed by \textit{have} and an \textit{-ed} participle, this shows that you are talking about the past.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{must have heard} of him.
    
    She \underline{may have gone} already.
    
    I \underline{ought to have sent} the money.
\end{example}
In passive structures, a modal is followed by \textit{be} or \textit{have been} and an \textit{-ed} participle.
\begin{example}
    The name of the winner \underline{will be announced}.
    
    They \underline{ought to be treated} fairly.
    
    Such charges \underline{may have been justified}.
\end{example}
A modal is never followed by the auxiliary verb \textit{do}, or by another modal.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.103} Modals do not inflect.

This means there is no \textit{-s} form in the 3rd person singular, and there are no \textit{-ing} or \textit{-ing} or \textit{-ed} forms.
\begin{example}
    There's nothing I \underline{can} do about it.
    
    I am sure he \underline{can} do it.
    
    I \underline{must} leave fairly soon.
\end{example}
\textit{Could} is sometimes thought to be the past tense of \textit{can}.

This is discussed in paragraphs \textbf{5.112--5.113}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.104} Negatives are formed by putting a \textbf{negative word} e.g. \textit{not} immediately after the modal.

\textit{Can not} is usually written as 1 word, \textit{cannot}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{must not} worry.
    
    I \underline{cannot} go back.
\end{example}
In the case of \textit{ought to}, you put the negative word after \textit{ought}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{ought not to} have done so.
\end{example}
[AE] In American English, the \textit{to} of \textit{ought to} is optional in negative statements.
\begin{example}
    News organizations \underline{ought not} treat them so poorly. (American)
\end{example}
After \textit{could, might, must, ought, should}, and \textit{would, not} is often shortened to \textit{-n't} and is added to the modal.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{mustn't} talk about Ron like this.
    
    Perhaps I \underline{oughtn't to} confess this.
    
    He \underline{oughtn't} turn away from those who have supported him. (American)
\end{example}
\textit{Shall not, will not}, and \textit{cannot} are shortened to \textit{shan't, won't}, and \textit{can't}.

\textit{May not} is not shortened at all.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shan't} get much work done tonight.
    
    He \underline{won't} be finished for at least another half an hour.
    
    I \underline{can't} go with you.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.105} Questions are formed by putting the modal in front of the subject.

In the case of \textit{ought to}, you put \textit{ought} in front of the subject and \textit{to} after it.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Could you} give me an example?
    
    \underline{Ought we to} tell someone about it?
    
    \underline{Might}n't \underline{it} be better to leave things as they are?
    
    Why \underline{could they} not leave her alone?
    
    \underline{Must we} explain everything we do it such detail?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.106} Modals are used in \textbf{question tags}.
\begin{example}
    They can't all be right, \underline{can they}?
    
    You won't tell anyone, \underline{will you}?
\end{example}
With a negative tag, the shortened form of the negative is used.
\begin{example}
    It would be handy, \underline{wouldn't it}?
    
    It'll give you time to think about it, \underline{won't it}?
\end{example}
Question tags are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.12--5.20}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.107} In spoken English, when \textit{will} and \textit{would} are used after a pronoun, they are often shortened to \textit{-'ll} and \textit{-'d} and added to the pronoun.
\begin{example}
    I hope you\underline{'ll} agree.
    
    She\underline{'ll} be all right.
    
    They\underline{'d} both call each other horrible names.
    
    If I went back on the train, it\underline{'d} be better.
\end{example}
\textit{Will} and \textit{would} cannot be shortened like this when they are used on their own, without a following verb.

E.g., you can say \textit{Paul said he would come, and I hope he will}, but you cannot say \textit{Paul said he would com, and I hope he'll}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.108} [i] You sometimes use a modal on its own, without a following verb.

You do this when you are repeating a modal.

E.g., if someone says \textit{I expect Margaret will come tonight}, you can say \textit{I hope she will}, meaning \textit{I hope she will come}.
\begin{example}
    `I \underline{must go}.' - `I suppose you \underline{must}.'
    
    `You \underline{should have become an archaeologist}.' - `You're dead right, I \underline{should}.'
    
    If you \underline{can't do it}, we'll find someone who \underline{can}.
\end{example}
You also omit the verb following a modal when this verb has just been used without a modal, or with a different modal.

E.g., if someone says \textit{George has failed his exam}, you can say \textit{I thought he would}, meaning \textit{I thought he would fail his exam}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{love} him and I always \underline{will}.
    
    They had come to believe that it not only \underline{must go on for ever} but that it \underline{should}.
\end{example}
However you cannot omit the verb \textit{be} after a modal when you have just used it without a modal.

E.g., if someone says \textit{Is he a teacher?}, you cannot say \textit{I think he might}.

You must say \textit{I think he might be}.
\begin{example}
    Weather forecasts \underline{are}n't very reliable and never \underline{will be}.
    
    The Board's methods \underline{are} not as stringent as they \underline{could be}.
    
    Relations between the 2 countries \underline{have not been} as smooth as they \underline{might have been}.
\end{example}
For more information on leaving out words in verb phrases see paragraphs \textbf{10.60--10.81}.

\subsection{Referring to time}
\noindent\fbox{5.109} Modals do not usually show whether you are talking about the past, the present, or the future.

Usually you indicate this in other ways, e.g. by putting an auxiliary verb and a participle after the modal.

Sometimes the general context makes it clear whether you are talking about a past, present, or future event or situation.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.110} \textit{Shall} and \textit{will} are exceptions to this.

%
\textit{Shall} always indicates that you are talking about a future event or situation.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall} do what you suggested.
    
    Eventually we \underline{shall} find a solution.
\end{example}
\textit{Will} usually shows that you are talking about a future event or situation.
\begin{example}
    The farmer \underline{will} feel more responsible towards his workers.
    
    He \underline{will} not return for many hours.
\end{example}
However, \textit{will} is sometimes used to talk about present situations.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{will} not feel much love for him at the moment.
\end{example}
This use of \textit{will} is described in paragraph \textbf{5.123}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.111} \textit{Could} and \textit{would} are sometimes described as past tense forms of \textit{can} and \textit{will}.

However, this is true in only a few minor ways.

These are explained in the following paragraphs.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.112} \textit{Could} can be regarded as the past tense of \textit{can} if you are simply talking about the ability of a person or thing to do something.

%
E.g., if you are talking about a living person, you can say \textit{He can speak Russian and Finnish}.

If you are talking about a dead person, you can say \textit{He could speak Russian and Finnish}.

%
For a fuller explanation of these uses see paragraphs \textbf{5.118--5.120}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.113} \textit{Could} and \textit{would} are sometimes used in place of \textit{can} and \textit{will} when you are reporting what someone has said.

%
E.g., if your friend has said \textit{I can come}, you might report this as \textit{He said that he could come}.

Similarly, if he has said, \textit{I will come}, you might report this as \textit{He said that he would come}.

%
For a full explanation of \textbf{reported speech} see Chap. 7.

%
