\noindent\fbox{5.114} \textit{Would} is used to talk about something that happened regularly in the past, but no longer happens.
\begin{example}
    The other children \underline{would} tease me and call me names.
    
    A man with a list \underline{would} come round and say you could go off duty.
\end{example}
When you use \textit{would} like this, you often add a time expression.
\begin{example}
    She would \underline{often} hear him grumblin.
    
    \underline{Once in a while} she'd give me some lilac to take home.
    
    \underline{Every day} I'd ring up home and ask if they'd changed their minds.
\end{example}
You can use \textit{used to} instead to \textit{would}.

\textit{Used to} is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.254--5.258}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.115} \textit{Would} is also used in stories to talk about the thoughts that someone is having about the future.

E.g., if a character in a story is thinking that she will see a girl called Jane the next day, the author might simply say \textit{She would see Jane the next day}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{would} recognize it when he heard it again.
    
    They \underline{would} reach the castle some time.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.116} When \textit{would} is used with \textit{not} to talk about something that happened in the past, it has a special meaning.

It is used to say that someone refused to do something.
\begin{example}
    They just \underline{would not} believe what we told them.
    
    After all this, I \underline{wouldn't} come back to the farm.
\end{example}
The use of modals to express a refusal is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.196--5.201}.

\subsection{Talking about possibility}
\noindent\fbox{5.117} The following 4 sections explain the different ways in which modals are used to talk about the possibility of something happening or being done.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.118--5.121} explain how \textit{can} and \textit{could} are used to talk about the ability of a person or thing to do something.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.122--5.141} explain how modals are used to express degrees of certainty about past, present, and future situations and events.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.142--5.145} explain how modals are used to say that something is permissible.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.146--5.153} explain how modals are used to say that something is forbidden or unacceptable.

\subsection{Talking about ability}
\noindent\fbox{5.118} \textit{Can} is used to say that someone has a particular skill or ability.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{can} all read and write.
    
    Some people \underline{can} ski better than others.
    
    He \underline{cannot} dance.
    
    $\ldots$ the girl who \underline{can't} act.
\end{example}
\textit{Could} is used to say that someone had a skill or ability in the past.
\begin{example}
    When I arrived, I \underline{could} speak only a little English.
    
    I \underline{could} barely walk.
    
    He \underline{could} kick goals from anywhere.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.119} \textit{Can} is also used to say that someone is aware of something through 1 of their senses.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{can} see you.
    
    I \underline{can} smell it. \underline{Can't} you?
\end{example}
\textit{Could} is used to say that someone was aware of something through 1 of their senses on a particular occasion in the past.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{could} see a few faint stars in a clear patch or sky.
    
    I \underline{could} feel my heart bumping.
    
    Police said they \underline{could} smell alcohol on his breath.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.120} \textit{Can} and \textit{could} are also used to express facts that are generally the case, particularly when they relate to something or someone being capable of having a particular effect, or of behaving in a particular way.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{can} be very unpleasant.
    
    Art \underline{can} be used to communicate.
    
    Throwing parties \underline{can} be hard work.
    
    He \underline{could} be very stiff, could Haggerty.
    
    He \underline{could} really frighten me, and yet at the same time he \underline{could} be the most gentle and courteous of men.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.121} [!] You cannot use \textit{can} or \textit{could} to say that someone or something will have a particular ability in the future.

Instead you use \textit{be able to} or \textit{be possible to}.

%
\textit{Be able to} and \textit{be possible to} can also be used to talk about someone's ability to do something in the present or the past.

%
\textit{Be able to} and \textit{be possible to} are dealt with in paragraphs \textbf{5.224--5.231}.

\subsection{Talking about likelihood}
\noindent\fbox{5.122} The following paragraphs explain how modals are used to express different degrees of certainty about past, present, and future situations and events.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.123--5.129} explain the main ways in which modals are used to express degrees of certainty.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.130--5.134} explain special uses of modals when talking about possible future situations.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.135--5.141} explain special uses of modals when talking about possible situations in the past.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.123} You use \textit{will} when are assuming that something is the case, and you do not think there is any reason to doubt it.
\begin{example}
    Those of you who are familiar with the game \underline{will} know this.
    
    He \underline{will} be a little out of touch, although he's a rapid learner.
    
    She \underline{will} have forgotten all about it by now.
\end{example}
Similarly, you use \textit{will not} or \textit{won't} when you are assuming that something is not the case.
\begin{example}
    The audience \underline{will not} be aware of such exact details.
    
    You \underline{won't} know Gordon. He's out new doctor.
\end{example}
After \textit{you}, you can use \textit{would} instead of \textit{will}, if you want to be more polite.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{would} agree that the United States should be involved in assisting these countries.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.124} You also use \textit{would} to say that something is certain to happen in particular circumstances.
\begin{example}
    Even an illiterate person \underline{would} understand that.
    
    Few people \underline{would} agree with this as a general principle.
    
    A picnic \underline{wouldn't} be any fun without you.
\end{example}
After \textit{I}, you can use \textit{should} instead of \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    The very 1st thing I \underline{should} do would be to teach you how to cook.
    
    I \underline{should} be very unhappy on the continent.
\end{example}
[AE] This is unusual in American English, which normally uses \textit{would} after \textit{I}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.125} You use \textit{must} to show that you believe something is the case, because of particular facts or circumstances.
\begin{example}
    Oh, you \underline{must} be Sylvia's husband.
    
    Fashion \underline{must} account for a small percentage of sales.
    
    This article \underline{must} have been written by a woman.
\end{example}
When you are indicating that something is not the case, you use \textit{cannot}.

You do not use \textit{must not}. (See paragraph \textbf{5.128}.)
\begin{example}
    The 2 conflicting messages \underline{cannot} possibly both be true.
    
    You \underline{can't} have forgotten me.
    
    He \underline{can't} have said that. He just \underline{can't}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.126} You use \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may} to say that there is a possibility of something happening or being the case.

\textit{May} is slightly more formal than \textit{could} or \textit{might}; otherwise there is very little difference in meaning between these modals.
\begin{example}
    Don't eat it. It \underline{could} be poisonous.
    
    His route from the bus stop \underline{might} be the same as yours.
    
    In rare cases the jaw \underline{may} be broken during extraction.
\end{example}
If you put \textit{well} after \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may}, you are saying that it is fairly likely that something is the case.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{could well} be that the economic situation is getting better.
    
    His predictions \underline{could well} have come true.
    
    You \underline{might well} be right.
    
    I think that \underline{may well} have been the intention.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.127} You use \textit{might not} or \textit{may not} to say that it is possible that something is not the case.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{might not} be in England at all.
    
    That \underline{mightn't} be true.
    
    That \underline{may not} sound very imposing.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.128} You use \textit{could not} or \textit{cannot} to say that it is impossible that something is the case.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ knowledge which \underline{could not} have been gained in any other way.
    
    It \underline{couldn't} possibly the poison.
    
    He \underline{cannot} know everything that is going on.
    
    You \underline{can't} talk to the dead.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.129} \textit{Could} is sometimes used in negative constructions with the comparative form of an adjective.

You use \textit{could} like this to say that it is not possible for someone or something to have more of a particular quality.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{couldn't} be happier.
    
    You \underline{couldn't} be more wrong.
    
    The setting \underline{couldn't} have been lovelier.
    
    He \underline{could hardly} have felt more ashamed of himself.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.130} The following paragraphs explain how modals are used when you are talking about possible future situations.

The uses of \textit{must, cannot, could, might}, and \textit{may} are similar to their uses when you are talking about possible situations in the present.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.131} You use \textit{will} to say that something is certain to happen or be the case in the future.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{will} see everything.
    
    The price of food \underline{will} go up.
    
    The service \underline{will} have been running for a year in May.
\end{example}
\textit{Be going to} can also be used to say that something is certain to happen in the future.

This use of \textit{be going to} is dealt with in paragraph \textbf{5.233}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.132} \textit{Shall} is also used to say that something is certain to happen.

You usually use \textit{shall} when you are talking about events and situations over which you have some control.

E.g., you can use \textit{shall} when you are making a resolution or a promise.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall} be leaving as soon as I am ready.
    
    Very well, my dear. You \underline{shall} have the coat.
    
    Of course he \underline{shall} have water.
    
    `You'll make a lot of money.' - `I \underline{shall} 1 day.'
\end{example}
[AE] This usage is considered very formal in American English and is not often used.

%
You use \textit{must} to say that something is certain to happen because of particular facts or circumstances.
\begin{example}
    This research \underline{must} eventually lead to computer decision-making.
\end{example}
You use \textit{cannot} to say that something is certain not to happen because of particular facts or circumstances.

You do not use \textit{must not}.
\begin{example}
    A team \underline{cannot} hope to win consistently without a good coach.
    
    The bad weather \underline{can't} last.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.133} You use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} to say that you expect something to happen.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{should} be back any time now.
    
    This course \underline{should} be quite interesting for you.
    
    The Court of Appeal \underline{ought to} be able to help you.
    
    It \underline{ought to} get better as it goes along.
\end{example}
\textit{Should} and \textit{ought to} are also used when you are talking about the importance of doing something.

This use is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.215}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.134} You use \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may} to say that it is possible that a particular thing will happen.
\begin{example}
    England's next fixture in Salzburg \underline{could} be the decisive match.
    
    The river \underline{could} easily overflow.
    
    They \underline{might} be able to remember what he said.
    
    Clerical work \underline{may} be available for 2 students who want to learn about publishing.
\end{example}
If you put \textit{well} after \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may}, you are indicating that it is fairly likely that something will happen or be the case.
\begin{example}
    When it is finished it \underline{may well} be the largest cathedral in the world.
    
    We \underline{might well} get injured.
\end{example}
If you put \textit{possibly} or \textit{conceivably} after \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may}, you are indicating that is possible, but fairly unlikely, that something will happen or be the case.
\begin{example}
    These conditions \underline{could possibly} be accepted.
    
    Rates \underline{could conceivably} rise by as much as a whole percentage point.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.135} The following paragraphs explain how you use modals when you are talking about possible situations in the past.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.136} You use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} with \textit{have to} say that you expect something to have happened already.
\begin{example}
    Dear Mom, you \underline{should have} heard by now that I'm O.K.
\end{example}
You also use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} with \textit{have} to say that something was expected to happen, although it has not in fact happened.
\begin{example}
    Muskie \underline{should have} won by a huge margin.
    
    She \underline{ought to have} been home by now.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.137} You use \textit{would} with \textit{have} to talk about actions and events that were possible in the past, although they did not in fact happen.
\begin{example}
    Denial \underline{would have} been useless.
    
    I \underline{would have} said yes, but Julie persuaded us to stay at home.
    
    You \underline{wouldn't have} pushed him, would you?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.138} You use \textit{could} or \textit{might} with \textit{have} to say that there was a possibility of something happening in the past, although it did not in fact happen.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{could have} been awful.
    
    I \underline{could} easily \underline{have} spent the whole year on it.
    
    You \underline{could have} got a job last year.
    
    A lot of men died who \underline{might have} been saved.
    
    You \underline{might have} found it very difficult.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.139} You also use \textit{could, might}, or \textit{may} with \textit{have} to say that it is possible that something was the case, but you do not know whether it was the case or not.
\begin{example}
    It is just possible that such a small creature \underline{could have} preyed on dinosaur eggs.
    
    They \underline{might have} moved house by now.
    
    I \underline{may have} seemed to be overreacting.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.140} You use \textit{might not} or \textit{may not} with \textit{have} to say that it is possible that something did not happen or was not the case.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{might not have} considered me as their friend.
    
    My father \underline{mightn't have} been to blame.
    
    The parents \underline{may not have} been ready for this news.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.141} You use \textit{could} with a negative and \textit{have} to say that it is impossible that something happened or was the case.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{couldn't have} been wrong.
    
    The money was not, and \underline{never could have} been, the property of the organization.
\end{example}

\subsection{Talking about permission}
\noindent\fbox{5.142} \textit{Can} is used to say that someone is allowed to do something.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{can} drive a van up to 3-ton capacity using an ordinary driving license.
\end{example}
If you are giving permission for something, you use \textit{can}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{can} borrow that pen if you want to.
    
    You \underline{can} go off duty now.
    
    She \underline{can} go with you.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.143} In more formal situations, \textit{may} is used to give permission.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{may} speak now.
    
    They \underline{may} do exactly as they like.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.144} \textit{Could} is used to say that someone was allowed to do something in the past.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{could} go to any part of the island we wanted to.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.145} [!] You cannot use \textit{can} or \textit{could} to say that someone will be allowed to do something in the future.

Instead you use \textit{be able to}.

%
\textit{Be able to} is dealt with in paragraphs \textbf{5.224--5.231}.

\subsection{Saying that something is unacceptable}
\noindent\fbox{5.146} Modals are often used in negative structures to say that an action is forbidden or unacceptable.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.147} \textit{Cannot} is used to say that something is forbidden, e.g. because of a rule or law.
\begin{example}
    Children \underline{cannot} bathe except in the presence of 2 lifesavers.
    
    We're awfully sorry we \underline{can't} let you stay here.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.148} \textit{May not} is used in a similar way to \textit{cannot}, but is more formal.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{may not} make amendments to your application once we have received it.
    
    This material \underline{may not} be published, broadcast, or redistributed in any manner.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.149} \textit{Will not} is used to tell someone very firmly that they are not allowed to do a particular thing.

Usually, the speaker has the power to prevent the hearer from doing this thing.
\begin{example}
    `I'll just go upstairs.' - `You \underline{will not}.'
    
    Until we have cured you, you \underline{won't} be leaving here.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.150} \textit{Shall not} is used to say formally that a particular thing is not allowed.

\textit{Shall not} is often used in written rules, laws, and agreements.
\begin{example}
    Persons under 18 \underline{shall not} be employed in nightwork.
    
    Equality of rights under the law \underline{shall not} be denied or abridged by the United States or by any State.
\end{example}
\textit{Shan't} is used in a similar way to \textit{will not} and \textit{won't}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{shan't} leave without my permission.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.151} You can also say that something is not allowed by using an \textbf{imperative} sentence.

Imperative sentences are explained in paragraph \textbf{5.4} and in paragraphs \textbf{5.37--5.41}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.152} \textit{Should not} is used to tell someone that an action is unacceptable or undesirable.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{should not} take her help for granted.
    
    You \underline{shouldn't} do that.
    
    You \underline{shouldn't} be so unfriendly.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.153} \textit{Must not} is used to say much more firmly that something is unacceptable or undesirable.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{must not} accept it.
    
    You \underline{mustn't} do that.
    
    You \underline{mustn't} breathe a word of this to anyone.
\end{example}

\subsection{Interacting with other people}
\noindent\fbox{5.154} You often say things in order to get someone to behave in a particular way.

E.g., you may wants someone to take a particular action, to accept an offer, or to give their permission for something to be done.

%
In these situations, modals are often used.

The modal you choose depends on several factors.

The main ones are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item the formality or informality of the situation
    \item the relationship between yourself and the person you are speaking to
    \item the degree of politeness you want to show.
\end{itemize}
In particular situations, other factors can be important.

E.g., if you are making an offer or suggestion, the modal you choose may depend on how persuasive you want to be.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.155} The following sections explain how to use modals in different situations.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.156--5.172} explain how to give instructions and make requests.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.173--5.181} explain how to make an offer or an invitation.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.182--5.189} explain how to make suggestions.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.190--5.195} explain how to state an intention.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.196--5.201} explain how to express unwillingness or a refusal to do something.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.202--5.210} explain how to express a wish.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.211--5.215} explain how to indicate the importance of doing something.

%
Paragraphs \textbf{5.216--5.222} explain various ways of introducing what you are going to say.

\subsection{Giving instructions \& making requests}
\noindent\fbox{5.156} When you give an instruction or make a request, you usually use a modal in an interrogative sentence.

%
You use \textit{will, would, can}, or \textit{could} with \textit{you} to tell someone to do something, or to task someone to do something.

%
You use \textit{can, could, may}, or \textit{might} with \textit{I} or \textit{we} or with other personal pronouns or noun phrases to ask someone's permission to do something.

%
Instructions and requests are always made more polite by adding \textit{please}.

\textit{Please} and other markers of politeness are explained in paragraph \textbf{5.172}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.157} \textit{Will, would}, and \textit{could} are used with \textit{you} in 2 ways:
\begin{itemize}
    \item you use them to give an instruction or an order
    \item you use them to ask for help or assistance.
\end{itemize}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.158} \textit{Will} is used to give an instruction or order in a fairly direct way.

It is slightly less forceful than using the imperative.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Will} you pick those toys up please?
    
    \underline{Will} you pack up and leave at once, please.
\end{example}
\textit{Will} is used to ask for help in fairly informal situations.
\begin{example}
    Mummy, \underline{will} you help me?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.159} When \textit{would} is used to give an instruction or order, it is more polite than \textit{will}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Would} you tell her that Adrian phoned?
    
    \underline{Would} you ask them to leave, please?
\end{example}
When \textit{would} is used to ask for help, it is less informal and more polite than \textit{will}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Would} you do me a favor?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.160} When \textit{could} is used to give an instruction or order, it is more polite than \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Could} you follow me please?
    
    \underline{Could} you just switch the projector on behind you?
\end{example}
When \textit{could} is used to ask for help, it is more polite than \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Could} you show me how to do this?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.161} \textit{Can} can be used with \textit{you} to ask for help.

You usually use \textit{can} when you are not sure whether someone will be able to help you or not.
\begin{example}
    Oh hello. \underline{Can} you help me? I've been trying this number for 10 minutes and I can't get through.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.162} \textit{Can, could, may}, and \textit{might} are used with \textit{I} or \textit{we} when you are asking for something, or are asking permission to do something.

%
These modals can also be used with \textit{he, she}, or \textit{they}, or with other noun phrases, when you are asking for something on behalf of someone else.

E.g., you can say \textit{Can she borrow your car?} or \textit{Could my mother use your telephone?}

%
\noindent\fbox{5.163} \textit{Can} is used to make a request in a simple and direct way.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Can} I ask a question?
    
    `\underline{Can} I change this?' I asked the box office lady, offering her my ticket.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.164} \textit{Could} is more polite than \textit{can}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Could} I have a bottle of lemonade, please?
    
    \underline{Could} I just interrupt a minute?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.165} You can make a request sound more persuasive by using \textit{can't} or \textit{couldn't} instead of \textit{can} or \textit{could}.

E.g., you can say \textit{Can't I come with you?} instead of \textit{Can I come with you?}
\begin{example}
    \underline{Can't} we have some music?
    
    \underline{Couldn't} we stay here?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.166} \textit{May} and \textit{might} are more formal than \textit{can} and \textit{could}.

In the past, people were taught that, when asking for something, it was correct to say \textit{may} rather than \textit{can}, and \textit{might} rather than \textit{could}.

However \textit{can} and \textit{could} are now generally used.

Requests beginning with \textit{might} are unusual, and are considered by most people to be old-fashioned.
\begin{example}
    \underline{May} I have a cigarette?
    
    \underline{May} we have something to eat?
    
    \underline{May} I ask what your name is?
    
    \underline{Might} I inquire if you are the owner?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.167} \textit{Would like} can be used with \textit{I} or \textit{we} in a declarative sentence to give an instruction or order.

It is followed by \textit{you} and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    OK, everyone, I \underline{would like} you to get into a circle.
\end{example}
\textit{Want} can be used in a similar way to \textit{would like}.

This use of \textit{want} is dealt with in paragraph \textbf{5.236}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.168} An instruction or order can also be given using \textit{will} in a declarative sentence.

This form is used when the speaker is angry or impatient.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{will} go and get 1 of your parents immediately.
    
    You \underline{will} give me those now.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.169} \textit{Shall} is sometimes used in a declarative sentence to give an instruction or order.

This is a very formal use.
\begin{example}
    There \underline{shall} be no further communication between you.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.170} The \textbf{imperative} can also be used to give an instruction or order.

This use is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.37}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.171} You can use \textit{would like} or (in British English only) \textit{should like} in a declarative sentence to make a request.

\textit{Would like} and \textit{should like} are followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause or a noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would like} to ask you 1 question.
    
    I\underline{'d like} to have a little talk with you.
    
    I \underline{should like} a list of your customers over the past year.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.172} All the ways of giving instructions or making requests described above can be made more polite by using \textit{please}.
\begin{example}
    Can I speak to Nicola, \underline{please}?
    
    \underline{Please} may I have the key?
\end{example}
You can also make a request more polite by adding the name of the person you are addressing at the beginning or end of your question.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Martin}, could you make us a drink?
    
    Can I talk to you, \underline{Howard}?
\end{example}
Another way of making a request more polite is to add an adverb e.g. \textit{perhaps} or \textit{possibly} after the subject of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Could I \underline{perhaps} bring a friend with me?
    
    May I \underline{possibly} have a word will you?
\end{example}
In spoken English, you can make a request more polite by adding \textit{just} after the subject of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Could you \underline{just} come into my office for a minute?
\end{example}

\subsection{Making an offer or an invitation}
\noindent\fbox{5.173} Modals are often used to make an offer or an invitation.

%
You use \textit{will} or \textit{would} with \textit{you} to ask someone to accept something, or to make an invitation.

%
You use \textit{can, may, shall}, or \textit{should} with \textit{I} or \textit{we} when you are offering to help someone.

%
Some of these structures are similar to those described in the previous section.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.174} \textit{Will} is used with \textit{you} in an interrogative sentence to offer something to someone, or to make an invitation in a fairly informal way.

You use \textit{will} when you know the person you are talking to quite well.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Will} you have a whisky, Doctor?
    
    \underline{Will} you stay for lunch?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.175} A more polite way of offering something or making an invitation is to use \textit{would} with a verb that means \textit{to like}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Would} you like a drink?
    
    \underline{Would} you care to stay with us?
\end{example}
If you want to sound more persuasive without seeming impolite or insistent, you can use \textit{wouldn't} instead of \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Wouldn't} you like to come with me?
    
    \underline{Wouldn't} you care for some more coffee?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.176} When you are offering to do something for someone, you usually use \textit{can} followed by \textit{I} or \textit{we}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Can} I help you with the dishes?
    
    \underline{Can} we give you a lift into town?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.177} \textit{May} is also used when you are offering to do something for someone.

It is less common than \textit{can}, and is rather formal and old-fashioned.
\begin{example}
    \underline{May} I help you?
    
    \underline{May} I take your coat?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.178} You can also use \textit{shall} or \textit{should} when you are offering to do something.

%
If you are offering to do something that you can do immediately, or you are fairly confident that your offer will be accepted, you use \textit{shall}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Shall} I shut the door?
    
    \underline{Shall} I spell that for you?
\end{example}
If you talking about a less likely or less immediate situation, or if you are uncertain whether your offer will be accepted, you use \textit{should}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Should} I give her a ring?
    
    \underline{Should} I put all these meetings on my calendar?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.179} If you want to emphasize your ability to help, you can make an offer using \textit{can} in a declarative sentence.
\begin{example}
    I have a car. I \underline{can} drop you off on my way home.
    
    I \underline{can} pop in at the shop tomorrow.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.180} If you want to make an invitation in a very persuasive way, you can use a declarative sentence beginning with \textit{you} and \textit{must} or \textit{have to}.

\textit{Have to} is more common in American English.
\begin{example}
    \emph{[AE]} You \underline{must} join us for drinks this evening.
    
    You \underline{have to} come and visit me.
\end{example}
You only use \textit{must} and \textit{have to} like this with people who you know well.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.181} Other ways of making an offer or invitation are dealt with in paragraph \textbf{5.44}.

\subsection{Making suggestions}
\noindent\fbox{5.182} Suggestions can be made by using a modal in a declarative or interrogative sentence.

The subject of the sentence is usually \textit{we} or \textit{you}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.183} You can make a suggestion by using \textit{could} in a declarative sentence or \textit{couldn't} in an interrogative sentence.
\begin{example}
    If the business doesn't work out we \underline{could} sell it.
    
    You \underline{could} have a nursery there.
    
    \underline{Couldn't} you just build more factories?
    
    \underline{Couldn't} some international agreement be concluded to ban these weapons?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.184} If you are making a suggestion and you want to indicate that you feel strongly that it is a good idea, you can use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{should} ask Norry about this.
    
    I think you \underline{should} get in touch with your solicitor.
    
    We \underline{ought to} celebrate. Let's get a bottle of champagne.
    
    I think you \underline{ought to} try a different approach.
\end{example}
A more polite way of making a suggestion that you feel strongly about is to use \textit{shouldn't} or \textit{oughtn't to} in an interrogative sentence.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Shouldn't} we at least give her a chance?
    
    \underline{Oughtn't} we \underline{to} phone the police?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.185} If you are suggesting an action and you are trying to persuade someone that it should be done, you use \textit{must}.

You only use \textit{must} like this when you are talking to someone you know well.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{must} say hello to your daughter.
    
    We \underline{must} go to the place, perhaps have a weekend there.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.186} If you want to make a suggestion in a very polite way, you can use \textit{might} with \textit{you} in a declarative sentence.

\textit{Might} is followed by a verb meaning \textit{to like} or \textit{to want}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{might} want to comment on his latest proposal.
    
    I thought perhaps you \underline{might} like to come along with me.
\end{example}
You can also make a polite suggestion by using a sentence beginning with \textit{It might be}, followed by a noun phrase or adjective and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    I think it \underline{might} be a good idea to stop now.
    
    It \underline{might} be better to wait a while.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.187} You can also make suggestions using the expressions \textit{might as well} and \textit{may as well}.

%
You use \textit{might as well} when what you are suggesting seems to be the only sensible course of action, although you are not enthusiastic about it.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{might as well} take the car.
    
    We \underline{might as well} call the whole thing off.
\end{example}
You use \textit{may as well} to show that it is not important to you whether your suggestion is accepted or not.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{may as well} open them all.
    
    We \underline{may as well} give her a copy.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.188} You can make a suggestion about what you and someone else could do by using an interrogative sentence beginning with \textit{shall} and \textit{we}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Shall} we go and see a film?
    
    \underline{Shall} we go on to question number 6?
    
    \underline{Shall} we talk about something different now?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.189} Other ways of making suggestions are described in paragraphs \textbf{5.48}.

\subsection{Stating an intention}
\noindent\fbox{5.190} Intentions are usually stated by using \textit{will, shall}, or \textit{must} in a declarative sentence.

The subject is \textit{I} or \textit{we}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.191} The usual way to state an intention is to use \textit{I} or \textit{we} with \textit{will}.

The shortened forms \textit{I'll} and \textit{we'll} are very common.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{will} call you when I am ready.
    
    We \underline{will} stay here.
    
    \underline{I'll} write again some time.
    
    \underline{We'll} discuss that later.
\end{example}
You state your intention not to do something using \textit{will not} or \textit{won't}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{will not} follow her.
    
    I \underline{won't} keep you any longer.
    
    We \underline{won't} let them through the gate.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.192} You can show that you are very determined to do something by using the full form \textit{I will} or \textit{we will} and stressing \textit{will}.

%
You can show that you are very determined not to do something either by using \textit{I won't} or \textit{we won't} and stressing \textit{won't}, or by using \textit{I will not} or \textit{we will not} and stressing \textit{not}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.193} Another way of stating an intention is to use \textit{I} or \textit{we} with \textit{shall}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall} be leaving soon.
    
    I \underline{shall} make some enquiries and call you back.
    
    We \underline{shall} continue to monitor his progress.
\end{example}
[AE] This use is slightly old-fashioned, rather formal, and uncommon in American English.

%
You can show that you are very determined not to do something by using \textit{shall not} or \textit{shan't}.

This is more emphatic than using \textit{will not} or \textit{won't}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall not} disclose his name.
    
    I \underline{shan't} go back there.
\end{example}
[AE] The form \textit{shan't} is not used in American English.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.194} If you want to show that it is important that you do something, you can use \textit{I must}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{must} leave fairly soon.
    
    I \underline{must} ask her about that.
    
    I \underline{must} call my mum - it's her birthday today.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.195} Ways of stating an intention without using a modal are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.237--5.240}.

\subsection{Expressing unwillingness or refusal}
\noindent\fbox{5.196} A refusal can be expressed by using a modal in a negative declarative sentence.

The subject is usually \textit{I} or \textit{we}, but other personal pronouns or noun phrases can be used.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.197} If you want to say firmly that you refuse to do something, you use \textit{will not} or \textit{won't}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{will not} hear a word said against the National Health Service.
    
    I \underline{won't} let this happen.
\end{example}
You can just say \textit{I won't}.
\begin{example}
    `Tell me your secret.' - `\underline{I won't}. It wouldn't be a secret if I told you.'
    
    It isn't that I \underline{won't}. I can't.
\end{example}
You can use \textit{won't} to say that someone else is refusing to do something.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{won't} give her the money.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.198} If you want to say that you refused to do something in the past, you use \textit{would not} or \textit{wouldn't}.
\begin{example}
    He thought I was a freak because I \underline{wouldn't} carry a weapon.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.199} If you want to show that you have strong feelings that prevent you from doing something, you use \textit{cannot} or \textit{can't}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{cannot} leave everything for him.
    
    I \underline{can't} give you up.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.200} \textit{Couldn't} is used in 2 ways to express unwillingness to do something.

%
You use it to show that you are unwilling to do something because you are afraid, embarrassed, or disgusted.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{couldn't} possibly go out now.
    
    I \underline{couldn't} let him touch me.
\end{example}
You use it to indicate that you are unwilling to do something because you think it would be unfair or morally wrong.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{couldn't} leave Hilary to cope on her own.
    
    I \underline{couldn't} take your last chocolate.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.201} Other ways of expressing refusal and unwillingness are described in paragraphs \textbf{5.241--5.243}.

\subsection{Expressing a wish}
\noindent\fbox{5.202} Wishes can be expressed by using a modal in a declarative sentence.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.203} You can say what someone wants by using \textit{would} followed by a verb meaning \textit{to like}.

After the verb meaning \textit{to like} you put a \textit{to}-infinitive clause or a noun phrase.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would} like to know the date.
    
    I \underline{would} prefer to say nothing about this problem.
    
    We\underline{'d} like to keep you here.
    
    Oh, I hope it will be twins. I\underline{'d} love twins.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.204} You can say what someone does not want by using \textit{would not}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would not} like to see it.
    
    We \underline{wouldn't} like to lose you.
\end{example}
Normally, when you are using \textit{would} with \textit{like} to say what someone does not want, you put \textit{not} after \textit{would}.

If you put \textit{not} after \textit{like}, you change the meaning slightly.

%
E.g., if you say \textit{I would not like to be a student}, you mean you are not a student and do not want to be one.

But if you say \textit{I would like not to be a student}, you mean you are a student and do not want to be one.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{would like not} to have to go through all that.
\end{example}
You can also say what someone does not want by using \textit{would} with \textit{hate}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would} hate to move to another house now.
    
    Personally, I \underline{would} hate to be dragged into this dispute.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.205} You can also say what someone wants or does not want by using \textit{should}.

\textit{Should} is less common than \textit{would}, and is slightly more formal.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{should} like to live in the country.
    
    I \underline{should} hate to see them disappear.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.206} You can say that someone prefers 1 situation to another by using \textit{would rather} or \textit{would sooner}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{would rather} have left it.
    
    She\underline{'d rather} be left alone.
    
    I\underline{'d sooner} walk than do any of these things.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.207} If you want to say that someone wanted something to happen, although it did not happen, you use \textit{would have} and an \textit{-ed} participle.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would have} liked to hear more from the patient.
    
    She \underline{would have} liked to remain just where she was.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.208} [i] Another way of saying that you want something is to use \textit{wouldn't} with a verb or expression that is normally used to refuse something, e.g. \textit{mind} or \textit{object to}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{wouldn't mind} being a manager of a store.
    
    `Drink, Ted?' - `I \underline{wouldn't say no}, Bryan.'
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.209} In very old-fashioned English, \textit{would} is used without a subject to express a wish that a situation might be different, or to express regret that something did not happen in the past.

\textit{Would} is followed by a \textit{that}-clause.
\begin{example}
    `Are they better off now than they were 2 years ago?' - `\underline{Would that} they were.'
    
    \underline{Would that} the developments had been so easy.
\end{example}
When \textit{I, he, she}, or \textit{it} is the subject of the \textit{that}-clause, the verb is usually \textit{were}, not \textit{was}.
\begin{example}
    Would that you \underline{were} here tonight.
    
    2 years ago we were told that they would be much better off by now. Would that they \underline{were}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.210} In very formal English, \textit{may} is used to express a hope or wish.
\begin{example}
    Long \underline{may} they continue to do it.
    
    \underline{May} he justify our hopes and rise to the top.
\end{example}

\subsection{Indicating importance}
\noindent\fbox{5.211} Modals can be used in declarative sentences to say that it is important that something is done.

Different modals indicate different degrees of importance.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.212} \textit{Must} is used in 3 common ways to talk about the importance of doing something.

%
You use \textit{must} with \textit{you} or \textit{we} to urge someone to do something, because you feel it is important.

\textit{Must not} is used to urge someone not to do something.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{must} come at once.
    
    We \underline{must} accept the truth about ourselves.
    
    You \underline{must not} worry.
    
    You \underline{mustn't} let her suffer for it.
\end{example}
\textit{Have to, have got to}, and \textit{need to} can be used instead of \textit{must} to talk about the importance of doing something.

This is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.244--5.245}.

%
You use \textit{must} to say that something is required by a rule or law.
\begin{example}
    People who qualify \underline{must} apply within 6 months.
    
    European Community standards \underline{must} be met.
\end{example}
You use \textit{must} to say that it is necessary that something happens or is done, in order that something else can happen.
\begin{example}
    Meadows \underline{must} have rain.
    
    To travel properly you \underline{must} have a valid ticket.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.213} If you want to say that an action will be necessary in the future, you use \textit{will have to} or \textit{will need to}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{will have to} pay for the repairs.
    
    Mr Smith \underline{will have to} make the funeral arrangements.
    
    You \underline{will need to} cover it with some kind of sheeting.
    
    Electric clocks \underline{will need to} be reset.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.214} \textit{Shall have to} is sometimes used instead of \textit{will have to} after \textit{I} or \textit{we}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{shall have to} speak about that to Peter.
    
    We \underline{shall have to} assume that you are right.
\end{example}
[AE] This is a slightly formal use, and is rarely used in American English.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.215} \textit{Should} and \textit{ought to} are used in 3 different ways when you are talking about the importance of doing something.

%
You use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} when you are trying to help someone by advising them to do something.
\begin{example}
    Carbon steel knives \underline{should} be wiped clean after use.
    
    You \underline{should} claim your pension 3-4 months before you retire.
    
    You \underline{ought to} try a different approach.
\end{example}
You use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} when you are saying that something is the right or correct thing to do.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{should} send her a postcard.
    
    The judges \underline{should} offer constructive criticism.
    
    We \underline{ought to} stay with him.
    
    You \underline{ought} not \underline{to} do that.
\end{example}
You use \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} with \textit{have} and an \textit{-ed} participle to say that something was desirable in the past, although it did not in fact happen.
\begin{example}
    1 sailor \underline{should have} been asleep and one on watch.
    
    We \underline{ought to have} stayed in tonight.
    
    A more junior member of staff \underline{ought to have} done the work.
\end{example}
You also use \textit{should} and \textit{ought to} to say that you expect something to happen.

%
This use is explained in paragraph \textbf{5.133}.

\subsection{Introducing what you are going to say}
\noindent\fbox{5.216} Sometimes you introduce what you are going to say by using a modal followed by a verb e.g. \textit{say} or \textit{ask} that refers to the act of saying something.

You can also combine a modal with a verb e.g. \textit{think} or \textit{believe} that refers to the holding of an opinion.

%
You use a modal in order to sound more polite, or to show your feelings about what you are going to say.

%
In structures like these, the subject is usually \textit{I}.

Sometimes you use an impersonal structure beginning with \textit{it} or \textit{you}.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{I ought to mention that he had never been there}, you can say \textit{It ought to be mentioned that he had never been there}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.217} If you feel strongly that what you are saying is important, you use \textit{must}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{must} apologize to you.
    
    I \underline{must} object.
    
    It \underline{must} be said that he has a point.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.218} If you feel that it is important or appropriate that something is said, you indicate that you are going to say it by using \textit{should} or \textit{ought to}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{should} explain at this point that there are 2 different sorts of microscope.
    
    It \underline{should} also be said that I learned a great deal from the experience.
    
    I \underline{ought to} stress that this was not a trial.
    
    Perhaps I \underline{ought to} conclude with a slightly more light-hearted question.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.219} If you want to say something during a discussion, you can indicate politely that you are going to say it by using \textit{can}.
\begin{example}
    Perhaps I \underline{can} mention another possibility.
    
    If I \underline{can} just intervene for 1 moment$\ldots$
\end{example}
If you want to be even more polite, you use \textit{could}.
\begin{example}
    Perhaps I \underline{could} just illustrate this by mentioning 2 cases that I know of personally.
    
    Perhaps I \underline{could} just ask you this$\ldots$
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.220} You also use \textit{can} and \textit{could} when you are mentioning an opinion or way of describing something.

%
\textit{Can} suggests that you approve of the opinion or description.
\begin{example}
    Such behavior \underline{can} be a reaction to deep emotional upset.
\end{example}
\textit{Could} is more neutral.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{could} argue that this is irrelevant.
    
    You \underline{could} call it a political offence.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.221} \textit{May} and \textit{might} can also be used to mention an option or a way of describing something.

%
\textit{May} suggests that you approve of the opinion or description.

It is more formal than \textit{can}.
\begin{example}
    This, it \underline{may} be added, greatly strengthened him in his resolve.
\end{example}
\textit{Might} also suggests that you approve of the opinion or description.

You use \textit{might} when you think there is a possibility that the person you are talking to will disagree with you.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{might} say she's entitled to get angry.
    
    That, one \underline{might} argue, is not too terrible.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.222} If you are stating an opinion of your own, you can show politely that you are going to state it by using \textit{should}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{should} think it would last quite a long time.
\end{example}
\textit{Would} is used in a similar way, but is less common.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{would} guess it may well come down to cost.
\end{example}

\subsection{Expressions used instead of modals}
\noindent\fbox{5.223} Several ordinary verbs and fixed expressions are used to express the same attitudes and ideas as modals.

These verbs and expressions are explained in the following paragraphs.

Each group of paragraphs corresponds to an earlier section in the chapter dealing with the use of modals in a particular type of situation.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.224} \textit{Be able to} and \textit{be possible to} can be used instead of \textit{can} and \textit{could} to say whether or not something is possible.

%
The subject of \textit{be able to} and \textit{be unable to} usually refers to a person or group of people, but it can refer to any living thing.

It can also refer to something organized or operated by people, e.g. a company, a country, or a machine.

%
The subject of \textit{be possible to} is always the impersonal pronoun \textit{it}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.225} If you want to say that it is possible for someone or something to do something, you can use \textit{be able to}.
\begin{example}
    All members \underline{are able to} claim traveling expenses.
    
    The college \underline{is able to} offer a wide choice of subjects.
\end{example}
You use \textit{be able to} with a negative to say that it is not possible for someone or something to do something.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{are not able to} run fast or throw a ball.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.226} You also use \textit{be unable to} to say that it is not possible for someone or something to do something.
\begin{example}
    I am having medical treatment and I\underline{'m unable to} work.
    
    We \underline{are unable to} comment on this.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.227} You also use \textit{be possible to} with \textit{it} as the subject to say that something is possible.

You usually use this expression to say that something is possible for people in general, rather than for an individual person.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{is possible to} insure against loss of earnings.
    
    \underline{Is} it \underline{possible to} programme a computer to speak?
\end{example}
If you use \textit{be possible to} to say that something is possible for a particular person or group, you put \textit{for} and a noun phrase after \textit{possible}.
\begin{example}
    It is possible \underline{for us to} measure his progress.
    
    It's possible \underline{for each department to} support new members.
\end{example}
You use \textit{be possible to} with a negative to say that something is not possible.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{is not possible to} quantify the effect.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.228} You can also use \textit{be impossible to} to say that something is not possible.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{is impossible to} fix the exact moment in time when it happened.
    
    It \underline{is impossible} for him \underline{to} watch TV and talk.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.229} To change the tense of \textit{be able to, be unable to, be possible to}, or \textit{be impossible to}, you simply change the form of \textit{be} to an appropriate simple form.
\begin{example}
    The doctor \underline{will be able to} spend more time with the patient.
    
    Their parents \underline{were unable to} send them any money.
    
    It \underline{was not possible to} dismiss his behavior as a contributing factor.
    
    It \underline{was impossible} for her \underline{to} obey this order.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.230} All modals except \textit{can} and \textit{could} can be used with these expressions.
\begin{example}
    A machine \underline{ought to be able to} do this.
    
    The United States \underline{would be unable to} produce any wood.
    
    It \underline{may be impossible to} predict which way things will develop.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.231} \textit{Used to} can be used with \textit{be able to} and \textit{be possible to}.
\begin{example}
    You \underline{used to be able to} go to the doctor for that.
    
    It \underline{used to be possible to} buy 2nd-hand wigs.
\end{example}
For more information about \textit{used to}, see paragraphs \textbf{5.254--5.258}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.232} You can use \textit{have to} or \textit{have got to} instead of \textit{must} to show that you think something is the case, because of particular facts or circumstances.
\begin{example}
    `That looks about right.' - `It \underline{has to} be.'
    
    Money \underline{has got to} be the reason.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.233} You can use \textit{be going to} instead of \textit{will} to say that something is certain to happen or be the case in the future.
\begin{example}
    The children \underline{are going to} be fishermen or farmers.
    
    Life \underline{is going to} be a bit easier from now on.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.234} You can use \textit{be bound to} or \textit{be sure to} to say emphatically that something is certain to happen in the future.
\begin{example}
    Marion\underline{'s bound to} be back soon.
    
    It \underline{was bound to} happen sooner or later.
    
    The roads \underline{are sure to} be busy this weekend.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.235} Instead of beginning a question with \textit{can} or \textit{could} when you are making a request, you can begin it with \textit{is} and the impersonal pronoun \textit{it}.

After \textit{it}, you put an expression e.g. \textit{all right} and either a \textit{to}-infinitive clause or an \textit{if}-clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Is it} all right for him to come in and sit and read his paper?
    
    \underline{Is it} okay if we have lunch here?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.236} You can use \textit{want} instead of \textit{would like} to give an instruction or make a request.

\textit{Want} is more direct and less polite than \textit{would like}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{want} you to turn to the front of the atlas.
    
    I \underline{want} to know what you think about this.
    
    I \underline{want} to speak to the manager.
\end{example}
\textit{Wanted} is also sometimes used.

It is more polite than \textit{want}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{wanted} to ask if you could give us any advice.
    
    Good morning, I \underline{wanted} to book a holiday in the South of France.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.237} You can use \textit{be going to} instead of \textit{will} to state an intension.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{am going to} talk to Boris.
    
    I\underline{'m going to} show you our little school.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.238} You use \textit{intend to} to state a fairly strong intention.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{intend to} go to Cannes for a month in August.
    
    I don't \underline{intend to} stay very long.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.239} You use \textit{be determined to} or \textit{be resolved to} to indicate a very strong intention to do something.

\textit{Be resolved to} is rather formal.
\begin{example}
    I\underline{'m determined to} try.
    
    She \underline{was resolved to} marry a rich American.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.240} You can use \textit{have to} or \textit{have got to} instead of \textit{must} to show that it is important that you do something.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{have to} get home now.
    
    It's something I \underline{have got to} overcome.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.241} You can use \textit{I am not} instead of \textit{I will not} to say firmly that you are unwilling to do or accept something.

\textit{I am not} is followed by an \textit{-ing} participle.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I am not} staying in this hospital.
    
    \underline{I'm not} having dirty rugs.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.242} You can use \textit{refuse} instead of \textit{will not} when you are refusing to do something.

\textit{Refuse} is followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{refuse} to list possible reasons.
    
    I \underline{refuse} to pay.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.243} You can use \textit{unwilling} or \textit{reluctant} with a \textit{to}-infinitive clause to say that someone is not willing to do or accept something.
\begin{example}
    He is \underline{unwilling} to answer the questions.
    
    They seemed \underline{reluctant} to talk about what had happened.
\end{example}
You can use several adjectives after \textit{not} to say that someone is unwilling to do or accept something.
\begin{example}
    Exporters are \underline{not willing} to supply goods on credit.
    
    I'm \underline{not prepared} to teach him anything.
    
    Thompson is \underline{not keen} to see history repeat itself.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.244} You can use \textit{have to} or \textit{have got to} instead of \textit{must} to say that something is necessary or extremely important.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{have to} look more closely at the record of their work together.
    
    This \underline{has got to} be put right.
    
    You\underline{'ve got to} be able to communicate.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.245} \textit{Need to} can also be used instead of \textit{must} or \textit{have to}.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{need to} change the balance of power.
    
    You do not \underline{need to} worry.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.246} You can also say that something is important or necessary by using a sentence beginning with the impersonal pronoun \textit{it}, followed by \textit{is}, an adjective e.g. \textit{important} or \textit{necessary}, and a \textit{that}-clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It is important that} you should know precisely what is going on.
    
    \underline{It is essential that} immediate action should be taken.
    
    \underline{It is vital that} a mother takes time to get to know her baby.
\end{example}
\textit{Important} and \textit{necessary} can also be followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It's important to} recognize what industry needs at this moment.
    
    \underline{It is necessary to} examine this claim before we proceed any further.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.247} You can use \textit{had better} instead of \textit{should} or \textit{ought to} to say that something is the right or correct thing to do.

You use \textit{had better} with \textit{I} or \textit{we} to show an intention.

You use it with \textit{you} when you are giving advice or a warning.
\begin{example}
    I think I \underline{had better} show this to my brother.
    
    He decided that we \underline{had better} meet.
    
    You\underline{'d better} go.
\end{example}

\subsection{Semi-modals}
\noindent\fbox{5.248} \textit{Dare, need}, and \textit{used to} can be used as modals, or they can be used in other ways.

When they are used as modals, they have some characteristics that other modals do not have.

For these reasons, they are sometimes called \textbf{semi-modals}.

%
The use of \textit{dare} and \textit{need} as modals is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.249--5.253}.

%
The use of \textit{used to} as a modal is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.254--5.258}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.249} When \textit{dare} and \textit{need} are used as modals, they have the same meaning as when they are followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.

However, they are normally used as modals only in negative sentences and in questions.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Nobody dare} disturb him.
    
    No parent \underline{dare} let their child roam free.
    
    He told her that she \underline{need not} worry.
    
    How \underline{dare} you speak to me like that?
    
    \underline{Need} you go so soon?
\end{example}
%
[AE] \textit{Need not} is often shortened to \textit{needn't}.

\textit{Dare not} is sometimes shortened to \textit{daren't} in British English but this contraction is very rare in American English.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{daren't} ring Jeremy again.
    
    We \underline{needn't} worry about that.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.250} Unlike other modals, \textit{dare} has some inflected forms that are occasionally used.

%
In the present simple, the 3rd person singular form can be either \textit{dare} or \textit{dares}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{dare} not admit he had forgotten her name.
    
    What nobody \underline{dares} suggest is that the children are simply spoilt.
\end{example}
In the past simple, either \textit{dare} or \textit{dared} to can be used.

\textit{Dare} is more formal than \textit{dared}.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{dare} not take his eyes off his assailant.
    
    He \underline{dared} not show he was pleased.
\end{example}
\textit{Need} is not inflected when it is used as a modal.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.251} Normally, modals cannot be used with other modals.

However \textit{dare} can be used with \textit{will, would, should}, and \textit{might}.
\begin{example}
    No one \underline{will dare} override what the towns decide.
    
    I \underline{wouldn't dare} go to there alone.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.252} Unlike other modals, \textit{dare} can be used with the auxiliary verb \textit{do}.
\begin{example}
    We \underline{do not dare} examine it.
    
    \underline{Don't} you ever \underline{dare} come here again!
\end{example}
In ordinary speech, \textit{did not dare} and \textit{didn't dare} are much more common than \textit{dared not} or \textit{dare not}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{did not dare} leave the path.
    
    I \underline{didn't dare} speak or move.
    
    We \underline{didn't dare} say that we would prefer to go home.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.253} Besides being used as modals, \textit{dare} and \textit{need} are used in other ways in which they are not followed by the base form of another verb.

Both verbs can be followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive clause, and \textit{need} is a common transitive verb.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.254} \textit{Used to} cannot be used with other modals.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{used to} get quite cross with Lily.
    
    $\ldots$ these Westerns that \underline{used to} do so well in Hollywood.
    
    What did we \underline{use to} call it?
\end{example}
However, \textit{used to} can be used with the auxiliary verb \textit{do}.

This is explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.257--5.258}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.255} \textit{Used to} is used to say that something happened regularly or existed in the past, although it no longer happens or exists.

%
\textit{Used to} is similar to \textit{would} when it is used to describe repeated actions in the past.

However, unlike \textit{would, used to} can also describe past states and situations.
\begin{example}
    I'm not quite as mad as I \underline{used to} be.
    
    You \underline{used to} bring me flowers all the time.
\end{example}
The use of \textit{would} to talk about things that happened regularly in the past is dealt with in paragraph \textbf{5.114}.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.256} \textit{Used to} can be used on its own without a following verb phrase when it is clear from the context what the subject matter is.
\begin{example}
    People don't work as hard as they \underline{used to}.
    
    I don't feel British any more. Not as much as I \underline{used to}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{5.257} \textit{Used to} is not common in negative structures.

%
In informal speech, people sometimes make negative statements by putting \textit{didn't} in front of \textit{used to}.

This is sometimes represented as \textit{use to}.
\begin{example}
    They \underline{didn't use to} mind what we did.
\end{example}
However, many people consider this use to be incorrect.

%
Another way to form the negative is to put \textit{never} in front of \textit{used to}.
\begin{example}
    Where I was before, we \underline{never used to} have posters on the walls.
\end{example}
Sometimes \textit{not} is put between \textit{used} and \textit{to}.

This is a fairly formal use.
\begin{example}
    It \underline{used not to} be taxable.
\end{example}
Some grammar books give a contracted form for the negative, \textit{usedn't to} or \textit{usen't to}.

This is now rarely used, and is thought to be very old-fashioned.

%
\noindent\fbox{5.258} You normally form questions with \textit{used to} by putting \textit{did} in front of the subject, followed by \textit{used to} or \textit{use to}.

\textit{Wh}-questions are formed by putting the \textit{wh}-word at the beginning, followed by \textit{used to}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Did she used} to be nice?
    
    \underline{What used to} annoy you most about him?
\end{example}
You can form negative questions by putting \textit{didn't} in front of the subject, followed by the subject and \textit{used to} or \textit{use to}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Didn't} they \underline{use to} mind?
\end{example}
In more formal English, \textit{did} is put in front of the subject and \textit{not} after it, followed by \textit{used to} or \textit{use to}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Did} she \underline{not use to} smile?
\end{example}

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------%

\chapter{Expressing manner \& place: other adverbials}

\section{Introduction}
\noindent\fbox{6.1} When you are talking about an even or a situation, you sometimes want to say something about it that has not been indicated by the subject, verb, object, or complement.

You do this by using an \textbf{adverbial}.

%
An adverbial is a word or group of words that you use when you want to say when an event or situation occurs, how it occurs, how much it occurs, or where it occurs.
\begin{example}
    I was \underline{soon} lost.
    
    She laughed \underline{quietly}.
    
    She was \underline{tremendously} impressed.
    
    He fumbled \underline{in his pocket}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.2} The 2 main types of adverbial are \textbf{adverb phrases} and \textbf{prepositional phrases}.
\begin{example}
    He acted \underline{very clumsily}.
    
    I cannot speak \underline{too highly} of their courage and skill.
    
    He takes his job \underline{very seriously indeed}.
    
    He did not play \underline{well enough} throughout the week to deserve to win.
\end{example}
However, adverbs very often occur on their own.
\begin{example}
    I shook her \underline{gently}.
    
    He \underline{greatly} admired Cezanne.
    
    He \underline{scarcely} knew his aunt.
    
    The number will \underline{probably} be higher than we expected.
\end{example}
For more information about adverbs, see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.16}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.3} Adverbials that consist of a preposition and a noun, e.g. \textit{in a box} and \textit{to the station}, are called \textbf{prepositional phrases}.

These are dealt with in detail in the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.73}.
\begin{example}
    Large cushions lay \underline{on the floor}.
    
    The voice was coming \underline{from my apartment}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.4} Occasionally, \underline{noun phrases} can also be used as adverbials.
\begin{example}
    He was looking really ill \underline{this time yesterday}.
    
    I'm going to handle this \underline{my way}.
\end{example}
When noun phrases are used as adverbials, they most often relate to time.

Time adverbials are dealt with in Chap. 4.

Noun phrases that relate to place are dealt with at paragraph \textbf{6.72}; those that relate to manner are dealt with at paragraph \textbf{6.44}, and those that relate to degree at paragraph \textbf{6.52}.

%
For more information on noun phrases in general, see Chaps. 1--2.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.5} The most common way in which adverb phrases give additional information is by adding something to the meaning of a verb phrase.
\begin{example}
    He nodded and smiled \underline{warmly}.
    
    The report says that hospitals and rescue services coped \underline{extremely well}.
    
    I could find that out \underline{fairly easily}.
\end{example}
Prepositional phrases have a wide range of meanings.
\begin{example}
    It was estimated that at least 2,000 people were \underline{on the 2 trains}.
    
    Kenny Stuart came 2nd, knocking 2 minutes \underline{off his previous best time}.
    
    \underline{For the 1st time since I'd been pregnant} I felt well.
\end{example}
Many \textbf{intransitive verbs} normally require and adverbial.

See paragraph \textbf{3.10} for more information about these.
\begin{example}
    Ashton had behaved \underline{abominably}.
    
    She turned and rushed \underline{out of the room}.
\end{example}
Some \textbf{transitive verbs} normally require an adverbial after the object of the verb.

For more information about these, see paragraph \textbf{3.19}.
\begin{example}
    I put my hand \underline{on the door}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.6} Adverbials can also add meaning to a whole clause, e.g. by giving the writer's or speaker's comment on it.

For more information, see the section on \textbf{sentence adverbials} beginning at paragraph \textbf{9.56}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Obviously} crime is going to be squeezed in a variety of ways.
    
    \underline{Fortunately}, the damage had been slight.
    
    \underline{Ideally} the dairy should have a concrete or tiled floor.
    
    \underline{No doubt} she loves Gertrude too.
\end{example}
They can also show the way in which 1 sentence is linked to another clause.

For more information, see the section on \textbf{sentence connectors} beginning at paragraph \textbf{10.48}.
\begin{example}
    The 2nd paragraph repeats the information given in the 1st paragraph. \underline{Therefore}, it isn't necessary.
\end{example}

\subsection{Position of adverbials}
\noindent\fbox{6.7} The position of adverbials within clauses is flexible, allowing many changes of emphasis and focus.

%
Adverbials are normally placed at the end of the clause after the verb phrase, or after an object if there is one.
\begin{example}
    She packed \underline{carefully}.
    
    They would go on talking \underline{for hours}.
    
    I enjoyed the course \underline{immensely}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.8} You can emphasize the adverbial by placing it at the beginning of the clause, in front of the subject.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Gently} Fiona leaned forward and wiped the old lady's tears away.
    
    \underline{In his excitement} Billy had forgotten the letter.
\end{example}
The adverbial is often separated by a comma from the rest of the clause.
\begin{example}
    \underline{After much discussion}, they had decided to take the coin to the jeweler.
\end{example}
This position is often used in written stories to draw attention to the adverbial.

For more information, see paragraph \textbf{9.70}.

%
Note that adverbs of degree are rarely used at the beginning of a clause: see paragraph \textbf{6.45}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.9} Adverbials can also be placed between the subject and the main verb.

This focuses on the adverbial more than when it is at the end of the clause, but not as much as putting it at the beginning of the clause.

However, this position is much more common with adverbs than with prepositional phrases.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{quickly} became aware that she was looking at me.
    
    We \underline{often} swam in the surf.
    
    He \underline{carefully} wrapped each component in several layers of foam rubber.
    
    He \underline{noisily} opened the fridge and took out a carton of milk.
\end{example}
Note that in verb phrases containing auxiliaries, the adverbial is still placed in front of the main verb.
\begin{example}
    I had \underline{almost} forgotten about the trip.
    
    We will \underline{never} have enough money to provide all the services that people want.
    
    It would not \underline{in any case} be for him.
\end{example}
Long adverbials in this position are usually separated by commas from the rest of the clause.
\begin{example}
    Fred, \underline{in this own way}, was a great actor.
\end{example}
Adverbials of place rarely occur in this position.

For more information about adverbials of place, see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.53}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.10} Some adverbials are often placed in front of the main verb:
\begin{itemize}
    \item most adverbs of indefinite frequency (see paragraph \textbf{4.114})
    \begin{example}
        always, constantly, continually, continuously, ever, frequently, hardly ever, never, normally, occasionally, often, rarely, regularly, repeatedly, seldom, sometimes, usually
    \end{example}
    \item some adverbs of indefinite time (see paragraph \textbf{4.41})
    \begin{example}
        again, already, earlier, finally, 1st, just, last, previously, recently, since
    \end{example}
    \item some adverbs of degree (see paragraph \textbf{6.45}), especially emphasizing adverbs (see paragraph \textbf{6.49})
    \begin{example}
        absolutely, almost, altogether, badly, completely, deeply, entirely, fairly, greatly, largely, nearly, perfectly, quiet, rather, really, somewhat, totally, utterly, virtually, well
    \end{example}
    \item focusing adverbs, when modifying a verb (see paragraph \textbf{9.67})
    \begin{example}
        even, just, merely, only, really, simply
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
Note that some adverbs have a different reference when they are placed in front of the main verb rather than at the end of the clause:
\begin{example}
    The Trade Unions have acted \underline{foolishly}.
    
    Baldwin had \underline{foolishly} opened the door.
\end{example}
The 1st example means that the Unions acted in a foolish way.

The 2nd example means that opening the door was a foolish action, and not that the door was opened in a foolish way.
\begin{example}
    Americans always tip \underline{generously}.
    
    He \underline{generously} offered to drive me home.
\end{example}
The 1st example tells us how well Americans tip, the 2nd example indicates that his offer was a generous action.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.11} [i] If the verb is a \textit{to}-infinitive, you usually put an adverb after it, or after the object if there is one.
\begin{example}
    He tried to leave \underline{quietly}.
    
    Thomas made an appointment to see him \underline{immediately}.
\end{example}
Some people, however, particularly when they are speaking, do put adverbs between the \textit{to} and the infinitive.

This use is considered to be incorrect by some speakers of English.
\begin{example}
    My wife told me \underline{to probably expect} you, he said.
    
    Vauxhall are attempting \underline{to really break} into the market.
\end{example}
Sometimes, however, if you avoid putting the adverb between the \textit{to} and the infinitive, you change the emphasis of the sentence, or it can sound clumsy.

In such cases, \textit{splitting the infinitive}, as it is called, is now generally considered acceptable.
\begin{example}
    Participants will be encouraged \underline{to actively participate} in the workshop.
    
    I want you \underline{to really enjoy} yourself.
\end{example}
Note that the 2nd example above means \textit{I want you to enjoy yourself very much}/

If you said \textit{I really want you to enjoy yourself}, you would mean \textit{It is very important for me that you enjoy yourself}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.12} If a clause has 2 adverbials, and one is an adverb and the other is a prepositional phrase, you can usually place either of them 1st.
\begin{example}
    Miss Burns looked \underline{calmly at Marianne}.
    
    They were sitting \underline{happily in the car}.
