    The women shouted \underline{at me savagely}.
    
    He got \underline{into the car quickly} and drove off.
\end{example}
However, if the prepositional phrase is rather long, it is more common to place the adverb 1st, immediately after the verb.
\begin{example}
    He listened \underline{calmly} to the report of his aides.
    
    She would sit \underline{cross-legged} in her red robes.
\end{example}
Similarly, if the verb phrase is followed by a long object, the adverb comes after the verb and before the object.
\begin{example}
    She sang \underline{beautifully} a school song the children had taught her when they were little.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.13} In clauses with more than 1 adverbial, the meaning of the adverbials can also affect their order.

The usual order is adverbial of manner, then adverbial of place, then time adverbial.
\begin{example}
    They knelt \underline{quietly in the shadow of the rock}.
    
    I tried to reach you \underline{at home several times}.
    
    He was imprisoned \underline{in Cairo in Jan 1945}.
    
    Parents may complain that their child eats \underline{badly at meals}.
    
    The youngsters repeat this \underline{in union at the beginning of each session}.
\end{example}
However, if a clause contains an adverb of manner and an adverb of direction e.g. \textit{down, out}, or \textit{home}, the adverb of direction is usually put in front of the adverb of manner.
\begin{example}
    Lomax drove \underline{home fast}.
    
    I reached \underline{down slowly}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.14} Adverbials of different types can be placed together, sometimes separated by a comma, but adverbials of the same type, e.g. 2 adverbials of manner, are usually linked by conjunctions e.g. \textit{and} and \textit{but}, or structures e.g. \textit{rather than}.

For more information about how to link adverbials using conjunctions, see paragraph \textbf{8.188}.
\begin{example}
    She sang \underline{clearly} and \underline{beautifully}.
    
    They help to combat the problem \underline{at source}, rather than \underline{superficially}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.15} When clauses begin with an adverbial, the normal order of subject and verb is sometimes changed.

E.g., after adverbials of place, the verb usually comes before the subject.

For more information about adverbials of place, see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.53}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Next to it} stood a pile of paper cups.
    
    \underline{Beyond them} lay the fields.
\end{example}
This also happens when \textbf{broad negative} adverbs e.g. \textit{hardly} and \textit{barely}, and some other \textbf{negative words} are placed at the beginning of the clause.

For more information about these, see paragraphs \textbf{5.49--5.93}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Never} in history had technology made such spectacular advances.
    
    \underline{Seldom} can there have been such a happy meeting.
\end{example}
Both of these cases are particularly common in written stories.

A change in the normal order of subject and verb can occur after other adverbs, but only in poetry or old-fashioned English.

The following example is from a Christmas carol written in 1843:
\begin{example}
    \underline{Brightly} shone the moon that night, though the frost was cruel.
\end{example}

\section{Adverbs}

\subsection{Types of adverb}
\noindent\fbox{6.16} There are several types of adverb:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{adverbs of time, frequency}, and \textbf{duration}, e.g. \textit{soon, often}, and \textit{always}.
    
    Because these are all related to time, they are dealt with fully in Chap. 4.
    \item \textbf{adverbs of place}, e.g. \textit{around, downstairs}, and \textit{underneath}.
    
    These are dealt with in the section on \textbf{place} beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.53}.
    \item \textbf{adverb of manner}, e.g. \textit{beautifully, carefully}, and \textit{silently}.
    
    See the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.36} for more information about these.
    \item \textbf{adverb of degree}, e.g. \textit{almost, badly, terribly}, and \textit{well}.
    
    See the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.45} for more information about these.
    \item \textbf{sentence connectors}, e.g. \textit{consequently, furthermore}, and \textit{however}.
    
    These are dealt with in paragraphs \textbf{10.48--10.56}.
    \item \textbf{sentence adverbs}, e.g. \textit{alas, apparently, chiefly}, and \textit{interestingly}.
    
    See the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{9.79} for more information on these.
    \item \textbf{broad negative adverbs}, e.g. \textit{barely, hardly, rarely, scarcely}, and \textit{seldom}.
    
    These are dealt with at paragraphs \textbf{5.82--5.89}.
    \item \textbf{focusing adverbs}, e.g. \textit{especially} and \textit{only}.
    
    These are dealt with in the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{9.64}.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Adverb forms \& meanings related to adjectives}
\noindent\fbox{6.17} Many adverbs are related to adjectives.

The main relationships and rules of formation are explained below.

%
Many adverbs are formed by adding \textit{-ly} to an adjective.

E.g., the adverbs \textit{quietly} and \textit{badly} are formed by adding \textit{-ly} to the adjectives \textit{quiet} and \textit{bad}.

%
Most of the adverbs formed in this way are \textbf{adverbs of manner}, so some people refer to adverbs of manner as \textit{-ly} adverbs.
\begin{example}
    Sit there \underline{quietly}, and listen to this music.
    
    I didn't play \underline{badly}.
    
    He reported \underline{accurately} what they said.
    
    He nodded and smiled \underline{warmly}.
\end{example}
For more information about adjectives, see Chap. 2.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.18} Some \textit{-ly} adverbs have slightly different spellings from the adjectives they are related to, e.g. \textit{nastily, gently, terribly, academically, truly}, and \textit{fully}.

For information about these adverbs, see the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.19} Not all adverbs ending in \textit{-ly} are adverbs of manner.

Some are adverbs of degree, e.g. \textit{extremely} and \textit{slightly}: see the list at paragraph \textbf{6.45}.
\begin{example}
    I enjoyed the course \underline{immensely}.
    
    Sales fell \underline{slightly} last month.
\end{example}
A few are adverbs of time, duration, or frequency, e.g. \textit{presently, briefly}, and \textit{weekly}: see the lists of these in Chap. 4.
\begin{example}
    At 10.15 a.m. soldiers \underline{briefly} opened fire again.
    
    These allegations are \underline{currently} being investigated by my legal team.
\end{example}
Others are adverbs of place, e.g. \textit{locally} and \textit{internationally}, linking adverbs e.g. \textit{consequently}, or sentence adverbs e.g. \textit{actually}.

For lists of adverbs of place, see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.53}.

For lists of sentence adverbs, see Chap. 9.
\begin{example}
    They live \underline{locally} and they have never caused any bother.
    
    These efforts have received little credit \underline{internationally}.
    
    They did not preach. \underline{Consequently}, they reached a vastly wider audience.
    
    They still remains something to say. Several things, \underline{actually}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.20} Most adverbs formed by adding \textit{-ly} to an adjective have a similar meaning to the adjective, e.g. \textit{quietly} and \textit{beautifully} have similar meanings to \textit{quiet} and \textit{beautiful}.
\begin{example}
    She is thoughtful, \underline{quiet} and controlled.
    
    `I'm going to do it,' I said \underline{quietly}.
    
    His costumes are \underline{beautiful}, a big improvement on the previous ones.
    
    The girls had dressed more \underline{beautifully} than ever, for him.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.21} Some \textit{-ly} adverbs have a different meaning from the meanings of their related adjectives.

E.g., \textit{hardly} means \textit{not very much} or \textit{almost not at all} and is not used with any of the meanings of the adjective \textit{hard}.
\begin{example}
    This has been a long \underline{hard} day.
    
    Her bedroom was so small she could \underline{hardly} move in it.
\end{example}
Here is a list of adverbs ending in \textit{-ly} that have a different meaning from the meanings of their related adjectives:
\begin{example}
    barely, hardly, lately, presently, scarcely, shortly
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.22} Some \textit{-ly} adverbs are not related to adjectives, e.g. \textit{accordingly}.

Some are related to nouns, e.g. \textit{bodily, purposely, daily}, and \textit{weekly}.

For lists of these, see the Reference section.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.23} Adverbs ending in \textit{-ly} are very rarely formed from some types of adjective:
\begin{itemize}
    \item most classifying adjectives, e.g. \textit{racist, eastern, female, urban, foreign}, and \textit{available}.
    
    See Chap. 2 for lists of classifying adjectives.
    \item most color adjectives, although \textit{-ly} adverbs from these are occasionally found in works of literature.
    \begin{example}
        The hills rise \underline{greenly} to the deep-blue sky.
        
        He lay still, staring \underline{blackly} up at the ceiling.
    \end{example}
    \item some very common qualitative adjectives that refer to basic qualities:
    \begin{example}
        big, fat, old, small, tall, tiny, wet, young
    \end{example}
    \item adjectives that already end in \textit{-ly}, e.g. \textit{friendly, lively, cowardly, ugly}, and \textit{silly}.
    \item most adjectives that end in \textit{-ed}, e.g. \textit{frightened} and \textit{surprised}.
    
    see the Reference section for a list of the common ones that do form \textit{-ly} adverbs, e.g. \textit{excitedly} and \textit{hurriedly}.
\end{itemize}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.24} In some cases, an adverb has the same form as an adjective and is similar in meaning.

E.g., \textit{fast} is an adverb in the sentence \textit{News travels fast} and an adjective in the sentence \textit{She likes fast cars}.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a \underline{fast} rail link from London to the Channel Tunnel.
    
    The driver was driving too \underline{fast} for the conditions.
\end{example}
In these cases, the adverb is usually placed immediately after the verb or object, and rarely in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    alike, downtown, extra, far, fast, inside, long, next, outside, overseas, past, straight, through
\end{example}
Some words ending in \textit{-ly} are both adverbs and adjectives, e.g. \textit{daily, monthly}, and \textit{yearly}.

These relate to frequency and are explained in paragraph \textbf{4.120}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.25} Several postdeterminers, including \textit{further, next, only, opposite}, and \textit{same}, have the same form as adverbs but no direct relation in meaning.

Note that \textit{well} is an adverb and adjective, but usually means \textit{not ill} as an adjective, and \textit{with skill or success} as an adverb.
\begin{example}
    He has done \underline{well}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.26} Sometimes, 2 adverbs are related to the same adjective.

1 adverb has the same form as the adjective, and the other is formed by adding \textit{-ly}.
\begin{example}
    He closed his eyes \underline{tight}.
    
    He closed his eyes \underline{tightly}.
    
    Failure may yet cost his country \underline{dear}.
    
    Holes in the road are a menace which costs his country \underline{dearly} in lost man hours every year.
    
    The German manufacturer was urging me to cut out the middle man and deal with him \underline{direct}.
    
    The trend in recent years has been to deal \underline{directly} with the supplier.
\end{example}
Here is a list of common adverbs that have both these forms:
\begin{example}
    clear clearly, close closely, dear dearly, deep deeply, direct directly, easy easily, fine finely, 1st 1stly, hard hardly, high highly, last lastly, late lately, thick thickly, thin thinly, tight tightly
\end{example}
Note that the \textit{-ly} adverb often has a different meaning from the adverb with the same form as the adjective,
\begin{example}
    The river was running \underline{high} and swiftly.
    
    I though \underline{highly} of the idea.
    
    He has worked \underline{hard}.
    
    Border could \underline{hardly} make himself heard above the din.
    
    When the snake strikes, its mouth opens \underline{wide}.
    
    Closing dates for applications vary \underline{widely}.
\end{example}
Note that, with some words that are adverbs and adjectives, the addition of \textit{-ly} forms a new adverb and a new adjective, e.g. \textit{dead} and \textit{deadly}, \textit{low} and \textit{lowly}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.27} Some adjectives do not form adverbs at all.

These include the common qualitative adjectives listed in paragraph \textbf{6.23}, e.g. \textit{big} and \textit{old}.

%
Here is a list of some more adjectives that do not form adverbs:
\begin{example}
    afraid, alive, alone, asleep, awake, content, difficult, drunk, foreign, good, hurt, ill, little, long, sorry, standard
\end{example}
note that the adverbs relating to \textit{content} and \textit{drunk} are formed by adding \textit{-ly} to the forms \textit{contented} and \textit{drunken}, thus giving \textit{contentedly} and \textit{drunkenly}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.28} [i] If there is no adverb related to an adjective, and you want to give additional information about an event or situation, you can often use a prepositional phrase.

%
In some cases, the prepositional phrase involves a noun that is related to the adjective.

E.g., there is no adverb related to the adjective \textit{difficult}, but you can use the related noun \textit{difficulty} in the prepositional phrase \textit{with difficulty} instead.
\begin{example}
    He stood up slowly and \underline{with difficulty}.
\end{example}
In other cases, e.g. with adjectives that end in \textit{-ly}, a general noun e.g. \textit{way, manner}, or \textit{fashion} is used.
\begin{example}
    He walks \underline{in a funny way}.
    
    He greeted us \underline{in his usual friendly fashion}.
\end{example}
Prepositional phrases may be used even if an adverb does exist, e.g. when you want to add more detailed information or to add emphasis.
\begin{example}
    She comforted the bereaved relatives \underline{in a dignified, compassionate and personalized manner}.
    
    At these extreme velocities, materials behave \underline{in a totally different manner from normal}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.29} Some adverbs are not related to adjectives at all.

This is especially true of adverbs of time and place.

See Chap. 4 for adverbs of time, and the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.53} for adverbs of place.
\begin{example}
    It will \underline{soon} be Christmas.
\end{example}
There are also some other adverbs that are not related to adjectives.

%
For a list of the common adverbs that are not related to adjectives, see the Reference section.

\subsection{Comparative \& superlative adverbs}
\noindent\fbox{6.30} You may want to say how something happens or is done in relation to how it happens on a different occasion, or how it was done by someone or something else.

You can do this by using adverbs in the \textbf{comparative} or \textbf{superlative}.
\begin{example}
    He began to speak \underline{more quickly}.
    
    This form of treatment is \underline{most commonly} used in younger patients.
\end{example}
Most \textbf{adverbs of manner} (see paragraph \textbf{6.36}) have comparatives and superlatives.

%
A few other adverbs also have comparatives and superlatives: some adverbs of \textbf{time} (\textit{early} and \textit{late}, see paragraph \textbf{4.71}), \textbf{frequency} (\textit{often} and \textit{frequently}, see paragraph \textbf{4.114}), \textbf{duration} (\textit{briefly, permanently}, and \textit{long}, see paragraph \textbf{4.123}), and \textbf{place} (\textit{near, close, deep, high, far}, and \textit{low}, see paragraphs \textbf{6.88} and \textbf{6.60}).

%
\noindent\fbox{6.31} The forms and uses of comparative and superlative adverbs are generally similar to those of adjectives.

For more information about comparatives and superlatives of adjectives, see paragraphs \textbf{2.103--2.122}.

%
However, unlike adjectives, the comparative of an adverb is usually formed with \textit{more} and the superlative with \textit{most}, and not by adding \textit{-er} and \textit{-est}.
\begin{example}
    The people needed business skills so that they could manage themselves \underline{more effectively}.
    
    $\ldots$ the text that Prof. Williams's work \underline{most closely} resembles.
    
    Valium is \underline{most often} prescribed as an anti-anxiety drug.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.32} Some very common adverbs have comparatives and superlatives that are single words and not formed using \textit{more} and \textit{most}.

Note that adverbs that have irregular comparatives also have irregular superlatives.

%
\textit{Well} has the comparative \textit{better} and the superlative \textit{best}.
\begin{example}
    She would ask him later, when she knew him \underline{better}.
    
    I have to find out what I can do \underline{best}.
\end{example}
\textit{Badly} has the comparative \textit{worse} and the superlative \textit{worst}.
\begin{example}
    `I don't think the crowd helped her,' Gordon admitted. `She played \underline{worse}.'
    
    The expedition from Mozambique fared \underline{worst}.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{worse} and \textit{worst} are also the comparative and superlative of \textit{ill} when it is an adverb or adjective.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.33} Adverbs that have the same form as adjectives also have the same comparatives and superlatives as the adjectives.

E.g., \textit{fast} has \textit{faster} and \textit{fastest}, and \textit{hard} has \textit{harder} and \textit{hardest}.

For a list of common adverbs that have the same form as adjectives, see paragraph \textbf{6.24}.
\begin{example}
    They worked \underline{harder}, they were more honest.
    
    The winning blow is the one that strikes \underline{hardest}.
    
    This would enable claim to be dealt with \underline{faster}.
    
    This type of sugar dissolves \underline{fastest}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.34} Some adverbs have comparatives and superlatives with \textit{more} and \textit{most}, but also have single-word comparatives and superlatives.
\begin{example}
    They can be built \underline{more quickly}.
    
    You probably learn \underline{quicker} by having lessons.
    
    Those women treated \underline{quickest} were those most likely to die.
    
    The American computer firm will be relying \underline{more heavily} on its new Scottish plant.
    
    It seems that the rights of soldiers weigh \underline{heavier} than the rights of those killed.
    
    The burden fell \underline{most heavily} on Kanhai.
    
    Illiteracy weights \underline{heaviest} on the groups who are already disadvantaged in other ways.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.35} [i] The structures involving comparatives and superlatives are generally the same for adverbs as for adjectives:
\begin{itemize}
    \item the use of \textit{no} and \textit{any} with comparatives: see paragraph \textbf{2.163}
    \begin{example}
        He began to behave \underline{more and more erratically}.
        
        Omoro didn't want to express it \underline{any more strongly}.
    \end{example}
    \item the optimal use of \textit{the} with superlatives: see paragraph \textbf{2.117}
    \begin{example}
        His shoulders hurt \underline{the worst}.
        
        Old people work \underline{hardest}.
    \end{example}
    \item the use of words like \textit{much} or \textit{a little} with comparatives and superlatives: see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{2.157}.
    \begin{example}
        The situation resolved itself \underline{much more easily} than I had expected.
        
        There the process progresses \underline{even more rapidly}.
    \end{example}
    \item the use of \textit{than} after comparatives: see paragraph \textbf{2.106}
    \begin{example}
        This class continues to grow \underline{more rapidly than any other group}.
        
        Prices have been rising \underline{faster than incomes}.
        
        You might know this \underline{better than me}.
    \end{example}
    \item repeating comparatives to show changes in extent: see paragraph \textbf{2.161}
    \begin{example}
        He began to behave \underline{more and more erratically}.
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Adverbs of manner}
\noindent\fbox{6.36} You often want to say something about the way something is done or about the circumstances of an event or situation.

The most common way of doing this is by using \textbf{adverbs of manner}.

Adverbs of manner give more information about the way in which an event or action takes place.
\begin{example}
    He nodded and smiled \underline{warmly}.
    
    She \underline{accidentally} shot herself in the foot.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.37} Many adverbs of manner are used to describe the way in which something is done.

E.g., in the sentence \textit{He did it carefully, carefully} means \textit{in a careful way}.
\begin{example}
    They think, dress and live \underline{differently}.
    
    He acted very \underline{clumsily}.
    
    You must be able to speak \underline{fluently} and \underline{correctly}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.38} Here is a list of common \textit{-ly} adverbs that describe the way in which something is done:
\begin{example}
    abruptly, accurately, awkwardly, badly, beautifully, brightly, brilliantly, briskly, carefully, carelessly, casually, cheaply, clearly, closely, clumsily, comfortably, consistently, conveniently, correctly, dangerously, delicately, differently, discreetly, distinctly, dramatically, easily, economically, effectively, efficiently, evenly, explicitly, faintly, faithfully, fiercely, finely, firmly, fluently, formally, frankly, freely, gently, gracefully, hastily, heavily, honestly, hurriedly, intently, meticulously, neatly, nicely, oddly, patiently, peacefully, peculiarly, perfectly, plainly, pleasantly, politely, poorly, professionally, properly, quietly, rapidly, readily, richly, rigidly, roughly, ruthlessly, securely, sensibly, sharply, silently, simply, smoothly, softly, solidly, specifically, splendidly, steadily, steeply, stiffly, strangely, subtly, superbly, swiftly, systematically, tenderly, thickly, thinly, thoroughly, thoughtfully, tightly, truthfully, uncomfortably, urgently, vaguely, vigorously, violently, vividly, voluntarily, warmly, widely, willingly, wonderfully
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.39} Adverbs formed from adjectives that describe people's feelings, e.g. \textit{happily} or \textit{nervously}, indicate both the way in which something is done and the feelings of the person who does it.

%
E.g., the sentence \textit{She laughed happily} means both that she laughed in a happy way and that she was feeling happy.
\begin{example}
    We laughed and chatted \underline{happily} together.
    
    Gaskell got up \underline{wearily} and headed for the stairs.
    
    They looked \underline{anxiously} at each other.
    
    The children waited \underline{eagerly} for their presents.
    
    The children smiled \underline{shyly}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.40} Here is a list of adverbs that describe the feelings of the person who does something as well as the way in which it is done:
\begin{example}
    angrily, anxiously, bitterly, boldly, calmly, cheerfully, confidently, desperately, eagerly, excitedly, furiously, gladly, gloomily, gratefully, happily, helplessly, hopefully, hopelessly, impatiently, miserably, nervously, passionately, proudly, reluctantly, sadly, shyly, sincerely, uncomfortably, uneasily, unhappily, wearily
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.41} Adverbs of manner can also indicate the circumstances in which something is done, rather than how it is done.

E.g., in the sentence \textit{He spoke to me privately, privately} means \textit{when no one else was present} rather than \textit{in a private way}.
\begin{example}
    I need to speak to you \underline{privately}.
    
    He had \underline{publicly} called for an investigation of the entire school system.
    
    Britain and France \underline{jointly} suggested a plan in 1954.
    
    I have undertaken all the enquiries \underline{personally}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.42} Here is a list of adverbs that are used to show the circumstances in which an action takes place:
\begin{example}
    accidentally, alone, artificially, automatically, bodily, collectively, commercially, deliberately, directly, duly, 1st-class, full-time, illegally, independently, indirectly, individually, innocently, instinctively, involuntarily, jointly, legally, logically, mechanically, naturally, officially, openly, overtly, part-time, personally, politically, privately, publicly, regardless, retail, scientifically, secretly, solo, specially, symbolically, wholesale
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.43} Most adverbs of manner are formed from qualitative adjectives, e.g. \textit{stupidly} from \textit{stupid}, and \textit{angrily} from \textit{angry}.

For more information about the forms of adverbs, see paragraph \textbf{6.17}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.44} [i] Instead of using an adverb of manner, you can sometimes use prepositional phrases or noun phrases to give more information about the manner or circumstances of an action.
\begin{example}
    `Come here', he said \underline{in a low voice}.
    
    I know I have to do it \underline{this way}.
\end{example}
In some cases you may have to do this, because there is no adverb.

See paragraph \textbf{6.23}.

\subsection{Adverbs of degree}
\noindent\fbox{6.45} When you want to give more information about the extent of an action or the degree to which an action is performed, you often use an \textbf{adverb of degree}.
\begin{example}
    I enjoyed the course \underline{immensely}.
    
    I had \underline{almost} forgotten about the trip.
    
    A change of 1 word can \underline{radically} after the meaning of a statement.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.46} Here is a list of adverbs of degree:
\begin{example}
    absolutely, adequately, almost, altogether, amazingly, awfully, badly, completely, considerably, dearly, deeply, drastically, dreadfully, enormously, entirely, exceedingly, excessively, extensively, extraordinarily, extremely, fairly, fantastically, fully, greatly, half, hard, hugely, immensely, incredibly, intensely, just, largely, moderately, nearly, noticeably, outright, partly, perfectly, poorly, positively, powerfully, practically, pretty, profoundly, purely, quite, radically, rather, really, reasonably, remarkably, significantly, simply, slightly, somewhat, soundly, strongly, sufficiently, supremely, surprisingly, suspiciously, terribly, totally, tremendously, truly, unbelievably, utterly, very, virtually, well, wonderfully
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.47} Adverbs of degree are often formed from adjectives by adding \textit{-ly}.

Some are formed from qualitative adjectives, e.g. \textit{deeply, hugely}, and \textit{strongly}, and some from classifying adjectives, e.g. \textit{absolutely, perfectly}, and \textit{utterly}.

%
A few adverbs of degree are formed from postdeterminers, e.g. \textit{entirely}.

%
See Chap. 2 for more information about types of adjectives.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.48} You can use adverbs of degree in the usual positions for adverbials.
\begin{example}
    I admired him \underline{greatly}.
    
    I \underline{greatly} enjoyed working with them.
    
    Yoga can \underline{greatly} diminish stress levels.
\end{example}
However, you rarely use an adverb of degree at the beginning of a clause.

E.g., you do not usually say \textit{Greatly I admired him}.

For more information about placing adverbs at the beginning of a clause, see paragraph \textbf{9.70}.

%
A few adverbs of degree are nearly always used in front of the main verb:
\begin{example}
    almost, largely, nearly, really, virtually
\end{example}
E.g., you usually say \textit{He almost go there}, not \textit{He got there almost}.
\begin{example}
    This type of institution has \underline{largely} disappeared now.
    
    He \underline{really} enjoyed talking about flying.
    
    The result \underline{virtually} ensures Scotland's place in the finals.
\end{example}
Some adverbs of degree are almost always used after the main verb:
\begin{example}
    altogether, enormously, hard, outright, somewhat, tremendously, well
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    This was a different level of communication \underline{altogether}.
    
    The proposal was rejected \underline{outright}.
    
    I enjoyed the book \underline{enormously}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.49} A group of adverbs of degree are called \textbf{emphasizing adverbs}.

These are formed from emphasizing adjectives (see paragraph \textbf{2.36}).
\begin{example}
    absolutely, completely, entirely, just, outright, perfectly, positively, purely, quite, really, simply, totally, truly, utterly
\end{example}
Note that the emphasizing adverb \textit{outright} has the form as an adjective, an adverb of manner, and an adverb of degree.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.50} You use an emphasizing adverb e.g. \textit{absolutely, just, quite}, or \textit{simply} to add emphasis to the action described by a verb.

Emphasizing adverbs usually come in front of verbs.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{quite} agree.
    
    I \underline{absolutely} agree.
    
    I \underline{just} know I'm going to be late.
    
    I \underline{simply} adore this flat.
\end{example}
In a verb phrase, the emphasizing adverb comes after the auxiliary or modal and in front of the verb.
\begin{example}
    Someone had \underline{simply} appeared.
    
    I was \underline{absolutely} amazed.
\end{example}
However, \textit{absolutely} is occasionally used after verbs as well.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{agree absolutely} with what Geoffrey has said.
\end{example}
For other uses of emphasizing adverbs, see paragraphs \textbf{9.62--9.63}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.51} You can use some adverbs of degree e.g. \textit{very} and \textit{rather} in front of other adverbs.

When adverbs of degree are used like this they are called \textbf{submodifying adverbs}.

%
They can also be used in front of adjectives; this use is explained in paragraphs \textbf{2.140--2.168}, where lists of submodifying adverbs and their meanings are also given.
\begin{example}
    He prepared his speech \underline{very} carefully.
    
    He was having to work \underline{awfully} hard.
    
    Things changed \underline{really} dramatically.
    
    We get on \underline{extremely} well with our neighbors.
    
    We were able to hear everything \underline{pretty} clearly.
    
    The paper disintegrated \underline{fairly} easily.
    
    He dressed \underline{rather} formally.
    
    Every child reacts \underline{somewhat} differently.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{moderately} and \textit{reasonably} are mainly used in front of adverbs that do not end in \textit{-ly}.
\begin{example}
    He works \underline{reasonably} hard.
\end{example}
A few adverbs of degree can be used as in this way with comparatives: see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{2.157}.
\begin{example}
    This could all be done \underline{very much more quickly}.
    
    I thanked him again, \underline{even more profusely} than before.
    
    I hope you can see \underline{slight more clearly} what is going on.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{still} can also be placed after the comparative.
\begin{example}
    They're doing better in some respects now. Of course they've got to do \underline{better still}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.52} There are some special adverbs of degree.

These include \textit{much}, which is used as an adverb of degree in negative clauses, and in reported questions after \textit{how}.
\begin{example}
    She was difficult as a child and hasn't changed \underline{much}.
    
    These definitions do not help \underline{much}.
    
    Have you told him how \underline{much} you love him?
\end{example}
\textit{Very much} is also used in a similar way.
\begin{example}
    She is charming. We like her \underline{very much}.
\end{example}
The comparative adverbs \textit{better} and \textit{worse} and the superlative adverbs \textit{best} and \textit{worst} are also adverbs of degree.
\begin{example}
    You know him \underline{better} than anyone else.
    
    It is the land itself which suffers \underline{worst}.
\end{example}
\textit{More} and \textit{less} can be used as comparative adverbs of degree.
\begin{example}
    Her tears frightened him \underline{more} than anything that had every happened to him before.
    
    The ground heats up \underline{less} there.
\end{example}
\textit{Most} and \textit{least} can be used as superlative adverbs of degree.
\begin{example}
    She gave me the opportunity to do what I wanted to do \underline{most}.
    
    They staged some of is \underline{least} known operas.
\end{example}
Comparative adverbs and superlative adverbs are explained in the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.30}.

%
The noun phrases \textit{a bit, a great deal, a little}, and \textit{a lot} are also used as adverbs of degree.
\begin{example}
    I don't like this \underline{a bit}.
    
    The situation's changed \underline{a great deal} since then.
\end{example}

\subsection{Adverbs of place}
\noindent\fbox{6.54} Here is a list of words that are used as adverbs to show position.

Note that some adverbs consist of more than 1 word, e.g., \textit{out of doors}.
\begin{example}
    abroad, ahead, aloft, ashore, away, close to, downstairs, downstream, downtown, downwind, eastward, halfway, here, indoors, inland, midway, nearby, next door, northward, offshore, outdoors, out of doors, overhead, overseas, southward, there, underfoot, underground, underwater, upstairs, upstream, uptown, upwind, westward, yonder \emph{(American)}
\end{example}
The common adverbs of place that are used as adverbs and as prepositions are sometimes called \textbf{adverb particles} or \textbf{adverbial particles}.

The following words are used as adverbs to show position, and can also be used as prepositions:
\begin{example}
    aboard, about, above, alongside, behind, below, beneath, beside, beyond, close by, down, in, in between, inside, near, off, opposite, outside, over, round, throughout, underneath, up
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.55} An adverb can be used alone after a verb to show place or direction.
\begin{example}
    The young men hated working \underline{underground}.
    
    The engine droned on as we flew \underline{northward}.
\end{example}
You can also use an adverb showing place or direction when it is clear from the context what place or direction you are referring to.

E.g., you may have mentioned the place earlier, or the adverb may refer to your own location, or to the location of the person or thing being talked about.
\begin{example}
    He moved to Portugal, and it was \underline{there} where he learnt to do the samba.
    
    She walked \underline{away} and my mother stood in the middle of the road, watching.
    
    They spent the autumn of 1855 in Japan. It was \underline{here} that Hilary wrote her 1st novel.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.56} [i] A few adverbs of position are used to show the area in which a situation exists:
\begin{example}
    globally, internationally, locally, nationally, universally, widely, worldwide
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    Everything we used was bought \underline{locally}.
\end{example}
Unlike most other adverbs of position, they cnanot be used after \textit{be} to state the position of something.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.57} [i] A few other adverbs are used to show where 2 or more people or things are in relation to each other: \textit{together, apart, side by side} and \textit{abreast}.
\begin{example}
    All the villagers and visitors would sit \underline{together} round the fire.
    
    A figure stood at the window holding the curtains \underline{apart}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.58} Some adverbs of position are normally followed by another adverbial of position.

This is particularly common when the verb \textit{be} is used as a main verb.
\begin{example}
    Barbara's \underline{down at the cottage}.
    
    Adam was \underline{halfway up the stairs}.
    
    \underline{Out on the quiet surface of the river}, something moved.
    
    She is \underline{up in her own bedroom}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.59} The adverbs \textit{deep, far, high}, and \textit{low}, which indicate distance as well as position, are also usually followed by another adverbial or position, or are modified or qualified in some other way.
\begin{example}
    Many of the eggs remain buried \underline{deep among the sand grains}.
    
    1 plane, flying \underline{very low}, swept back and forth.
\end{example}
\textit{Deep down, far away, high up}, and \textit{low down} are often used instead of the adverbs on their own.
\begin{example}
    The window was \underline{high up}, miles above the rocks.
    
    Sita scraped a shallow cavity \underline{low down} in the wall.
\end{example}
\textit{Far} and \textit{far away} are often qualified by a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{from}.
\begin{example}
    I was standing \underline{far away from the ball}.
    
    We lived \underline{far from the nearest village}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.60} Some adverbs have comparative and superlative forms.

The superlative form is not used to show position, but to specify which of several things you are talking about.

%
\textit{Deeper, further} (or \textit{farther}), \textit{higher}, and \textit{lower} are usually followed by a prepositional phrase showing position.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Further along the beach}, a thin trickle of smoke was climbing into the sky.
    
    The beans are a bit \underline{higher on the stalk} this year.
\end{example}
\textit{Nearer} can be used as an adverb as well as a preposition (see paragraph \textbf{6.88}).

%
\textit{Closer} can only be used as an adverb.
\begin{example}
    The hills were \underline{nearer} now.
    
    Thousands of tourists stood watching or milled around trying to get \underline{closer}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.61} There are 4 indefinite adverbs of position: \textit{anywhere, everywhere, nowhere}, and \textit{somewhere}.

They are used to talk about a position that is not definite or that is very general.
\begin{example}
    I dropped my cigar \underline{somewhere} round here.
    
    I thought I'd seen you \underline{somewhere}.
    
    There were bicycles \underline{everywhere}.
    
    No-one can find Howard or Barbara \underline{anywhere}.
\end{example}
\textit{Nowhere} makes a clause negative.
\begin{example}
    There was \underline{nowhere} to hide.
\end{example}
If \textit{nowhere} is at the beginning of a clause, the subject of the verb must be placed after an auxiliary or a form of \textit{be} or \textit{have}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Nowhere have I seen} any serious mention of this.
    
    \underline{Nowhere are they} overwhelmingly numerous.
\end{example}
%
[AE] American English has informal variants for all 4 of these adverbs in which the word \textit{place} replaces \textit{-where}.

These can be written as 1 or 2 words.
\begin{example}
    Haven't you got \underline{some place} to go?
    
    Video-conferencing can connect anyone, anytime, \underline{anyplace}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.62} There are several structures you can use with indefinite place adverbs in order to give more information.

You can use:
\begin{itemize}
    \item an adverb of place:
    \begin{example}
        I would like to work \underline{somewhere abroad}.
        
        We're certainly \underline{nowhere near}.
    \end{example}
    \item an adjective:
    \begin{example}
        We could go to Majorca if you want \underline{somewhere lively}.
        
        Are you going \underline{somewhere nice}?
    \end{example}
    \item a prepositional phrase:
    \begin{example}
        The waiter wasn't \underline{anywhere in sight}.
        
        In 1917, Kollontai was the only woman in any government \underline{anywhere in the world}.
    \end{example}
    \item or a \textit{to}-infinitive clause:
    \begin{example}
        We mentioned that we were looking for \underline{somewhere to live}.
        
        I wanted to have \underline{somewhere to put it}.
    \end{example}
    You can also use a relative clause.
    
    Note that the relative pronoun is usually omitted.
    \begin{example}
        Was there \underline{anywhere you wanted to go}?
        
        \underline{Everywhere I went}, people were angry or suspicious.
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.63} \textit{Else} is used after the indefinite place adverb to indicate a different or additional place.
\begin{example}
    We could hold the meeting \underline{somewhere else}.
    
    More people die in bed than \underline{anywhere else}.
\end{example}
\textit{Elsewhere} can be used instead of \textit{somewhere else}.
\begin{example}
    Gwen sat next to the window. The other girls had found seats \underline{elsewhere}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.64} \textit{Everywhere} and \textit{anywhere} can also be used as the subjects of verbs, especially \textit{be}.
\begin{example}
    Sometimes I feel that \underline{anywhere}, just \underline{anywhere}, would be better than this.
    
    I looked around for a shop, but \underline{everywhere} was closed.
\end{example}

\subsection{Destinations \& directions}
\noindent\fbox{6.65} Adverbs can be used to indicate destinations and targets.
\begin{example}
    I have expected you, she said, inviting him \underline{inside}.
    
    No birds or animals came \underline{near}.
\end{example}
The following adverbs are used to indicate destinations or targets:
\begin{example}
    aboard, abroad, ashore, close, downstairs, downtown, heavenward, home, homeward, in, indoors, inland, inside, inward, inwards, near, next door, outdoors, out of doors, outside, overseas, skyward, there, underground, upstairs, uptown
\end{example}
The comparative forms \textit{nearer} and \textit{closer} are more commonly used than \textit{near} or \textit{close}.
\begin{example}
    Come \underline{nearer}.
\end{example}
\textit{Deep, far, high}, and \textit{low} are also used as adverbs showing a destination or target but only when they are modified in some other way.
\begin{example}
    The dancers sprang \underline{high into the air} brandishing their spears.
\end{example}
The comparative forms \textit{deeper, further} (or \textit{farther}), \textit{higher}, and \textit{lower} are also used, and so is the superlative form \textit{furthest} (or \textit{farthest}).

These do not have to be modified in any way.
\begin{example}
    We left the waterfall and climbed \underline{higher}.
    
    People have to trek \underline{further and further}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.66} Adverbs can be used to show direction in relation to the particular position of the person or thing you are talking about.

For more information on \textbf{phrasal verbs}, see paragraphs \textbf{3.83--3.116}.
\begin{example}
    Go \underline{north} from Leicester Square up Wardour Street.
    
    Don't look \underline{down}.
    
    $\ldots$  the part of the engine that was spinning \underline{around}.
    
    Mrs James gave a little cry and hurried \underline{on}.
    
    They grabbed him and pulled him \underline{backwards}.
    
    He turned \underline{left} and began strolling slowly down the street.
\end{example}
They can also show the direction in which someone or something is facing in relation to the front of the place they are in.
\begin{example}
    The seats face \underline{forward}.
\end{example}
The following adverbs are used to show direction of this sort:
\begin{example}
    ahead, along, back, backward, backwards, forward, forwards, left, on, onward, right, sideways
    
    anti-clockwise, around, clockwise, counterclockwise, \emph{(American)} down, downward, downwards, east, eastward, eastwards, north, northward, northwards, north-east, north-west, south, southward, southwards, south-east, south-west, round, up, upward, upwards, west, westward, westwards
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.67} The adverbs \textit{round, about}, and \textit{around} show movement in several directions within a place.
\begin{example}
    Stop rushing \underline{about}!
    
    They won't want anyone else trampling \underline{around}.
\end{example}
The following adverbial expressions are used to talk about repeated movement in different directions:
\begin{example}
    back and forth, backwards and forwards, from side to side, in and out, round and round, to and fro, up and down
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    At other times she would pace \underline{up and down} outside the trailer.
    
    Burke was walking \underline{back and forth} as he spoke.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.68} The following adverbs are used to talk about movement away from someone or something:
\begin{example}
    aside, away, off, out, outward
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    The farmer just laughed and rode \underline{away}.
    
    It took just 1 tug to pull them \underline{out}.
\end{example}
The adverb \textit{apart} indicates that 2 or more things move away from each other.
\begin{example}
    I rushed in and tried to pull the dogs \underline{apart}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.69} The following adverbs are used to talk about movement along a road, path, or line:
\begin{example}
    alongside, beside, downhill, downstream, uphill, upstream
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    Going \underline{downhill} was easy.
    
    It wasn't the moving that kept me warm; it was the effort of pushing Daisy \underline{uphill}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.70} The following adverbs are used to talk about movement across or past something:
\begin{example}
    across, by, over, overhead, past, round, through
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    There's an aircraft coming \underline{over}.
    
    `Where are you going?' demanded Miss Craig as Florrie rushed \underline{by}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.71} The indefinite place adverbs are used to talk about a destination or direction when you want to be more general or vague.
\begin{example}
    He went off \underline{somewhere} for a shooting weekend.
    
    Dust blew \underline{everywhere}, swirling over dry caked mountains.
    
    There was hardly \underline{anywhere} to go.
    
    Can't you play \underline{elsewhere}?
\end{example}
\textit{Nowhere} is mainly used metaphorically, to indicate lack of progress.
\begin{example}
    They were getting \underline{nowhere} and had other things to do.
\end{example}
See paragraph \textbf{6.61} for more information on these indefinite adverbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.72} Like prepositional phrases, adverbs can also be placed after nouns.
\begin{example}
    They watched him from the terrace \underline{above}.
    
    The man \underline{opposite} got up.
    
    People \underline{everywhere} are becoming aware of the problem.
    
    We took the road \underline{south}.
\end{example}

\section{Prepositions}
\noindent\fbox{6.73} This section explains how \textbf{prepositional phrases} are used to show the place where an action occurs, the place where someone or something is, the place they are going to or coming from, or the direction they are moving in.

%
A \textbf{prepositional phrase} consists of a \textbf{preposition} and its \textbf{object}, which is nearly always a noun.

%
The most basic use of mos prepositions is to indicate position and direction.
\begin{example}
    He fumbled \underline{in his pocket}.
    
    \underline{On your left} is the river.
    
    Why did he not drive \underline{to Valence}?
    
    The voice was coming \underline{from my apartment}.
    
    I ran inside and bounded \underline{up the stairs}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.74} A \textbf{preposition} is a word that allows you to say more about a thing or an action, because you can choose any appropriate noun after it as its object.

Most prepositions are single words, although there are some that consist of more than 1 word, e.g. \textit{out of} and \textit{in between}.

%
Here is a list of common 1-word prepositions that are used to talk about place or destination:
\begin{example}
    about, above, across, along, alongside, among, around, at, before, behind, below, beneath, beside, between, beyond, by, down, from, in, inside, into, near, off, on, opposite, outside, over, past, round, through, throughout, to, toward \emph{(Am)}, towards, under, underneath, up, within
\end{example}
%
[AE] Note that \textit{toward} and \textit{towards} are both used in American English, with no difference in meaning.

%
Here is a list of prepositions that consist of more than 1 word and that are used to talk about place or destination:
\begin{example}
    across from, ahead of, all over, away from, close by/to, in between, in front of, near to, next to, on top of, out of
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.75} Many prepositions can also be adverbs; i.e., they can be used without an object.

See paragraph \textbf{6.54} for a list of these.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.76} [!] Because English has a large number of prepositions, some of them, e.g. \textit{beside, by, near}, and \textit{next to}, are very close in meaning.

Other prepositions, e.g. \textit{at} and \textit{in}, have several different meanings.

The meaning and usage of prepositions should be checked where possible in a dictionary.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.77} Prepositions have an \textbf{object}, which comes after the preposition.
\begin{example}
    The switch is by \underline{the door}.
    
    Look behind \underline{you}, Willie!
\end{example}
Note that if a personal pronoun is used as the object of a preposition, it must be the object pronoun: \textit{me, you, him, her, it, us, them}.

%
Prepositions also combine with complex noun phrases to describes places in some detail.

See paragraph \textbf{2.280} for information on the use of \textit{of} in noun phrases.
\begin{example}
    I stood alone \underline{in the middle of the yard}.
    
    He was sitting \underline{towards the end of the room}.
    
    He went \underline{to the back of the store}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Position of prepositional phrases}
\noindent\fbox{6.78} Prepositional phrases are most commonly used after verbs.

They are used after verbs that indicate position in order to specify where something is.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{lives} in Newcastle.
    
    An old piano \underline{stood} in the corner of the room.
    
    You ought to \underline{stay} out of the sun.
\end{example}
The following verbs are often used to show position:
\begin{example}
    be, belong, hang, lie, live, remain, sit, be situated, stand, stay
\end{example}

\subsection{After verbs indicating movement}
\noindent\fbox{6.79} Prepositional phrases are used after verbs indicating movement to specify the direction of movement.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{went} into the kitchen and began to make the dinner.
    
    Mrs Kaul \underline{was leading} him to his seat.
    
    The others \underline{burst} from their tents.
    
    The storm \underline{had uprooted} trees from the ground.
    
    He \underline{took} her to Edinburgh.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.80} Prepositional phrases are used after verbs indicating activities to specify where an activity takes place.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ children \underline{playing} in the street.
    
    The meeting \underline{was held} at a community center in Logan Heights.
    
    He \underline{was practicing} high jumps in the garden.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.81} Prepositional phrases usually come at the end of the clause, after the verb, or after the object of the verb if there is one.
\begin{example}
    We landed \underline{at a small airport}.
    
    We put the children's toys \underline{in a big box}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.82} If you want to focus on the prepositional phrase for emphasis or contrast, it can be placed at the beginning of the clause.

This ordering is mainly used in descriptive writing or reports.
\begin{example}
    \underline{In the garden} everything was peaceful.
    
    \underline{At the top of the tree} was a brown cat.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.83} If you put a prepositional phrase that refers to the position of something at the beginning of the clause when you are using a verb with no object, the normal word order after it is often changed, and the verb is placed before the subject.
\begin{example}
    On the ceiling \underline{hung dustpans and brushes}.
    
    Inside the box \underline{lie the group's US mining assets}.
    
    Beyond them \underline{lay the fields}.
\end{example}
If you are using \textit{be} as a main verb, the verb always comes before the subjects; so, e.g., you cannot say \textit{Under her chin a colossal brooch was}.
\begin{example}
    Under her chin \underline{was a colossal brooch}.
    
    Next to it \underline{is a different sign} which says simply Beware.
    
    Alongside him \underline{will be Mr Mitchell Fromstein}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Showing position}
\noindent\fbox{6.84} The prepositional phrases in the following examples show the place where an action occurs, or the place where someone or something is.
\begin{example}
    The children shouted, waving leafy branches \underline{above their heads}.
    
    The whole play takes place \underline{at a beach club}.
    
    2 minutes later we were safely \underline{inside the taxi}.
    
    He stood \underline{near the door}.
    
    She kept his picture \underline{on her bedside table}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.85} The following prepositions are used to show position:
\begin{example}
    aboard, about, above, across, against, ahead of, all over, along, alongside, amidst, among, around, astride, at, away from, before, behind, below, beneath, beside, between, beyond, by, close by/to, down, in, in between, in front of, inside, near, near to, next to, off, on, on top of, opposite, out of, outside, past, through, under, underneath, up, upon, with, within
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.86} [i] Some prepositions are only used with a restricted group of nouns.

%
E.g., \textit{aboard} is used with a noun referring to a form of transport, e.g. \textit{ship, plane, train}, or \textit{bus}, or with the name of a particular ship, the flight number for a particular plane journey, and so on.
\begin{example}
    There's something terribly wrong \underline{aboard this ship}, Dr Marlowe.
    
    More than 1500 people died \underline{aboard the Titanic}.
    
    $\ldots$ getting \underline{aboard that flight to Rome}.
    
    He climbed \underline{aboard a truck}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of nouns that you use with \textit{aboard} to indicate position:
\begin{example}
    aircraft carrier, boat, bus, coach, ferry, jet, plane, rocket, ship, sled \emph{(American)}, sledge, space shuttle, train, trawler, truck, wagon, yacht
\end{example}
\textit{Astride} is mainly used to say that a person has 1 leg on each side of something, usually sitting on it or riding it.
\begin{example}
    He whipped out a chair and sat \underline{astride it}.
    
    He spotted a man sitting \underline{astride a horse}.
\end{example}
When \textit{before} is used to show position, the object is usually a person or group of people.
\begin{example}
    Leading representatives were interviewed \underline{before a live television audience}.
    
    He appeared \underline{before a disciplinary committee}.
\end{example}
\textit{All over} usually has a large or indefinite area as its object.
\begin{example}
    Through the site, thousands of people \underline{all over the world} are being reunited with old friends.
    
    There were pieces of ship \underline{all over the place}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.87} [i] Some prepositions have several meanings.

E.g., \textit{on} can be used to say that someone or something is resting on a horizontal surface or is attached to something, or that someone's place of work is an area e.g. a farm or a building site.
\begin{example}
    The phone was \underline{on} the floor in the hallway.
    
    I lowered myself down \underline{on} a rope.
    
    My father worked \underline{on} a farm.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.88} \textit{Near, near to}, and \textit{close to} have comparative forms that can also be used as prepositions.
\begin{example}
    We're moving \underline{nearer my parents}.
    
    Venus is much \underline{nearer to the Sun} than the Earth.
    
    The judge's bench was \underline{closer to me} than Ruchell's chair.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.89} If you want to say more exactly which part of the other thing an object is nearest to, or exactly which part of an area or room it is in, you can use 1 of the following prepositions: \textit{at, by, in, near, on, round}.

\textit{To} and \textit{towards}, usually used to indicate direction, are used to express position in a more approximate way.

%
The objects of the prepositions are nouns referring to parts of an object or place, e.g. \textit{top, bottom}, and \textit{edge}.

Here is a list of words that are used to talk about parts of an object or place:
\begin{example}
    back, bottom, edge, end, front, left, middle, right, side, top
    
    east, north, north-east, north-west, south, south-east, south-west, west
    
    bankside, bedside, dockside, graveside, hillside, kerbside, lakeside, mountainside, poolside, quayside, ringside, roadside, seaside, waterside
\end{example}
%
[AE] Note that the compound direction prepositions (\textit{northeast, southwest}, etc.) may be spelled either with our without a hyphen in British English.

They are hardly ever spelled with a hyphen in American English.

%
When the place that you referring to is obvious or has been stated earlier, you use the nouns in the singular with the determiner \textit{the}.
\begin{example}
    I ran inside and bounded up the stairs. Wendy was standing \underline{at the top}.
    
    He was sitting \underline{towards the rear}.
    
    \underline{To the north} are the main gardens.
    
    We found him sitting \underline{by the fireside}.
\end{example}
Other determiners, e.g. \textit{this} and \textit{each}, are used with nouns e.g. \textit{side, end}, and \textit{edge}, because an object or place may have several sides, ends, or edges.
\begin{example}
    Loosen the 2 screws \underline{at each end} of the fuse.
    
    Standing \underline{on either side} of him where 2 younger men.
\end{example}
If the person or thing has been mentioned or is obvious, a possessive determiner can be used.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a doll that turns brown in the sun, except for \underline{under its swimsuit}.
    
    There was a gate \underline{on our left}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.90} Note that 2- or 3-word prepositions that include the word \textit{of} are more specific because \textit{of} can be followed by any noun.
\begin{example}
    She turned and rushed \underline{out of the room}.
    
    There was a man standing \underline{in front of me}.
    
    My sister started piling the books \underline{on top of each other}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.91} The place where an action occurs, or where someone or something is, can also be shown by stating its distance from another object or place.

%
You mention the actual distance before a prepositional phrase with \textit{from} or \textit{away from}.
\begin{example}
    Here he sat on the terrace \underline{a few feet from the roaring traffic}.
    
    The ball swerved \underline{2 feet away from her}.
\end{example}
Distance is also expressed in terms of the time taken to travel it.
\begin{example}
    My house is only \underline{20 minutes from where I work}.
    
    They lived only \underline{2 or 3 days away from Juffure}.
\end{example}
The method of traveling can be stated to be even more precise.
\begin{example}
    It is less than \underline{an hour's drive from here}.
    
    It's about \underline{5 minutes' walk from the bus stop}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.92} To show both where something is and how far from another object or place it is, the distance is stated before the following prepositions:
\begin{example}
    above, along, behind, below, beneath, beyond, down, inside, outside, past, under, up
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    The volcano is only \underline{a few hundred meters below sea level}.
\end{example}
The distance can also be stated before prepositional phrases including \textit{left} and \textit{right} or points of compass e.g. \textit{north} and \textit{south-east}.
\begin{example}
    We lived \underline{40 miles to the east of Ottawa}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Showing direction}
\noindent\fbox{6.93} The prepositional phrases in the following examples show the place that someone or something is going to, or the place that they are moving towards.
\begin{example}
    I'm going with her \underline{to Australia}.
    
    They jumped \underline{into the water}.
    
    He saw his mother running \underline{towards him}.
    
    He screwed the lid tightly \underline{onto the top of the jar}.
    
    She stuck her knitting needles \underline{into a ball of wool}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.94} The following prepositions are used to show destinations and targets:
\begin{example}
    aboard, all over, along, alongside, around, at, away from, beside, down, from, inside, into, near, off, onto, out of, round, to, toward \emph{(American)}, towards, up
\end{example}
Note that \textit{onto} is sometimes written as 2 words.
\begin{example}
    The bird hopped up \underline{on to} a higher branch.
\end{example}
In American English and some varieties of British English, \textit{out} is used as a preposition without \textit{of} to show direction.
\begin{example}
    He walked \underline{out the door} for the last time.
\end{example}
The prepositional phrases \textit{to the left} and \textit{to the right} are also used to indicate direction, from your own viewpoint or that of someone else.

See paragraph \textbf{6.96}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.95} There are some restrictions in the choice of preposition.

%
\textit{At} is not usually used to show the place that the subject of the verb is moving to or towards.

It is used to show what someone is looking at, or what they cause an object to move towards.
\begin{example}
    They were staring \underline{at a garage roof}.
    
    Supporters threw petals \underline{at his car}.
\end{example}
\textit{After} is used to show that someone or something is following another moving person or thing, or is moving in the same direction but behind them.
\begin{example}
    He hurried \underline{after his men}.
    
    $\ldots$ dragging the sacks \underline{after us} along the ground.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.96} You use the prepositional phrases \textit{to the left} and \textit{to the right} to say which direction someone or something is moving in relation to the direction they are facing.
\begin{example}
    They turned \underline{to the left} and drove away.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.97} The prepositions \textit{about, round, around}, and \textit{all over} are used to show movement in several directions within a place.
\begin{example}
    I wandered \underline{round the garden}.
    
    She jumped \underline{around the room} in front of the children as she acted out her story.
    
    The boys began climbing \underline{all over the ship}.
\end{example}
[AE] \textit{Round} is not used as a preposition in American English; \textit{around} is always used instead.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.98} Prepositional phrases show the place or object that is the starting point of a movement.

%
The following prepositions are used: \textit{away from, from, off}, and \textit{out of}.
\begin{example}
    The coffee was sent up by the caterer \underline{from the kitchens} below.
    
    She turned and rushed \underline{out of the room}.
    
    He took his hand \underline{off her arm}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.99} \textit{From} is also used before another preposition or before some adverbs to talk about the starting point of a movement.
\begin{example}
    I had taken his drinking bowl \underline{from beneath the kitchen table}.
    
    $\ldots$ good imported \underline{from abroad}.
    
    Thomas had stopped bringing his lunch \underline{from home}.
\end{example}
\textit{From} is used before the following adverbs:
\begin{example}
    above, abroad, anywhere, behind, below, beneath, downstairs, elsewhere, everywhere, here, home, inside, next door, nowhere, outside, overseas, somewhere, there, underneath, upstairs
\end{example}

\subsection{Prepositional phrases after nouns}
\noindent\fbox{6.100} As well as being used after verbs, prepositional phrases can be used after nouns to give information about place.
\begin{example}
    The muscles \underline{below Peter's knees} were beginning to ache a little.
    
    The chestnut trees \underline{in the back garden} were a blazing orange.
    
    They stood and watched the boats \underline{on the river}.
    
    $\ldots$ the clock \underline{in her bedroom}.
    
    $\ldots$ the low wall \underline{round the garden}.
    
    $\ldots$ the black shapeless masses \underline{to the left and right of the road}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.101} Prepositional phrases can be added after roads, routes, and so on, to specify them by indicating their destination or direction.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ \underline{the main road from Paris to Marseilles}.
    
    $\ldots$ \underline{the road between the camp and the hospital}.
    
    $\ldots$ \underline{the road through the canyon}.
\end{example}
Similarly, doors, entrances, and so on can be specified by adding prepositional phrases indicating where you get to by going through them.
\begin{example}
    He opened \underline{the door to his room}.
    
    $\ldots$ at \underline{the entrance to the library}.
\end{example}
Prepositional phrases are also used after nouns to say where someone comes from.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a veterinary officer \underline{from Singapore}.
    
    $\ldots$ an engineer \underline{from Hertfordshire}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Other uses of prepositional phrases}
\noindent\fbox{6.102} Prepositions are often used to talk about things other than place as well, e.g. to talk about a time, a way of doing something, or a feeling or quality.

The following paragraphs from \textbf{6.103--6.110} describe these uses briefly, and give cross references to fuller explanations elsewhere.

%
The following prepositions are only or mainly used to indicate things other than place:
\begin{example}
    after, as, despite, during, except, for, like, of, since, until
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.103} Although the main use of prepositional phrases is to talk about position or direction, they are also used to refer to time.
\begin{example}
    I'll se you \underline{on Monday}.
    
    They are expecting to announce the sale \underline{within the next few days}.
\end{example}
The use of prepositions to talk about time is explained in paragraphs \textbf{4.100--4.108}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.104} Prepositional phrases are also used to say something more about the way in which an action was performed, or the way in which it should be done.
\begin{example}
    `Oh yes,' Etta sneered \underline{in an offensive way}.
    
    A bird can change direction \underline{by dipping 1 wing and lifting the other}.
    
    He brushed back his hair \underline{with his hand}.
\end{example}
Prepositional phrases e.g. \textit{on foot} or \textit{by bus} can be used to talk about a method of traveling.
\begin{example}
    I usually go to work \underline{on foot}.
    
    I traveled home \underline{by bus}.
\end{example}
The use of adverbs to talk about the way things are done is explained in the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{6.36}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.105} You can also use prepositional phrases to give more information about the feelings of the person performing the action.
\begin{example}
    Fanny saw \underline{with amazement} that the letter was addressed to herself.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.106} You can use the preposition \textit{like} to show that someone or something behaves in a similar way to someone or something else.
\begin{example}
    She treated me \underline{like a servant}.
    
    She shuffled \underline{like an old lady}.
\end{example}
There is more information about comparison in general in the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{2.103}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.107} You can also use \textit{like} and \textit{as} to say that someone or something is treated in a similar way to someone or something else.

The noun phrase after \textit{like} or \textit{as} describes the person or thing affected by the action, not the person or thing doing the action.
\begin{example}
    My parents dressed me \underline{like a little doll}.
    
    Their parents continue to treat them \underline{as children}.
    
    She treated her \underline{more like a daughter than a companion}.
\end{example}
You can also use expressions e.g. \textit{like this} or \textit{like that} to refer to a particular manner of doing something.
\begin{example}
    If you're going to behave \underline{like this}, the best thing you can do is to go back to bed.
    
    How dare you speak to me \underline{like that}?
\end{example}
The use of \textit{like} and \textit{as} in subordinate clauses is explained in paragraphs \textbf{8.78--8.80}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.108} You can say that 1 way of doing something has as much of a quality as another way of doing something, by using \textit{as} followed by an adverb followed by another \textit{as}.

The 2nd \textit{as} is followed by a noun phrase, a pronoun, an adverbial, or a clause.
\begin{example}
    The company has not grown \underline{as quickly as many of its rivals}.
    
    She wanted someone to talk to \underline{as badly as I did}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.109} You use prepositional phrases to say something about the circumstances of an action.
\begin{example}
    `No,' she said \underline{with a defiant look}.
    
    $\ldots$ struggling to establish democracy \underline{under adverse conditions}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.110} Prepositional phrases can also be used to say something about the reason for an action, or the cause or purpose of it.
\begin{example}
    In 1923, the Prime Minister resigned \underline{because of ill health}.
    
    He was dying \underline{of pneumonia}.
\end{example}
\textit{As} is used to show the function or purpose of something.
\begin{example}
    He worked \underline{as a truck driver}.
    
    During the war they used the theater \underline{as a warehouse}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Prepositions used with verbs}
\noindent\fbox{6.111} Some verbs always have a prepositional phrase after them in particular meanings.

They are called \textbf{phrasal verbs}, and information about them can be found in paragraphs \textbf{3.83--3.116}.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{sailed through} her exams.
    
    What are you \underline{getting at}?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.112} Some verbs can have a prepositional phrase instead of a direct object.

For more information on these verbs, see paragraph \textbf{3.10}.
\begin{example}
    The Polish Army fought the Germans for nearly 5 weeks.
    
    She was fighting \underline{against history}.
    
    We climbed the mountain.
    
    I climbed \underline{up the tree}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.113} A prepositional phrase is used as an indirect object when the indirect object comes after the direct object.

%
For information on verbs with 2 objects, see paragraphs \textbf{3.73--3.82}.

%
If the action described by the verb involves the transfer of something from 1 person or thing to another, the preposition \textit{to} is used.
\begin{example}
    I passed the letter \underline{to my husband}.
    
    The recovered animals will be given \underline{to zoos}.
\end{example}
If the action involves a person doing something for the benefit of another person, the preposition \textit{for} is used.
\begin{example}
    She left a note \underline{for her} on the table.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.114} Some \textbf{reciprocal verbs} require a prepositional phrase when a 2nd noun phrase is mentioned.

%
For information on reciprocal verbs, see paragraphs \textbf{3.68--3.72}.
\begin{example}
    Our return coincided \underline{with the arrival of bad weather}.
    
    She has refused to cooperate \underline{with investigators}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.115} Prepositional phrases are used after verbs in the \textbf{passive}.
\begin{example}
    90 men were cut off \underline{by storms}.
    
    Moisture is drawn out \underline{with salt}.
\end{example}
The use of prepositional phrases after passive verbs is explained in paragraphs \textbf{9.14--9.16}.

%
\noindent\fbox{6.116} When verbs are followed by prepositional phrases and adverbs, a long prepositional phrase is usually placed after the adverb.
\begin{example}
    He listened calmly \underline{to the report of his aides}.
\end{example}
A short prepositional phrase can come before or after the adverb.
\begin{example}
    The woman shouted \underline{at me} savagely.
    
    Miss Burns looked calmly \underline{at Marianne}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Prepositional phrases after nouns \& adjectives}
\noindent\fbox{6.117} Prepositional phrases are sometimes used after nouns and adjectives to describe the subject or object of a clause rather than the manner of an action or situation.

See the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{2.275} for more information.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a girl \underline{in a dark grey dress}.
    
    $\ldots$ a man \underline{with a quick temper}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.118} Particular prepositions are used after some nouns and adjectives when you are adding information.

See paragraphs \textbf{2.45--2.50} and \textbf{2.287--2.290}.
\begin{example}
    My respect \underline{for her} is absolutely enormous.
    
    Women's tennis puts an emphasis \underline{on technique}, not strength.
    
    He is responsible \underline{for pursuing the claim}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.119} A prepositional phrase with \textit{than} often shows the person or thing that is the basis of a comparison.
\begin{example}
    He was smarter \underline{than you}.
    
    She was more refined \underline{than her husband}.
\end{example}
For more information on \textbf{comparisons}, see the section beginning at paragraph \textbf{2.103}.

%
The preposition \textit{like} is used to show that someone or something is similar to someone or something else, without comparing any specific quality.
\begin{example}
    The British forces are \underline{like permanent tourists}.
    
    We need many more people \underline{like these}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.120} \textit{Of} is used in prepositional phrases after any noun to indicate various relationships between 1 noun phrase and another, especially belonging, possession, and connection.

It can be used to state what something is, what it contains, what it is made from, or how much of it there is.
\begin{example}
    He was a member \underline{of the golf club}.
    
    She's a friend \underline{of Stephen's}.
    
    $\ldots$ the Mayor \underline{of Moscow}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Extended meanings of prepositions}
\noindent\fbox{6.121} The uses of prepositional phrases to express time and manner are really extended or metaphorical uses that cover a wide range of prepositions and are part of a metaphor that affects many other aspects of language as well.

E.g., when you talk about \textit{approaching a point in time, a short stretch of time}, and son on, you are using words that refer to space to talk about time.

%
However, there are also extended meanings that apply only to small groups of prepositions, or sometimes only to individual prepositions.

%
E.g., \textit{in} basically indicates position inside a container.
\begin{example}
    What's that \underline{in your bag}?
    
    It will end up \underline{in the dustbin}.
\end{example}
However, it is often used with reference to areas rather than containers.
\begin{example}
    Emma sat \underline{in an armchair} with her legs crossed.
    
    Then we were told what had happened \underline{in Sheffield}.
\end{example}
\textit{In} is also used to talk about relative position.
\begin{example}
    We had to do something \underline{in the center} of the town to attract visitors.
\end{example}
However, \textit{in} is also used in ways that extend its meaning further way from physical position.

E.g., it can be used to say that someone is involved in a particular situation, group, or activity.
\begin{example}
    They were \underline{in no danger}.
    
    The child was \underline{in trouble} with the police.
    
    This government won't be \underline{in power} for ever.
    
    Mr Matthews has remained \underline{in office} but the island has no Parliament.
\end{example}
It can show inclusion in a more abstract way.
\begin{example}
    Some of her early Hollywood experiences were used \underline{in her 1923 film}, Mary of the Movies.
    
    \underline{In any book}, there is a moral purpose.
\end{example}
It can also indicate that something has reached a particular stage, or appears in a particular way.
\begin{example}
    The 1st primroses are \underline{in flower}.
    
    Her hair was \underline{in pigtails} over either shoulder.
\end{example}
A few other prepositions with a basic meaning relating to containers are used in similar ways: e.g. \textit{within, into, out of}/
\begin{example}
    Anything \underline{within reason} should be considered.
    
    When we get those men \underline{into the police force}, they are going to be real heros.
    
    Heroines who were considered attractive by earlier generations now seem hopelessly \underline{out of touch}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Other ways of giving information about place}
\noindent\fbox{6.122} Some verbs of position and movement are followed by noun phrases referring to places.

These are described in paragraph \textbf{3.21}.
\begin{example}
    Peel approached \underline{the building}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.123} Instead of using a noun phrase to refer to a place, you can use the name of the place.
\begin{example}
    This great block of land became \underline{Antarctica}.
    
    $\ldots$ an island roughly the size of \underline{Martha's Vineyard}.
    
    Her work is on show at the \underline{National Museum of Film \& Photography} in \underline{Bradford}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.124} Most place names are singular nouns, although some look like plural nouns, e.g. \textit{The Netherlands}.
