Some place names, e.g. those referring to groups of islands or mountains, are plural nouns.

Verbs used with place names follow the normal rules, so a singular verb form is used with a singular noun and a plural verb form with a plural noun.
\begin{example}
    Milan \underline{is} a very interesting city.
    
    The Andes \underline{split} the country down the middle.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.125} The name of a place can be used to talk about the people who live there.

If the place name is a singular noun, a singular verb form is still used, even though the noun is being used to refer to a plural concept.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Europe} was sick of war.
\end{example}
The name of a country or its capital city is often used to talk about the government of that country.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Britain} and \underline{France} jointly suggested a plan.
    
    \underline{Washington} put a great deal of pressure on \underline{Tokyo}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.126} Place names are also used to talk about a well-known historical or recent event that occurred there, e.g. a battle, a disaster, an international sports competition, a scandal, or an important political meeting.
\begin{example}
    After \underline{Waterloo}, trade and industry surged again.
    
    What was the effect of \underline{Chernobyl} on British agriculture?
    
    $\ldots$ the chain of events that led to \underline{Watergate}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{6.127} Many place names can be used as modifiers, to show where things come from or are characteristic of, as well as where things are.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ a \underline{London} hotel.
    
    $\ldots$ the \underline{New Zealand} rugby team.
\end{example}
If a place name begins with \textit{the}, you omit it when you use the name as a modifier.
\begin{example}
    $\ldots$ \underline{Arctic} explorers.
    
    She has a \underline{Midlands} accent.
\end{example}
Note that the names of continents and of many countries cannot be used as modifiers.

Instead, you use classifying adjectives e.g. \textit{African} and \textit{Italian}.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------%

\chapter{Reporting what people say or think}

\noindent\fbox{7.1} This chapter explains the different ways of reporting what people say or thing.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.2} 1 way of reporting what someone has said is to repeat their actual words.
\begin{example}
    `I don't know much about music,' Judy said.
\end{example}
Repeating someone's actual words like this is called \textbf{direct speech}.

%
Instead of repeating Judy's words, the writer could say, \textit{Judy said that she didn't know much about music}.

This is called \textbf{reported speech}.

Some grammars called this \textbf{indirect speech}.

%
Directed speech and reported speech both consist of 2 clauses.

The main clause is called a \textbf{reporting clause}.

The other clause shows what someone said or though.

%
In \textbf{direct speech}, this other clause is called the \textbf{quote}.
\begin{example}
    `\underline{Have you met him}?' I asked.
    
    `\underline{I'll see you tomorrow},' said Tom.
\end{example}
In \textbf{reported speech}, the other clause is called the \textbf{reported clause}.
\begin{example}
    He mentioned \underline{that he had a brother living in London}.
    
    He asked \underline{if you would be able to call and see him}.
    
    He promised \underline{to give me the money}.
\end{example}
Note that the reported clause can begin with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.3} In ordinary conversation, we use \textbf{reported speech} much more often than \textbf{direct speech}.

This is because we usually do not know, or cannot remember, the exact words that someone has said.

Direct speech is mainly used in written stories.

%
When we report people's thoughts, we almost always use reported speech, because thoughts do not usually exist in the form of words, so we cannot quote them exactly.

Reported speech can be used to report almost any kind of thought.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.4} Paragraphs \textbf{7.5--7.15} explain verbs used in \textbf{reporting clauses}.

Paragraphs \textbf{7.16--7.26} explain \textbf{direct speech}.

Paragraphs \textbf{7.27--7.71} explain \textbf{reported speech}.

Paragraphs \textbf{7.72--7.81} explain how to refer to the speaker and hearer in direct speech or reported speech.

Paragraphs \textbf{7.82--7.85} explain other ways of indicating what someone says or talks about.

\section{Showing that you are reporting: using reporting verbs}
\noindent\fbox{7.5} You show that you are quoting or reporting what someone has said or thought by using a \textbf{reporting verb}.

Every reporting clause contains a reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    `I don't see what you are getting at,' Jeremy \underline{said}.
    
    He looked old, Harold \underline{thought}, and sick.
    
    They \underline{were complaining} that Canton was hot and noisy.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.6} You use \textit{say} when you are simply reporting what someone said and do not want to add any more information about what you are reporting.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{said} that she didn't want to know.
\end{example}
You use \textit{ask} when you are a reporting a question.
\begin{example}
    `How's it all going?' Derek \underline{asked}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.7} Some reporting verbs e.g. \textit{answer, complain}, and \textit{explain} tell you what purpose and utterance was intended to serve.

E.g., \textit{answer} tells you that a statement was intended as an answer, and \textit{complain} tells you that a statement was intended as a complaint.
\begin{example}
    He \underline{answered} that the price would be 3 pounds.
    
    He never told me, sir, Watson \underline{complained}.
    
    `Please don't,' I \underline{begged}.
    
    I \underline{suggested} that it was time to leave.
\end{example}
Some reporting verbs used in direct speech show the manner of speaking.

See paragraph \textbf{7.19}.

%
Here is a list of reporting verbs that can be used to report what people say:
\begin{example}
    acknowledge, add, admit, advise, agree, allege, announce, answer, argue, ask, assert, assure, beg, begin, boast, call, chorus, claim, command, comment, complain, concede, confess, confirm, contend, continue, convince, cry, declare, decree, demand, deny, describe, direct, discuss, dispute, enquire, explain, forbid, grumble, guarantee, hint, imply, inform, inquire, insist, instruct, invite, lament, maintain, mention, mumble, murmur, muse, mutter, note, notify, object, observe, order, persuade, plead, pledge, pray, predict, proclaim, promise, prophesy, propose, reassure, recall, recite, recommend, record, refuse, remark, remind, repeat, reply, report, request, respond, reveal, rule, say, scream, shout, shriek, state, stipulate, storm, suggest, swear, teach, tell, threaten, thunder, urge, vow, wail, warn, whisper, write, yell
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.8} [!] Note that the verbs \textit{address, converse, lecture, speak}, and \textit{talk}, although they mean \textit{to say something}, cannot be used as reporting verbs.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.9} Many reporting verbs are used to talk about people's thoughts, rather than what they say.

Reporting verbs are used to talk about many different kinds of thought, including beliefs, wishes, hopes, intentions, and decisions.

They can also be used to talk about remembering an forgetting.
\begin{example}
    We both \underline{knew} that he was lying.
    
    `I'll go to him in a minute,' she \underline{thought}.
    
    I \underline{had} always \underline{believed} that 1 day I would see him again.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that can be used to report people's thoughts:
\begin{example}
    accept, agree, assume, believe, consider, decide, determine, doubt, dream, estimate, expect, fear, feel, figure, foresee, forget, guess, hold, hope, imagine, intend, judge, know, long, mean, muse, note, plan, ponder, pray, prefer, propose, reason, recall, reckon, reflect, regret, remember, resolve, suppose, think, understand, vow, want, wish, wonder, worry
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.10} Some reporting verbs are used for talking about learning and perceiving facts.
\begin{example}
    I have since \underline{learned} that the writer of the letter is now dead.
    
    Then she \underline{saw} that he was sleeping.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that refer to learning and perceiving facts:
\begin{example}
    conclude, discover, elicit, find, gather, hear, infer, learn, note, notice, observe, perceive, read, realize, see, sense
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.11} Some of the verbs in the above lists, e.g. \textit{tell} and \textit{promise}, must be or can be followed by an object showing who the hearer is.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.75--7.76}.

%
Note that some verbs appear in more than 1 list, because they have more than 1 meaning.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.12} When you use direct speech or reported speech, you can give more information about the way that something is said by putting an adverb or a prepositional phrase after the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    `I've got the key!' he announced \underline{triumphantly}.
    
    His secretary explained \underline{patiently} that this was the only time he could spare.
    
    I know what you mean, Carrie replied \underline{with feeling}.
\end{example}
You show how the thing that is said fits into the conversation by using a prepositional phrase.
\begin{example}
    A gift from my mother, he added \underline{in explanation}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.13} With a small number of reporting verbs, the negative is often expressed in the reporting clause rather than in the reported clause.

\textit{I don't think Mary is at home} means the same as \textit{I think Mary is not at home}, and \textit{She doesn't want to see him} means \textit{She wants not to see him}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{do not think} she suspects me.
    
    She \underline{didn't believe} she would ever see him again.
    
    He \underline{didn't want} to go.
    
    We \underline{don't intend} to put him on trial.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that are often used with a negative in this way:
\begin{example}
    believe, expect, feel, imagine, intend, plan, propose, reckon, suppose, think, want, wish
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.14} In informal spoken English, the reporting verb \textit{go} is sometimes used to introduce direct speech.

The reporting clause with \textit{go} always comes before the quote.
\begin{example}
    I said, `Well, what do you want to talk about?' He \underline{goes}, `I don't care.'
    
    I told her what I'd heard and she \underline{went}, `Oh, my gosh.'
    
    When I heard that I'd got the job \underline{I went}, `Oh, no, what have I done?'
\end{example}
Note that you cannot add an adverb after \textit{go}.

E.g., you cannot say, \textit{He went angrily, `Be quiet!'}

%
\noindent\fbox{7.15} Another reporting structure that is used in informal spoken English is \textit{be like}.

\textit{Be like} can represent either speech or thought.

In writing, \textit{be like} is usually followed by a comma.

The quote is sometimes in quotation marks, and sometimes not.
\begin{example}
    He got a call from Oprah, and he \underline{was like}, `Of course I'll go on your show.'
    
    He\underline{'s like}, `It's boring! I hate chess!' And I\underline{'m like}, `Please teach me!'
    
    The minute I met him, \underline{I was like}, he's perfect.
\end{example}
As with other reporting verbs, you can use \textit{be like} with a noun or a personal pronoun: e.g., you can say \textit{She was like,$\ldots$, The doctor was like,$\ldots$} or \textit{Jane was like,$\ldots$}, followed by the thing that she/the doctor/Jane said or thought.

%
Unlike other reporting verbs, you can also use \textit{be like} after the pronoun \textit{it}.

This structure is often used to present a mixture of speech and thought, or a general situation.

E.g., if you say \textit{It was like, Oh wow!} it is possible that nobody actually said or thought \textit{Oh wow!}

Rather, the sentence gives us an idea of the situation and means something like \textit{It was amazing/surprising}.
\begin{example}
    So I get back in the bus, quarter of an hour passes and \underline{it's like}, Where's Graham?
    
    When that happened \underline{it was like}, Oh, no, not again.
\end{example}
\textit{Be like} always comes before the reported clause.

\section{Reporting someone's actual words: direct speech}
\noindent\fbox{7.16} When you want to say that a person used particular words, you use \textbf{direct speech}.

You can do this even if you do not know, or do not remember, the exact words that were spoken.

When you use direct speech, you report what someone said as if you were using their own words.

%
Direct speech consists of 2 clauses.

1 clause is the \textbf{reporting clause}, which contains the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    `I knew I'd seen you,' \underline{I said}.
    
    Yes please, \underline{replied John}.
\end{example} 
The other part is the \textbf{quote}, which represents what someone says or has said.
\begin{example}
    `\underline{Let's go and have a look at the swimming pool},' she suggested.
    
    `\underline{Leave me alone},' I snarled.
\end{example}
You can quote anything that someone says - statements, questions, orders, suggestions, and exclamations.

In writing, you use quotation marks (also called \textit{inverted commas} in British English) (`'') or (``'') at the beginning and end of a quote.
\begin{example}
    `Thank you,' I said.
    
    After a long silence he asked: `What is your name?'
\end{example}
Note that, in written stories, quotes are sometimes used without reporting clauses if the speakers have been established, and if you do not wish to indicate whether the quotes are questions, suggestions, exclamations, etc.
\begin{example}
    `When do you leave?' - `I should be gone now.' - `Well, good-bye, Hamo.'
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.17} Thinking is sometimes represented as speaking to oneself.

You can therefore use some verbs that refer to thinking as reporting verbs in \textbf{direct speech}.
\begin{example}
    I must go and see Lynn, Marsha thought.
\end{example}
When you are using direct speech to say what someone thought, you usually omit the quotation marks at the beginning and end of the quote.
\begin{example}
    How much should he tell her? Not much, he decided.
    
    Perhaps that's no accident, he reasoned.
    
    Why, she wondered, was the flag at half mast?
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.18} Here is a list of reporting verbs that are often used with direct speech:
\begin{example}
    add, admit, advise, agree, announce, answer, argue, ask, assert, assure, beg, begin, boast, claim, command, comment, complain, conclude, confess, continue, decide, declare, demand, explain, grumble, inquire, insist, muse, observe, order, plead, ponder, pray, proclaim, promise, read, reason, recite, reflect, remark, reply, report, respond, say, state, suggest, tell, think, urge, vow, warn, wonder, write
\end{example}
A few of these verbs can or must be used with an object that refers to the hearer.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.75--7.76}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.19} If you want to indicate the way in which something was said, you can use a reporting verb e.g. \textit{shout, wail}, or \textit{scream}.

Verbs like these usually occur only in written stories.
\begin{example}
    Jump! \underline{shouted} the old woman.
    
    Oh, poor little thing, she \underline{wailed}.
    
    Get out of there, I \underline{screamed}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that indicate the way in which something is said:
\begin{example}
    bellow, call, chorus, cry, mumble, murmur, mutter, scream, shout, shriek, storm, thunder, wail, whisper, yell
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.20} [$\star$] Another way of describing the way in which something is said is to use a verb that is usually used to describe the sound made by a particular kind of animal.
\begin{example}
    Excuse me! Susannah \underline{barked}.
\end{example}
You can use a verb e.g. \textit{smile, grin}, or \textit{frown} to indicate the expression on someone's face while they are speaking.
\begin{example}
    `I'm awfully sorry.' - `Not at all,' I \underline{smiled}.
    
    It was a joke, he \underline{grinned}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.21} [$\star$] You use verbs like \textit{bark} and \textit{smile} in direct speech when you want to create a particular effect, especially in writing.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.22} There are several positions in which you can put the reporting verb in relation to a quote.

The usual position is after the quote, but it can also go in front of the quote or in the middle of the quote.
\begin{example}
    You have to keep trying, he said.
    
    He stepped back and said, Now look at that.
    
    You see, he said, my father was an inventor.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.23} If you put the reporting verb in the middle of a quote, it must go in 1 of the following positions:
\begin{itemize}
    \item after a \textbf{noun phrase}
    \begin{example}
        That man, I said, never opened a window in his life.
    \end{example}
    \item after a \textbf{vocative} e.g. \textit{darling} or \textit{Dad}
    \begin{example}
        `Darling', Max said to her, `don't say it's not possible.'
    \end{example}
    \item after a \textbf{sentence adverb}
    \begin{example}
        Maybe, he said hesitantly, maybe there is a beast.
    \end{example}
    \item after a \textbf{clause}
    \begin{example}
        `I know you don't remember your father,' said James, `but he was a kind and generous man.'
    \end{example}
\end{itemize}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.24} You can use most reporting verbs in front of a quote.
\begin{example}
    She replied, My 1st thought was to protect him.
    
    1 student commented: He seems to know his subject very well.
\end{example}
However, the reporting verbs \textit{agree, command, promise}, and \textit{wonder} are hardly ever used in front of a quote.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.25} When a reporting verb comes after a quote, the subject is often put after the verb.
\begin{example}
    `Perhaps he isn't a bad sort of chap after all,' \underline{remarked Dave}.
    
    I see, \underline{said John}.
    
    I am aware of that, \underline{replied the Englishman}.
\end{example}
Note that this is not done when the subject is a pronoun, except in some literary contexts.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.26} The following examples show how you punctuate quotes in British English.

You can use either single quotation marks (`') or double quotation marks (``'').

The ones used to begin a quote are called \textbf{opening} quotation marks, and the ones used to end a quote are called \textbf{closing} quotation marks.
\begin{example}
    `Let's go,' I whispered.
    
    ``We have to go home,'' she told him.
    
    Mona's mother answered: `Oh yes, she's in.'
    
    He nodded and said, `Yes, he's my son.'
    
    `Margaret', I said to her, `I'm so glad you came.'
    
    What are you doing? Sarah asked.
    
    `Of course it's awful!' shouted Clarissa.
    
    What do they mean, she demanded, by a ``population problem''?
\end{example}
Note that in the last example shown before, there is a quote within a quote.

If you are using single quotation marks for the main quote, the quoted words within the main quote are enclosed in double quotation marks.

If you are using double quotation marks for the main quote, the quoted words within the main quote are enclosed in single quotation marks.

%
[AE] In American English, you always use double quotation marks (``''), except when you have a quote within a quote.

In such cases, the quoted words within the main quote are enclosed in single quotation marks (`').

This is shown in the 2nd example below.
\begin{example}
    ``What are you doing?'' Sarah asked.
    
    ``What do they mean,'' she demanded, ``by a `population problem'?''
\end{example}
If you are quoting more than 1 paragraph, you put opening quotation marks at the beginning of each paragraph, but you put closing quotation marks only at the end of the last paragraph.

\section{Reporting in your own words: reported speech}
\noindent\fbox{7.27} When you report what people have said using your own words rather than the words they actually used, you use \textbf{reported speech}.
\begin{example}
    The woman said she had seen nothing.
    
    I replied that I had not read it yet.
\end{example}
You usually use a reported speech structure when you report what someone thinks.
\begin{example}
    He thought she was worried.
\end{example}
Reported speech is sometimes called \textbf{indirect speech}.

Reported speech consists of 2 parts.

1 part is the \textbf{reporting clause}, which contains the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I told him} that nothing was going to happen to me.
    
    \underline{I have agreed} that he should do it.
    
    \underline{I wanted} to be alone.
\end{example}
The other part is the \textbf{reported clause}.
\begin{example}
    He answered \underline{that he thought the story was extremely interesting}.
    
    He felt \underline{that he had to do something}.
    
    He wondered \underline{where they could have come from}.
\end{example}
You usually put the reporting clause 1st, in order to make it clear that you are reporting rather than speaking directly yourself.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Henry said} that he wanted to go home.
\end{example}
The exact words that Henry used are unlikely to have been \textit{I want to go home}, although they might have been.

It is more likely that he said something like \textit{I think I should be going now}.

You are more likely to report what he meant rather than what he actually said.

%
There are many reasons why you do not quote a person's exact words.

Often you cannot remember exactly what was said.

At other times, the exact words are not important or not appropriate in the situation in which you are reporting.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.28} There are several types of \textbf{reported clause}.

The type you use depends on whether you are reporting a statement, a question, an order, or a suggestion.

%
Most reported clauses either are \textit{that}-clauses or begin with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

When a question is being reported, the reported clause begins with \textit{if, whether}, or a \textit{wh}-word.

The use of \textit{that}-clauses as reported clauses is discussed in paragraphs \textbf{7.29--7.31}.

\textbf{Reported questions} are discussed in paragraphs \textbf{7.32--7.38}.

The use of \textit{to}-infinitive clauses in reported speech is discussed in paragraphs \textbf{7.39--7.48}.

\subsection{Reporting statements \& thoughts}
\noindent\fbox{7.29} If you want to report a statement or someone's thoughts, you use a reported clause beginning with the conjunction \textit{that}.
\begin{example}
    He said \underline{that the police had directed him to the wrong room}.
    
    He wrote me a letter saying \underline{that he understood what I was doing}.
    
    Mrs Kaul announced \underline{that the lecture would now begin}.
\end{example}
In informal speech and writing, the conjunction \textit{that} is commonly omitted.
\begin{example}
    They said \underline{I had to see a doctor 1st}.
    
    She says \underline{she wants to see you this afternoon}.
    
    He knew \underline{the attempt was hopeless}.
    
    I think \underline{there's something wrong}.
\end{example}
In each of these sentences, \textit{that} could have been used.

E.g., you can say either \textit{They said I had to see a doctor 1st} or \textit{They said that I had to see a doctor 1st}.

%
\textit{That} is often omitted when the reporting verb refers simply to the act of saying or thinking.

You usually include \textit{that} after a verb that gives more information, e.g. \textit{complain} or \textit{explain}.
\begin{example}
    The FBI \underline{confirmed that} the substance was an explosive.
    
    I \underline{explained that} she would have to stay in bed.    
\end{example}
This kind of reported clause is often called a \textit{that}-clause, even though many occur without \textit{that}.

%
Note that some relative clauses also begin with \textit{that}.

In such clauses, \textit{that} is a relative pronoun, not a conjunction.

\textbf{Relative clauses} are explained in paragraphs \textbf{8.83--8.116}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.30} Here is a list of verbs that are often used as reporting verbs with \textit{that}-clause:
\begin{example}
    accept, acknowledge, add, admit, agree, allege, announce, answer, argue, assert, assume, assure, believe, boast, claim, comment, complain, concede, conclude, confirm, consider, contend, convince, decide, deny, determine, discover, dispute, doubt, dream, elicit, estimate, expect, explain, fear, feel, figure, find, foresee, forget, gather, guarantee, guess, hear, hold, hope, imagine, imply, inform, insist, judge, know, lament, learn, maintain, mean, mention, note, notice, notify, object, observe, perceive, persuade, pledge, pray, predict, promise, prophesy, read, realize, reason, reassure, recall, reckon, record, reflect, remark, remember, repeat, reply, report, resolve, reveal, say, see, sense, speculate, state, suggest, suppose, suspect, swear, teach, tell, think, threaten, understand, vow, warn, wish, worry, write
\end{example}
Note that some of these verbs are only used in reported speech in some of their senses.

E.g., if you say \textit{He accepted a present} you are using \textit{accept} as an ordinary verb.

%
A few of these verbs can or must be used with an object that refers to the hearer.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.75--7.76}.

%
Some of these verbs, e.g. \textit{decide} and \textit{promise}, can also be used with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.39--7.45}.

%
Some other verbs, e.g. \textit{advice} and \textit{order}, can be used as reporting verbs with \textit{that}-clauses only if the \textit{that}-clause contains a \textbf{modal} or a \textbf{subjunctive}.

\textit{That}-clauses of this kind are discussed in paragraph \textbf{7.43}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.31} You usually put the reporting clause before the \textit{that}-clause, in order to make it clear that you are reporting rather than speaking directly yourself.
\begin{example}
    I said that I would rather work at home.
    
    Georgina said she was going to bed.
\end{example}
However, if you want to emphasize the statement contained in the reported clause, you can change the order and put the reported clause 1st, with a comma after it.

You do not use \textit{that} to introduce the clause.
\begin{example}
    All these things were trivial, he said.
    
    She was worried, he thought.
\end{example}
If the reported clause is long, you can put the reporting clause in the middle.
\begin{example}
    10 years ago, Moumoni explained, some government people had come to inspect the village.
\end{example}

\subsection{Reporting questions}
\noindent\fbox{7.32} As well as reporting what someone says or thinks, you can also report a question that they ask or wonder about.

%
Questions in reported speech are sometimes called \textbf{reported questions} or \textbf{indirect questions}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.33} The reporting verb most often used for reporting questions is \textit{ask}.

Questions can be reported in a more formal way using \textit{enquire} or \textit{inquire}.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{asked} if I could stay with them.
    
    He \underline{asked} me where I was going.
    
    She \underline{inquired} how Ibrahim was getting on.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.34} [!] When you report a question:
\begin{itemize}
    \item you do not use interrogative word order
    \item you do not use a question mark.
\end{itemize}
So the question \textit{Did you enjoy it?} could be reported: \textit{I asked her if she had enjoyed it}.

%
\textbf{Questions} are explained in paragraphs \textbf{5.10--5.36}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.35} There are 2 main types of question, and so 2 main types of reported speech structure for questions.

%
1 type of question is called a \textit{yes/no} question.

These are questions that can be answered simply with \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.

%
When you report a \textit{yes/no} question, you use an if-clause beginning with the conjunction \textit{if}, or a \textit{whether}-clause beginning with the conjunction \textit{whether}.

%
You use \textit{if} when the speaker has suggested 1 possibility that may be true.

\textit{Do you know my name?} could be reported as \textit{A woman asked if I knew her name}.
\begin{example}
    She asked him \underline{if his parents spoke French}.
    
    Someone asked me \underline{if the work was going well}.
    
    He inquired \underline{if her hair had always been that color}.
\end{example}
You use \textit{whether} when the speaker has suggested 1 possibility but has left open the question of other possibilities.

After \textit{whether}, you can suggest another possibility, or you can leave it unstated.
\begin{example}
    I was asked \underline{whether I wanted to stay at a hotel or at his home}.
    
    She asked \underline{whether the servants were still there}.
    
    I asked Prof. Fred Bailey \underline{whether he agreed}.
    
    A policeman asked me \underline{whether he could be of help}.
\end{example}
Sometimes the alternative possibility is represented by \textit{or not}.
\begin{example}
    The barman didn't asked \underline{whether or not} they were over eighteen.
    
    They asked \underline{whether} Britain was \underline{or} was \underline{not} a Christian country.
\end{example}
For more information about \textbf{\textit{yes/no} questions}, see paragraphs \textbf{5.12--5.14}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.36} There are a few other verbs that can be used before \textit{if}-clauses or \textit{whether}-clauses, because they refer to being unsure of facts or to discovering facts.
\begin{example}
    I didn't know \underline{whether to believe him or not}.
    
    Simon wondered \underline{if he should make conversation}.
    
    She didn't say \underline{whether he was still alive}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of other verbs that can be used before \textit{if}-clauses and \textit{whether}-clauses:
\begin{example}
    consider, determine, discover, doubt, know, remember, say, see, tell, wonder
\end{example}
Note that \textit{know, remember, say, see}, and \textit{tell} are usually used in a negative or interrogative clause, or a clause with a modal.

%
All the verbs in the list, except \textit{wonder}, can also be used with \textit{that}-clauses: see paragraph \textbf{7.30}.

They can all also be used with clauses beginning with \textit{wh}-words: see paragraph \textbf{7.38}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.37} The other type of question is called a \textit{wh}-question.

These are questions in which someone asks for information about an event or situation.

\textit{Wh}-questions cannot be answered with \textit{yes} or \textit{no}.

%
When you report a \textit{wh}-question, you use a \textit{wh}-word at the beginning of the reported clause.
\begin{example}
    He asked \underline{where I was going}.
    
    She enquired \underline{why I was so late}.
    
    She started to ask \underline{what had happened}, then decided against it.
    
    I asked \underline{how they had got there so quickly}.
    
    I never thought to ask \underline{who put it there}.
\end{example}
When the details of the question are clear from the context, you can sometimes leave out everything except the \textit{wh}-word.

This happens mostly in spoken English, especially with \textit{why}.
\begin{example}
    I asked \underline{why}.
    
    They enquired \underline{how}.
\end{example}
For more information about \textbf{\textit{wh}-questions} see paragraphs \textbf{5.23--5.36}.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.38} Other verbs can be used before clauses beginning with \textit{wh}-words, because they refer to knowing, learning, or mentioning 1 of the circumstances of an event or situation.
\begin{example}
    She doesn't know \underline{what we were talking about}.
    
    They couldn't see \underline{how they would manage without her}.
    
    I wonder \underline{what's happened}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of other verbs that can be used before clauses beginning with \textit{wh}-words:
\begin{example}
    decide, describe, determine, discover, discuss, explain, forget, guess, imagine, judge, know, learn, realize, remember, reveal, say, see, suggest, teach, tell, test, think, understand, wonder
\end{example}
Note that \textit{imagine, say, see, suggest}, and \textit{think} are usually used in a negative or interrogative clause, or a clause with a modal.

%
All the verbs in the list, except \textit{describe, discuss}, and \textit{wonder}, can also be used with \textit{that}-clauses: see paragraph \textbf{7.30}.

\subsection{Reporting orders, requests, advice, \& intentions}
\noindent\fbox{7.39} If someone orders, requests, or advises someone else to do something, this can be reported by using a \textit{to}-infinitive after a reporting verb e.g. \textit{tell}.

The person being addressed, who is going to perform the action, is mentioned as the object of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    He told her to wait there for him.
    
    He commanded his men to retreat.
    
    He ordered me to fetch the books.
    
    My doctor advised me to see a neurologist.
\end{example}
For more on this type of structure, see paragraphs \textbf{3.202} and \textbf{3.206}.

%
Here is a list of reporting verbs that can be used with a person as object followed by a \textit{to}-infinitive:
\begin{example}
    advise, ask, beg, command, forbid, instruct, invite, order, persuade, remind, teach, tell, urge, warn
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.40} [i] A few verbs can be used with a \textit{to}-infinitive to report requests when the hearer is mentioned in a prepositional phrase.
\begin{example}
    An officer shouted \underline{to us} to stop all the noise.
    
    I pleaded \underline{with him} to tell me.
\end{example}
Here is a list of these verbs and the prepositions used with them:
\begin{example}
    appeal to, pleaded with, shout at/to, whisper to, yell at
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.41} In ordinary conversation, requests are often put in the form of a question.

E.g., you might say \textit{Will you help me?} instead of \textit{Help me}.

Similarly, reported requests often look like reported questions.
\begin{example}
    He asked me if I could lend him 50 dollars.
\end{example}
When you report a request like this, you can mention both the person receiving it and the person making it.
\begin{example}
    \underline{She} asked \underline{me} whether I would help her.
\end{example}
Alternatively, you can just mention the person making it.
\begin{example}
    \underline{He} asked if I would answer some questions.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.42} You can report a request in which someone asks for permission to do something by using a \textit{to}-infinitive after \textit{ask} or \textit{demand}.
\begin{example}
    I asked to see the manager.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.43} When someone makes a suggestion about what someone else (not their hearer) should do, you report it by using a \textit{that}-clause.

In British English, this clause often contains a \textbf{modal}, usually \textit{should}.
\begin{example}
    He proposes \underline{that the Government should hold an inquiry}.
    
    Travel agents advise \underline{that people should change their money before they travel}.
\end{example}
Note that this structure can also be used to report a suggestion about what the hearer should do.

Consider the example: \textit{Her father had suggested that she ought to see a doctor}; her father might have suggested it directly to her.

%
[AE] If you do not use a modal, the result is considered more formal in British English.

In American English, however, this is the usual verb form that is used after suggesting verbs.
\begin{example}
    Someone suggested \underline{that they break into small groups}.
\end{example}
Note that when you leave out the modal, the verb in the reported clause still has the form it would have if the modal were present.

This verb form is called the \underline{subjunctive}.
\begin{example}
    It was his doctor who advised that he \underline{change} his job.
    
    I suggested that he \underline{bring} them all up to the house.
    
    He urges that the restrictions \underline{be lifted}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that can be followed by a \textit{that}-clause containing a modal or a subjunctive:
\begin{example}
    advise, agree, ask, beg, command, decree, demand, direct, insist, intend, order, plead, pray, prefer, propose, recommend, request, rule, stipulate, suggest, urge
\end{example}
Note that \textit{advise, ask, beg, command, order}, and \textit{urge} can also be used with an object and a \textit{to}-infinitive, and \textit{agree, pray}, and \textit{suggest} can also be used with \textit{that}-clauses without a modal.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.44} When someone makes a suggestion about what someone else should do, or about what they themselves and someone else should do, you can report this using 1 of the reporting verbs \textit{suggest, advise, propose}, or \textit{recommend} followed by an \textit{-ing} participle.
\begin{example}
    Barbara suggested \underline{going to another coffee-house}.
    
    Deirdre proposed \underline{moving to New York}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.45} When you are reporting an action that the speaker (the subject of the reporting verb) intends to perform, you can report it in 2 ways.

You can either report it simply as an action, using a \textit{to}-infinitive clause, or you can report it as a statement or fact, using a \textit{that}-clause.

%
E.g., promises relate to actions (e.g., \textit{He promised to phone her}) but they can also be seen as relating to facts (e.g., \textit{He promised that he would phone her}).

%
The verb phrase in the \textit{that}-clause always contains a \textbf{modal}.
\begin{example}
    I promised \underline{to come back}.
    
    She promised \underline{that she would not leave hospital until she was better}.
    
    I decided \underline{to withhold the information till later}.
    
    She decided \underline{that she would leave her money to him}.
    
    I had vowed \underline{to fight for their freedom}.
    
    She vowed \underline{that she would not leave her home}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used either with a \textit{to}-infinitive or with a \textit{that}-clause containing a modal:
\begin{example}
    decide, expect, guarantee, hope, pledge, promise, propose, resolve, swear, threaten, vow
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.46} [i] \textit{Claim} and \textit{pretend} can also be used with these 2 structures, when you are saying that someone is claiming or pretending something about himself or herself.

E.g., \textit{He claimed to be a genius} has the same meaning as \textit{He claimed that he was a genius}.
\begin{example}
    He claimed \underline{to have witnessed the accident}.
    
    He claimed \underline{that he had found the money in the forest}.
\end{example}
Note that the \textit{to}-infinitive can be in the perfect form: \textit{to have} $+$ \textit{-ed} participle, when you are referring to a past event or situation.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.47} Note that a few verbs that indicate personal intentions can be used only with a \textit{to}-infinitive.
\begin{example}
    I intend to say nothing for the present.
    
    They are planning to move to the country.
    
    I don't want to die yet.
\end{example}
Here is a list of these verbs:
\begin{example}
    intend, long, mean, plan, refuse, want
\end{example}

\subsection{Reporting uncertain things}
\noindent\fbox{7.48} When you are reporting an action that someone is wondering about doing themselves, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive beginning with \textit{whether}.
\begin{example}
    I've been wondering \underline{whether to retire}.
    
    He didn't know \underline{whether to feel glad or sorry at his dismissal}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used with \textit{to}-infinitive clauses of this kind:
\begin{example}
    choose, debate, decide, know, wonder
\end{example}
Note that \textit{choose, decide}, and \textit{know} are usually used in a negative or interrogative clause, or a clause with a modal.

%
When you are mentioning information about something involved in an action, you can use a \textit{to}-infinitive after a \textit{wh}-word as the reported clause.
\begin{example}
    I asked him \underline{what to do}.
    
    I shall teach you \underline{how to cook}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of verbs that can be used with \textit{to}-infinitives of this kind:
\begin{example}
    describe, discover, discuss, explain, forget, guess, imagine, know, learn, realize, remember, reveal, say, see, suggest, teach, tell, think, understand, wonder
\end{example}
As an alternative to both kinds of \textit{to}-infinitive, you can use a clause containing \textit{should}.
\begin{example}
    I wondered \underline{whether I should call for help}.
    
    He began to wonder \underline{what he should do now}.
\end{example}
All the verbs in the above lists, except \textit{choose} and \textit{debate}, can also be used with ordinary clauses beginning with \textit{whether} or \textit{wh}-words.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.35--7.38}.

\subsection{Time reference in reported speech}
\noindent\fbox{7.49} This section explains how to show time reference in reported speech.

Descriptions of time reference in reported speech often give a simplified system that involves changing the tense in the actual words spoken, so that a present tense would be changed to a past tense, and a past tense would be changed to the past perfect.

In fact, tense changes in reported speech are affected by several factors other than time, e.g. whether you wish to distance yourself from what was said, or whether you want to emphasize the fact that a statement is still true.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.50} If you are reporting something that was said or believed in the past, or if you want to distance yourself from what the other person said, you usually use a past tense for both the reporting verb and the verb in the reported clause.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{said} you \underline{threw} away her sweets.
    
    Brody \underline{asked} what \underline{happened}.
    
    In the Middle Ages, people \underline{thought} the world \underline{was} flat.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.51} If you are reporting something that someone says or believes at the time that you are speaking, you use a present tense of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    A 3rd of adults \underline{say} that work is bad for your health.
    
    I \underline{think} it's going to rain.
\end{example}
However, you can also use a present tense of the reporting verb when you are reporting something said in the past, especially if you are reporting something that someone often says or that is still true.
\begin{example}
    She \underline{says} she wants to see you this afternoon.
    
    My doctor \underline{says} it's nothing to worry about.
\end{example}
If you are predicting what people will say or think, you use a future form of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    No doubt he \underline{will claim} that his car broke down.
    
    They \underline{will think} we are making a fuss.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.52} Whatever the tense of your reporting verb, you put the verb in the reported clause into a tense that is appropriate at the time that you are speaking.

%
If the event of situation described in the reported clause is in the past at the time you are speaking, you generally use the past simple, the past progressive, or the present perfect: \textit{She said the enjoyed the course, She said she was enjoying the course}, or \textit{She said she has enjoyed the course}.

See Chap. 4 for information on when to use these forms.
\begin{example}
    Dad explained that he \underline{had} no money.
    
    She added that she \underline{was working} too much.
    
    He says he \underline{has} never \underline{seen} a live shark in his life.
\end{example}
When the reporting verb is in a past tense, a past tense is also usually used for the verb in the reported clause even if the reported situation still exists.

E.g., you could say \textit{I told him I was 18} even if you are still 18.

You are concentrating on the situation at the past time that you are talking about.
\begin{example}
    He said he \underline{was} English.
\end{example}
A present form is sometimes used instead, to emphasize that the situation still exists.
\begin{example}
    I told him that I \underline{don't eat} more than anyone else.
\end{example}
If the event or situation was in the past at the time the reported statement was made, or had existed up to that time, you use the past perfect: \textit{She said she had enjoyed the course}.
\begin{example}
    He knew he \underline{had behaved} badly.
    
    Mr Benn said that he \underline{had been} in hospital at the time.
\end{example}
If the event or situation is still going on, you use a present form if you are using a present form of the reporting verb: \textit{She says she's enjoying the course}.
\begin{example}
    Don't assume I\underline{'m} a complete fool.
    
    He knows he\underline{'s being watched}.
\end{example}
If the event or situation was in the future at the time of the statement or is still in the future, you use a modal.

See paragraphs \textbf{7.53--7.56}, below.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.53} The basic rules for using modals in reported clauses are as follows.

%
If the verb in the reporting clause is in a past form or has \textit{could} or \textit{would} as an auxiliary, you usually use \textit{could, might}, or \textit{would} in the reported clause.

%
If, as is less common, the verb in the reporting clause is in the present or has \textit{can} or \textit{will} as an auxiliary, you usually use \textit{can, may}, or \textit{will} in the reported clause.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.54} [i] When you want to report a statement or question about someone's ability to do something, you normally use \textit{could}.
\begin{example}
    They believed that war \underline{could} be avoided.
    
    Nell would not admit that she \underline{could} not cope.
\end{example}
If you want to report a statement about possibility, you normally use \textit{might}.
\begin{example}
    They told me it \underline{might} flood here.
    
    He said you \underline{might} need money.
\end{example}
If the possibility is a strong one, you use \textit{must}.
\begin{example}
    I told her she \underline{must} be out of her mind.
\end{example}
When you want to report a statement giving permission or a request for permission, you normally use \textit{could}.

\textit{Might} is used in more formal English.
\begin{example}
    I told him he \underline{couldn't} have it.
    
    Madeleine asked if she \underline{might} borrow a pen and some paper.
\end{example}
When you want to report a prediction, promise, or expectation, or a question about the future, you normally use \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    She said they \underline{would} all miss us.
    
    He insisted that reforms \underline{would} have the system, not destroy it.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.55} If the reported event or situation still exists or is still in the future, and you are using a present tense of the reporting verb, you use \textit{can} instead of \textit{could, may} instead of \textit{might}, and \textit{will} or \textit{shall} instead of \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    Helen says I \underline{can} share her apartment.
    
    I think the weather \underline{may} change soon.
    
    I don't believe he \underline{will} come.
\end{example}
Note that you cannot use \textit{can have} instead of \textit{could have}, or \textit{will have} instead of \textit{would have}.

You cannot use \textit{may have} instead of \textit{might have} if you are using it like \textit{could have} to talk about something that did not happen.

%
You can also use \textit{can, may, will}, and \textit{shall} when you are using a past tense of the reporting verb, if you want to emphasize that the situation still exists or is still in the future.
\begin{example}
    He claimed that the child's early experiences \underline{may} cause psychological distress in later life.
\end{example}
If you are using a present reporting verb and want to indicate that the reported event or situation is hypothetical or very unlikely, you can use the modals \textit{could, might}, or \textit{would}.
\begin{example}
    I believe that I \underline{could} live very comfortably here.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.56} When you want to report a statement about obligation, it is possible to use \textit{must}, but the expression \textit{had to} (see paragraph \textbf{5.244}) is more common.
\begin{example}
    He said he really \underline{had to} go back inside.
    
    Sita told him that he \underline{must} be especially kind to the little girl.
\end{example}
You use \textit{have to, has to}, or \textit{must} if the reported situation still exists or is in the future.

%
When you want to report a statement prohibiting something, you normally use \textit{mustn't}.
\begin{example}
    He said they \underline{mustn't} get us into trouble.
\end{example}
When you want to report a strong recommendation, you can use \textit{ought to}.

You can also use \textit{should}.
\begin{example}
    He knew he \underline{ought to} be helping Harold.
    
    I felt I \underline{should consult my family}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.57} When you want to report a habitual past action or a past situation, you can use the semi-modal \textit{used to}.
\begin{example}
    I wish I knew what is favorite dishes \underline{used to} be.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.58} The use of \textbf{modals} in reported clauses can be compared with the ordinary use of modals (see paragraphs \textbf{5.94--5.258}).

Many of the functions are similar, but some are rarely or never found in reported clauses.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.59} When you are reporting a \textbf{conditional statement}, the tenses of the verbs are, in most cases, the same as they would be normally.

However, they are different if you are using the past simple form of a reporting verb, and reporting a conditional statement e.g. \textit{If there is no water in the radiator, the engine will overheat}.

In this case, you can use the past simple instead of the present simple and \textit{would} instead of \textit{will} in the reported conditional statement: \textit{She said that if there was no water in the radiator, the engine would overheat}.

%
For information about conditional statements, see paragraphs \textbf{8.25--8.42}.

\section{Making your reference appropriate}
\noindent\fbox{7.60} People, things, times, and places can be referred to in different ways, depending on who is speaking or on when or where they are speaking.

E.g., the same person can be referred to as \textit{I, you}, or \textit{she}, and the same place can be referred to as \textit{over there} or \textit{just here}.

%
If you use \textbf{reported speech} to report what someone has said, the words you use to refer to things must be appropriate in relation to yourself, the time when you are speaking, and the place in which you are speaking.

The words you use may well be different from the words originally spoken, which were appropriate from the point of view of the speaker at the time.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.61} E.g., if a man is talking to someone about a woman called Jenny, and he says, \textit{I saw her in the High Street}, there are several ways in which this statement can be reported.

If the original speaker repeats what he said, he could say, \textit{I said I saw her in the High Street}.

\textit{I} and \textit{her} do not change, because they still refer to the same people.

%
If the original listener reports what was said, he or she could say, \textit{He said he saw her in the High Street}.

\textit{I} becomes \textit{he}, because the statement is reported from the point of view of a 3rd person, not from the point of view of the original speaker.

%
If the original listener reports the statement to Jenny, \textit{her} becomes \textit{you}: \textit{He said he saw you in the High Street}.

%
The original listener might report the statement to the original speaker.

This time, \textit{I} has to change to \textit{you}: \textit{You said you saw her in the High Street}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{You}'re crazy.
    
    I told him \underline{he} was crazy.
\end{example}
Possessive determiners and pronouns change in the same way as personal pronouns in order to keep the same reference.

So the following sentences could all report the same question: \textit{She asked if he was my brother, She asked if you were my brother, I asked if he was her brother}.

The original question might have been expressed as \textit{Is he your brother?}

%
\noindent\fbox{7.62} When reporting, you may need to change \textbf{time adverbials} e.g. \textit{today, yesterday}, or \textit{next week}.

%
E.g., if someone called Jill says \textit{I will come tomorrow}, you could report this statement the following days as \textit{Jill said she would come today}.

At a later time, you could report the same statement as \textit{Jill said she would come the next day} or \textit{Jill said she would come the following day}.
\begin{example}
    We decided to leave the city \underline{the next day}.
    
    I was afraid people might think I'd been asleep during \underline{the previous 24 hours}.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.63} You may need to change words that relate to position or place.

%
E.g., if you are talking to a man about a restaurant, he might say \textit{I go there every day}.

If you report his statement while you are actually in the restaurant, you could say \textit{He said he comes here every day}.

\subsection{Using reporting verbs to perform an action}
\noindent\fbox{7.64} Reporting verbs are often used when people explicitly say what function their statement is performing.

They do this using \textit{I} and the present simple of a verb e.g. \textit{admit} or \textit{promise} that refers to something that is done with words.

E.g., instead of saying \textit{I'll be there} you could say \textit{I promise I'll be there}, which makes the statement stronger.
\begin{example}
    \underline{I suggest} we draw up a document.
    
    I'll be back at 1, \underline{I promise}.
    
    I was somewhat shocked, \underline{I admit}, by these events.
\end{example}
The following verbs can be used in this way:
\begin{example}
    acknowledge, admit, assure, claim, concede, contend, demand, deny, guarantee, maintain, pledge, predict, promise, prophesy, propose, say, submit, suggest, swear, tell, vow, warn
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.65} Some other verbs that refer to doing something with words are used without a \textit{that}-clause after them.

When used without a \textit{that}-clause, the use of the present simple with \textit{I} performs the function of a statement in itself, rather than commenting on another statement.
\begin{example}
    I apologize for any delay.
    
    I congratulate you with all my heart.
    
    I forgive you.
\end{example}
The following verbs are commonly used in this way:
\begin{example}
    absolve, accept, accuse, advise, agree, apologize, authorize, baptize, challenge, confess, congratulate, consent, declare, dedicate, defy, forbid, forgive, name, nominate, object, order, pronounce, protest, refuse, renounce, resign, second, sentence
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.66} The verbs in the above lists are sometimes called \textbf{performative verbs} or \textbf{performatives}, because they perform the action they refer to.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.67} [i] Some of these verbs are used with modals when people want to be emphatic, polite, or tentative.
\begin{example}
    I \underline{must} apologize for Mayfield.
    
    I \underline{would} agree with a lot of their points.
    
    She was very thoroughly checked, I \underline{can} assure you.
    
    \underline{May} I congratulate you again on your excellent performance.
\end{example}

\subsection{Avoiding mention of the person speaking or thinking}
\noindent\fbox{7.68} There are several reporting structures that you can use if you want to avoid saying whose opinion or statement you are giving.

%
\noindent\fbox{7.69} If you want to show or suggest that something is an opinion held by an unspecified group of people, you can use a passive form of a reporting verb with \textit{it} as the \textbf{impersonal} subject.
\begin{example}
    It is assumed that the government will remain in power.
    
    In former times it was believed that all enlarged tonsils should be removed.
    
    It is now believed that foreign languages are most easily taught to young children.
    
    It was said that half a million dollars had been spent on the search.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that are used in the passive with \textit{it} as their subject:
\begin{example}
    accept, acknowledge, admit, agree, allege, announce, argue, assert, assume, believe, claim, comment, concede, conclude, confirm, consider, decide, decree, discover, estimate, expect, explain, fear, feel, find, foresee, forget, guarantee, hold, hope, imply, know, mention, note, notice, object, observe, predict, propose, realize, recall, reckon, recommend, record, remember, report, request, reveal, rule, rumor, say, state, stipulate, suggest, suppose, think, understand
\end{example}
This structure has much in common with a structure using a passive reporting verb and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.

In this structure, the main person or thing involved in the reported opinion is put as the subject of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    Intelligence is assumed to be important.
    
    He is said to have died a natural death.
    
    He is believed to have fled to France.
\end{example}
Note that the \textit{to}-infinitive is most commonly \textit{be} or \textit{have}, or a perfect infinitive.

%
Here is a list of reporting verbs, from the list above, that are also used in this type of structure:
\begin{example}
    agree, allege, assume, believe, claim, consider, discover, estimate, expect, feel, find, guarantee, hold, know, observe, think, understand
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.70} If you want to say that something appears to be the case, you can use either of the verbs \textit{seem} and \textit{appear}.

These verbs can be used as reporting verbs followed by a \textit{that}-clause or they can be used with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

You can use this structure to give your own opinion or that of someone else.

The subject of \textit{seem} or \textit{appear} is \textit{it}, used impersonally.
\begin{example}
    It seemed that she had not been careful enough.
    
    It seemed that he had lost his chance to win.
    
    It appears that he followed my advice.
\end{example}
Alternatively, you can use a structure involving \textit{seem} or \textit{appear} and a \textit{to}-infinitive clause.

The main person or thing involved in the fact that appears to be true is put as the subject of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    She seemed to like me.
    
    He appears to have been an interesting man.
    
    The system appears to work well.
\end{example}
If you want to mention the person whose viewpoint you are giving, you can add a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{to} after \textit{seem} or \textit{appear}.
\begin{example}
    It seemed \underline{to Jane} that everyone was against her.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.71} [i] There are a few expressions containing impersonal \textit{it} that are used as reporting clauses before \textit{that}-clauses to show that someone suddenly thought of something: \textit{It occurs to me, It struck me}, and \textit{It crossed my mind}.
\begin{example}
    \underline{It occurred to her} that someone was missing.
    
    \underline{It crossed my mind} that somebody must have been keeping things secret.
\end{example}

\subsection{Referring to the speaker \& hearer}
\noindent\fbox{7.72} You usually use a reporting verb to report what 1 person has said or thought, so the subject of a reporting verb is usually a singular noun.
\begin{example}
    \underline{Henry} said that he wanted to go home.
    
    \underline{He} claimed his health had been checked several times at a clinic.
\end{example}
When you report the statements, opinions, orders, or questions of a group of people, you can use a plural noun or a collective noun as the subject of the reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{The judges} demanded that the race be run again.
    
    \underline{The committee} noted that this was not the 1st case of its kind.
\end{example}
When you report what was said on television or radio, or what is printed or written in a newspaper or other document, you can mention the source or means of communication as the subject of a reporting verb.
\begin{example}
    \underline{The newspaper} said he was hiding somewhere near Kabul.
    
    \underline{His contract} stated that his salary would be \pounds50,000 a year.
\end{example}
Note that you can also use \textit{say} with nouns e.g. \textit{sign, notice, clock}, and \textit{map} as the subject.
\begin{example}
    The notice said that attendants should not be tipped.
    
    A sign over the door said Dreamland Cafe.
    
    The road map said it was 210 kilometers to the French frontier.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.73} As explained in paragraph \textbf{7.69}, when you want to avoid mentioning the person who said something, you can use a reporting verb in the passive.
\begin{example}
    It was said that some of them had become insane.
    
    He was said to be the oldest man in the firm.
\end{example}
If you want to avoid mentioning the person giving an order or giving advice, you use a passive reporting verb with the person who receives the order or advice as the subject.
\begin{example}
    Harriet was ordered to keep away from my room.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.74} [i] If you want to distance yourself from a statement you are making, you can show that you are reporting what someone else has said by using a phrase beginning with \textit{according to}, rather than using reported speech.
\begin{example}
    \underline{According to Dime}, he had strangled Jed in the course of a struggle.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.75} After some reporting verbs that refer to speech, you have to mention the hearer as a direct object.

\textit{Tell} is the most common of these verbs.
\begin{example}
    I told \underline{them} you were at the dentist.
    
    I informed \underline{her} that I was unwell and could not come.
    
    Smith persuaded \underline{them} that they must support the strike.
\end{example}
You can use these verbs in the passive, with the hearer as the subject.
\begin{example}
    \underline{She had been told} she could leave hospital.
    
    \underline{Members had been informed} that the purpose of the meeting was to elect a new chairman.
    
    \underline{She was persuaded} to look again.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that must have the hearer as the direct object when they are used with a \textit{that}-clause:
\begin{example}
    assure, convince, inform, notify, persuade, reassure, remind, tell
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that must have the hearer as the direct object when they are used with a \textit{to}-infinitive clause:
\begin{example}
    advise, beg, command, forbid, instruct, invite, order, persuade, remind, teach, tell, urge, warn
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.76} After a few reporting verbs that refer to speech, you can choose whether or not to mention the hearer.
\begin{example}
    I promised that I would try to phone her.
    
    I promised Myra I'd be home at 7.
    
    The physicians warned that, without the operation, the child would die.
    
    Thomas warned \underline{her} that his mother was slightly deaf.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that can be used with or without the hearer as object when used with a \textit{that}-clause:
\begin{example}
    ask, promise, teach, warn
\end{example}
\textit{Promise} can also be used with or without an object when it is used with a \textit{to}-infinitive.

\textit{Ask} has to be used with an object when it is used with a \textit{to}-infinitive clause to report a request for the hearer to do something, but it is used without an object when the request is for permission to do something (see paragraphs \textbf{7.39--7.42}).

%
\noindent\fbox{7.77} With many other reporting verbs, if you want to mention the hearer, you do so in a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{to}.
\begin{example}
    I explained \underline{to her} that I had to go home.
    
    `Margaret', I said \underline{to her}, `I'm so glad you came.'
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that are used with \textit{that}-clauses or quotes and that need the preposition \textit{to} if you mention the hearer:
\begin{example}
    admit, announce, boast, complain, confess, declare, explain, hint, insist, mention, murmur, propose, report, reveal, say, shout, suggest, swear, whisper
\end{example}
\textit{Propose} and \textit{swear} can also be used with a \textit{to}-infinitive, but not if you mention the hearer.
\begin{example}
    I propose to mention this at the next meeting.
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.78} When you are describing a situation in which a speaker is speaking forcefully to a hearer, you can mention the hearer in a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{at}.
\begin{example}
    The tall boy shouted \underline{at them}, Choir! Stand still!
    
    Shut up! he bellowed \underline{at me}.
\end{example}
here is a list of reporting verbs that are used to describe forceful speech.

If you want to mention the hearer, you use a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{at}:
\begin{example}
    bark, bellow, growl, grumble, howl, roar, scream, shout, shriek, snap, storm, thunder, wail, yell
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.79} With verbs that describe situations where both the speaker and the hearer are involved in the speech activity, you can mention the hearer in a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{with}.
\begin{example}
    He agreed \underline{with us} that it would be better to have no break.
    
    Can you confirm \underline{with Ray} that this date is ok?
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that take the preposition \textit{with} if you mention the hearer:
\begin{example}
    agree, argue, confirm, plead, reason
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.80} With verbs that describe situations where someone is getting information from someone or something, you use a prepositional phrase beginning with \textit{from} to mention the source of the information.
\begin{example}
    I discovered from her that a woman prisoner had killed herself.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs where the source of the information is mentioned using \textit{from}:
\begin{example}
    discover, elicit, gather, hear, infer, learn, see
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.81} A \textbf{reflexive pronoun} is sometimes used as the object of a reporting verb or preposition in order to say what someone is thinking.

E.g., \textit{to say something to yourself} means to think it rather than to say it aloud.
\begin{example}
    I told \underline{myself} that he was crazy.
    
    It will soon be over, I kept saying to \underline{myself}.
\end{example}

\subsection{Other ways of indicating what is said}
\noindent\fbox{7.82} Sometimes you use a noun e.g. \textit{question, story}, or \textit{apology} to refer to what someone has said or written.

You can use a reporting verb with 1 of these nouns as its object instead of a reported clause.
\begin{example}
    He asked \underline{a number of questions}.
    
    Simon whispered \underline{his answer}.
    
    He told \underline{funny stories} and made everyone laugh.
    
    Philip repeated \underline{his invitation}.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that are often used with nouns that refer to something spoken or written:
\begin{example}
    accept, acknowledge, ask, begin, believe, continue, demand, deny, expect, explain, forget, guess, hear, imagine, know, lay out, learn, mention, mutter, note, notice, promise, refuse, remember, repeat, report, set down, shout, state, suggest, tell, understand, whisper, write
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.83} Some reporting verbs can have as their objects nouns that refer to events or facts.

These nouns are often closely related to verbs.

E.g., \textit{loss} is closely related to \textit{lose}, and instead of saying \textit{He admitted that he had lost his passport}, you can say \textit{He admitted the loss of his passport}.
\begin{example}
    British Airways announce the \underline{arrival} of flight BA 5531 from Glasgow.
    
    The company reported a 45 per cent \underline{drop} in profits.
\end{example}
Here is a list of reporting verbs that are often used with nouns that refer to events or facts:
\begin{example}
    accept, acknowledge, admit, announce, demand, describe, discover, discuss, doubt, expect, explain, fear, foresee, forget, imagine, mean, mention, note, notice, observe, predict, prefer, promise, recommend, record, remember, report, see, sense, suggest, urge
\end{example}
%
\noindent\fbox{7.84} Note that \textit{say} is usually only used with an object if the object is a very general word e.g. \textit{something, anything}, or \textit{nothing}.
\begin{example}
    I must have said something wrong.
    
    The man nodded but said nothing.
\end{example} 
%
\noindent\fbox{7.85} A few verbs referring to speech and thought can be used with a prepositional phrase rather than a reported clause, to indicate the general subject matter of a statement or thought.
\begin{example}
    Thomas explained \underline{about the request from Paris}.
\end{example}
Here are 3 lists of verbs that can be used with a prepositional phrase referring to a fact or subject.

In each list, the verbs in the 1st group are used without an object, and the verbs in the 2nd group are used with an object referring to the hearer.

Note that \textit{ask} and \textit{warn} can be used with or without an object.

%
The following verbs are used with \textit{about}:
\begin{example}
    agree, ask, boast, complain, decide, dream, explain, forget, grumble, hear, inquire, know, learn, mutter, read, wonder, worry, write
    
    ask, teach, tell, warn
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    No one knew \underline{about my interest in mathematics}.
    
    I asked him \underline{about the horses}.
\end{example}
The following verbs are used with \textit{of}:
\begin{example}
    complain, dream, hear, know, learn, read, think, warn, write
    
    assure, convince, inform, notify, persuade, reassure, remind, warn
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    They never complained \underline{of the incessant rain}.
    
    No one had warned us \underline{of the dangers}.
\end{example}
The following verbs are used with \textit{on}.

None of them take an object referring to the hearer.
\begin{example}
    agree, comment, decide, determine, insist, remark, report, write
\end{example}

\begin{example}
    He had already decided \underline{on his story}.
    
    They are insisting \underline{on the release of all political prisoners}.
\end{example}
Note that \textit{speak} and \textit{talk} are used with \textit{about} and \textit{of} but not with reported clauses.
